args: The patient was a 58-year-old non-smoking male Ohio native with hyperlipidemia, environmental allergies and no other known medical problems who presented to the emergency department within hours of his return from a ski trip to Breckenridge, Colorado (altitude 2926 m) complaining of dyspnea, orthopnea, dizziness, chest tightness and weakness in the pre-Covid-19 era. He developed mild dyspnea within 48 hours of arrival to Colorado but returned to Ohio after five days with increasing dyspnea that did not improve with descent. His initial evaluation revealed a pale mildly diaphoretic adult in severe respiratory distress who was able to speak in short sentences. His initial vital signs were blood pressure 189/101mmHG, temperature 37°C, respirations of 40 breaths per minute, and pulse rate of 112 beats per minute. Physical exam was significant for normal cardiac exam except tachycardia, increased work of breathing, conversational dyspnea, bibasilar rales, normal heart sounds, 2+ distal pulses without peripheral edema and mildly diaphoretic cool skin. Oxygen saturation on room air was 72% and chest X-ray demonstrated pulmonary edema without cardiomegaly (). Electrocardiogram revealed sinus tachycardia of 104 beats per minute with minimal flattening of the T-waves in V5 and V6. Lab work revealed an elevated white blood cell count of 21.6 Reference Interval (RI) (4.5–11.5E9/L), a normal hemoglobin of 13.7 RI (12.5–16.5g/dl) and an elevated brain natriuretic peptide level of 1031 RI (0–125pg/dl), three serial troponin levels were less than 0.01 RI (0.00–0.03ng/mL). D-dimer level was not obtained due to the pulmonary infiltrates and a basic metabolic panel was normal, as was a viral respiratory film array panel, and two sets of blood cultures. A trans-thoracic echocardiogram revealed normal valvular function and an ejection fraction of 65% with normal diastolic function. The echocardiogram did not include a pulmonary ultrasound and B-lines were not assessed. Pulmonary consultation noted rapid resolution of the pulmonary infiltrates as well as hypoxia with the emergency department treatment and a decision not to perform bronchoscopy was made. In the emergency department, nitroglycerine 0.4mg sublingually, extended-release nifedipine 30 mg orally, and dexamethasone 10mg intravenously were administered. A brief emergency department literature review led to this therapeutic regimen. Due to persistent dyspnea despite resolving hypoxia, he was placed on bilevel positive airway pressure (BIPAP) at 15/5 cmH2O with further improvement in his dyspnea. Due to the local lack of familiarity with HAPE, he was admitted to the intensive care unit where the BIPAP was continued until he could be weaned to room air and was subsequently discharged on no medications. He was counseled against further trips to high altitude without acclimatization.

args: A 47-year-old man presented with melena, abdominal pain, and syncope for two days. He had no prior history of gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding. The patient underwent IGB (ReShape Integrated Dual Balloon System; ReShape Medical, Inc., San Clemente, CA, USA) placement with the instillation of 900 mL of saline for weight loss five months prior to presentation. He had no past history of alcohol consumption, liver disease, or past surgeries. He was taking esomeprazole 40 mg delayed release oral capsules once daily and had recently taken ibuprofen 400 mg daily for the past five days for back pain. Vital signs included blood pressure 65/55 mm Hg, heart rate 98 beats/minute, O2 saturation 97% on room air, temperature 98.5°F, and respiratory rate 19 breaths/minute. His pre-Rockall score was 2. His hemoglobin dropped to a nadir of 7.1 gm/dL during hospitalization from a level of 14.3 gm/dL eight months prior to admission. The patient was admitted to the medical intensive care unit due to hemodynamic instability and was resuscitated with normal saline and one unit of packed red blood cells. Abdominal computed tomography showed two connected saline-filled gastric balloons (). He underwent esophagogastroduodenoscopy; a small 8 mm gastric ulcer Forrest class III) was found in the incisura, and the balloon was removed (). Biopsy revealed severe chronic gastritis and Helicobacter pylori on immunoperoxidase staining. He was discharged after a five-day hospital stay.

args:
A 28-year-old man presented with severe neck pain mainly on the left side with painful swallowing for 5 days. The patient initially had asked for medical advice at a primary health care facility and was treated with antibiotics and anti-inflammatory drugs, but there was no improvement and the pain was increasing in severity. On detailed history, the patient reported that he had an attack of choking and coughing, while he was eating chicken during dinner one day before the onset of symptoms.
On examination, there was a low-grade fever and tenderness in the left side of the neck, and the pain was exaggerated by swallowing attempts. A telescopic laryngeal examination was performed. Frothy saliva was detected accumulating in the left pyriform fossa. After giving the patient some few sips of water, the examination was repeated. A white linear chicken bone was seen impacted at the left pyriform fossa with a relatively free broad base pointing anteriorly, and the other tapering end was impacted in the posterior wall of the pyriform fossa (). To rule out development of a retropharyngeal abscess, anteroposterior and lateral X-ray views of the neck were performed for the patient. Apart from the chicken bone that was poorly visualized in the lateral X-ray view of the neck, there was no evidence of retropharyngeal abscess formation.
The bone was removed in an office-based setting under guidance of video laryngeal rigid telescopic examination. In this procedure, a video laryngeal examination was carried out using a 70-degree rigid telescope, 3CCD camera, LCD monitor, and a light source. The patient was seated in the upright position with his head supported from the back. Using a cotton swab soaked with Xylocaine 10% and held by a Magill's forceps, the patient's oro- and hypopharynx were adequately anesthetized. The patient was instructed to protrude his tongue and hold it by his hand with a piece of gauze. The telescope was held by the physician's left hand and introduced carefully into the oropharynx. When the foreign body was identified in the left pyriform fossa and clearly visualized on the monitor, it was carefully removed by a curved laryngeal forceps held by the right hand of the physician (). After that, the patient was prescribed oral antibiotics in the form of Amoxycillin-Clavulanic acid (1 gm b.i.d. for 10 days) to control possible infection and an analgesic and anti-inflammatory drug in the form of Diclofenac Sodium (50 mg b.i.d. for 10 days). Then, he was discharged with a scheduled followup appointment after 7 days. On the followup visit, the patient was completely improved with disappearance of the constitutional symptoms, no pain or tenderness on the neck and normal swallowing function.

args:
An 89-year-old male presented with a 5-day history of worsening dyspnoea and cough. Past history included hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, permanent pacemaker implantation for complete heart block, chronic kidney disease stage 3 and atrial fibrillation. He was taking long-term Rivaroxaban therapy for stroke and systemic embolism prophylaxis. No compliance issues were reported. No antiplatelet or additional antithrombotic medication was being taken prior to hospital admission. Despite advanced age, the gentleman was fully independent with activities of daily living. Hospital admission was required due to hypoxia. Maximal oxygen requirements during the course of admission were 4 l/min. Reverse transcriptase viral polymerase chain reaction testing subsequently confirmed COVID-19 infection.
Admission blood testing demonstrated lymphopenia (0.82 × 109/l) and a markedly elevated C-reactive protein of 345 mg/l. Coagulation studies were prolonged (activated partial thromboplastin time 31.7 s, prothrombin time 14.4 s) due to concurrent anticoagulation with Rivaroxaban. Fibrinogen level was markedly elevated at 9 g/l. Creatinine (106 μmol/l) and estimated glomerular filtration rate (52 ml/min) were at the patient’s baseline level. Remaining biochemistry results returned within normal limits. Chest radiography confirmed new bi-basal and peripheral left mid zone pulmonary infiltrates suggestive of COVID-19 pneumonitis (). Oral doxycycline was commenced to cover for possible secondary superadded bacterial infection, in line with local institutional protocols during the early period of the COVID-19 pandemic.
On Day 4 of his hospital stay, and despite an improvement in COVID-19 symptoms with reducing oxygen requirements, the patient developed a rapid and dense left-sided hemiplegia with dysarthria, alongside clinical signs of left gaze palsy and left homonymous hemianopia. National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale was 21, signifying symptoms suggestive of acute severe stroke. Within 1 h of onset of neurological symptoms (Day 4 of hospital admission), computed tomography imaging of the brain was performed. This showed a region of low attenuation in the right basal ganglia and high density in the right M1 segment of the middle cerebral artery consistent with an acute infarct due to large-vessel occlusion (). Computed tomography angiography was requested to evaluate a potential target for mechanical thrombectomy. This demonstrated extensive thrombus occluding the right cervical internal carotid artery and a T occlusion affecting the right anterior and middle cerebral arteries ().
The patient was not a candidate for thrombolytic therapy due to concurrent anticoagulation with Rivaroxaban. Mechanical thrombectomy was discussed with interventional radiology colleagues, but it was decided that this would offer little clinical benefit given the patient’s age and comorbid status. Unfortunately, despite maximal ward-based medical treatment, the patient deteriorated and passed away 5 days later.

args: A 64-year-old female was brought to the emergency department after being found slumped in the passenger seat of her vehicle outside a small convenience store by a passerby who noticed that she did not respond to repeated knocks on the window. According to the initial report from the individual who summoned assistance, she had been sitting there in the same position for an undetermined amount of time, with her head resting against the window and no observable reactions when they attempted to garner her attention through polite gestures. On arrival, paramedics observed that she made no effort to open her eyelids in response to normal conversation or louder vocal prompts, and even gentle attempts at nudging her shoulder elicited minimal reaction aside from occasional slow murmurs that suggested she was at least partially aware of her surroundings. She did manage to speak in incomplete but coherent phrases at times, responding to basic inquiries about how she felt, yet the content of her answers showed clear signs of disorientation. When asked about the current day or location, she provided statements that were confusing or unrelated, such as referencing an unrelated setting she believed she had just visited earlier, which conflicted with the eyewitness account that she had been parked at that convenience store for well over an hour. Her mental status appeared muddled, as she repeated some questions back and then trailed off into silence, giving no firm grasp of the reality of the situation. Despite this perplexed speech pattern, when explicitly directed to perform simple motor tasks, she demonstrated an ability to comply with specific requests. For instance, she could raise her right arm when asked, and later, she was able to release and reapply her grip on a nurse’s hand upon repeated instruction. These actions indicated that her strength and coordination were at least somewhat intact and that she was able to comprehend spoken commands on a rudimentary level. However, she continued to keep her eyes firmly shut through most of the examination, remaining passive and largely still, except when asked to move or shift as part of the clinician’s assessment. Her past medical records, retrieved from an old hospital card located in her wallet, mentioned a prior hospitalization several years ago for an ischemic stroke that allegedly left her with mild residual weakness in her left leg, although there was no documentation of any severe long-term mobility limitations. She also had a documented history of labile blood pressure, often swinging towards the higher end of normal, for which she had been prescribed medication in the past. It was unclear whether she had been adhering to that medication regimen in recent months. Family members, who were reached by telephone shortly after her arrival, mentioned that she had occasionally reported episodes of dizziness and general fatigue, but they had not noticed any acute changes in her mental status until just a few days prior, when she started to appear withdrawn and less interactive during phone calls. She reportedly had some difficulty recalling recent conversations, although she was not formally evaluated for cognitive decline. On direct questioning in the emergency department—prior to any administration of sedatives, analgesics, or other treatments—she sometimes managed a mumbled acknowledgment that she was uncomfortable but displayed no voluntary attempt to open her eyes or appropriately orient herself to the exam area. Repeated tests of her neurological function showed that she could be prompted to execute tasks involving intentional movement of limbs, such as bending her arm or lifting her foot, yet she never lifted her eyelids spontaneously or displayed any apparent reaction to vivid visual stimuli passing in front of her face. There was no active resistance to gentle manipulations, and when her blood pressure cuff inflated, she followed a request to hold her arm still and then release it on command. Her vitals revealed a heart rate slightly above normal, with borderline elevated pressure readings and respirations that were steady but somewhat shallow, though not urgently concerning. No significant cuts or bruises were found on her head or neck, and she did not complain of headache when queried. A more comprehensive workup was launched, including blood tests to check for renal function imbalances or possible infectious processes, imaging studies to rule out a new cerebrovascular event, and a toxicological screen to exclude possible external contributors. Throughout the initial evaluation period, she continued to speak in short, jumbled statements that hinted at an underlying confusion, sometimes contradicting her previously stated words without seeming to realize the disparity. Her expression remained passive, and she maintained a lack of visual engagement, even when staff gently raised her eyelids to assess pupil size and reaction. While she did show a positive response to direct commands that required purposeful limb movement, her overall presentation was one of perplexity and reduced awareness, warranting immediate investigation into potential causes of this change in mental status and persistent lack of visual opening.

args:
An 18-year-old Black female who had been diagnosed with SCD in childhood, subsequently suffering frequent pain crises and undergoing repeated transfusions, presented to the emergency room with a 21-day history of progressive lower limb oedema accompanied by dyspnoea and orthopnoea. In addition, she reported a 3-day history of severe chest pain, together with a productive cough and fever as high as 38 °C. She reported no reduction in urine volume and no recent history of skin infection or tonsillitis. On physical examination, she was found to be very pallid and normotensive (blood pressure, 130/80 mmHg), with a heart rate of 104 bpm, a respiratory rate of 28 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation of 94% on room air, as well as being found to have developed anasarca. One month prior, she had undergone outpatient laboratory tests, which had shown that her renal function was normal—with a serum creatinine concentration of 0.75 mg/dl and an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), as determined by the Chronic Kidney Disease–Epidemiology Collaboration (CKD–EPI) equation, of 134.9 ml/min/1.73 m2—urine analysis showing no haematuria or proteinuria.
Blood tests performed at admission to the emergency room revealed the following: haemoglobin, 6.1 g/dl; haematocrit, 18.8%; haptoglobin, < 10 mg/dl; reticulocyte fraction, 6.11%; no schistocytes; lactate dehydrogenase, 433 U/L; indirect bilirubin, 0.36 mg/dl; leucocyte count, 18,820 cells/mm3; neutrophil count, 13,630 cells/mm3; lymphocyte count, 2790 cells/mm3; platelet count, 622;000 cells/mm3; and BUN, 29 mg/dl. The patient showed signs of hyperfiltration, including a serum creatinine concentration of 0.49 mg/dl and a CKD–EPI-determined eGFR of 148.5 ml/min/1.73 m2, although no hydroelectrolytic or acid-base disorders were identified. A urine sample was found to contain 100 leucocytes/field and 70 erythrocytes/field. In addition, 24-h proteinuria was 8.99 g. Further analysis of the blood samples showed the following: total proteins, 3.9 g/dl; albumin, 1.2 g/dl; globulins, 2.7 g/dl; total cholesterol, 210 mg/dl, low-density lipoprotein, 114 mg/dl; high-density lipoprotein, 37 mg/dl; triglycerides, 205 mg/dl; C4, 26.1 mg/dl (normal range, 10–38 mg/dl); C3, 62 mg/dl (normal range, 73–149 mg/dl); C-reactive protein, 24.9 mg/dl (normal range, < 5 mg/dl). The serology was negative for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C. The patient also tested negative for antinuclear factor, anti-DNA, rheumatoid factor, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies. Serum protein electrophoresis showed hypoalbuminaemia and an anti-streptolysin O level of 816 IU/ml (normal range, < 200 IU/ml). Blood and urine cultures were negative. A chest X-ray showed mild congestion and infiltrate in the right lung base. Ultrasound of the kidneys and urinary tract showed that the kidneys were normal in size and appearance.
Given the clinical presentation, the diagnostic hypothesis was pneumonia and anasarca due to nephritic-nephrotic syndrome. The patient was started on ceftriaxone (1 g, twice daily). Computed tomography angiography of the chest ruled out pulmonary thromboembolism and acute chest syndrome, thus indirectly confirming the diagnosis of pneumonia.
After the infection had been controlled, the patient underwent renal biopsy. Light microscopy revealed 7 glomeruli with global endocapillary proliferation; neutrophil and macrophage infiltrate (Fig. a); synechiae in 10% of the glomeruli; occasional splitting of the glomerular basement membrane; and a preserved Bowman’s capsule. The tubules were dilated, with foci of epithelial regeneration and neutrophils within their lumina. There was focal oedema in the interstitium. The interlobular arteries had a normal aspect, with intimal proliferation in the arterioles. Immunofluorescence performed on frozen sections showed intensity of deposition of C3 (2+/3+), IgG (1+/3+), IgA (1+/3+) and lambda light chains (1+/3+), with a granular pattern, distributed diffusely throughout the capillary loop. Collectively, the histological findings were consistent with a diagnosis of APIGN accompanied by microangiopathic changes related to the underlying disease.
Treatment with a diuretic and an anti-proteinuric agent (angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor) was instituted, and the patient evolved to clinical improvement (normalisation of blood pressure, as well as resolution of the proteinuria and haematuria). However, the signs of hyperfiltration persisted even after resolution of the proteinuria. In the final evaluation, the serum creatinine concentration was 0.33 mg/dl and the CKD–EPI-determined eGFR was 187.8 ml/min/1.73 m2. Evolution of laboratorial tests are available in Fig. .

A 72-year-old Caucasian (white) man with a history of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), immunoglobin M (IgM) monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS), and chronic back pain secondary to osteoarthritis presented with dyspnea and a left pleural effusion. His social history was notable for a 60 pack-year cigarette smoking, and he had cut down to five cigarettes a day after diagnosis of non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (NHL). He denied any significant past alcohol abuse, illicit drug abuse, or prescription drug abuse and reported consuming a beer or two on rare social occasions. Our patient was adopted so he is unaware of any past family history in regard to his parents. His brother has high blood pressure; otherwise he reported no other known malignancy or cardiopulmonary disease in the family. He worked as a mechanic and retired 20 years ago. He denied any known environmental exposures or allergies. Home medications included a daily probiotic, florastor 250 mg 2 tablets once daily, and acetaminophen/oxycodone – 325 mg/5 tablets once a day as needed for severe back pain. Our patient explained that he took opiate pain medication two to three times a week. Seven years prior, a bone marrow (BM) biopsy had revealed DLBCL, stage IVB, and he achieved complete remission (CR) with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone (R-CHOP). Two years prior, he was diagnosed with monoclonal IgM kappa MGUS and BM biopsy revealed B-cell lymphoma with plasmacytic differentiation (10% of the marrow cellularity). The differential diagnosis at this time included LPL and marginal zone lymphoma. Given that he was asymptomatic at this time, he was monitored without treatment.
In the Emergency Department (ED), his vital signs were a pulse of 88 beats per minute, his blood pressure was 145/62, and temperature was 98.7 degrees Fahrenheit. His oxygen saturation was 95% on room air and he was mildly tachypneic with a respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute. At physical examination, he appeared well, he was well nourished, awake, and in no apparent distress. An ear, nose, mouth and throat examination showed clear nasal mucosa and normal mouth mucosa. His head was atraumatic/normocephalic. His heart rate was normal, with a regular rhythm, heart sounds were S1, S2, and there were no murmurs, rubs or gallops. There were rales in his left lung base without wheeze or rhonchi. His abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. His lumbar spine showed a mild reduction in range of motion. He showed superficial bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. He was alert and oriented × 4, with no focal deficits, and no motor or sensory deficits. His CN II-XII were intact, extremities showed no cyanosis or edema, and his skin was dry, intact, with no rash. Our patient was given one ipratropium bromide/albuterol nebulizer treatment and two doses of morphine 1 mg for back pain. During the admission, our patient received tramadol 50 mg once for back pain and required no other medication. His complete blood count showed a white blood cell count of 6.2 × 109/L, hemoglobin of 13.8 g/dL, platelet count of 156 × 109/L with normal differential. The complete metabolic panel showed sodium of 141 mEq/L, potassium of 3.8 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen of 18 mg/dL and creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL, glucose of 95 mg/dL, calcium of 9.2, albumin 3.8 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase of 23 IU/L, and alanine aminotransferase of 32 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase of 46, and total bilirubin of 0.6. The urinalysis was within normal limits with no evidence of hematuria, pyuria, nitrates were not detected, and there were no bacteria. A computed tomography scan of his chest (Fig. ), abdomen, and pelvis showed layering left pleural effusion and para-aortic retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy extending to the iliacs and superficial inguinal nodes. Pleural fluid (PF) cytology was positive for malignant cells consistent with B-cell lymphoma and positive for MYD88 L265P mutation.
PF flow cytometry showed monoclonal kappa B-cell population with moderate CD19, CD20, CD22, and CD38 expression (Fig. ).
Inguinal lymph node biopsy showed lymphoplasmacytic, monoclonal B-cell population, CD20-positive, CD5-negative, CD10-negative B-cells, consistent with LPL/WM (Fig. ).
In comparison to his BM biopsy 2 years prior, the current study shows an increase in marrow involvement by B-cell lymphoma with plasmacytic differentiation. The findings in conjunction with IgM monoclonal paraprotein favor LPL, and the positive MYD88 L265P mutation analysis supports this impression. Congo red stain was negative for amyloid (Fig. ).
The pleural effusion recurred post-thoracentesis and 420 mg ibrutinib was initiated, but due to intolerance with nausea and vomiting, required a dose reduction to 240 mg. He achieved a lasting CR as defined by National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guidelines []. Seven months after initial presentation to the ED, he remains in CR and is tolerating a reduced dose of imantinib without any side effects.

args: A 41-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department by her family, who reported that she had been found unresponsive on the floor of her living room approximately one hour prior to arrival. According to those present at the scene, she had been complaining of a severe headache earlier in the day, though they were unable to specify the nature or onset of her discomfort. On arrival, her vital signs showed a heart rate slightly above normal limits, with blood pressure on the higher end of her usual range based on prior medical documentation. Her respirations were notably shallow, and her oxygen saturation required supplemental oxygen to remain at acceptable levels. She has a past medical history including mild hypertension diagnosed a few years ago, but no significant prior neurological events. Family also mentioned that she had not been feeling well for the past two days, perhaps due to a viral illness, though there was no clear indication that she had sought medical care for it. Examination at the time of presentation revealed that she neither opened her eyes spontaneously nor did she visibly respond when spoken to in a normal voice. Repeated attempts to engage her by calling her name and clapping near her ears were unsuccessful, although she did show a reaction when firm pressure was applied to her sternum and supraorbital notch, indicating a complete lack of spontaneous visual attention but some response to more intense stimuli. When these painful stimuli were applied, her limbs stiffened and rotated outward in a pattern that suggested a severe lesion in the brain stem or upper motor tracts rather than a purposeful withdrawal or flexion. This posture raised immediate concern among the emergency team, as it typically points to a more advanced neurological compromise. On further assessment of her speech and interaction, she was able to utter individual words that did not form coherent sentences or appropriate replies to questions. For instance, when asked about her name, she replied with random expressions unrelated to personal details or the environment, and she quickly descended into incomprehensible vocalizations that were neither groans nor normal language. She seemed incapable of maintaining any meaningful conversation, and there was no sign of consistent orientation to person, place, or time. Her pupils were moderately sized and reactive to light, but their reaction was sluggish, and there was no symmetrical tracking of objects placed before her eyes. A quick survey of her cardiovascular and pulmonary systems yielded no immediate abnormalities aside from the elevated heart rate and the need for oxygen supplementation. Skin turgor was normal, and there were no distinct signs of trauma on her head or cervical spine, though she did have an older scar on her forehead that might have been from a previous injury, according to her family’s report of past falls. No significant bruising was found along the torso or extremities, and she did not have any obvious contusions or lacerations that might suggest a recent external injury. At the time of this initial evaluation, she was not moving spontaneously and appeared unable to follow any commands, even simple ones such as showing two fingers or moving a thumb. Her reflexes were unremarkable except for the abnormal posture elicited by painful stimuli. Based on these clinical findings, the team recognized an urgent need for further imaging and laboratory work to explore possible causes, including intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke, or other acute neurological insults. Efforts were made to stabilize her airway and circulation while obtaining more diagnostic information, but no specific therapeutic interventions had been provided before this detailed neurologic evaluation was completed.

args: A 42-year-old woman with no history of venous thromboembolism, malignancy, chronic heart or kidney disease, or connective-tissue disorders walked into the emergency department this afternoon because her entire right lower extremity had felt “heavy and bloated” since the previous evening. She recalls stepping out of her car after a thirty-minute commute from work yesterday when she noticed her right shoe felt snugger than usual; by the time she prepared for bed the fullness had crept from the ankle to the groin, making her jeans difficult to remove and leaving a faint impression of her sock cuff around the malleolus. She denies fever, chills, sweats, rigors, loss of appetite, unintended weight change, insect bites, trauma, or recent skin abrasions. She also denies chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, shortness of breath, palpitations, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, flank pain, or neurological deficits. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable for rash, joint swelling, urinary urgency, or menstrual abnormalities. Five weeks earlier she underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy for symptomatic leiomyomas; the procedure lasted two and a half hours under general anesthesia, required a single overnight stay, and she was ambulating in the ward the same evening. Since discharge she has resumed her job as a receptionist at a recreational vehicle dealership, alternating between standing at the front counter and walking the showroom; she has not required assistive devices or prolonged periods in bed and she completed the prescribed postoperative incentive-spirometry and ambulation goals without issue. She has taken only acetaminophen 650 mg as needed for incisional discomfort and a daily combined calcium–vitamin D supplement; she is on no hormonal therapy, corticosteroids, anticoagulants, or erythropoiesis-stimulating agents. Her last long-distance travel was a two-hour flight three months ago for a family reunion. She never smoked, consumes wine socially once or twice per month, and does not use recreational drugs. Both parents are alive without cardiovascular or thrombotic disease, and no siblings or children have experienced clots or bleeding disorders. On arrival she appeared well and conversed comfortably but was concerned about the asymmetry of her legs. Vital signs at triage: temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs were clear to auscultation and percussion. The abdomen was soft with healed 1-cm trocar scars in the infra-umbilical and suprapubic regions, nontender and without palpable masses. Examination of the lower extremities showed a noticeable enlargement of the entire right limb from mid-thigh to dorsum of the foot; the mid-femoral circumference measured 54 cm on the right and 50 cm on the left, while 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38 cm on the right and 36.5 cm on the left. The overlying skin was warm and taut but retained normal color without streaking, ecchymosis, or ulceration. When steady thumb pressure was applied for five seconds over the anterior tibia, a 3-mm indentation slowly refilled over approximately 15 seconds; no such indentation could be produced on the contralateral limb. There were no distended subcutaneous venous channels, varicosities, or palpable cords along the course of the great saphenous, femoral, popliteal, or posterior tibial veins. Gentle palpation of those tracks elicited only a diffuse sense of tightness rather than focal tenderness, and dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain. Hip, knee, and ankle joints moved through full active and passive range without crepitus or guarding. Distal pulses—posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis—were 2+ bilaterally with brisk capillary refill; sensation to light touch, vibration, and pinprick was intact, and motor strength was 5/5 throughout both lower limbs. The inguinal lymph nodes were not enlarged, and there was no groin hernia. There were no signs of cellulitis, lymphedema from prior lymph-node dissection, musculoskeletal strain, or ruptured Baker cyst. Point-of-care laboratory tests obtained before any therapy revealed hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 243 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, electrolytes within reference range, international normalized ratio 1.0, activated partial thromboplastin time 28 s, and C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/dL. Electrocardiography displayed normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. A plain radiograph of the right femur ordered to exclude occult fracture showed only soft-tissue fullness. Given the acute, unilateral, whole-limb swelling with demonstrable pitting confined to that leg, the recent pelvic surgery performed within the past six weeks, the absence of visible collateral cutaneous veins, and the lack of a more compelling musculoskeletal, infectious, or inflammatory explanation, there is substantial concern for obstruction within the deep venous system of the right lower extremity. Compression duplex ultrasonography of the entire right leg has been requested urgently, and baseline laboratory values were documented before consideration of anticoagulation.

args: A 47-year-old male presents with persistent left knee discomfort that has worsened over the past three months, prompting an evaluation for possible arthroscopic repair. He describes pain that began insidiously, without a clear history of traumatic injury, and reports that conservative measures such as rest, activity modification, and over-the-counter analgesics have provided minimal relief. He has maintained a fairly active lifestyle until recently, when his discomfort started limiting his ability to exercise and climb stairs. Notably, he continues to walk independently and does not require assistive devices. He denies any history of severe recent infection, including episodes of intractable fever or serious respiratory problems, and states he has not been hospitalized in the last few weeks for any overwhelming inflammatory or infectious condition. He has no indication of an acute neurological deficit, denies having experienced any form of sudden limb weakness or acute numbness, and mentions no concern regarding abrupt changes in speech or cognition. He also denies any recent need for specific casting or rigid immobilization of the leg, and clarifies that he has not required splints, braces, or specialized supports that would substantially reduce his mobility. Physical examination of the lower extremities reveals no abnormal superficial venous distention, although he does exhibit mild swelling concentrated around the left lower leg, particularly near the ankle, where there is mild pitting edema but intact distal pulses. The contralateral leg shows no obvious edema. Beyond his current knee issues, he reports no background of serious lung problems or sudden episodes of extreme shortness of breath that might suggest a past pulmonary event. He has never been told he had a blood clot in his legs or lungs in the past, and he has not required therapeutic anticoagulation beyond occasional short courses for minor ailments or prophylaxis during travel. He does, however, report that a close blood relative had a notable history of clot formation at a relatively early age, and he is aware that there may be some familial predisposition to such events. Despite that, he states he has never tested positive for any known hereditary issue pertaining to factor mutations or specific markers of dysregulated coagulation, and he denies ever receiving a medical diagnosis indicating a unique predisposition to clotting. He has not shown evidence of autoimmune coagulation problems, nor has he suffered adverse reactions during prior occasions when he was briefly exposed to heparin in dental or minor procedural contexts. His mobility appears fully intact, and upon questioning, he confirms he is able to complete the majority of his basic and more strenuous daily activities if not for the knee pain. He has no persistent gastrointestinal symptoms and does not describe any chronic conditions that would suggest an ongoing inflammatory disorder in his bowels. Regarding cardiac status, he has never been informed by a physician that he had an ischemic event suggestive of heart tissue damage, nor has he required urgent invasive cardiac evaluation. He indicates no personal history of tumor or malignant disease, and he has not undergone chemotherapy, radiation, or major oncologic surgery in the past. Currently, he does not harbor any vascular catheter or intravenous device, and his vitals remain stable, with no fevers, stable blood pressure, and a heart rate within normal limits. His primary complaint remains the unrelenting left knee discomfort, which interferes with sports and labor-intensive tasks. After clinical assessment and imaging, an arthroscopic intervention has been proposed to address what appears to be structural pathology within the knee, likely involving a meniscal lesion. Given his unyielding pain and the effect on mobility, he expresses readiness to proceed with operative management, pending final clearance and risk assessment. Overall, he appears well-nourished, of normal body size, and without external signs of systemic disease aside from the localized swelling in the affected limb. He is hopeful that the planned procedure will allow him to resume his usual level of function and alleviate the activity-limiting knee problems that have progressively curtailed his ability to remain physically active.

args: A 69‑year‑old male arrived at the hospital complaining of several days of persistent fatigue, mild confusion, and reduced exercise tolerance. He had been living independently until recently, but neighbors noticed him becoming unusually forgetful and withdrawn over the past week. At the time of presentation, he did not require the placement of a breathing tube to secure his airway, yet he was observed to be wearing a specialized mask that delivered continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) with a fraction of inspired oxygen of 31 %. Staff measured his arterial oxygen concentration while he was receiving that support and found a PaO₂ of 91 mm Hg, suggesting that his gas exchange, though not severely compromised, required close observation. When further examined, he displayed sluggish verbal responses and difficulty staying focused on questions, indicating a moderate degree of mental‑status impairment rather than complete unresponsiveness. Vital signs on arrival showed blood pressure at 120/76 mm Hg and a regular pulse. Laboratory testing revealed a platelet count of 125 000 /µL, modestly decreased compared with standard thresholds, though still sufficient to avoid spontaneous bleeding. Total bilirubin measured 2.9 mg/dL, prompting a search for possible hemolysis, hepatic distress, or another process that might cause such a pattern of laboratory derangements; he indeed showed slight scleral icterus. Renal studies showed a serum creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL, higher than expected in a healthy adult male of his age, though not so high as to suggest end‑stage compromise. Nursing documentation confirmed a 24‑hour urine output of 1,100 mL/day, recognized as borderline yet not severely reduced, consistent with mild or moderate impairment of kidney filtration. His Glasgow  Coma  Score was 10. No widespread rash or localized infections were evident on exam, and the patient reported no recent hospitalizations or procedures, though he struggled to recall many details about his medical history during questioning. He was fully vaccinated against common respiratory pathogens, according to a relative, reducing suspicion for certain causes of acute pulmonary infection, but that possibility could not be definitively ruled out without further investigation. On physical examination, his chest revealed relatively clear breath sounds, though the CPAP mask was helping to maintain stable oxygen levels. He did not appear to be working excessively hard to breathe, and he maintained an adequate rate of ventilation without signs of accessory‑muscle use or overt fatigue. Heart sounds were generally normal, with no distinct murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Abdominal inspection was significant for mild right‑upper‑quadrant tenderness on palpation, possibly correlating with the elevated bilirubin. Neurologically, he was inconsistent in following commands and sometimes responded only after the examiner repeated questions several times, though he retained basic awareness of his identity. In the absence of focal neurologic deficits, a thorough search for electrolyte imbalances, infections, or toxic causes of his confusion was initiated. Blood cultures, imaging of his chest and abdomen, and a panel of metabolic studies were ordered, along with plans to monitor his urinary output and repeat hepatic indicators. Given his moderate ability to communicate, the care team opted for close observation rather than invasive support, while remaining vigilant for any signs of deteriorating respiratory effort or cardiovascular stability. The overall impression was of a complex interplay involving moderate neurologic dysfunction, laboratory signals of potential liver involvement, and mild renal decline, all occurring without overt circulatory collapse.

args:
A 52-year-old male presented to the emergency department with four days of sore throat, odynophagia, and productive cough with clear-white, blood-tinged sputum. He also noted unintentional weight loss of approximately 10 pounds over the past three months but denied dyspnea, night sweats, fever, chills, or prior pulmonary complaints. Additional complaints included nausea, vomiting, polydipsia, and polyuria for the past few days after he stopped taking his long-acting insulin to adjust for decreased oral intake. He was sent to the emergency department by his primary care physician for abnormalities on chest X-ray taken the previous day, at which time he had been prescribed cephalexin empirically for suspected community acquired pneumonia.
His past medical history was significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus controlled with metformin and long-acting insulin without previous hospitalizations. He immigrated to the United States approximately 40 years ago from Central America and had never left the United States since. He works as short-distance truck driver and previously smoked heavily for a decade before quitting 20 years ago.
Upon arrival to the emergency department, his vital signs included blood pressure 123/90 mmHg, heart rate 134 beats per minute, respiratory rate 40 breaths per minute, temperature 97.8°F, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. His physical exam revealed a cachectic man in mild distress with tachypnea, without nasal flaring or retractions, with dry mucous membranes, and hoarse voice. Lung auscultation revealed diffuse bilateral crackles. Cardiac exam revealed tachycardia with regular rhythm. Abdominal exam did not demonstrate hepatosplenomegaly or tenderness. There were no other significant physical findings on examination.
His chest X-ray revealed diffuse bilateral reticulonodular opacities and airspace disease with a possible lingular cavitary lesion (). A thoracic CT without contrast was obtained which revealed numerous, bilateral tiny nodular densities, most prominently in the upper lobes, and confirmed the lingular cavitary lesion (). A few scattered mediastinal lymph nodes were also noted. Serum chemistry was remarkable for an anion gap of 35, beta-hydroxybutyrate of 8.09 mmol/L, and blood glucose of 532 mg/dL. Hematology showed a white blood cell count of 13 000/μL with 0% eosinophils. Arterial blood gas analysis revealed a pH of 7.13, pCO2 of 32, and lactic acid of 5.46 mmol/L. Urinalysis was positive for 4+ glucose and 3+ acetone. Later, HIV antigen testing for types 1 and 2 was negative and CD4 count was 343 cells/μL. Vancomycin 1 g and piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375 g were given intravenously in the emergency department. Intravenous insulin was started with dose adjustment according to an in-house nursing algorithm alongside vigorous hydration.
He was admitted to the ICU on airborne isolation with differential diagnoses including coccidioidomycosis, tuberculosis, and lymphangitic carcinomatosis, complicated by diabetic ketoacidosis. In the ICU, empiric antifungal therapy with fluconazole 800 mg/day was started intravenously for suspected pulmonary Coccidioides infection. Although serum anion gap and bicarbonate normalized on day two, he remained tachycardic and tachypneic and required supplemental oxygen. He also continued to have odynophagia that necessitated enteral tube feeding until hospital day five, when he was finally able to tolerate an oral diet. At this point, he developed a mild elevation of transaminases and fluconazole was changed to itraconazole 200 mg twice daily due to concern for hepatotoxicity, and abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography (CT) were obtained to rule out hepatic involvement from the suspected fungal infection. Both of these studies were negative for ascites, focal hypoattenuation within the liver, or hepatic or splenic enlargement. The CT additionally did not demonstrate abdominal adenopathy. Aminotransferases trended from their peak on hospital day three to normal on day eight.
A week after admission, he remained tachypneic, hypoxic, and hoarse. Three sputum samples were negative for acid-fast bacilli but Coccidioides antibody titer and complement fixation had not yet resulted; thus, bronchoscopy was performed, revealing irregular-appearing borders of false and true vocal cords with erythema and edema, two vocal cord polyps, inflamed upper trachea in the subglottic space, and several flesh-colored endobronchial lesions bilaterally. Forceps biopsies and brushings were performed on the endobronchial lesions, true and false cords, and posterior trachea just below the larynx. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) was performed in the right middle lobe and lingula. BAL fluid cell count and differential showed a neutrophilic predominance with spherules and endospores, consistent with Coccidioides immitis or Rhinosporidium seeberi. The biopsy specimens demonstrated the same pathogen in the laryngeal tissue. The culture of the BAL fluid eventually grew Coccidioides immitis on the day prior to discharge (day 14), but the laryngeal tissue was not cultured.
Otolaryngology consultant recommended outpatient follow-up with consideration for laryngoscopy after resolution of his acute illness. Two days after bronchoscopy, Coccidioides antibody titer returned positive at 1 : 16. The infectious disease consultant recommended continuing itraconazole for a minimum of 12 months with serial monitoring of Coccidioides titer, transaminases, and itraconazole level every three months. He continually improved on antifungal therapy until successfully weaning from supplemental oxygen and discharge after two weeks of hospitalization.
Coccidioides complement fixation returned strongly positive at 1 : 1024 nine days after discharge. Histoplasma urine antigen was positive but below the limit of quantification. At his follow-up appointments two and twelve weeks after discharge, his productive cough was present but improved and his hoarseness, dyspnea, and odynophagia had resolved. His liver enzymes remain within normal limits. At time of writing, the patient had not yet returned to have his Coccidioides titer or itraconazole level measured.

calculator_name: PSI Score: Pneumonia Severity Index for CAP

args: A 64-year-old male presented to the emergency department from an extended-care facility with three days of productive cough, low-grade chills, and a sense of breathlessness that had gradually worsened. At the time of arrival, he was alert, oriented to person, place, and time, and in no apparent distress beyond his labored breathing, reporting mild fatigue but denying any confusion or clouded thinking. He described a background of an event years ago that resulted in transient weakness and difficulty speaking, though he had largely recovered with no persistent deficits. He also reported no lingering concerns related to blood circulation in his heart or fluid accumulation that would impact his oxygenation, recalling no episodes of significant swelling or fluid overload in the past. He denied ever receiving treatment for any worrisome growths and reported no recollection of medical instructions regarding long-term kidney problems. According to the staff at his place of residence, he was previously ambulatory with minimal assistance and had maintained stable health until this recent febrile illness with increased sputum production. He stated that he had been generally consistent with his diet and fluid intake, taking care to avoid salty foods but without any major dietary restrictions. On examination prior to any interventions, his pulse was measured at 92 beats per minute, blood pressure was recorded as 112 mm Hg systolic, and he was breathing at about 21 breaths per minute. He appeared warm to the touch, with his core temperature measured at 38.7 degrees Celsius. His oxygen level was assessed through arterial testing, revealing a partial pressure of 90 mm Hg, which was concerning given his complaint of breathlessness. Additional laboratory evaluation showed a pH of 7.39, indicating no major disturbance in acid-base balance at presentation. His blood urea nitrogen concentration was 28 mg/dL, prompting closer observation, although there was no known prior indication that his kidneys were compromised. His serum sodium was found to be 141 mEq/L, and a hematocrit measurement of 39% suggested that his red blood cell volume was adequate for tissue oxygenation. His serum glucose was 92 mg/dL, suggesting stable glycemic control in the setting of his acute illness. He reported no nausea or vomiting, and abdomen and extremities were unremarkable on gross examination. On auscultation of the lungs, there were coarse breath sounds and some dullness at the right lung base. A chest radiograph confirmed a patchy opacity in the affected region along with evidence of fluid track in the pleural space, which correlated with his clinical presentation of recent respiratory symptoms. Despite these findings, he was cooperative and followed commands without difficulty, showing no overt neurological deficits on basic screening. He remained hemodynamically stable, with no indications of a chronic circulatory disorder that would compromise cardiac function. In conversation, he displayed no memory lapses, was able to recount events of his recent illness, and stayed awake and engaged throughout the encounter. He did note that he frequently relied on staff at his living facility for medication compliance and transportation to medical appointments, though he managed some tasks independently. Overall, given his fever, elevated respiratory rate, and the radiographic findings in conjunction with lab results at the time of presentation, he was deemed to have an acute infection in his lungs that demanded prompt attention.

args: A 53-year-old male arrived at the emergency department in severe respiratory distress, transferred from a smaller facility where he had been placed on a form of advanced airway intervention due to his inability to maintain adequate oxygenation on minimal support. According to the paramedics, his level of alertness had declined en route, with him barely responding to painful stimuli and failing to follow instructions, suggestive of a notable reduction in mental clarity at the time of presentation. On formal neurologic assessment, his Glasgow  Coma  Score was  7. The arterial blood gas sample obtained upon arrival indicated a partial pressure of oxygen of 74  mm Hg range in the face of a supplemental oxygen concentration of 28 % (FiO₂ 0.28), underscoring the severity of his respiratory compromise. In addition, he received a specialized mode of respiratory assistance that provides a constant level of increased pressure in his airways, presumably to help improve alveolar recruitment. Clinicians initiated dopamine at  4 mcg/kg/min to support or enhance cardiac output in this critically ill individual. Laboratory evaluations at the time of his admission showed a platelet count in the 180,000 µL, which was neither severely depressed nor indicative of pronounced risk for hemorrhage. His bilirubin level measured at 2.2 mg/dL, which corresponded with the presence of subtle jaundice noted on examination and raised questions about underlying hepatic dysfunction or possible hemolysis. While no overt chronic liver disease had been previously documented, he did endorse occasional alcohol use and had recently complained of a decreased appetite. Further labs revealed that his renal status was around the upper range of normal for his demographic, with a creatinine reading of 1 mg/dL, and his reported daily urine output—based on a measurement conducted before any significant therapeutic intervention—was  1800 milliliters, suggesting reasonable kidney performance despite his acute presentation. Clinicians performed a careful review of his cardiovascular findings, affirming that he had a stable pulse and no signs of profound shock, which was reassuring considering the severity of his neurologic and pulmonary problems. Imaging of his thorax pointed to possible bilateral processes, although the precise etiology was not immediately evident, prompting further investigations to rule out a severe infectious or inflammatory trigger. The decision was made to keep him on the advanced airway modality with continuous baseline pressure to optimize oxygenation while allowing for close monitoring of his neurological status, which was compromised enough to warrant concern about airway protection without mechanical support. His depressed consciousness, accompanied by limited eye opening and minimal vocalization, raised the urgency to secure imaging of his central nervous system to exclude any acute intracranial insult. Given the combination of mild hyperbilirubinemia, moderate respiratory failure, and depressed cognition, he was rapidly triaged to a higher level of care for immediate supervision, with further diagnostic exploration aimed at identifying the underlying cause of his multifaceted presentation. Despite the serious nature of his condition, his stable systemic blood pressure, acceptable platelet figure, and apparently preserved renal output offered some hope that his circulatory and renal systems were not yet in grave jeopardy, though the team recognized the potential for rapid changes in this precarious scenario. The tentative plan included aggressive investigation for possible infections, supportive management of his respiratory compromise, and continuous vigilance over his cardiovascular parameters to ensure that his perfusion remained adequate and that no additional hemodynamic agents beyond the single moderate‑dose infusion would be necessary. Additional laboratory panels, cultures, and specialized imaging would help clarify whether his elevated bilirubin stemmed from an acute infectious hepatic insult, hemolytic process, or another hepatic issue altogether. Careful neurological checks were also deemed essential to track any improvement or worsening of his severely lowered awareness level, in the hope that progress could be made once the probable underlying trigger for his respiratory decompensation was identified and addressed.

args:
The patient was a 75-year-old Japanese male who had prostate cancer with bone metastasis and high serum PSA level (7040 ng/ml, reference range < 4 ng/dL) that responded well to luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LH-RH) agonist for 8 months. Abdominal CT scan revealed no evidence of prostate cancer progression. He was referred to our department due to a 4-week history of epigastric discomfort. Physical examination was not remarkable. Laboratory work-up was not significant except for elevated ALP, LDH and PSA levels, which were improved compared to values before hormone therapy (Table ).
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) was performed and revealed a slightly depressed, discolored lesion with sharp margin against non-atrophic mucosa on the anterior wall of the middle gastric body (Fig. ). Magnifying endoscopy (ME) with blue laser imaging (BLI) and linked color imaging (LCI) demonstrated a sparse and partially absent microsurface pattern with irregular microvessels in the depressed area. These findings are compatible with UD-EGC. Biopsy showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma and immunohistochemistry with PSA was negative. Contrasted computed tomography demonstrated absence of significantly enlarged perigastric lymph nodes and also there were no new sites of metastatic disease. Thus, we initially diagnosed it as a primary early gastric cancer. Considering his prostate cancer and estimated prognosis of several years, endoscopic submucosal dissection was performed. En bloc resection was successfully achieved without complication. Histopathologic findings from the resected specimen were remarkable for moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, which predominantly existed in the superficial layer of the submucosa. Atrophy of the gastric fundic glands, which were replaced with fibrous tissue, were observed focally near the tumor infiltration site (Fig. ). As metastasis was suspected, immunochemical staining was performed. The tumor was negative for PSA, cytokeratin (CK) 7, CK 20, and positive for PSAP (Fig. ). Consequently, the lesion was finally confirmed as a metastatic gastric lesion of the prostate cancer.
At the time when the pathological diagnosis of the gastric metastases was made, patient’s extragastric lesions were responding to endocrine therapy, and because of this we did not change his systemic treatment for prostate cancer.

args:
A 41-year-old female patient with a past medical history of bipolar disorder, cocaine use disorder, and tobacco use disorder, presented with increased respiratory distress and combativeness. On initial examination, her vital signs included a temperature of 100.9°F, heart rate of 140 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 30 breaths per minute and blood pressure of 103/66 mmHg. On physical examination, she was only able to speak in two-word sentences. There were bilateral coarse crackles all over the chest. She did not have any focal neurological deficits. Cardiovascular examination did not reveal abnormal heart sounds. Abdominal examination was unremarkable. The patient was immediately placed on a nonrebreather mask. However, the patient kept taking off her nonrebreather mask, continued to have worsening hypoxia, and eventually required tracheal intubation. Chest radiograph showed right upper lobe pneumonia (Figure ).
She was started on vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam and levofloxacin, and was transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU). Broad spectrum antibiotic therapy was chosen due to patient's critical condition and unclear etiology of the infecting pathogen. The patient had a white cell count of 11.8 x 103/microliter (µL) that trended to 2.2 x 103/µL, normal troponins, and a lactic acid of 1.8 mmol/Liter (L) initially that trended up to 2.5 mmol/L. Urine drug screen was positive for cocaine, benzodiazepine, and opiates. Urine Legionella antigen and Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen were negative. Influenza A was positive on rapid antigen testing and the patient was started on oseltamivir. This raised our concern for secondary bacterial pneumonia from MRSA as a complication of influenza virus infection. The patient had computed tomography (CT) of the chest that showed right upper, middle, and lower lobe consolidations with cystic destruction of the lung parenchyma (Figures , ).
Sputum culture and blood cultures grew MRSA that was sensitive to both vancomycin and clindamycin, confirming our diagnosis. Vancomycin had already been started on admission and was continued. Fluid resuscitation was also done early in the course, but eventually progressed to septic shock. The patient was then started on norepinephrine infusion and vasopressin infusion for blood pressure support. She then developed acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Her hypoxia became refractory to conventional ventilation, and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) was considered. She had a transesophageal echocardiogram which showed severe biventricular systolic dysfunction, global hypokinesis with an ejection fraction (EF) of 5%. Her cardiomyopathy was felt to be acute and related to sepsis. She was managed for septic and cardiogenic shock, and venous-arterial ECMO was started. She was also started on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) for hypervolemia refractory to diuresis, inotropic and vasopressor support. A follow-up transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) five days later showed that the EF recovered to 45%. She continued to remain intubated for 16 days and a repeat CT chest showed worsening and multiple cavitary lesions in the right lung (Figures , ).
A repeat TTE showed EF of 60% and normal left and right heart function. ECMO was stopped and she was decannulated on day 9, CRRT was stopped on day 9. Unfortunately, she did not tolerate withdrawal of ECMO and continued to deteriorate and became difficult to wean from the ventilator. Given her lack of response to vancomycin, she was switched to daptomycin and linezolid, and the vancomycin was discontinued. She did not improve with new antibiotic regimen, continued to accumulate volume, and could not tolerate being off CRRT that was resumed for volume removal. Her hypoxia continued to be refractory despite all efforts. The family eventually decided to transition to comfort care due to her poor prognosis.

A 52-year-old man comes to to the emergency department with fatigue and shortness of breath that has become progressively worse over the past week. He had an upper respiratory tract infection 2 weeks ago, for which he was given an antibiotic. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, nonvalvular atrial fibrillation, and colonic polyps diagnosed on screening colonoscopy 2 years ago. His mother has systemic lupus erythematosus and his brother has a bicuspid aortic valve. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. Current medications include lisinopril and metformin. His temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 91/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 145/84 mm Hg. His conjunctivae are pale. Cardiac examination shows a late systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte Count 9,500/mm3
Hematocrit 24%
Platelet Count 178,000/mm3
LDH 215 U/L
Haptoglobin 22 mg/dL (N=41–165 mg/dL)
Serum
Na+ 140 mEq/L
K+ 4.6 mEq/L
CL- 100 mEq/L
HCO3- 25 mEq/L
Urea nitrogen 21 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL
A peripheral blood smear is shown.

args: A 54-year-old man presented to the cardiology clinic for routine follow-up of non-valvular atrial fibrillation that had first been documented two years ago after evaluation for palpitations and exertional fatigue. He remains in intermittent paroxysmal rhythm despite a maximally titrated beta-blocker, so long-term oral anticoagulation with warfarin was initiated eighteen months ago; his target therapeutic range has generally been achieved on a stable dose, and he has not required bridging therapy with heparin. The patient reports that in the past week he has noticed no hematuria, epistaxis, or gingival oozing, and he denies easy bruising since his last visit. Three years prior to today’s encounter, however, he was hospitalized with melena and symptomatic anemia; endoscopy at that time revealed portal hypertensive gastropathy with a clean-based oozing lesion that required endoscopic therapy and two units of packed red blood cells, after which he recovered fully. That episode occurred in the setting of advanced hepatic steatosis that had progressed to compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class A), attributed to a long history of metabolic syndrome, remote obesity, and moderate alcohol intake; his most recent liver panel, drawn one month ago, demonstrated an ALT of 78 U/L and AST of 84 U/L, with total bilirubin of 1.9 mg/dL and an albumin of 3.4 g/dL. Ultrasound at that time showed a coarsened echotexture without ascites and a portal vein diameter at the upper limit of normal. He reports that he currently consumes wine with dinner on most evenings and an additional two beers on weekends, totaling approximately 8 alcoholic drinks each week, and he denies binge patterns or liquor use. He has never been diagnosed with chronic kidney disease; last week’s metabolic panel documented a creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL and an estimated glomerular filtration rate well above 60 mL/min/1.73 m². He has no personal history of hypertension, cerebrovascular accident, transient ischemic attack, or intracranial hemorrhage, and serial outpatient blood pressure readings have remained in the 118–124/68–74 mm Hg range without pharmacologic therapy. The patient explicitly avoids over-the-counter ibuprofen, naproxen, and aspirin after counseling from his hepatologist, and he is not taking clopidogrel, ticagrelor, or any herbal supplements known to affect hemostasis. Current medications include warfarin 5 mg nightly with weekly INR monitoring at a dedicated anticoagulation clinic, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily for rate control, and vitamin D supplementation. He denies recent falls, head trauma, gastrointestinal upset, dark stools, hematochezia, hemoptysis, or retroperitoneal pain. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. Physical examination today shows an alert, well-nourished male in no acute distress with a body mass index of 26 kg/m². Vital signs are temperature 36.7 °C, heart rate irregularly irregular at 82 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98 % on room air, and blood pressure 120/72 mm Hg seated. Sclera are anicteric, mucous membranes moist without petechiae, and no telangiectasias or spider nevi are observed on the skin. Cardiac auscultation reveals variable S1 intensity consistent with atrial fibrillation; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops are heard. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdominal exam demonstrates a soft, nondistended abdomen with a palpable but non-tender left liver edge 1 cm below the costal margin; there is no shifting dullness or splenomegaly. Extremities show no edema or ecchymoses, and gait is steady. Point-of-care INR today is pending, and routine laboratory studies have been sent to reassess hepatic function. The patient is counseled about minimizing alcohol, recognizing signs of recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding, and maintaining adherence with weekly anticoagulation clinic visits.

args: A 61-year-old male arrived at the hospital with increasing shortness of breath, mild confusion, and a two-day history of productive cough. He has a past medical history of controlled hypertension and mild fatty liver disease but denies any recent changes in medication or significant dietary shifts. On initial evaluation, he appeared in moderate respiratory distress, prompting careful assessment of his ability to oxygenate and ventilate. Arterial blood gas sampling revealed his partial pressure of oxygen was 70 mm Hg, concerning given his respiratory complaints. He required advanced airway support on arrival due to insufficient oxygenation on lower levels of supplemental oxygen, and he has been receiving a mixture of inspired gas that surpasses room air concentration to help maintain adequate oxygenation. The inspired oxygen concentration is 40% (FiO₂ 0.40). He remains hemodynamically stable with a systolic pressure of 115 mm Hg and a diastolic pressure of 75 mm Hg, showing no clinical indications of severely decreased perfusion or other concerning circulatory compromise. Physical exam demonstrates that while he opens his eyes spontaneously and can communicate coherently most of the time, he occasionally struggles with more complex tasks, which suggests a mild cognitive deficit rather than a profound alteration in consciousness. On formal assessment, his Glasgow Coma Score was 14. Laboratory data at presentation show a platelet count of 150,000 per microliter, which has decreased slightly compared to his baseline but has not yet fallen into a range typically associated with a high bleeding risk. Additional labs reveal a total bilirubin of 1.8 mg/dL, indicating a mild degree of bilirubin elevation that may be influenced by his known hepatic condition but does not yet show overt signs of severe hepatic dysfunction. Renal function testing indicates a serum creatinine of 1.2 mg/dL, an observation that hovers at the upper boundary of normal for his demographic, and he currently has a daily urine output of about 1500 milliliters, indicating that his kidneys are still generating an adequate volume of urine in the setting of his acute illness. He exhibits no overt signs of severe circulatory compromise, and therefore he has not required continuous intravenous medications specifically aimed at supporting blood pressure or cardiac output. At this time, the clinical team has chosen to monitor his cardiovascular parameters closely, particularly because of his potential for sudden changes in respiratory status and the known risk of progression to sepsis secondary to a suspected pulmonary infection. Despite the mild confusion, he retains an overall favorable prognosis for neurological recovery in the absence of additional insults. Efforts are focused on controlling any underlying infectious etiology through appropriate antimicrobial therapy, maintaining stable ventilatory parameters to improve his oxygen saturation, and providing supportive care to address his mild hepatic and renal concerns. The current plan includes serial reassessment of his neurological status, repeat laboratory tests to track platelet trends and liver enzyme fluctuations, and continued close observation of fluid balance to ensure his urine output remains in a safe range. His clinical status upon arrival underscores the importance of prompt recognition of respiratory compromise in individuals with evidence of possible infection, and his moderate deficits in consciousness along with borderline lab abnormalities warrant vigilant monitoring to prevent further organ dysfunction. Although he remains intubated for now, there is cautious optimism that he will improve with medical management and may be weaned from advanced respiratory support once his oxygenation stabilizes and his condition proves to be on an upward trajectory.

args:
History
A 52-year-old African American male presented to the emergency department complaining of pleuritic, sharp pain in his right chest wall that had begun three days prior to arrival. The pain had since worsened in intensity and did not radiate. He had taken acetaminophen without relief of his symptoms. The patient denied any fever, coughing up blood, shortness of breath, headache, or rash. His review of symptoms was otherwise negative. The patient had no significant past medical history and was taking no medications. He denied any recent history of surgery or trauma, but did note weight lifting two days prior to the onset of symptoms. Family history was negative for coagulopathies and autoimmune conditions.
Exam findings
Vital signs on arrival were blood pressure 146/97, heart rate 97, temperature 36.2°C, respiratory rate 20, and SpO2 99% on room air. He was alert and oriented, and in no acute distress. Lung sounds were clear bilaterally and heart sounds were normal. A raised cord was visible crossing from the epigastrium across the right breast toward the axilla, that was tender to palpation and mobile. There was no warmth, erythema, or rash overlying the cord. Head/eyes/ears/nose/throat, abdominal, and neurological examinations were unremarkable.
Diagnostic evaluation
Basic metabolic panel and troponin levels were normal, and a complete blood count and differential was unremarkable with platelets of 212 bil/L. Chest radiograph showed clear lung fields but did reveal remote right-sided posterior fractures of ribs five through eight. Given the pleuritic nature of the pain, a computed tomography pulmonary angiogram was ordered to rule out pulmonary embolism. As shown in Figure , it revealed a subtle density in the right chest wall over the area of the palpable cord, with no evidence of a pulmonary embolism. The diagnosis of superficial chest wall thrombophlebitis, or Mondor’s disease, was made.
Patient course
The patient was treated with ibuprofen in the emergency department and discharged with a naproxen prescription. He was referred to his primary care physician for further work-up. The pain improved in the days following his emergency department visit, resolving within a week of discharge. The patient was contacted two months after his emergency department visit. He noted that he was still able to appreciate a firm mass in his chest wall with some reduction in size since it was first diagnosed.

args: A 26-year-old Sri Lankan male was admitted to hospital with fever for 3 days and body aches. He did not have any other significant symptoms. He was diagnosed of having schizophrenia 4 years ago and was attending a psychiatric clinic. He was started on clozapine, 4 weeks ago (200 mg daily), because of inadequate control of symptoms with conventional antipsychotics. On examination he was febrile (102 °F), not pale or icteric. He did not have lymphadenopathy. Blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg and pulse rate was 110 bpm with good volume. His respiratory rate was 32 cycles per minute. Examination of the abdomen, cardiovascular and respiratory systems were unremarkable. His full blood count (FBC), which was done a day prior to hospital admission showed white blood cell count (WBC) 2.9 × 103/µl, absolute neutrophil count 667/µl (23 %), lymphocytes 2117/µl (73 %), Hemoglobin (Hb) 11.3 g/dl, platelet 188 × 103/µl. The first FBC during the hospital stay showed reduced blood counts, of which WBC 0.2 × 103/µl with absolute neutrophil count (ANC) 110/µl, lymphocyte count 80/µl, Hb 12.2 g/dl and platelet 972 × 103/µl. The C reactive protein was (CRP) 270 mg/L (<6 mg/L). His chest X ray, ultrasound scan (USS) of abdomen and pelvis, urine full report was normal. Serology for dengue virus, human immunodeficiency virus and parvo B19 virus were negative. Electrocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus tachycardia. Clinical diagnosis of neutropenic sepsis was made. He was treated with intravenous imipenum, vancomycin, clindamycin, metronidazole and fluconazole. His serial blood cultures, urine cultures and sputum cultures were negative. His FBC was monitored twice daily throughout hospital stay, showed severe neutropenia and thrombocytopenia which later complicated by anemia from the 3rd day of hospital stay (Table ; Fig. ). The reticulocyte count was 1.1 % (normal 0.5–2.5). The patient was transferred to intensive care unit (ICU) on the 3rd day and treated with granulocyte colony stimulating factors (G-CSF) 300 µg daily for 10 days. On the 5th day he developed swelling of his left inguinal region with mild skin induration. The initial USS of left groin showed only subcutaneous tissue oedema. This subsequently progressed to an abscess which was drained. His white cell count and platelet count improved with 10 days of treatment with G-CSF. Hemoglobin normalized in 4 weeks of treatment. At this stage his WBC count was 12 × 103/µl with absolute neutrophil count 7200/µl (60 %), hemoglobin 12.6 g/dl and platelets 212 × 103/µl. He was started on conventional antipsychotics without clozapine for his psychiatric condition.
