args:
A 55-year-old man with a past medical history of alcohol dependence, persistent atrial fibrillation, and severe biventricular systolic heart failure presented to the emergency department with complaints of weakness, cough, exertional dyspnea, and malaise. He denied any chest pain, palpitations, lightheadedness, fever, chills, sweats, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. He was found to have atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response rate of 173. On physical examination, he was alert but anxious with an irregular heart rate. The rest of the physical examination was unremarkable. He developed subsequent diltiazem-induced hypotension and resultant global ischemic organ injury requiring cardioversion, intubation, and vasopressor support. Laboratory studies at the time of presentation were significant for sodium of 130 mmol/L, potassium of 4.6 mmol/L, creatinine of 2.4 mg/dL, albumin of 2.4 g/dL, AST of 10,050 U/L, ALT of 3.056 U/L, alkaline phosphatase of 119 U/L, and total bilirubin of 3.6 mg/dL. His complete blood count was significant for hemoglobin of 11.0 g/dL, MCV of 97.1 fl, platelet count of 148 × 109/L, and WBC of 18.3/μl. He was also noted to be coagulopathic with an INR of 2.2. A CT scan of the thorax was performed to rule out pulmonary embolism, and this showed an incidental liver lesion measuring 2 × 2 cm with residual contrast in the noncontrast series and was read as a hemangioma (Fig. ). He presented to the emergency department 9 months later with syncopal episodes. CT angiography showed no signs of pulmonary embolism, but an enlarging liver lesion with intralesional calcifications measuring 4.3 × 3.9 cm with borderline enlarged lymph nodes in the porta hepatis (Fig. ). He was referred to our hepatobiliary neoplasia clinic for further management. An ultrasound-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lesion was obtained (Fig. ).
The liver biopsy showed a neoplasm with epithelioid and spindle cell features, prominent nucleoli, occasional intranuclear pseudoinclusions, and frequent mitotic activity. Immunostains showed the tumor cells to be positive for S100, Melan-A, and SOX10, but negative for keratins (AE1/AE3, Oscar), desmin, CD34, and arginase, consistent with a diagnosis of metastatic melanoma. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy were performed to exclude other primitive malignant cancers. Complete skin and eye examination in addition to further imaging studies did not identify the primary site. His lesion was treated with stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT), which he tolerated well.

args: A 56-year-old man with a history of well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to the urgent care unit late this afternoon complaining of progressive fullness and tightness isolated to his left lower leg that began roughly thirty-six hours ago after spending most of the weekend helping his neighbor pour a concrete patio, an activity that required frequent squatting and pushing a wheelbarrow up a short incline. He recalls a sudden cramp-like sensation in the posterior calf while straightening from a crouched position, followed by a dull ache that has persisted; he notes the leg seemed only mildly enlarged at first but by this morning his sock left a deeper impression than usual and his shoe felt snugger around the ankle. He denies any similar problem in the past, states that he has remained mobile and returned to his job as a delivery dispatcher yesterday, and slept in his own bed without difficulty turning or repositioning. He has not been hospitalized, had orthopedic casts, or undergone any surgical procedure in the past year, travels only short regional distances for work, and exercises three evenings a week on a stationary bicycle. Throughout this period he has remained fully ambulatory and explicitly denies having spent even a single 24-hour period confined to bed; certainly he has not been bedridden for three or more consecutive days. He denies fever, chills, sweats, weight loss, night pain, rash, or redness around the leg; likewise no chest discomfort, dyspnea, palpitations, hemoptysis, or syncope. He reports no numbness, weakness, or tingling of the limb, and is able to bear weight, though prolonged standing increases the sense of pressure. Daily medications include amlodipine 5 mg, atorvastatin 20 mg, and an over-the-counter multivitamin; he does not use hormone supplementation, steroids, or anticoagulants. Family history is notable only for paternal coronary artery disease; no relatives have known thromboembolic disorders. He is a never-smoker, drinks one to two beers on weekends, and denies illicit substance use. On examination he appears comfortable, converses easily, and moves about the room without assistance. Vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 per minute, oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are unrevealing with regular rhythm, no murmurs, clear breath sounds, and no jugular venous distention. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no organomegaly or masses. Inspection of the lower extremities reveals asymmetry confined to the left calf and ankle region; the skin color is normal without ecchymosis or streaking. A tape measure placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity records a circumference of 42 cm on the left versus 38 cm on the right, confirming a disparity exceeding 3 cm. Circumference measured 15 cm above the superior pole of the patella is equal at 52 cm bilaterally, indicating no swelling of the thigh and thus sparing of the entire limb. The left foot and distal calf pit 2 mm with thumb pressure and rebound within 10 seconds, whereas the contralateral side shows no indentation. Palpation along the expected course of the femoral, popliteal, and posterior tibial veins elicits no focal tenderness; instead, the patient reports a diffuse muscular soreness most pronounced over the medial belly of the gastrocnemius, and he experiences a mild pull when asked to perform a heel raise. Homan sign is not provoked. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no visible engorgement of superficial channels, and capillary refill of toes is under two seconds bilaterally. Distal pulses are intact and equal, sensation and power in ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion are symmetric, and straight-leg raising is negative for radicular pain. The knee and ankle joints are nontender with full range of motion; no warmth or effusion is detected. Given the unilateral calf enlargement with measurable girth difference and localized pitting edema, an intraluminal venous obstruction remains on the differential; however, the temporal relationship to strenuous physical activity, the absence of pain specifically over deep venous pathways, and the predominance of myofascial discomfort raise the possibility of a partial gastrocnemius tear or rupture of a small Baker cyst as a competing explanation. At present, the musculoskeletal explanation is judged at least as plausible as a primary venous thrombotic event. Baseline laboratories, including complete blood count, serum electrolytes, creatinine, and coagulation profile, have been obtained, and a focused duplex ultrasonography of the left lower limb is being arranged. The patient has been counseled on return precautions such as sudden worsening swelling, new shortness of breath, or chest pain while awaiting imaging.

args: A 75-year-old man was admitted to our hospital due to exertional dyspnea that had been manifesting for several months. The patient had coronary risk factors, including hypertension, dyslipidemia, family history of coronary disease, and past history of smoking, and was taking medication prescribed by his regular physician (nifedipine 20 mg/day for hypertension; bezafibrate 400 mg/day for hyperlipidemia). On admission, physical examination and laboratory data revealed no specific findings: white blood cell count, 3990 cells/μL; hemoglobin, 14.8 g/dL; platelets, 26.7 × 104 cells/μL; glucose, 95 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen, 14 mg/dL; creatinine, 0.71 mg/dL; estimated glomerular filtration rate, 81.7 mL/min; uric acid, 6.0 mg/dL; aspartate transaminase, 21 U/L; alanine aminotransferase, 15 U/L; total bilirubin, 0.6 mg/dL; creatine kinase, 85 IU/L; creatine kinase-MB, 8 IU/L; C-reactive protein, 0.1 mg/dL; low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, 150 mg/dL; high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, 63 mg/dL; triglycerides, 113 mg/dL; glycated hemoglobin, 5.4%; brain-type natriuretic peptide, 24 pg/mL. However, the exercise stress test revealed slight ST depression in leads V4-6 on electrocardiography. Based on the clinical symptoms, the patient was suspected of coronary artery disease. Adenosine triphosphate-stress radionuclide myocardial perfusion imaging revealed inferolateral wall ischemia. Coronary computed tomography indicated that the RCA originated from the left coronary sinus and passed between the aorta and the pulmonary artery (). Total occlusion in the midportion of the abnormal RCA and 90% stenosis of the left anterior descending coronary artery (LAD) were suspected. The RCA lesion had atherosclerotic findings such as spotty calcification and mild positive remodeling (). The patient was diagnosed with effort angina pectoris and underwent coronary angiography, which revealed tight stenosis at the LAD-D1 bifurcation and a completely occluded RCA originating from the left coronary sinus. The abnormal RCA had multiple collaterals from the LAD and left circumflex branch (Figures and ). Because the patient had a coronary artery anomaly and multivessel stenosis, coronary artery bypass grafting was proposed for revascularization, but the patient refused any surgically invasive treatment. Therefore, percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) was performed for revascularization. The following revascularization systems were used: right femoral artery approach; guiding catheter, 7-Fr Amplatz Left 2 Mach 1 (Boston Scientific, Natick, MA, USA); guide wire, Grand Slam, XT-R, and Sion blue (both from Asahi Intecc, Aichi, Japan); microcatheter, Mizuki (Kaneka Medical, Osaka, Japan) and Caravel (Asahi Intecc); balloon, Ikazuchi 1.0 × 10 mm (Kaneka Medical), Tazuna 2.0 × 15 mm (Terumo Corporation, Tokyo, Japan), and Raiden 3.5 × 10 mm (Kaneka Medical); stent, Ultimaster 3.0 × 18 mm (Terumo Corporation); and intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) catheter, 40 MHz rotational OptiCross (Boston Scientific). Cannulating the guiding catheter to the anomalous orifice of the RCA was difficult, and it was not possible to achieve adequate backup support (). Using the XT-R guide wire, the Mizuki microcatheter could not be passed though the chronic total occlusion lesion until it was replaced with a Caravel microcatheter. The wire was then changed to Sion blue (). IVUS revealed diffuse eccentric calcified plaque. The intramural course of the proximal ectopic artery was elliptical with some lateral compression. However, the stenosis of the proximal intramural course was not so severe that we did not deploy the stent in the proximal portion (). Multiple ballooning and angioplasty with a drug-eluting stent were performed (), and an optimal result was obtained (). At the same time, PCI was performed for LAD revascularization, and an optimal result was obtained (). No exertional dyspnea was noted following PCI. At approximately one year after intervention, exercise stress radionuclide myocardial perfusion imaging and coronary angiography revealed no in-stent restenosis or ischemia. The patient expressed satisfaction with the outcome of the intervention.

args:
A 53-year-old female was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after slipping on a wet floor at her workplace earlier in the day. Witnesses reported that she lost her balance while carrying a box of office supplies, landing primarily on her right side and briefly complaining of soreness in the right arm and hip. She did not report any immediate loss of consciousness, but coworkers noted that she appeared somewhat shaken, prompting them to call for emergency assistance out of concern for potential head or spinal injury. On arrival at the hospital, she was seated upright on the gurney and maintained her gaze toward staff members entering the room without any prompting, suggesting that she was responsive to her environment. During the initial examination, she was engaged in clear dialogue with the nurse, accurately detailing her name, the current date, and the nature of her workday, as well as explaining the sequence of events leading up to the fall. She recalled precisely what she had been doing and appeared receptive to questions, answering them cogently and with appropriate detail. When queried about her surroundings, she could identify that she was in the hospital and recognized it as the local facility nearest to her workplace. She continued to interact in a calm, coherent manner, showing no sign of confusional states or hesitation in speech. As the clinician proceeded with the neurological assessment, she responded promptly to each request, including moving her arms and legs when asked, thereby allowing the evaluator to check for any focal deficits or signs of weakness. Although she mentioned mild discomfort in her right hip and wrist, she did not demonstrate any difficulty completing simple tasks such as extending both arms or flexing her knees. The positional changes did not elicit additional pain, though she did briefly note mild tenderness around her right femoral region when palpated. Further examination of her cranial nerves did not reveal any abnormality; her pupils were equal and reactive, ocular movements were aligned, and there was no facial drooping or asymmetry in her smile. She voiced an ability to feel light touch and pressure on her upper and lower extremities without issue, and she could localize gentle tactile stimuli. Her vital signs on admission were stable, with blood pressure slightly elevated but still within a reasonable range, which she attributed to feeling anxious after the fall. She denied any nausea, vomiting, or dizziness, though she did admit to feeling somewhat stiff and achy from the impact of the fall. Laboratory tests were ordered to rule out any underlying problems, and imaging studies of her hip and wrist were requested to ensure there were no fractures or significant soft tissue injuries. She reported a past medical history of lower back discomfort for which she occasionally took over-the-counter analgesics, but she stated she had no chronic neurological conditions, had never undergone surgery, and did not have any major medical diagnoses such as diabetes or hypertension. She also denied any known allergies. In fact, she described her overall health as generally good, explaining that she usually remains quite active, walking or cycling when weather permits. calculator_name: Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk

args:
A 61-year-old woman with a history of treated pulmonary tuberculosis in childhood, bronchiectasis, hypertension, hypothyroidism, polymyalgia rheumatica, and hyperlipidemia presented to our clinic with two months of shortness of breath on exertion associated with worsening lower extremity edema, vomiting, subjective fever, and watery diarrhea three days prior to admission.
Upon admission, she presented hemodynamically stable. On physical examination, she had bibasilar lung crackles and pitting edema bilaterally. Her blood urea nitrogen level was 82 mg/dL, creatinine level was 11.91 mg/dL, sodium was 129 mmol/L, potassium was 8.6 mmol/L, bicarbonate was 19 mmol/L, and hemoglobin was 7.3 g/dL. She was admitted due to acute kidney injury (AKI) secondary to acute tubular necrosis, presumably from volume depletion. An attempt was made to manage the AKI medically, but oliguria and worsening acidosis and hyperkalemia prompted hemodialysis.
Further studies revealed the presence of serum myeloperoxidase (MPO)-anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), serum anti-glomerular basement membrane (GBM), and red blood cells in her urine. She received intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg for three days. A renal biopsy showed MPO-ANCA mediated with concurrent anti-GBM disease crescentic necrotizing and focal sclerosing glomerulonephritis, establishing the diagnosis of Goodpasture syndrome with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. She received four cycles of rituximab and continued with prednisone 60 mg daily.
The hospital course was complicated by a Clostridium difficile infection and hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP). Also, she acutely presented an episode of respiratory distress: her respiration rate was >24 breaths/minute, oxygen saturation was <90%, heart rate was >120, blood pressure was 180/100 mmHg, and we noted labored breathing via accessory muscles, expiratory wheezing, and expectoration of frothy secretions. The patient was intubated and transferred to the cardiac care unit. A 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) showed an ST and T wave abnormality (Figure ) compatible with anterolateral ischemia.
A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) revealed an ejection fraction (EF) of 20% to 25%, grade 2 diastolic dysfunction, systolic right atrial pressure (RAP) of 15 mmHg, and right ventricle systolic pressure (RVSP) of 59 mmHg. The left ventricle was normal in size, but there were regional wall motion abnormalities. Apical and basal segments contracted normally, but the mid-anteroseptal, inferolateral, and anteroapical walls were hypokinetic. The anteroapical wall appeared to be dyskinetic. She was started on carvedilol and lisinopril. A follow-up TTE three weeks later revealed an EF of 50%, grade 1 diastolic dysfunction, systolic RAP of 3 mmHg, and RVSP of 33 mmHg with an improvement in the previously described wall motion abnormalities, suggestive of resolving the mid-ventricular variant of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (Video ). Pheochromocytoma was not ruled out given the absence of typical signs and symptoms.
Late in her hospital course, she presented two episodes of respiratory distress considered secondary to Goodpasture syndrome and fluid overload; these episodes were managed with nasal intermittent positive pressure ventilation and furosemide. The patient required resumed intermittent dialysis and was successfully discharged. On an 18-month follow-up office visit, she remained asymptomatic, cardiovascular wise. However, because of the chronic kidney disease, she underwent a kidney transplant.

What is the patient's score of the Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

calculator_name: HAS-BLED Score for Major Bleeding Risk

args: A 62-year-old woman was seen in the hospital-affiliated anticoagulation service this morning for routine follow-up of her chronic oral vitamin K antagonist therapy that had been initiated nine months ago after non-valvular atrial fibrillation was identified on telemetry during an overnight stay for hypertensive urgency; cardiology had targeted an INR of 2.0–3.0. She reports that since starting the drug she has presented to an outside laboratory every week, yet her printed log shows values as low as 1.7 and as high as 4.6 within the last six weeks, prompting telephone instructions to alternate 5-mg and 7.5-mg doses on alternating evenings, hold one dose after supratherapeutic readings, and on one occasion receive 1 mg oral phytonadione. She has never needed plasma, prothrombin complex concentrate, or hospital admission for reversal, and she denies epistaxis, gingival oozing, hematuria, hematochezia, melena, hematemesis, easy bruising, or conjunctival pallor. The chief complaint today is concern that her blood pressure “still runs too high” despite medication and that the combination of her anticoagulant with the other tablet she takes daily for her coronary history might make her “bleed inside.” She was diagnosed with systolic pressure elevation at age 55 after an incidental reading of 168/94 mm Hg at an occupational health visit and has since cycled through several agents; the current regimen includes amlodipine 10 mg nightly and losartan 100 mg each morning, yet her home cuff routinely documents values in the mid-150s systolic, and the manually measured pressure in clinic after five minutes of seated rest was 162/88 mm Hg with an irregularly irregular pulse of 78 beats/min and respirations 14/min. Five years ago she underwent percutaneous coronary intervention with a drug-eluting stent to the mid-left anterior descending artery for an NSTEMI and has remained on chewable acetylsalicylic acid 81 mg daily as secondary prevention. Other long-standing conditions include primary hyperlipidemia controlled with atorvastatin 40 mg nightly and mild gastroesophageal reflux for which she takes pantoprazole 40 mg before breakfast. She has never experienced a transient ischemic attack, ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, carotid disease, peripheral embolus, peptic ulcer, inflammatory bowel disease, diverticular hemorrhage, or hemophilia. Baseline renal function has remained stable; a comprehensive metabolic panel drawn last week showed serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 78 mL/min/1.73 m², blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, sodium 139 mmol/L, and potassium 4.2 mmol/L. She denies dark urine, frothy urine, flank pain, or lower-extremity edema. She has never smoked cigarettes and denies illicit drug use. Regarding alcohol, she states that she typically drinks two glasses of wine with dinner on weeknights and three craft beers on each day of the weekend, summing to approximately 21 alcoholic beverages per week; she insists she never binges but acknowledges occasional morning sluggishness after celebratory evenings. Family history is notable for premature myocardial infarction in her father at 59 but lacks hemorrhagic stroke or congenital coagulopathy. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, new dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling, right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, pruritus, confusion, visual changes, focal weakness, headache, syncope, or recent weight loss. On physical examination she appears well nourished and in no acute distress, body mass index 26 kg/m², temperature 36.9 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. Sclerae are anicteric; mucous membranes are moist without petechiae; neck supple without jugular venous distention; carotid upstrokes brisk without bruit. Auscultation of the chest reveals variable first heart sound but no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs are clear to percussion and auscultation. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender with no palpable organomegaly or fluid wave. Extremities show no cyanosis, clubbing, edema, purpura, or ecchymosis; distal pulses are 2+ and symmetric. Neurologic screening demonstrates intact cranial nerves II–XII, full strength, normal coordination, and steady gait. Today’s point-of-care hemoglobin is 13.5 g/dL, platelet count 232 × 10⁹/L, and the clinic INR reads 3.8. A 12-lead electrocardiogram verifies atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response at 80 beats/min, normal axis, and no acute ST-T abnormalities. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed two months ago documented a mildly dilated left atrium, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction of 62 %, and no significant valvular lesions. She expresses a desire to understand whether her fluctuating coagulation profile, persistent systolic pressure elevation, daily antiplatelet use, and regular alcohol consumption collectively heighten her likelihood of a serious hemorrhage and what strategies might mitigate that risk while still guarding against cardioembolic stroke.

calculator_name: PSI Score: Pneumonia Severity Index for CAP

args: A 67-year-old male arrived at the emergency department reporting several days of worsening cough, mounting fatigue, and a high fever that he first noted upon waking the previous morning. He stated that his cough, initially nonproductive with mild throat irritation, had evolved into a deeper, more distressing pattern over the last day or so, and he began to experience chills and consistent warmth to the touch. At presentation, he was alert and engaged, conversing readily without any signs of confusion or mental cloudiness. According to him, he lives independently in a single-story residence and has not required ongoing skilled care or around-the-clock supervision. He recalls a neurological event several years ago that temporarily affected his speech and coordination, though he says he eventually recovered with rehabilitative support. He also mentions that he has required periodic evaluations for a medical condition tied to certain aspects of his metabolism, though he reports having remained stable in recent times with no major flare-ups. Nevertheless, he became concerned when his current symptoms failed to improve with usual home measures such as rest and oral hydration. On evaluation, his core temperature was measured at 38.9 degrees Celsius, reflecting a significant fever consistent with the abrupt onset of his symptoms. Physical assessment showed that he was breathing at a rate of about 17 breaths per minute and maintaining a heart rate of approximately 100 beats per minute, which he acknowledged felt faster than normal for him when he attempted mild activity. His blood pressure was recorded as 135 mm Hg on the systolic reading, aligning with a stable circulatory status for someone of his age. He displayed no outward evidence of fluid accumulation in his lower extremities or jugular venous distension, and he denied any chest pain beyond the occasional discomfort from deep coughing. When asked about his general medical history, he stated he had been followed intermittently over the years for a significant but well-controlled liver-related condition, which had not necessitated recent hospital admissions. Initial laboratory results showed a mild disturbance in his acid-base balance, with a pH documented at 7.46, though his clinical examination did not suggest any overt respiratory or metabolic compromise. He was found to have a partial pressure of oxygen of 87 mm Hg on room air, confirming that his oxygen level was somewhat lower than desired for an individual without known chronic lung issues. Additional lab testing highlighted an elevated blood urea measurement of 31 mg/dL, raising suspicion that he might be experiencing increased protein breakdown or a reduced intravascular volume status, which could complicate recovery. His serum sodium concentration was noted to be 132 mEq/L, just below normal limits, which, in conjunction with his ongoing fever and reduced oral intake, could signify evolving electrolyte fluctuations. A hematocrit value of 33% indicated a borderline low red cell volume, though he reported no significant dizziness or lightheadedness beyond what he assumed stemmed from feeling feverish. His measured glucose of 128 mg/dL was slightly above his baseline, which he attributed to decreased appetite and occasional sugary sports drinks he consumed to stay hydrated when his cough worsened. Chest auscultation revealed coarse breath sounds over the right lateral lung fields, and imaging confirmed findings of inflammation and fluid layering within the thoracic cavity, consistent with a localized infectious process and excess fluid accumulation. Despite his evident infection and the presence of fluid in the pleural space, he remained oriented throughout the conversation, recounting details about his daily activities and stressing that he has never relied on institutional living arrangements. The team acknowledged that his prior neurological history and ongoing liver-related concerns, though not actively flaring, could affect his overall resilience. With that in mind, he was prepared for further evaluation and management directed at controlling his acute condition, including monitoring of his oxygen levels, repeating relevant laboratory analyses, and ensuring any unstable parameters were addressed without delay.

args: A 55-year-old woman presented to the urgent care area of the medical ward late this morning with a chief complaint of progressive swelling of her right lower extremity that she first noted two days ago while getting dressed for work. She reports that on awakening that morning her shoe felt tight and the pant leg seemed snug from ankle to hip; by last night the limb had become visibly larger than the left with a heavy, taut sensation and mild aching that worsened during her usual 20-minute walk home but abated somewhat after she elevated the leg on a pillow. She denies fevers, night sweats, chills, trauma, insect bites, rashes, or new medications. She has not experienced chest pain, hemoptysis, pleuritic pain, palpitations, or light-headedness. Her surgical history is notable for a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy performed six weeks ago at an outside facility; she recovered well, returned to office work after ten days, and has been able to ambulate freely without any prolonged periods of recumbency, long-distance travel, air flights, or car rides exceeding one hour. Her past medical history includes class II obesity prior to surgery, well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 µg daily, and seasonal allergic rhinitis for which she takes cetirizine as needed. She has never been treated for thromboembolic disease, nor has she undergone vein ablation, sclerotherapy, or vascular bypass. She does not smoke, drinks wine socially, and denies hormone replacement therapy or oral contraceptive use since menopause three years ago. Family history is negative for thrombophilia, premature myocardial infarction, or stroke; both parents are alive in their eighties. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. On arrival, vital signs were temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 126/76 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air. She appeared mildly uncomfortable but was able to ambulate to the examination table without assistance. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally with good air entry. The abdomen was soft and nontender with well-healed trocar scars in the epigastrium and left upper quadrant, no palpable masses, and active bowel sounds. Lower-extremity inspection demonstrated obvious enlargement of the entire right leg from groin to ankle with a subtle glossy sheen of the skin but no erythema, warmth, ulceration, or weeping. A flexible tape measure placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity recorded a circumference of 39 cm on the right versus 37 cm on the left, confirming only a 2 cm difference at the calf. Measurement 15 cm above the patella revealed 55 cm on the right compared with 50 cm on the left, highlighting more pronounced proximal fullness. Soft-tissue compression over the anterior tibia produced a 2-mm depression that refilled within 20 seconds, and similar pitting was reproducible over the dorsum of the right foot; no such indentation occurred on the contralateral side. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) were palpable and symmetric, capillary refill was brisk, and skin temperature was comparable in both limbs. Sensation to light touch, proprioception, and vibration was intact, and motor strength was 5/5 throughout. There was no calf cord, no palpable popliteal mass, and no discoloration of the toes. Examination of joints revealed full, pain-free range of motion at the knee, ankle, and hip without effusion or crepitus. No enlargement of inguinal lymph nodes was noted. Laboratory studies obtained before any anticoagulant therapy showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, platelet count 254 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.8 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and normal transaminases; prothrombin time was 12.4 seconds with an INR of 1.0, and aPTT was 30 seconds. A serum β-hCG test was negative. Given the recent abdominal surgery within the past two months, unilateral swelling involving the full length of the limb, confined pitting edema, absence of prolonged bed rest, lack of prior thrombotic episodes, and no obvious alternative source such as cellulitis, lymphedema, orthopedic injury, or heart failure, an acute obstructive process in the deep venous system of the right lower extremity is considered high on the differential, and a duplex ultrasound encompassing the iliac, femoral, popliteal, and calf veins has been urgently ordered.

args: A 59-year-old man diagnosed with obstructive hydrocephalus was scheduled for an ETV. The patient had a medical history of well-controlled diabetes mellitus and hypertension that was being treated with an angiotensin receptor blocker, a calcium channel blocker, and a beta blocker. The body mass index was 33.8, but he had no respiratory symptoms, including sleep apnea. A preoperative chest X-ray showed mild cardiomegaly but electrocardiography and echocardiogram findings were normal. The patient's symptoms were amnesia, gait disturbance, and urinary incontinence that had persisted for 5 months; preoperatively, he was alert and cooperative and had no problem communicating. The preoperative vital signs were: blood pressure, 110/70 mmHg; heart rate, 59 beats/min; respiratory rate, 18 breaths/min; and oxygen saturation, 96%. Since the appearance of the patient suggested a possible difficult intubation, a video laryngoscope was used for tracheal intubation after induction of general anesthesia with 150 mg propofol 1%, 40 mg lidocaine 2%, and 60 mg rocuronium. The vocal cord was clearly visible on the video laryngoscope and the intubation was accomplished without trauma or other difficulties. Anesthesia was maintained with 5% desflurane and 0.02 µg/kg/min remifentanil, and air with 50% oxygen. The surgery lasted 2 hours and required no additional muscle relaxants. During the surgery, there was sudden and sustained hypotension (mean blood pressure 52–57 mmHg) with bradycardia (heart rate 45–55 beats/min) that did not respond well to ephedrine; these complications began about 10 min after the beginning of the surgery and lasted for 25 min until a dopamine infusion was administered that restored normal blood pressure. Remifentanil infusion was discontinued at the onset of hypotension and bradycardia. After the purge of volatile agents at the end of the surgery, the train of four (TOF) count was 4, and the ratio was 50%, so 2 mg/kg of sugammadex was injected. The patient recovered muscle power within a few minutes, showing firm hand grip, head elevation, and mouth opening, and was fully obeying verbal commands, but was not opening his eyes. As he was alert and cooperative at that point and no other neurologic deficit was observed, the neurosurgeons thought that the inability to open his eyes was not related to the surgery. We relieved the bandage on the head in order to reduce the chance of unintended pressure or tension on the forehead, and suggested that further neurologic examinations be done after full recovery from anesthesia. Spontaneous respiration was present, showing expired tidal volumes reaching 500 ml each time, but was tachypneic; the patient's respiratory rate ranged from 40 to 50 breaths/min. This condition continued for 5 min, at which time the patient was extubated and transferred to the PACU under mask ventilation. The patient showed signs of inspiratory stridor, chest retractions, and hoarse voice after the extubation. The patient's accessory respiratory muscles were not involved in the hyperventilation. There was no crackle or wheezing clear on auscultation besides the inspiratory stridor, but the findings were not clear on account of the hoarse voice and the rapid and dynamic ventilation. An additional 2 mg/kg of sugammadex was injected in the PACU under the theory that the patient's upper airway was obstructed because of possible residual paralysis but the condition did not change. The patient was fully responsive to verbal commands but was still unable to open his eyes or hold his breath and he complained of feeling like he was choking despite the hyperventilation. Arterial blood gas analysis (ABGA) at that point showed: pH, 7.35; pCO2, 37 mmHg; pO2, 76 mmHg; HCO3, 20.4 mmol/L; and O2 saturation, 94%. The vital signs at that point were: blood pressure, 150/90 mmHg; heart rate, 110 beats/min; and respiratory rate, 44 breaths/min. Since tachypnea (40–50 breaths/min) persisted for 20 min in the PACU and oxygen saturation started to drop under 95%, 200 mg thiopental sodium and 100 mg succinylcholine were injected and endotracheal intubation was performed. Endotracheal suction was done immediately after intubation and there was no sputum or discharge. Auscultation right after the re-intubation sounded smooth, without crackle or wheezing, and the oxygen saturation rapidly corrected to 100%. Tachypnea persisted even under sedation after the succinylcholine wore off, so a central cause was suspected. Computed tomography (CT) revealed a massive pneumocephalus with pneumoventricle and hydrocephalus (). A chest X-ray taken after the brain CT showed no pathologic findings such as pulmonary edema, congestion, or pneumothorax. On the neurosurgeon's recommendation, the patient was transferred to the intensive care unit and was kept there for three days under mechanical ventilation with sedation with midazolam, cisatracurium, and rocuronium until the pneumocephalus and tachypnea spontaneously resolved. Follow-up chest radiographs were taken over the next 3 weeks and showed nothing abnormal. According to a pulmonology consultation, the tachypnea was not likely to have originated from pulmonary problems. ABGA results stayed within normal limits under fully controlled mechanical ventilation but showed hypoxemia when the patient gained spontaneous respiration during the sedative period before the resolution of the pneumocephalus. Since the follow-up chest X-rays showed no abnormal findings, pulmonary embolism evaluation including embolism CT and laboratory examinations, which also showed nothing abnormal, were done on the recommendation of a pulmonologist. When a follow-up brain CT showed resolution of the pneumocephalus on the 3rd day after the surgery, sedative and neuromuscular blocking agents were discontinued and the mechanical ventilation mode was switched to synchronized intermittent mandatory ventilation. The respiratory rate at that point ranged from 15 to 20 breaths/min and ABGA results remained normal when the patient gained consciousness and self-respiration, so the endotracheal tube was extubated. The patient is now (30 days post-operation) in the general ward; he is mentally alert and has stable vital signs but still is unable to open his eyes because of bilateral ptosis and is receiving conservative treatment.

args: A 63-year-old woman came to the electrophysiology office for an annual evaluation of her paroxysmal non-valvular atrial fibrillation and to review the continued need for systemic anticoagulation as she prepares for an elective cataract extraction later this summer. She was first noted to be in fibrillation four years ago during an emergency department visit for palpitations, and cardiology subsequently initiated warfarin; during the past twelve months her dosing has been remarkably consistent, with every community laboratory check documenting an international normalized ratio between 2.1 and 2.7 without any holds or vitamin K rescue. She relates no episodes of epistaxis, hematuria, hematochezia, melena, gingival oozing, subconjunctival hemorrhage, or easy bruising since anticoagulation began, and she has never required a blood transfusion, endoscopic therapy, or interventional radiologic procedure for hemorrhage at any point in her life. Her past medical history is notable for stage 4 chronic kidney disease attributed to long-standing IgA nephropathy diagnosed in her late fifties; the most recent metabolic panel drawn one week ago revealed a serum creatinine of 2.7 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 39 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mmol/L, and an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 26 mL/min/1.73 m², values unchanged over the last six months. She follows nephrology quarterly, has never undergone dialysis, and has not been listed for transplant. She has no history suggestive of cerebrovascular accident, transient ischemic attack, carotid disease, uncontrolled blood-pressure elevations, myocardial infarction, peripheral vascular disease, peptic ulcer, liver dysfunction, or hematologic disorder. Her medication list includes warfarin 5 mg nightly, diltiazem ER 120 mg each morning for rate control, vitamin D3 2000 IU daily, and a phosphate binder taken with meals; she purposely avoids over-the-counter non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, aspirin, clopidogrel, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and herbal preparations after counseling from her nephrologist. She has never smoked and denies recreational drug use; alcohol consumption is moderate, averaging one glass of wine with dinner five nights per week and two craft beers while watching sports on Saturday, totaling approximately 9 alcoholic drinks per week, with no binge episodes. Family history reveals a father who died of sudden cardiac death at 78 and a mother with type 2 diabetes; there is no familial bleeding diathesis. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, abdominal discomfort, pruritus, dark urine, pruritic rash, or unexplained weight change. On examination she appears well nourished and relaxed, body mass index 24 kg/m². Vital signs after five minutes seated show blood pressure 118/70 mm Hg in both arms, heart rate irregularly irregular at 82 beats/min, respiratory rate 15/min, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % on ambient air. Sclera are anicteric, conjunctiva pink without petechiae, oral mucosa moist, and neck supple without jugular venous distention. Cardiac auscultation demonstrates variable S1 intensity consistent with atrial fibrillation, no murmurs or extra sounds. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft with normal bowel sounds, no hepatosplenomegaly, and no flank tenderness. There is neither peripheral edema nor ecchymosis on the extremities. Neurologic screening reveals intact cranial nerves, normal motor strength, and symmetric reflexes. Point-of-care hemoglobin today is 12.9 g/dL and platelet count 230 × 10⁹/L. A baseline coagulation panel drawn simultaneously shows INR 2.3 and activated partial thromboplastin time 33 seconds. Electrocardiogram confirms atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response and no acute ischemic changes. After discussion, the patient expresses interest in possibly transitioning to a direct oral anticoagulant once her ophthalmologist finalizes the surgical date, but she wishes first to understand her individual bleeding risk in the context of her renal impairment and regular alcohol intake; she also reiterates that her home blood-pressure readings remain consistently in the teens over seventies without pharmacologic therapy. She will continue current doses, maintain twice-monthly laboratory monitoring, and report any signs of bleeding or decline in urine output while shared decision-making regarding anticoagulant strategy proceeds.

args: A 66-year-old female was brought to the hospital after being found in acute respiratory distress, having progressively worsened over the prior two days. According to those who accompanied her, she had experienced steadily increasing shortness of breath and was no longer able to maintain adequate oxygenation through simpler measures, prompting urgent placement of a breathing tube before she was transferred. On arrival, prior to any new interventions, she was noted to have a partial pressure of oxygen of 60 mm Hg while receiving 50 % supplemental oxygen through the advanced airway device. Her oxygen saturation remained borderline despite the relatively high fraction of inspired oxygen, indicating significant compromise in gas exchange and explaining why more invasive support was deemed necessary in the field. She appeared fatigued but was alert enough to respond to spoken questions and show appropriate orientation, although she occasionally required prompts to focus on the conversation. She was able to move all four limbs on command without difficulty, and her verbal responses, while somewhat delayed, were essentially coherent and indicated only mild confusion. She had a Glasgow Coma Score was 14, consistent with near-intact comprehension and mostly preserved mental function despite the severity of her respiratory issue. Her circulation did not appear to be in crisis; blood pressure recordings demonstrated 118/72 mm Hg, suggesting that her vascular tone was sufficient. Findings from an initial blood draw showed a borderline platelet count of 137 000 /µL, though not alarmingly low. Her total bilirubin measured 0.7 mg/dL, within the low-normal range, suggesting no overt dysfunction in hepatic excretory processes or hemolysis at presentation. She likewise exhibited stable kidney indicators on preliminary laboratory analysis, with a creatinine of 0.75 mg/dL suggesting adequate filtration and no immediate sign of acute renal impairment. She mentioned that in the previous twenty-four hours, she had passed 1650 mL/day of urine, supporting the notion that her kidneys remained active and functional in spite of her compromised respiratory status. On physical examination, her chest showed diminished air entry bilaterally, and the mechanical support device delivered regular ventilatory cycles to ensure alveolar recruitment. She had no swelling in her lower extremities and no jugular distension, diminishing worry about fluid overload or cardiogenic causes for her respiratory deterioration. While her vital parameters were not profoundly unstable, the low arterial oxygen level in the face of significant additional oxygen pointed toward a serious pulmonary lesion, infection, or inflammatory process. She displayed no obvious discoloration of the skin or sclera, and her abdominal exam was largely benign apart from slight epigastric tenderness, which she attributed to a reduced appetite and minimal intake over the past day. There were no obvious signs of overt infection externally, though the possibility of a respiratory source remained high on the differential given her acute presentation. Blood cultures, sputum studies, and imaging were initiated promptly to investigate underlying causes. The clinical team planned careful observation of her neurological alertness, as any deterioration could signal further compromise warranting deeper sedation or escalation of respiratory strategies. They also intended to monitor her urine output closely to ensure continued protection of renal function. Given her stable blood pressure, lack of signs pointing to major hepatic distress, and adequate, if slightly diminished, platelet count, the immediate concern was to maintain proper ventilation through the advanced airway, address potential causes of severe lung injury or compromise, and support her general condition while awaiting diagnostic clarity. Despite the gravity of her situation, her near-normal awareness, relatively normal measures of central perfusion, and consistent urine output offered a degree of cautious optimism that swift identification of the etiology and targeted intervention could forestall further decompensation.

args: A 66-year-old female was brought to the hospital after her family noted two days of progressive confusion, mild breathlessness, and a general reluctance to engage in her usual activities. When she arrived, she was breathing on her own, yet her oxygen needs were clearly above room air, as testing at admission showed a partial pressure of oxygen of 83 mm Hg while she inhaled 41 % supplemental oxygen. She was awake but drowsy, communicating in halted sentences and occasionally struggling to track conversations, which pointed to a moderate deficit in her mental status. On closer inspection, she was able to respond to questions yet had difficulty obeying more complex commands, and her eye-opening was sometimes delayed; overall, her clinical exam suggested that her level of alertness was neither fully intact nor severely depressed but clearly represented a meaningful change from her normal baseline. Her Glasgow Coma Score was 11. She was found to have borderline low pressures in her vascular system on initial measurement, with a systolic value of 69 mm Hg and a diastolic reading of 55 mm Hg, signaling the need for cautious monitoring of her circulatory stability. Notably, she arrived with intravenous lines already infusing small to moderate doses of several medications that can support both heart function and blood vessel tone: dopamine 0.9 µg/kg/min, dobutamine 1.2 µg/kg/min, norepinephrine 0.13 µg/kg/min, and epinephrine 0.04 µg/kg/min. Her blood tests spoke to a multifaceted clinical picture. Platelet count was 172 000 /µL, below the upper end of normal but not frankly thrombocytopenic. A notable elevation in her bilirubin was discovered at 2.7 mg/dL, which correlated with mild yellowish discoloration observed in her sclera and raised the question of an underlying hepatic or hemolytic issue. By contrast, her kidney function was only slightly off standard references, with creatinine measuring 1.05 mg/dL, and she produced a daily urine volume of 1300 mL/day, showing that her filtration mechanism was not drastically impaired at presentation. Family members reported that she had displayed no obvious signs of infection or recent trauma, though she had complained of fatigue and mild abdominal discomfort earlier in the week. On examination, she had no specialized device supplying constant elevations in airway pressure, nor did she require a more advanced tube-based intervention, and her chest expansion appeared adequate if somewhat shallow. The remainder of her physical survey did not disclose obvious injuries, skin manifestations, or focal neurologic deficits outside of her overall reduced consciousness and slowed responses. She was not described as severely nauseated, yet she had taken very little by mouth over the past 48 hours. Her heart sounds were faint but regular, and there were no prominent murmurs or extra beats. General inspection did not reveal ascites, significant swelling in her extremities, or other stigmata that might point to chronic hepatic dysfunction, though her mild discoloration remained an important clue prompting further exploration. The team prioritized maintaining her circulation in a narrow optimal zone, given that her blood pressures were tenuous, and also watched her oxygenation closely due to her suboptimal gas exchange on moderate supplemental support. Imaging studies and cultures were promptly ordered to exclude an evolving infectious etiology, and additional laboratory panels were drawn to probe whether there was an emerging process in her liver or any new insult to her kidneys. While her platelet numbers were not dangerously low, the combination of confusion, mild pigment elevation, and borderline–though not profoundly abnormal–renal parameters mandated a comprehensive evaluation. Her mental status wavered between brief moments of lucidity and extended lapses where she required repeated prompting, so neurological checks were scheduled at frequent intervals. The family was asked about possible medication exposures or recent dietary changes that could shed light on her acute presentation. Ultimately, the early impression was that she faced a precarious blend of neurologic alteration, modestly stressful respiratory needs, and mild but potentially worsening bilirubin elevation, all taking place within the context of her uncertain blood pressure and reliance on mild to moderate circulatory support. Her condition demanded vigilant observation and further diagnostic steps, with the aim of pinpointing the cause behind this constellation of symptoms and laboratory abnormalities before any deeper organ dysfunction could take hold.
What is the patient's Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) Score?

args:
A 59-year-old woman attended her General Practitioner (GP) with a 3-week history of lethargy, myalgia, pyrexia and night sweats. She had no significant past medical history and was not on any regular medications. Her most recent travel had been to Spain a few months previously, and she lived at home with her husband and dog, who were in good health. Her GP performed some blood tests, shown in , which prompted urgent referral for admission to hospital due to the notably raised white cell count and CRP.
In the Emergency Department the patient was pyrexic with a temperature of 39.4 °C. Otherwise, her observations were normal. She had no audible murmurs and the rest of her examination was unremarkable.
Chest X-ray was normal and ECG showed sinus rhythm with a rate of 76 beats per minute and first-degree AV block. One set of blood cultures and urine cultures were sent and she was started on IV piperacillin/tazobactam for pyrexia of unknown origin. She was tested for influenza by PCR of a nasopharyngeal swab, which was negative.
Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE), carried out on day 2 of admission, demonstrated a mass on the aortic valve, which was further characterized by trans-oesophageal echocardiography (TOE), identifying a 1.8×0.9 cm mass with thickening of the aortic root, suspicious for root abscess.
On day 2 of admission, when the TTE raised the suspicion of endocarditis, she had seven further sets of blood cultures sent over a period of 72 h and was changed to IV amoxicillin 2 g 4 hourly, and gentamicin 3 mg kg−1 OD, as empirical treatment for native valve infective endocarditis.
She was transferred to cardiac surgery on day 4 of admission and underwent aortic valve replacement surgery (). Procedure note from Cardiothoracics as follows: ‘Via oblique aortotomy, tri-leaflet valve was seen with a huge vegetation and perforation at the annulus. There was a small abscess cavity in the annulus at the non-coronary cusp. The native valve was excised, and the cavity was debrided easily, with rifampicin washout. A size 21 Inspiris Reslia bioprosthetic valve was implanted in annular position with 15× Ethibond 2/0 interrupted technique (5 Teflon pledgeted at the Non coronary cusp area). Aorta closed in single layer, using Haemoseal pledgeted sutures. De-aired. Cross-clamp off. Right Ventricular pacing wire inserted and checked. Weaned off Cardio-Pulmonary Bypass. Good valve function with no paravalvular leak and adequate de-airing confirmed on Trans-oesophageal ECHO. Mediastinal and pericardial drains inserted. Protamine given, haemostasis checked/effected.’
The valve was sent by the cardiac surgeons to the microbiology laboratory for culture, and sent away for 16s PCR testing. It was not sent to histopathology.
On day 5 of admission, the aerobic bottle of the first set of blood cultures taken flagged positive after being incubated in Bact T/Alert 3-D (bioMérieux, France) for 3.5 days. Gram-negative rods were observed on Gram stain (). Given this information, the patient’s antibiotics were changed to ceftriaxone, vancomycin and gentamicin, whilst waiting for culture and identification of this organism. Subculture on Columbia blood agar yielded fine smooth, creamy white and non-haemolytic colonies. Species identification was performed using Vitek MS [matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS)], which confirmed the organism as C. carnimorsus. As there were no recommended European Committee on Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (EUCAST) antibiotic breakpoints, E-test strips were set up and minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) were measured against EUCAST PK-PD (non-species-related) breakpoints. This organism was susceptible to gentamicin, ceftriaxone, penicillin, ciprofloxacin and meropenem. The remaining sets of blood cultures were all negative after 5 days of incubation.
Given the identification of this organism, the patient’s antibiotic therapy was changed to Meropenem, Vancomycin and Gentamicin pending susceptibility testing. The heart valve was cultured and was negative after 7 days of incubation in enrichment broth. The valve was also sent to the Public Health England reference laboratory for 16S rDNA real-time PCR identification. Report: this identified species, with data indicating a 99 % sequence homology to C. cynodegmi, C. canimorsus and C. stomatis, providing robust evidence to confirm the responsible pathogen. The patient’s treatment was, therefore, targeted towards C. canimorusus, based on the antibiotic susceptibility test results () obtained through E-test, which were interpreted using EUCAST non-species-specific PK-PD breakpoints. Her antibiotic therapy was changed to IV benzylpenicillin, to complete 4 weeks of treatment.
The patient improved clinically and was discharged home after completion of treatment and a repeat ECHO prior to discharge showed normal left ventricular function and prosthetic aortic valve, with a repeat ECHO and follow-up planned in 1 year.

args:
A 42-year-old African American woman with a history of hypertension, morbid obesity, and obstructive sleep apnea presented to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of left-sided abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. At presentation, her medications included hydrochlorothiazide, acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and lisinopril. The patient is married, has three children, and has never smoked, consumed alcohol, or used illicit drugs.
The patient had travelled to Ghana for 5 days, 1 month prior to presentation. She denies any symptoms during or since her trip until her current symptoms started. She was prescribed lisinopril for hypertension, and her first dose of lisinopril 5 mg was 12 h before the onset of her symptoms. She had never taken an ACE inhibitor (ACEi) or angiotensin II receptor antagonist (ARB) before.
On presentation, she had crampy abdominal pain that radiated to the left side of her abdomen. Her pain was constant and increasing but did wax and wane in severity. She also had persistent nausea with one bout of emesis. She had one nonbloody bowel movement of normal caliber within 24 h of presenting and denied any recent diarrhea, constipation, or recent sick contacts.
Vitals at presentation included a blood pressure of 187/122, pulse of 81, temperature of 36.7°C, respirations of 20, and an O2 saturation of 100% on room air. Physical examination showed a patient that appeared uncomfortable and at times sitting up and writhing in bed with her eyes closed. Her abdomen was tender to palpation in the left epigastric area and left mid-abdomen, with mild left suprapubic tenderness. She also had hyperactive bowel sounds but no rebound tenderness or significant guarding.
In the emergency department, the patient received intravenous fluids, as well as pain and nausea medications. The laboratories showed an unremarkable CBC. Her C1 esterase inhibitor functional assay was within normal limits along with a normal C4 concentration making type I and type II hereditary angioedema unlikely. Stool studies were negative for infectious diseases. CT of the abdomen showed segmented target-like enhancement of a dilated loop of ileum with relatively high attenuation ascites (Figs and ). The initial differential included inflammatory bowel disease, infectious enteritis, vasculitis intramural hemorrhage, and lymphoma.
Given her admission clinical and radiographic findings, drug-induced visceral angioedema due to ACEi was suspected. Lisinopril was discontinued and the patient's abdominal symptoms resolved completely in 48 h. The patient was discharged to home on 5 mg of amlodipine for blood pressure control and instructed to follow-up with her primary care provider. On follow-up 2 and 8 weeks later, her symptoms had not returned.

args:
A 28-year-old man was brought to the Emergency Department (ED) by his father due to acute altered mental status and shortness of breath since earlier that morning. His medical history was significant for poorly controlled Type I Diabetes Mellitus with multiple hospitalizations due to diabetes ketoacidosis (DKA), severe chronic alcohol abuse for 8 years, and chronic pancreatitis. He was also diagnosed with nasopharyngeal carcinoma at age 18, for which he underwent concurrent chemoradiotherapy. According to his father, he had not eaten food since the night prior but consumed large amounts of alcohol. Upon arrival at the ED, the patient was stuporous, tachypneic at 26 breaths per minute, blood pressure 134/109 mmHg, heart rate 85 beats per minute, and afebrile. Initial blood work showed glucose of 420 mg/dL, high levels of alcohol (222.7 mg/dL), metabolic acidosis, elevated ketones (5.4 mmol/L), pancytopenia (WBC: 1.03 × 103/uL, Hemoglobin: 9.9 g/dL, Platelet count: 32 × 103/uL) prolonged prothrombin time (19.8 s) and activated partial prothrombin time (> 180 s), hyperammonemia (876 ug/dL), elevated lactate (19.9 mmol/L), elevated liver enzymes (AST 3112 U/L, ALT 328 U/L, Gamma-glutamyl-transferase 1100 U/L), elevated creatinine (2.8 mg/dL) and Blood urea nitrogen (22.0 mg/dL). Initial treatment for his DKA state was given at the ED. However, 3 h later, the patient developed hypotension (BP = 82/42). Norepinephrine was given and the patient was subsequently admitted to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for further workup and treatment.
At the ICU, the patient was intubated due to hypoxemic respiratory failure. He underwent continuous renal replacement therapy owing to acute kidney injury and severe metabolic acidosis. Empiric antibiotics were prescribed because of suspected sepsis (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment Score: 9). His blood culture further confirmed growth of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa and Acinetobacter Baumannii. Antibiotics were adjusted according to susceptibility. Additional blood work revealed negative HBV, HCV, and HIV antibodies. His condition gradually improved and was successfully extubated on day 10.
However, on day 11 of admission, massive hematochezia of 1500 mL was noted, and his hemoglobin dropped from 9.5 to 8.2 g/dL. Blood transfusion, vitamin K, and tranexamic acid were immediately administered. Being hemodynamically stable the next day, the patient underwent colonoscopy. During the procedure, other than blood clots and stool visualized along the proceeding scope, a deep ulcer covered with mucus with peripheral elevation was noted at the transverse colon (Fig. ). A biopsy was done and the specimen was sent to pathology. Histopathology of the biopsy revealed nonpigmented, wide (5–20 µm in diameter), thin-walled, ribbon-like hyphae with few septations and right-angle branching suggestive of mucormycosis demonstrated by Periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain (Fig. ). Blood cultures taken at that time were negative and upper endoscopy did not reveal similar lesions.
Upon receiving the pathology report 5 days after the colonoscopy, the patient was started on oral Posaconazole therapy. No hematochezia was observed after starting the treatment and his c-reactive protein levels continued to decline (from 3.2 to 0.16). The patient’s condition gradually stabilized and was subsequently stepped down from the ICU to the ward and discharged 2 weeks later. He received a total of 2 months of Posaconazole therapy. One month post treatment, a follow up colonoscopy did not reveal any lesions (Fig. ).

args: A 66-year-old man arrives for preoperative assessment in anticipation of a planned procedure that is expected to be of limited extent, targeting a minor lesion on his lower extremity. Over the past few weeks, he has remained largely confined to his bed due to persistent discomfort and difficulty ambulating, though he denies any recent large-scale injuries or major surgical interventions in the preceding weeks. Approximately one month ago, he sustained a moderate ankle injury that did not involve a full fracture but was still severe enough to warrant the placement of a firm protective cast to stabilize the affected joint. During that time, he was advised to keep weight-bearing to a minimum and rest as much as possible, further reducing his overall mobility. Even now, he continues to report limited activity and is frequently lying down in order to keep pressure off the affected limb. His past medical history includes a malignant process diagnosed several years prior, for which he underwent appropriate therapy; at present, he notes ongoing routine follow-up to monitor his condition, although he does not voice any acute oncologic concerns. He acknowledges having chronic changes in the superficial veins of his legs, which have been both visibly prominent and occasionally uncomfortable, though he indicates no new or worsening swelling in the limbs. He denies any episodes of extreme leg pain or tenderness that might suggest a previously undiagnosed clot in the deep veins. Moreover, he states that he has never been treated for any clots traveling to the lungs. He also reports no congenital or acquired disorders of the blood that would predispose him to unusual clotting, and there is no record of him requiring specialized medications or monitoring because of such conditions. During examination, his vital signs are stable, and no signs of severe heart or lung disease are evident. He has not experienced shortness of breath, orthopnea, or significant fluid retention that might point toward cardiac dysfunction, nor has he had fever, productive cough, or findings suggestive of a recent pulmonary infection. He specifically denies any hospital admissions for overwhelming bloodstream infections or other severe ailments in the past month, and there is no mention of neurologic deficits consistent with a cerebrovascular event or recent debilitating trauma. On physical inspection, his lower extremity shows some residual stiffness near the ankle, consistent with his recent immobilization, but the overlying skin is intact, and he has partial range of motion with mild discomfort. He is alert, oriented, and cooperative, though clearly frustrated by his prolonged inactivity. His current plan involves proceeding with a small-scale operation that his surgical team believes will adequately address his foot issue without the need for a lengthy hospital stay. Nonetheless, he expresses concern that his reduced activity level and past health issues might place him at higher risk for certain complications, and he wishes to ensure that all necessary precautions are in place. He has been counseled about the importance of following up promptly for any unexpected swelling or pain in his legs, as well as adhering to any recommended strategies to enhance circulation and reduce stasis postoperatively. In the meantime, he continues to rest but hopes that once the procedure is completed and he has recovered, he will be able to resume a more active lifestyle despite his history of venous discomfort. Overall, his vital parameters remain within normal limits, and his planned procedure will likely go forward as scheduled, with anticipatory steps in place to mitigate any potential adverse events related to his ongoing reduced mobility and background of previously treated malignancy.

args: A 42-year-old male patient presented with complaints of distension of abdomen, decreased urine output and edema of feet. He had a history of melena, paracentesis and upper GI endoscopy with banding of grade 3 varices. He had also history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. He was not alcoholic and his autoimmune antibody profile was negative. Twenty-four-hour urinary copper and serum ceruloplasmin were normal. Kayser Fleischer’s ring was not found on slit lamp examination. His lab findings on presentation included a total billirubin of 1.6 mg/dL (direct 1.0 mg/dL, indirect 0.6 mg/dL), alanine aminotransferase of 55 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase of 35 U/L, alkaline phosphatase of 120 U/L, international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.49, serum albumin of 2.8 g/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.62 mg/dL. Once diagnosed having decompensated cryptogenic cirrhosis of liver, he was placed on liver transplant waiting list. He was on diuretic on presentation. He was initially managed with plasma expander and omission of diuretics. Creatinine rose above 2 mg/dL. His urinalysis showed no evidence of microscopic proteinuria or microalbuminuria. All other possible causes of renal failure were ruled out. He was thus diagnosed with HRS type 1. The patient did not respond to combination therapy with albumin and terlipressin and his serum creatinine increased to 5 mg/dL. He was placed on hemodialysis. The patient was kept on hemodialysis for 10 weeks when he received a liver from a cadaver donor. Donor was a 50-year-old brain-dead woman who died of MVA. At that time, considering patient’s renal dysfunction, CLKT was carried out. The patient was maintained on continuous renal replacement therapy during perioperative period. The inferior epigastric artery was preserved to avoid wound-related complications. Postoperatively, the patient did not require any kinds of renal support. After two years of follow-up, the patient was maintained well on tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil and steroid.

His vital signs were: temperature 38.8° C, pulse 92 bpm, respiratory rate 24 per minute, BP 115 / 64 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 95% on room air. He had right-sided chest dullness and crackles. The rest of his exam was negative. His medication included insulin and tacrolimus: 1 mg orally twice a day. His laboratory findings were: WBC 0.5×106/L, hemoglobin 79 g/l, platelets 9×106/L, glucose 13.7 mmol/l, sodium 125 mmol/l, bicarbonate 13 mmol/l, serum creatinine 143 micromol/l, INR 1.5, PT 13.3 sec. Cultures, including acid-fast bacilli, were negative. On his admission, chest x-ray showed a large round opacity adjacent to the right hilum []. Computerized Tomography (CT) scan of the chest confirmed the presence of a 9 × 9 cm mass, with air bronchograms, occupying most of the right upper lobe []. He was admitted with the working diagnosis of neutropenic fever and right upper lobe lung mass. He was started on broad antibiotic coverage with ceftazidime, vancomycin and caspofungin. The patient underwent transthoracic core biopsy of this mass, the histopathologic diagnosis was: pulmonary mucormycosis []. He was subsequently switched to intravenous liposomal amphotericin B therapy. His condition continued to deteriorate requiring transfer to the Intensive Care Unit for mechanical ventilation. His blood count never recuperated despite treatment with granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF) and repetitive transfusions. He died 12 days after admission.

args: A 68-year-old woman came to the electrophysiology office for a scheduled visit to reevaluate her long-term rhythm management and antithrombotic regimen in the setting of persistent, non-valvular atrial fibrillation first detected two years ago during routine pre-operative screening for a cataract extraction. She states that over the past three months she has had intermittent episodes of rapid, irregular heartbeat accompanied by mild exertional dyspnea, each lasting several hours, but she has not experienced syncope, transient neurologic deficits, vision loss, or slurred speech. Her medical history is notable for blood-pressure elevation diagnosed in her early fifties; despite adherence to losartan 100 mg daily, her home cuff typically records readings in the mid-150s/90s, and in clinic today a manual measurement after five minutes of rest is 164/94 mm Hg. She underwent percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a drug-eluting stent in the obtuse marginal branch six years ago for unstable angina and has remained on enteric-coated aspirin 81 mg each morning, along with rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly. For chronic lumbar spinal stenosis she takes naproxen 220 mg up to twice daily during activity-related flare-ups, most recently three days ago after a long car ride, and she notes occasional bruises on her shins after gardening but denies nosebleeds, gum bleeding, hematuria, melena, hematochezia, or menorrhagia prior to menopause. She has never required hospitalization for gastrointestinal hemorrhage, intracranial bleeding, or anemia. There is no personal or familial history of hemorrhagic diatheses, connective-tissue disorders, or prior ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, and she has never been diagnosed with transient ischemic attack. She reports no history of chronic kidney problems; her most recent serum creatinine obtained last week during pre-visit laboratory evaluation was 0.8 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 88 mL/min/1.73 m², and urinalysis showed trace protein without hematuria. Comprehensive metabolic panel demonstrated normal transaminases, bilirubin, and albumin. She drinks a glass of white wine with dinner on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday evenings, totaling about 3 alcoholic drinks per week, does not smoke, and has never used recreational drugs. Medications in addition to those above include apixaban 5 mg twice daily for stroke prophylaxis, metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily, omeprazole 20 mg in the morning, calcium plus vitamin D, and an over-the-counter fish-oil supplement. She is retired from elementary-school teaching, lives with her spouse, walks two miles on a treadmill five mornings a week, and helps care for three young grandchildren. Review of systems is negative for weight loss, fevers, night sweats, easy fatigue, or abdominal pain. On physical examination she appears well nourished and in no acute distress, body mass index 26 kg/m². Pulse is irregularly irregular at an average rate of 82 beats per minute; respirations 14 per minute; temperature 36.6 °C. Carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruits, jugular venous pressure is not elevated, heart sounds reveal variable S1 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and the point of maximal impulse is nondisplaced. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly; bowel sounds are normoactive. Extremities are warm with 2+ dorsalis pedis pulses and no edema, petechiae, or ecchymoses except for faint yellowing bruises over the right anterior tibia. Neurologic examination shows normal mental status, cranial nerves II–XII intact, symmetric strength and reflexes, intact coordination, and normal gait. Skin is dry and intact with no telangiectasias or spider nevi. Current laboratory data reviewed today include hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, hematocrit 41%, platelet count 235 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, and international normalized ratio measured for baseline documentation at 1.0. Electrocardiogram obtained at this visit shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response averaging 78 beats per minute, QRS 92 ms, QTc 428 ms, and no acute ST-segment deviations. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed two months ago demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%, moderate left atrial enlargement, and mild concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy without valvular vegetations or pericardial effusion. She expresses a desire to minimize her likelihood of future hemorrhagic complications while maintaining adequate cerebrovascular protection, particularly as she anticipates a dental extraction in the coming months, and she is interested in strategies to tighten systolic blood-pressure control and reconsider the necessity of chronic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory use.
