args: A 58-year-old male arrived at the hospital complaining of worsening cough, shortness of breath, and feeling overly fatigued for the past four days. He noted that he had been more winded than usual when attempting familiar daily tasks at home and felt a persistent tightness in his chest that prompted him to seek medical attention. He admitted to having a history of a serious event in his brain’s circulation some years prior, although he reports he recovered enough to continue independent living without major neurologic limitations in his routine. He also acknowledged ongoing problems related to the function of his kidneys, which have required close monitoring of his blood work at regular intervals. The patient denied any confusion or loss of orientation, stating that despite feeling ill, he had remained fully aware of his surroundings and able to describe his symptoms accurately. On examination, his heart was beating at 89 times per minute, and his respiratory rate was noted at 31 breaths per minute, reflecting evident distress in drawing adequate air. His blood pressure measured 133 systolic over 78 diastolic mm Hg, and his temperature was recorded at 37.2 degrees Celsius, a figure close to normal limits but still representing an uncomfortable warmth for him subjectively. Inspection of his breathing revealed visibly labored inhalation, with the patient occasionally pausing between sentences to catch his breath. He reported several previous episodes over the last year in which abnormalities in his kidney-related lab work arose, though he did not recall ever requiring an advanced procedure to address the issue. Along with an appetitive slump and a sense of malaise, he mentioned that he rapidly became winded after modest exertion, such as climbing a single flight of stairs or walking across a large room. Laboratory studies sampled upon admission uncovered an elevated measurement of nitrogenous waste at 34 mg/dL, which the patient’s medical team found consistent with his known predisposition for kidney challenges, compounded by the possibility of reduced fluid intake given how unwell he felt. Further testing showed that his serum sodium was 149 mEq/L, from which the staff inferred that his electrolyte balance might have been affected by his recent decrease in oral intake and the physiologic strain of his illness. A check of his blood acidity level revealed a pH of 7.49, high enough to suggest he may be overventilating in response to his lung infection, with his rapid breathing driving off more carbon dioxide than usual. In keeping with his respiratory complaints, an arterial sample indicated that the pressure of oxygen in his blood was 73 mm Hg, illustrating the compromise in gas exchange suspected by his providers when they first noted his heightened respiratory rate. His red blood cell concentration, measured at 49 percent, hinted that his oxygen-carrying capacity remained relatively robust, though his ability to utilize it was diminished by his current pulmonary difficulty. A concurrent glucose measurement of 118 mg/dL was only slightly higher than ideal and might have reflected the stress his body was under rather than a chronic metabolic irregularity. On imaging, there was a clear sign of an infiltrative process affecting portions of his lung parenchyma; however, there was no discernible fluid collection in the space around the lungs, which helped focus attention on primary inflammatory changes rather than any significant accumulation requiring drainage. Despite feeling exhausted, he remained logical and coherent throughout the interaction, recounting how his reduced exercise tolerance had progressed quickly over the past few days and acknowledging that he had been trying to manage symptoms at home until they grew too severe to handle independently. He insisted that he had not needed assistance with daily tasks outside of occasional rides to medical visits, and he specifically recalled no requests for inpatient custodial care at any point. Nonetheless, he became genuinely concerned when his breathing seemed to worsen, and his history of prior vascular problems in the brain made him all the more vigilant about L seeking prompt evaluation whenever new or concerning symptoms emerged. The physical findings combined with his presenting complaints and laboratory values strongly suggested a lung infection acquired in his regular environment, posing additional risk given his kidney-related vulnerabilities and prior vascular disease. As a result, his providers moved to closely evaluate his status before initiating targeted interventions, gathering as much baseline data as possible to guide the decision on next steps, while the patient remained under watchful observation for any sign of further decline in his breathing or overall condition.

A 65-year-old woman was referred for colonoscopy because of abdominal pain and hematochezia. She had a history of end-stage renal disease, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus. She underwent hemodialysis three times per week. The patient's hemodynamic parameters were stable. On physical examination, she was found to have right abdominal pain and tenderness. Laboratory tests revealed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 13,900/mm2, a hemoglobin level of 9.4 g/dL, and a platelet count of 228,000/mm2. Serum biochemistry showed creatinine level, 6.6 mg/dL; albumin level, 3.0 g/dL; alanine aminotransferase level, 36 IU/L; aspartate aminotransferase level, 38 IU/L; bilirubin level, 1.2 mg/dL; and alkaline phosphatase level, 198 IU/L with normal electrolytes. Serum was negative for antinuclear antibody and HIV antibody. Anemia was associated with chronic renal failure. On stool examination, negative results were obtained for stool WBC, stool culture, and Clostridium difficile toxin A/B. The electrocardiogram showed normal results. An esophagogastroduodenoscopy revealed no evidence of bleeding. Colonoscopy was performed after standard bowel preparation and under sedation with 3 mg of midazolam. The colonoscopic findings showed diffuse bowel wall edema. Upon examination of the distal transverse colon, we found several deep linear red mucosal breaks resembling scratches ().
In the ascending colon, dark purple edematous mucosa and necrotic epithelial lesions were found (). Cecal intubation was not performed because of severe ischemic changes with necrosis. The remaining colorectal mucosa was grossly normal. Computed tomography (CT) scan showed moderate-to-severe diffuse bowel wall thickening and focally decreased mucosal enhancement in the right colon. The patient was diagnosed with ischemic colitis based on colonoscopic and CT findings, and clinical manifestations. The patient thus received supportive care. Intravenous fluids were administered to treat dehydration, and total parenteral nutrition with prophylactic antibiotics was provided. The patient was placed on bowel rest until the symptoms resolved. After 3 weeks, follow-up colonoscopy and CT were performed. On follow-up colonoscopy, the previous severe ischemic colonoscopic findings had disappeared, and ischemic mucosal change showed further resolution (). No further mucosal tearing was observed during the follow-up colonoscopy. On CT scans, improvement in ischemic colitis was noted. The patient no longer complained of abdominal pain or hematochezia.

args: A 38-year-old man was transported by ambulance to the hospital after noticing marked enlargement of his right lower leg when he attempted to stand from his couch earlier this evening. Five days ago he developed a febrile viral gastroenteritis that left him profoundly fatigued and he spent the better part of the past four days reclined on a sofa, rising only for brief bathroom visits and minimal meals; he returned to a standing position for the first time this afternoon and immediately perceived a heavy, tight sensation in the posterior calf. He denies any recent falls, direct trauma, twisting injury, insect bites, rashes, or strenuous exercise. His medical history includes mild intermittent asthma controlled with albuterol as needed and no prior thromboembolic episodes, and he has never required orthopedic casting, braces, or limb immobilization. He has not undergone any operative procedures, catheterizations, or endoscopies in the past year, takes no prescription medications other than an as-needed inhaler, and uses no hormonal agents or corticosteroids. He last traveled by air more than six months ago and works remotely as a software engineer. There is no family history of thrombophilia, early myocardial infarction, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease. He does not smoke, drinks beer socially, and denies illicit drug use. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, palpitations, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, dysuria, or fevers since the initial viral illness subsided two days ago. On arrival he appeared mildly anxious but in no respiratory distress. Vital signs: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 per minute, oxygen saturation 99 % on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear to auscultation with good air movement bilaterally. Abdominal examination showed soft, nontender bowel segments with normal active sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated visible fullness confined to the right calf: a flexible tape placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity measured 38.5 cm on the right and 34.8 cm on the left, a disparity of 3.7 cm. The overlying skin was warm and exhibited a lattice of engorged, blue-tinged superficial vessels tracing obliquely from the medial gastrocnemius toward the popliteal crease, distinct from his baseline and without the tortuosity characteristic of varicosities. Skin color was otherwise preserved with no erythema or streaking. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia produced no persistent indentation, and the dorsum of the foot was similarly non-pitting. Palpation of the femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and course of the peroneal compartments elicited no focal tenderness; he described a diffuse dull ache that intensified when he attempted to dorsiflex the ankle against resistance. Range of motion at the knee and ankle was full though movement augmented the sense of tightness. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) were palpable and symmetric, capillary refill was brisk under two seconds, and sensory examination to light touch, pinprick, and vibration was intact. Motor strength was 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy or joint effusion, and the contralateral limb appeared normal. Laboratory studies drawn before any anticoagulation showed hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, platelet count 268 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 7.2 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and normal transaminases; coagulation profile revealed a PT of 12.0 seconds with an INR of 1.0 and an aPTT of 29 seconds. A plain radiograph of the tibia and fibula ordered to exclude occult fracture showed only soft-tissue prominence without bony abnormality. No clinical features pointed toward cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, muscle tear, or lymphedema as an equally compelling explanation for the findings. In light of the objective calf asymmetry exceeding three centimeters, the network of newly distended superficial channels suggesting collateral venous flow, the absence of dependent pitting, his prolonged recumbency over the recent days, and lack of prior thrombotic history or recent surgical intervention, an occlusive process within the deep venous system of the right lower limb is considered highly suspicious and warrants immediate duplex ultrasonographic evaluation.

args: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diet-controlled type 2 diabetes presented to the emergency department late this afternoon complaining of progressive pain and swelling of his left calf that began five days ago while he was at home recuperating from an episode of community-acquired pneumonia. He reported that because of profound fatigue and shortness of breath related to that respiratory illness he had remained predominantly in bed for roughly the past week, getting up only to use the bathroom; he estimated that he had been supine or seated with legs dependent for well over three consecutive days. He denied any recent long-distance travel, trauma to the limb, or intravenous drug use. Four years earlier he experienced a thrombosis in the right popliteal vein that was documented on duplex ultrasonography at that time, for which he completed a six-month course of warfarin with appropriate follow-up and no known recurrence until now. He has not required any casts, splints, or braces to either lower extremity, and he demonstrates full active range of motion and strength in the hips, knees, and ankles without focal motor deficits or sensory loss. On arrival he was afebrile with a temperature of 98.4 °F, blood pressure 138/82 mm Hg, pulse 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air. He appeared mildly uncomfortable but in no acute distress. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs, and clear breath sounds bilaterally. Abdominal examination was benign. Inspection of the lower limbs showed the left calf to be visibly fuller than the right without involvement of the thigh or ankle; when measured 10 cm inferior to the tibial tuberosity, the left circumference was 43 cm compared with 38 cm on the contralateral side, yielding a difference of 5 cm. The skin over the swollen area was warm and mildly erythematous but intact, and there were no engorged, tortuous superficial veins coursing across the shin or posterior calf. Palpation elicited diffuse discomfort throughout the gastrocnemius muscle belly; however, tracing along the expected path of the femoral, popliteal, and posterior tibial veins did not reproduce focal tenderness. The entire limb distal to the knee was well-perfused with brisk capillary refill, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses palpable and symmetric, and no mottling or cyanosis was appreciated. There was no calf cord, no stasis skin changes, and no ulceration. Neurologic testing revealed intact light-touch sensation and normal deep tendon reflexes. He denied pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or sudden dyspnea suggestive of pulmonary embolism, and review of systems was otherwise unremarkable for fever, chills, night sweats, or recent weight loss. He takes lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 500 mg twice daily, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially; family history is noncontributory for clotting disorders. Laboratory studies drawn prior to any anticoagulation showed a complete blood count within normal limits, basic metabolic panel notable only for glucose 132 mg/dL, and hepatic function panel unremarkable; coagulation profile revealed an INR of 1.1 and aPTT of 29 seconds. Given the unilateral calf enlargement exceeding 3 cm, his prior documented thrombotic event, prolonged inactivity in bed over the past several days, absence of an equally plausible alternate explanation such as cellulitis or ruptured Baker cyst, lack of diffuse tenderness along the deep venous tract, and preservation of superficial venous patterns, the clinical suspicion for a recurrent lower-extremity thrombus remains significant. Duplex ultrasonography of the entire left lower limb has been ordered, and treatment decisions will await imaging confirmation.

A 59-year-old Caucasian man with past medical history significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to the emergency department with a threeday history of increasing throat pain and bilateral neck swelling. Associated symptoms included voice hoarseness, shortness of breath, dysphagia, and odynophagia. He denied recent dental procedures, upper respiratory tract infection or history of similar symptoms. Patient’s social history was negative for smoking, alcohol and illicit drug use. Initial vital signs were Blood Pressure 109/68, Pulse 101, Repiratory Rate 16, Temperature 98.1, and O2 saturation of 100% on room air. On exam, the patient was noted to have edema and tenderness to palpation of his entire right neck, with erythema tracking from the superior border of his right clavicle to the angle of the jaw on the right. No induration, crepitus or bullae were noted on the skin. Oral examination was remarkable for edema and erythema of the right anterior tonsil, without exudates or fluctuance, with mild deviation of the uvula to left. The laboratory values were significant for elevated leukocyte count of 29,000 with 89.9 % neutrophils and blood urea nitrogen and creatinine of 37 and 2.32, respectively. Computed tomography (CT) of the neck without contrast showed extensive edema in the oropharynx/hypopharynx, with edema and air within the retropharyngeal and danger space, as well as debris within the piriform sinus ( and ). Given the clinical exam, CT findings highly suspicious for “a gas-forming organism or necrotizing fasciitis,” and his laboratory results, blood cultures were drawn and intravenous clindamycin, vancomycin and ceftriaxone were empirically started. Otolaryngology was emergently consulted and the decision was made to immediately take the patient to the operating room for incision, drainage and washout.
Following nasotracheal intubation in the operating room (OR), the right neck was explored laterally into the retropharyngeal space. Purulent drainage was found to track via an overlying necrotic fascial plane into the parapharyngeal spaces as well as inferiorly into the superior mediastinum. Intraoperative gram stain showed gram positive cocci in both chains and clusters, gram negative rods and gram positive rods. The antibiotic regimen was changed to piperacillin/tazobactam and metronidazole (with discontinuation of clindamycin and ceftriaxone). Final wound culture grew Streptococcus anginosus and coagulase negative staphylococcus. The patient remained intubated postoperatively, and a repeat CT was performed on postoperative day 4 due to persistent leukocytosis. A residual phlegmon in the bilateral piriform sinuses was discovered, and the patient was then taken back to the OR for repeat right neck exploration, direct laryngoscopy, and bilateral incision and drainage of the peritonsillar space. Intra-operatively, cardiothoracic surgery was consulted to perform an open lateral thoracotomy to drain a posterior mediastinal phlegmon.
Despite repeat drainage, the patient began to decompensate, requiring multiple vasopressors to maintain adequate perfusion, and he suffered from persistent fevers, acute renal failure and transaminitis (aspartate aminotransferase 4202, alanine aminotransferase 1922). His leukocytosis continued to rise, peaking at 51,600. On postoperative day 7, the patient suffered a cardiac arrest requiring one round of chest compressions and epinephrine before return of spontaneous circulation. On postoperative day 9, his multisystem organ failure began to improve and the patient was slowly weaned off vasopressors, with successful extubation on postoperative day 12. He was discharged to a rehabilitation institution on postoperative day 21 with a peripherally inserted central catheter line to continue vancomycin, clindamycin and metronidazole for a total of four weeks. On recent follow-up with otolaryngology six weeks after his discharge, he was noted to be doing well and has elected to undergo cosmetic revision of right neck scar, the date of which is to be determined.

args:
A 90-year-old African American female with a history of type 2 diabetes, essential hypertension, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, atheroembolism and atrial flutter on chronic anticoagulation with apixaban presented for new-onset general tonic-clonic seizures, witnessed by her family. The patient had been bed-bound from arthritis in a nursing home, but her mental status had been intact. The patient had recently been hospitalized over 3 weeks ago for COVID-19 pneumonia and was discharged home with home hospice one week before readmission.
According to her family, the patient's mental status had severely declined during her previous hospitalization with COVID-19. She developed staring spells after discharge that culminated into generalized tonic-clonic seizures on the day of the current admission. The episode lasted two seconds. Per her hospice nurse, blood pressures and blood glucose were well controlled at home. The home medications were thoroughly reviewed and found not to have any typical pharmacologic culprits. When she presented to the emergency department, she had two more witnessed seizures. Her blood pressure during this episode was 227/95 mm Hg, her heart rate was 98 beats/min, the temperature was 36.7°C, her respiratory rate was 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation was 93% while she breathed room air. Intravenous labetalol, lorazepam, and levetiracetam were administered. Her blood pressure decreased to 160/70 mm Hg and remained well controlled during her hospitalization. Head CT and basic laboratory work () were unremarkable. Her physical exam was notable for postictal confusion. She was alert and only oriented to person. An electroencephalogram (EEG) detected no evidence of seizure or epileptiform discharges, but generalized slowing with an intermittent focal slowing in the bilateral temporal regions was noticed (). Brain MRI demonstrated subcortical and cortical FLAIR signal abnormality involving the left greater than right parieto-occipital lobes and the left temporal lobe, in a pattern most compatible with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) (Figures –). There was no acute intracranial hemorrhage or infarction.
The patient had no further seizures after being treated with levetiracetam. Her mental state gradually returned to normal. She became more eager to participate in physical therapy and be more independent. She set a goal to walk without her walker and to cook for her family and friends. Her family was pleasantly surprised by this improvement in her mentation. She had no further seizure activity in the hospital and was discharged back home with home care services. She is doing well six months after discharge, is seizure-free, and follows her scheduled appointments. Follow-up MRI four months later after presentation showed complete or near resolution of the lesions (Figures –).
No written consent has been obtained from the patient as there is no patient identifiable data included in the case report.

args: A 57-year-old female with a background of mild chronic joint aches and no known advanced comorbidities presented to the emergency department complaining of progressive malaise, persistent dizziness, and intermittent fever for the past three days. She noted a loss of appetite during this period, along with a decline in her usual level of activity. Upon arrival, she was found to be breathing on her own but requiring moderate supplementation of inspired air to maintain adequate oxygenation, and her arterial blood sample showed a partial pressure of oxygen measuring 86 mm Hg with an FiO2 of 35% oxygen. Clinicians noted that she appeared lethargic, with slurred speech and disorientation to time and place; a formal neurological assessment gave her a score of 9 for GCS. Concern also arose from the fact that she had a blood pressure recorded at 65/58 mm Hg, deemed low enough to prompt immediate investigation into her circulatory status. She was receiving dopamine  3 mcg/kg/min to augment cardiac performance and norepinephrine 0.04 mcg/kg/min to maintain vascular tone. Laboratory data performed shortly after her arrival highlighted several key abnormalities. Platelet count was measured at 140,000 per microliter, falling below normal limits but not at a level typically associated with severe bleeding risk. Her total bilirubin, meanwhile, was reported as 1.9 mg/dL, slightly elevated and suggestive of either hepatic involvement or hemolysis, though it was not high enough to cause overt jaundice or other acute liver failure indicators. With regard to renal status, her serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL placed her at the higher end of normal for her age, and her reported urine output of 1000 milliliters per day raised questions about whether she was receiving adequate fluid intake or whether early renal hypoperfusion might be playing a role in limiting her urine production. She denied any prolonged use of nephrotoxic agents, and her caregiver mentioned that she had been able to produce urine regularly at home until her energy levels fell. She displayed no signs of airway compromise that warranted mechanical support at the time she was first seen, which allowed the team to continue monitoring her spontaneous ventilation closely. Despite her moderate confusion, she was intermittently able to follow simple commands, and her speech—though mumbled—was at times coherent. Her peripheral perfusion exam revealed cool extremities, which correlated with her somewhat low blood pressure readings, but no overt signs of mottling were present. Auscultation of her chest was largely unremarkable except for a few scattered crackles at the bases, and her heart sounds were distant but otherwise normal in rhythm. She was afebrile on initial measurement in the department, but her relative tachycardia and report of fevers at home kept the clinicians vigilant for possible infection. Given her state, screening tests for sepsis and other inflammatory conditions were rapidly initiated, and a broad inquiry into her recent exposures, including dietary habits and any recent travel, was conducted. Family members indicated that she had not taken any newly prescribed medications and had no recorded history of severe allergic reactions, although she did acknowledge having had mild arthritic pain for several years. The plan at the time of presentation, before any escalated interventions, focused on stabilizing her circulation, maintaining her respiratory status through continued oxygen supplementation, and initiating a thorough diagnostic workup, including imaging of the chest and abdomen as needed, to locate any source of infection or inflammation. Confirmatory testing for possible hepatic or biliary issues was also ordered in light of her bilirubin result, and careful measurement of her urinary output was set to continue to gauge her renal perfusion and response to any fluid adjustments. The team remained alert to the possibility that she could deteriorate without prompt treatment, so close observation was prioritized. Despite her drowsiness and the borderline platelet count, no heavy sedation or immediate intubation was undertaken at the point she arrived, as the clinical judgment leaned toward aggressive monitoring with appropriate supportive measures while investigating the underlying cause of her presentation, given her partially preserved respiratory drive and ability to respond on a limited basis.

args: A 72-year-old man was seen in the pre-operative assessment clinic this morning for evaluation prior to tomorrow’s planned infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm repair, which is being undertaken electively after interval radiologic surveillance documented enlargement of the aneurysmal sac from 5.4 cm to 6.2 cm over the past year. He reports progressive abdominal pulsation awareness but no acute abdominal or back pain, and he specifically denies fevers, weight loss, gastrointestinal bleeding, syncope, or exertional dyspnea. The patient’s cardiovascular history is notable for a myocardial infarction seven years ago that was managed with primary percutaneous coronary intervention and drug-eluting stent placement to the proximal left anterior descending artery; he has experienced no further episodes of chest discomfort since that time, and he remains on low-dose aspirin, high-intensity atorvastatin, and a long-acting beta-blocker. He follows regularly with cardiology, last stress study eighteen months ago was negative for inducible ischemia, and his baseline functional capacity is estimated at 6–7 METs as he ambulates two flights of stairs and performs routine yard work without limitation. He has longstanding type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 58, controlled with metformin 1000 mg twice daily and a sodium–glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor; he has never used injectable glucose-lowering agents and finger-stick glucose logs provided today range from 105–160 mg/dL. There is no personal history of cerebrovascular accident or transient neurologic deficits, and no episodes of claudication, limb ischemia, or heart failure decompensation have been documented. Additional medical problems include stage 3a chronic kidney disease attributed to hypertensive nephrosclerosis, mild chronic obstructive pulmonary disease related to remote tobacco use (quit fifteen years ago, 30 pack-year history), and controlled essential hypertension managed with lisinopril and amlodipine. The patient consumes minimal alcohol, denies illicit drug use, and lives independently with his spouse. Family history is significant for coronary disease in both parents in their 60s but none for aneurysmal disease. Vital signs in clinic: blood pressure 138/72 mm Hg, heart rate 58 beats/min in sinus rhythm, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C, body mass index 27 kg/m². Cardiovascular examination reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; peripheral pulses are palpable and symmetric. Lungs are clear bilaterally without wheeze or crackles. Abdomen is soft and nontender with a palpable expansile mass in the midline; no bruit is appreciated. Extremities show no edema; capillary refill is brisk. Neurologic assessment is nonfocal, cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact, strength 5/5 throughout. Pre-operative laboratory data obtained this morning demonstrate hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40%, white blood cell count 6.1 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 210 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 34 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL, which is consistent with his documented baseline over the previous six months. Estimated glomerular filtration rate by CKD-EPI is 42 mL/min/1.73 m². Fasting glucose is 132 mg/dL and HbA1c last checked two weeks ago by his primary physician was 7.1%. Electrocardiogram today shows normal sinus rhythm at 56 beats/min with Q-waves in the anterior leads consistent with prior infarction, no acute ST-T changes, and a normal QTc. Chest radiograph reveals clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, and no pulmonary congestion. Pulmonary function testing on file demonstrates an FEV1 of 80% predicted and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.72 without significant bronchodilator responsiveness. The anesthesia team has reviewed the airway and predicts Mallampati class II, thyromental distance 7 cm, full range of neck motion. NPO instructions have been reiteratively provided, and home beta-blocker will be administered the morning of surgery with a sip of water per protocol. Aspirin will be continued, whereas the SGLT2 inhibitor will be held today and the morning of surgery to mitigate any peri-operative euglycemic ketoacidosis risk; metformin will be withheld on the day of surgery given renal function. Cross-matched blood products are reserved. After extensive counseling covering surgical approach, expected recovery, potential complications including bleeding, myocardial ischemia, renal injury, pulmonary compromise, and wound infection, the patient verbalized understanding and consents to proceed. No new changes in cardiopulmonary status were elicited during today’s visit, and he will be admitted directly to the surgical floor tomorrow morning for pre-incision preparation and cardiac telemetry monitoring.

args:
A 33-year-old Turkish man was admitted to our emergency department with complaints of nausea, vomiting, weakness, diarrhea, and fever up to 38.8°C which had lasted for one month. The patient, who had been diagnosed with FMF at 17 years of age, had been started on a regular hemodialysis program at 29 years of age upon the development of chronic renal failure due to amyloidosis. He was not taking any pills regularly except colchicine tablets.
At his physical examination, his general appearance was apathic and cachectic, his mucous membranes were dry and pale, and his skin was dry and rough with a urochromic color. His blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg, and his heart rate was 81 beats/minute. His heartbeat was rhythmic with auscultation, and there was no additional sound murmur. His abdominal examination showed that his liver extended 5 cm below the costal margin. The hepatic surface was rough and even and was painless on palpation. Traube's space was closed, and the spleen extended 2 cm below the costal margin. His muscles were atrophied at the extremities, and he had shedding hairs and an arteriovenous fistula on the left arm. No other pathological findings were detected during his systemic examinations.
At the time of his first admission to the hospital, his laboratory tests with abnormal values were hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, hematocrit 26.9%, mean corpuscular volume 79fL, bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL, creatinine 8.3 mg/dL, urea 60 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL, sodium 132 mmol/L, International Normalized Ratio 1.30, and prothrombin time 15.6 seconds. Hemoculture, stool microscopy, and stool culture were carried out after we observed recurring high fever attacks during his regular hemodialysis sessions, but he did not have high fever at his follow-up examinations. When no pathogenic microorganisms were detected, intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g twice daily was started empirically. Doppler ultrasonography and echocardiography were performed to identify fistulas, but there was still no obvious explanation for his fever. Abdominal US showed small fluid collections around the liver edge.
After a fever that persisted for more than five days, the patient was started on 1 g of intravenous vancomycin once every four days. Abdominal ultrasonography was performed, and hepatosplenomegaly was detected. The liver edge was regular with hepatic parenchyma and showed no pathological finding. His ductus choledochus (common bile duct), intra-hepatic bile ducts, and gallbladder were all normal. Minimal ascites was detected in the abdomen. Examination of the ascites puncture sample produced no findings in favor of tuberculosis. The patient was scheduled for gastroscopy and colonoscopy but had hypoglycemic attacks during the preparation phase. His esophagogastroscopic examination results were within normal ranges. Examination of rectal biopsy samples taken during colonoscopy revealed type AA amyloidosis.
The patient's fever was closely monitored for hypoglycemic episodes and decreased as a result of two weeks of antibiotic therapy. His ocular fundus examination and cranial MRI scans were normal, but he kept on vomiting frequently. During an attack of hypoglycemia, his laboratory results were blood glucose 36 mg/dL, insulin 0.2 μU/mL, C peptide 1.4 ng/mL, sodium 127 mmol/L, and cortisol 7.68 mcg/dL. A Synacthen stimulation test was planned with consultation by an endocrinologist to investigate for adrenal insufficiency. The maximum cortisol value measured was 8.87 μg/dL, and the patient was diagnosed with adrenal insufficiency, so he was started on prednisolone 40 mg per day administered intramuscularly. Subsequently, prednisolone tablets 10 mg/day were started. The patient's diarrhea came to an end, and his hypoglycemia did not recur. The patient's complaints of fatigue and loss of appetite decreased. He was discharged from the hospital with scheduled out-patient clinic monitoring. Three months later he died in the emergency department as a result of cardiac arrest caused by hypokalemia.
What is the patient's Child-Pugh Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

args: A 62-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department this afternoon for evaluation of progressive discomfort in her right calf that started abruptly 24 hours ago while she was seated at home. She described the pain as a constant dull ache that intensifies with ankle dorsiflexion or when she attempts to bear full weight, and she denied antecedent trauma, falls, or new exercise routines. Her history is notable for stage IIIB invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast diagnosed 18 months earlier; she completed neoadjuvant paclitaxel six weeks ago and continues to receive maintenance endocrine therapy under oncology follow-up. Two years ago she suffered a left middle cerebral artery ischemic stroke that left her with persistent mild right-sided hemiparesis; as a result, she ambulated with a single-point cane and wore a hinged ankle-foot orthosis but had remained largely independent in activities of daily living. She denied recent abdominal or pelvic operations, and her last hospitalization was more than a year ago for completion mastectomy. Medications include anastrozole, low-dose aspirin for secondary stroke prevention, and sertraline; she does not take hormonal contraceptives or glucocorticoids. She has never smoked, drinks wine socially, and lives with her partner. Review of systems revealed no fevers, chills, night sweats, shortness of breath, palpitations, chest pain, or hematuria. On arrival, blood pressure was 128/74 mm Hg, heart rate 84 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 37.1 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. She appeared comfortable at rest but guarded the right lower limb when shifting on the stretcher. Cardiopulmonary examination was unremarkable with regular rhythm, clear lungs, and no jugular venous distention. Abdominal exam showed a well-healed infra-mammary surgical scar without tenderness, masses, or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities revealed subtle fullness of the right calf, with a circumference of 36 cm measured 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity compared to 34.5 cm on the contralateral side. The thigh and ankle circumferences were symmetric, and the foot appeared normally perfused. Skin over the posteromedial calf was warm but not erythematous, and compression left no appreciable indentation. A web-like network of bluish subcutaneous channels was visibly engorged along the posteromedial aspect of the leg, distinct from typical varicosities. Palpation elicited a vague soreness deep to the gastrocnemius; there was no discrete cord, fluctuance, or crepitus. Active range of motion at the knee was limited mainly by her baseline weakness; passive motion was intact without joint tenderness. Distal pulses were palpable and equal. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, hip pain, or back tenderness. Neurologic exam confirmed chronic right lower-extremity power of 4/5 proximally and 3+/5 distally, unchanged from prior outpatient documentation. Laboratory studies obtained at the bedside included a complete blood count showing hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelet count 282 × 10⁹/L, and white-cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L. Comprehensive metabolic panel was within normal limits, except for mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase 152 U/L consistent with her oncologic history. Serum D-dimer was not yet resulted at the time of note completion. Plain radiographs of the tibia and fibula ordered to rule out fracture demonstrated only soft-tissue prominence. Point-of-care ultrasound was deferred pending radiology duplex scanning because the department was understaffed. No cutaneous lesions suggested cellulitis, and there was no evidence of ruptured popliteal cyst on initial ultrasound screening performed during residency teaching rounds. Given the acuity of pain onset, visible superficial channels suggestive of collateral flow, the patient’s active malignancy under treatment, and her chronic neurologic weakness necessitating external bracing, there is heightened concern for an occlusive process in the deep venous system of the symptomatic limb. The clinical picture does not currently favor musculoskeletal strain or peripheral neuropathy, and there is no clinical suspicion for arterial insufficiency. The plan includes urgent venous duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity, basic coagulation profile, and discussion with oncology regarding perioperative anticoagulation options should thrombosis be confirmed.

args: A 78-year-old man was brought to the hospital by his home-health nurse late this morning because of sudden onset enlargement of his left lower extremity that he first noticed while transferring from bed to his recliner the prior evening. He reported that the whole limb—from the groin through the ankle—looked “puffy” and felt heavy when he tried to pivot, forcing him to drag the leg while using his rollator. He denied falling, twisting the knee, sustaining any direct blow, or developing a skin abrasion, and he had not experienced fever, chills, rash, or erysipelas-like warmth. His medical background is notable for metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosed nine months earlier; he remains on a bi-weekly gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel regimen, with his most recent infusion five days ago, and imaging two months earlier had demonstrated stable hepatic lesions without new disease. Two years ago he suffered an incomplete L4 spinal cord infarction that left him with residual 3+/5 motor strength in the left hip flexors and knee extensors and brisk patellar reflexes; he wears an articulated ankle-foot orthosis during the day but no cast or rigid splint, and although he ambulates slowly he completes short household distances several times per day, including climbing three steps to reach the porch, so he has not been confined to bed or a chair for prolonged periods. He has never had a clot in the legs or lungs, has no known thrombophilia, and takes no anticoagulant medication. His regular drugs include long-acting oxycodone for oncologic pain, metoprolol for rate control of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and a proton-pump inhibitor; he does not use estrogenic compounds, corticosteroids, or erythropoietin stimulators. There is no family history of venous thromboembolism, and he quit smoking 25 years ago. On review of systems he denied new cough, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, palpitations, abdominal distention, urinary retention, or constipation. Vital signs at triage were blood pressure 126/70 mm Hg, heart rate 94 beats/minute and irregularly irregular, respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute, temperature 36.8 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % while breathing ambient air. He appeared frail but hemodynamically stable, answering questions in full sentences without dyspnea. Cardiac auscultation revealed an irregular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and lung fields were clear to auscultation bilaterally with no dullness on percussion. Abdominal examination showed a well-healed midline laparotomy scar and mild epigastric tenderness related to tumor burden but no organomegaly or ascites. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated a striking global enlargement of the entire left leg compared with the right; the thigh circumference measured at mid-femur was 57 cm on the left versus 52 cm on the right, and the calf circumference taken 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity was 40 cm on the left and 38 cm on the right, yielding a 2 cm discrepancy. The skin over the affected limb was smooth, taut, and slightly warmer than the contralateral side, yet there was no appreciable indentation after firm thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the foot, suggesting absence of meaningful pitting. Color remained normal without streaking or mottling, and no engorged superficial venous channels were visible along the thigh or calf. Palpation elicited a diffuse sense of pressure throughout the limb but did not localize to a cord-like structure along the femoral, popliteal, or posterior tibial tracks; ankle dorsiflexion produced only his baseline neuropathic tightness. Distal perfusion was intact with posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses 2+ bilaterally and capillary refill less than two seconds. Sensory examination showed diminished pinprick below the L4 dermatome on the left, consistent with prior spinal injury, but no new sensory loss. Manual muscle testing revealed unchanged baseline weakness of the left quadriceps while right-sided strength remained 5/5 throughout. Gait could not be assessed during the encounter because he was transported by wheelchair. Laboratory studies drawn before any treatment showed hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, platelet count 268 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.8 × 10⁹/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.1; serum electrolytes and liver enzymes were within his known chronic baseline. A plain radiograph of the femur obtained in the emergency department showed no lytic lesions or fracture that might account for the swelling. Given the unilateral, full-length enlargement of the limb without a convincing musculoskeletal or infectious etiology, the presence of an active malignancy, and chronic paresis of the same extremity, the index of suspicion for an obstructive process within the deep venous system is high, and vascular ultrasound of the entire left leg has been requested; therapeutic decisions will await definitive imaging because of concern for gastrointestinal bleeding risk with anticoagulation in the setting of pancreatic cancer.

args:
A 67-year-old male with a 6-day history of fever and shortness of breath was admitted to the emergency department on August 15, 2019. This patient had a fever, chill, cough, and progressive dyspnea after being in the rain on August 9, 2019, without a sore throat, chest pain, hemoptysis, paroxysmal dyspnea, dizziness, nausea, abdominal distension, abdominal pain or diarrhea. He first went to a local clinic and was treated with “anti-inflammatory drugs (the specific name is unknown)”, but his symptoms became more severe and rapidly progressive. One day before admission, the patient started having aggravated dyspnea, listlessness, and difficulty talking while lying down, then this patient presented to our emergency room (ER).
The physical exam revealed the following: T 36°C, P 82bpm, RR 28/min, BP 107/64mmHg, SpO2 72%, WBC 20.36×10^9/L, N 18.31×10^9/L; arterial blood gas (ABG) showed PH 7.12, PO2 55mmHg, PCO2 56mmHg, SaO2 85%, Lactate 4.4mmol/L, BE-11.7mmol/L; BUN 18.1mmol/L, creatinine 166umol/L; BNP 249.83pg/mL with peripheral cyanosis and bilateral wheezing. Chest computed tomography (CT) showed a dense consolidation site in the right lung with scattered shadows in both lungs, accompanying by mediastinal and right hilar lymphadenopathy, the CT results indicated the infection lesion (). He had been healthy previously, but he was a smoker with a 30-year history. He did not use illicit drugs and had no known allergies to medications. He worked as a farmer and have no family history. He was diagnosed as follows: 1. Severe pneumonia 2. Sepsis 3. Septic shock 4. Multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) 5. Type II respiratory failure 6. Heart failure 7. Acute renal insufficiency 8. Metabolic acidosis 9. Chronic bronchitis.
Laboratory examinations were supplemented and the results were as follows (August 31, 2019): Blood routine: neutrophil count 7.29×10^9/L, lymphocyte count 0.97×10^9/L, monocyte count 0.82×10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 79.2%, lymphocyte percentage 10.5%, red blood cell count 3.01×10^12/L, hemoglobin 86 g/L, hematocrit 0.28 L/L, mean corpuscular hemoglobin 305 g/L, platelet distribution width 9.2fl, C-reactive protein (CRP) 106.44 mg/L; hepatorenal function: ALT 15U/L, AST 13U/L, Creatinine 75umol/L. Orotracheal intubation was applied to help the patient’s respiration, assisting with the PB840 ventilator. Combined with the examination results of chest CT, the patient was provided with imipenem and cilastatin sodium 1.0g q12h, linezolid 0.6g q12h, and moxifloxacin 0.4g qd for anti-infection therapy. After a 3-day treatment, this patient still had a high fever with a maximum body temperature of 38.5°C. Continuous invasive mechanical ventilation was performed to assist the respiration, meanwhile, continuous vasoactive drugs and intravenous nutrition support were given to him.
Sputum cultures were collected and Gram stain revealed no squamous epithelial cells, few neutrophils, and Gram-negative rods. Within one day, sputum sample results showed P. aeruginosa and multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (). Besides, fiberscope secretion cultures showed P. aeruginosa, which is sensitive to amikacin, gentamicin and piperacillin sodium and tazobactam sodium, intermediate to ciprofloxacin and ceftazidime, but resistant to imipenem and meropenem (). Based on the culture results, we changed the treatment strategy to the combination of polymyxin B (50IU, q12h) and amikacin (0.6g, qd) for 30 days, instead of imipenem (1g q12h) and cilastatin sodium (500mg, q12h).
The patient’s body temperature was back to 37°C, measured by a clinical thermometer for armpit use for 10 minutes. His cough was significantly alleviated with white viscous sputum. On day 21, laboratory examinations demonstrated the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), CRP and procalcitonin were negative; AST 14U/L, total protein 63.2g/L, albumin 30.3g/L, total calcium 2.1mmol/L, phosphorus 0.84mmol/L, sialic acid 723mg/L. On day 23, chest CT was rechecked to confirm the curative effect, which showed the formation of a pulmonary thick-walled cavity in the apical segment of the left upper lobe, with multiple small opacities, branching signs, bronchial wall thickening, and fibrous lesion. The inflammation of the cavity wall was obviously reduced compared with the first CT images (). The patient was discharged in a stable condition on day 35, and he was given acetylcysteine tablets 0.6g/box, 1 tablet, bid. The final diagnoses on discharge were represented: 1. Severe pneumonia 2. Sepsis 3. Septic shock 4. MODS 5. Type II respiratory failure 6. Heart failure 7. Acute renal insufficiency 8. Metabolic acidosis 9. Chronic bronchitis 10. Hypoproteinemia.
After he was discharged, a regular follow-up of 2 weeks was performed to monitor his condition until most indicators came back to the normal level. The results of laboratory examinations showed as follows (September 17, 2019): Blood routine: white blood cell 9.57×10^9/L, neutrophil count 6.78×10^9/L, lymphocyte count 1.24×10^9/L, neutrophil percentage 70.8%, lymphocyte percentage 13%, red blood cell count 3.92×10^12/L, hemoglobin 111 g/L, hematocrit 0.35 L/L, mean corpuscular hemoglobin 318 g/L, platelet distribution width 9.2fl, C-reactive protein (CRP) 19.04 mg/L, PCT 0.19ng/mL; hepatorenal function: ALT 25U/L, AST 37U/L, Creatinine 75umol/L. As shown above, most laboratory results were improved, and the physical examination was normal.

args:
In October 2020, a 59-year-old man was admitted to our Internal Medicine inpatients unit because of the persistence for about 2 weeks of dyspnea, fatigue, and weight loss. The patient also reported right hypochondrium pain and loss of appetite. Past medical history was relevant for hypertension, type 2 diabetes and alcohol abuse (reported alcohol consumption: 3–5 drinks per day from the age of sixteen). In 2016 he was diagnosed with squamous cell lung carcinoma (stage T4N3M0) and treated with neoadjuvant polychemotherapy (cisplatine + vinorelbin) followed by right pneumonectomy. Despite a history of cancer, he was still an active smoker. His home therapy consisted of insulin and acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day.
At admission, blood pressure was 150/75 mmHg, heart rate 92 bpm, oxygen saturation 94% in room air, respiratory rate 20/min, body temperature was 36°C. Physical examination was non-significant apart from hepatomegaly. Acute phase reactants (e.g., fibrinogen, C-reactive protein, ferritin), transaminases, cholestasis enzymes and D-dimer were altered. Hepatitis B and C markers were negative. Sars-CoV-2 nasopharyngeal swab was negative. Chest X-ray showed opacification and volume loss of right hemi-thorax with consensual mediastinal shift, according to history of previous pneumonectomy; no signs of pulmonary consolidation in the left lung. Abdominal US-scan showed hyper-echogenicity of the liver compatible with steatosis and/or fibrosis and biliary sludge. No significant kidneys or spleen abnormalities nor ascites were found. The Esophago-Gastro-Duodenoscopy detected a grade B reflux disease (LA classification), congestive gastropathy and erosive bulb duodenitis. Basing on history, clinical features and Wells' score (0 points) (), PE was unlikely. An echocardiography showed a normal left ventricle ejection fraction, no right ventricle overload nor pulmonary hypertension. To rule out cancer recurrence, total body CT-scan with contrast injection was performed. Chest CT images showed a pulmonary thrombus within the right main artery stump, not present 1 year earlier (). Doppler US-scan of lower limbs was normal. Anticoagulant treatment with enoxaparin 100 ui/kg/bid was started, together with proton pump inhibitor (PPI). Tests for inherited and acquired thrombophilia were negative as well as antibodies against Sars-Cov-2. Patient's symptoms gradually improved and he was discharged 7 days after PAST diagnosis with anticoagulant prescription. Contrast-enhanced CT scan performed 4 months later demonstrated an almost complete resolution of right pulmonary thrombosis ().

An eighteen year old African American male presented to the emergency room with a 1-week history of sore throat, muscle aches, fever, headache, and photophobia. He also complained of nausea, one episode of vomiting, and abdominal pain. He was a college student and lived in a dormitory. He denied any recent travel or contact with ill people. Past medical history was significant for sickle cell trait and Osgood Schlatter’s disease. He was up to date on vaccinations.
Physical examination revealed a tall, thinly built young man, in mild pain with an oral temperature of 97.5°F, blood pressure of 114/69 mmHg, heart rate of 89 beats per minute (BPM) and respirations of 18/minute. He had injected conjunctiva of the right eye, and pharyngeal and tonsillar exudates. The examination of the central nervous system revealed neck stiffness; Kernig’s and Brudzinski’s signs were not elicited. Cranial nerves were intact. The power and deep tendon reflexes were essentially normal. The rest of the systemic examinations were unremarkable.

Chest X-ray revealed bilateral infiltrates in the middle and lower lung fields. Computed tomography (CT) of the head was unremarkable. The patient declined lumbar puncture. The blood culture grew penicillin sensitive Group C Streptococcus (GCS) within 24 hours of incubation. A few hours into the admission, he developed third and sixth nerve palsy. Further work-up revealed right cavernous and ophthalmic vein thrombosis on magnetic resonance imaging ( and ). Multiple patchy cavitary lesions on both lungs were seen in the chest CT. args: A 64-year-old woman arrived to the academic arrhythmia clinic for a scheduled visit to reassess her long-term antithrombotic regimen, which had been initiated fifteen months earlier after ambulatory rhythm monitoring documented symptomatic, non-valvular atrial fibrillation with a ventricular response occasionally exceeding 140 beats per minute. She relates that at the time of diagnosis she was started on warfarin with a target international normalized ratio of 2.0–3.0 and, for rate control, metoprolol succinate 50 mg each morning. Despite diligent pillbox use and weekly telephone contact with the community anticoagulation service, her printed log shows considerable INR variability: over the most recent ten-week span values have swung from 1.6 on two occasions (necessitating dose increases) to 4.1 on one occasion (for which she was told to hold a dose and recheck in forty-eight hours); only four of the ten readings fell squarely in the therapeutic window. She has not required vitamin K, hospitalization, or emergency department evaluation, and she denies hematuria, melena, hematemesis, epistaxis, gingival oozing, easy bruising, or menorrhagia at any time since anticoagulation began. Her past history is otherwise dominated by chronic liver dysfunction attributed to a combination of metabolic-associated steatohepatitis and decades of steady alcohol intake; abdominal ultrasound performed three months ago demonstrated a coarse, nodular hepatic contour with mild caudate lobe enlargement but no ascites, focal masses, or portal vein thrombosis. Laboratory studies on the same date revealed total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 94 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 156 U/L, albumin 3.0 g/dL, and a baseline clotting profile (drawn after withholding warfarin for forty-eight hours) with INR 1.4 and prothrombin time prolongation of 2.5 seconds above control—results that have remained in a similar range for the last year. She has never experienced hepatic encephalopathy, variceal hemorrhage, or spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and surveillance esophagogastroduodenoscopy six months prior did not identify varices. Kidney function has consistently been normal, with a serum creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL and an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 74 mL/min/1.73 m² on a metabolic panel drawn last week; urinalysis was bland. She emphatically reports no prior transient ischemic attack, ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, peripheral embolism, or carotid intervention. A transthoracic echocardiogram acquired two months ago documented preserved left ventricular ejection fraction of 60 %, moderate left atrial enlargement, and trivial tricuspid regurgitation. She expresses concern that the combination of her fluctuating warfarin levels, daily aspirin, intermittent ibuprofen, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor, underlying hepatic impairment, and sizable alcohol consumption might predispose her to a serious hemorrhagic event, and she seeks guidance on whether she should pursue closer INR monitoring, transition to a direct oral anticoagulant, or consider left atrial appendage occlusion as an alternative stroke prevention strategy.

args: A 58-year-old male was transported to the emergency department by local emergency services after being discovered by a family member in his bedroom, unresponsive and lying on his side without any obvious external injuries. According to initial reports, he had a history of chronic hypertension that was inconsistently managed and occasional episodes of lightheadedness reported over the past year, though he had never required hospitalization for these complaints. On arrival, his vital signs showed a mildly elevated heart rate, borderline blood pressure, and shallow but regular respirations. No immediate signs of respiratory obstruction were noted, and his oxygenation remained at an acceptable level on room air, though the clinical team closely monitored him for any downturn. He did not exhibit even a reflexive flutter of his eyelids despite repeated attempts to rouse him with normal speech, calling his name, loud claps near his ears, and even targeted painful stimulation such as squeezing the trapezius and applying pressure to the nail beds. These maneuvers failed to produce any flicker of consciousness or visual acknowledgment, and his gaze stayed persistently closed throughout the entire evaluation. Staff carefully evaluated for the presence of any audible vocalization, but he remained silent and did not offer any murmurs, moans, or nonsensical speech. Even when additional painful stimuli were applied, there was no grunt, no whispered response, and no sign that he attempted to form words. A review of his medical records revealed a history of borderline glucose intolerance discovered during a routine check, though it did not appear he was on any regular medication for this issue. There was also a mention of occasional alcohol use documented at an earlier visit, but it was unclear whether that played any role in his presentation. Initial physical examination noted no major deformities, contusions, or signs of traumatic injury, and his clothing appeared intact without stains or tears that might suggest a significant fall or collapse. Neurologically, when the clinical team administered a firm sternal rub and observed for purposeful withdrawal, he showed no apparent defensive action or reflex. There was no protective motion of any limb in response to pain, and his extremities remained limp when lifted. His pupils were equal, round, and sluggishly reactive, with no lateralizing signs that might suggest a focal brain injury. Cardiac examination revealed a slightly irregular rhythm thought to be consistent with his past hypertension, but no murmurs or gallops were clearly discernible. Pulmonary auscultation was relatively unremarkable apart from mildly diminished airflow at the bases, which could have been related to his reduced overall movement or a chronic underlying condition. Laboratory work was obtained immediately to check for metabolic derangements, possible toxic exposures, or infection that might explain his profoundly depressed level of responsiveness. An urgent imaging study of his head was also completed to look for acute intracranial processes like hemorrhage, infarction, or mass effect that could account for this unresponsive state. The medical team noted that there was no history of recent surgery, seizures, or pronounced head trauma on file, and family members reported that he had been speaking normally the previous evening before retiring to bed. Because he showed no capacity to track stimuli with his eyes, produce even fragmented vocalizations, or demonstrate instinctive retraction from discomfort, there was an immediate concern for a severe underlying pathology. He remained under constant observation in the emergency department while further tests were performed to determine if he suffered from a metabolic encephalopathy, a significant structural brain lesion, or a profound toxic ingestion. Throughout this period, no interventions had been performed aside from supportive measures such as maintaining a patent airway and ensuring stable vital signs. The absence of any movement or communicative sign when confronted with verbal or painful prompts underscored the gravity of his condition, prompting rapid consultations with neurology and critical care teams to guide diagnostic steps and consider urgent treatment options once the cause of his condition could be better understood.

args:
An obese 42-year-old African American male with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, coronary arterial disease, and atrial fibrillation presented with new onset right-sided chest pain and a palpable right chest mass. Ultrasound showed an anterior right chest, well-demarcated 7.5 × 6.5 × 4.8 cm, intramuscular mass, 0.6 cm deep to the skin. CT demonstrated a 9x9x9cm necrotic mass arising from the pectoralis major. CT-guided core biopsy was positive for high-grade spindle cell neoplasm (positive for smooth muscle actin, desmin, S100, and CD31; negative for CD34, PAX8, and beta-catenin) and verified by two independent pathologists.
Unfortunately, the patient was lost to follow up after referral to Oncology and Thoracic Surgery. He presented to oncology clinic with progressive symptoms including a rapidly enlarging chest mass, increasing pain, and new onset chest wall numbness. A repeat CT showed that the mass increased in size to 21.4 × 17.8 × 13.7 cm without evidence of metastatic disease over course of two months. MRI was consistent with 23 cm mass within the right pectoralis major without vascular or bony invasion. The patient chose to undergo neoadjuvant chemotherapy given his multiple comorbidities. He was hesitant to pursue resection and elected to attempt to shrink the tumor before resection.
Patient underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine and docetaxel for 2 cycles followed by radiation therapy (50Gy over 2 months to tumor bed + 3 cm margins). Restaging CT showed a stable tumor at 23 cm without any evidence of local or distant metastases.
The patient returned to Thoracic surgery clinic to discuss options for resection and reconstruction. He then underwent wide local surgical resection for definitive therapy. Tumor was removed en bloc with resection to the intercostal fascia including pectoralis major and minor (Fig. ). There did not appear to be any violation of the intercostal investing fascia and no entry into the thoracic cavity was made. Negative pressure dressing was used until post-operative day 7 when Plastic Surgery performed advancement flap coverage and skin grafting to the > 1,000cm2 defect (Fig. a, b).
Final pathology demonstrated 38x20x18 cm tumor with 70% gross necrosis and R0 resection. Microscopic examination confirmed high-grade sarcoma with smooth muscle differentiation (Fig. ). Final pathologic staging based on AJCC 7th edition was Stage III G3 pT2bNxMx.

args:
An 86-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation, aortic stenosis, and hypertension presented to the emergency department due to altered mental status. She was lethargic, confused, and was not answering questions appropriately for the past four days. On arrival, she was afebrile, and her vitals included a pulse of 139 beats per minute, blood pressure of 110/79 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute with saturation of 96% on room air. Physical exam was significant for a slow to respond female, orientated to self, who was able to follow commands with no focal neurological deficit. Her skin was warm and well-perfused with normal capillary refill, with no rashes or petechiae. The cardiovascular exam was significant for tachycardia, an irregular heart rhythm, and a systolic murmur heard best at the right upper sternal border. She had lower extremity pitting edema bilaterally. Her lab results were notable for elevated white blood cell count (WBC) 25.2 x 103 μL (3.9-11.3 x 103 μL), hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL (11.3-15.1 g/dL), platelets 58.0 x 103 μL (165-366 x 103 μL), troponin 0.164 ng/mL (<0.010 ng/mL), lactic acid 2.5 mmol/L (0.5-2.0 mmol/L), and a basic chemistry panel was within normal limits. The urinalysis was remarkable for the presence of WBCs, leukocyte esterase, and bacteria with a urine culture pending. Additionally, two sets of blood cultures were obtained. Her electrocardiogram was significant for atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular rate (RVR). She was started on ceftriaxone for probable UTI and was admitted to the hospital for further management. Further diagnostics included lower extremity Doppler ultrasound, which revealed deep venous thrombosis (DVT). A bedside echocardiogram indicated right ventricular strain, and the N-terminal proB-type natriuretic peptide level was elevated at 8,215 pg/mL (<449 pg/mL). Due to concern for possible pulmonary embolism in the setting of known DVT, the patient was started on a continuous heparin infusion. A transthoracic echocardiogram revealed severe aortic valve calcification with new aortic insufficiency. Blood and urine cultures returned positive for Aerococcus urinae. A transesophageal echocardiogram further confirmed moderate to severe aortic regurgitation with findings suggestive of infective endocarditis (IE).
Antibiotics were expanded to include gentamicin with ceftriaxone for IE, and surgical evaluation was consulted. Given she was a high-risk surgical candidate, surgery intervention was not offered at this time unless evidence of further clinical decompensation. Additional surgical recommendations included re-evaluation after completion of antibiotic course. She was therefore discharged on intravenous antibiotics to complete a total of six weeks of treatment with ceftriaxone and two weeks of gentamicin.

args: A 52-year-old male patient presented to the urology outpatient department with a history of decreased urine passage along with burning, hematuria and pain lower back and abdomen. History of loss of appetite, vomiting was also elicited, he also gave a history of smoking and alcohol consumption. Patient was advised an ultrasonogram (USG) along with biochemical and hematological investigation. Routine examination of urine was done which revealed few red blood cells. No atypical or malignant cells are seen in three consecutive specimen of urine for cytology His renal function tests were markedly deranged, with blood urea 346 mg/dl, serum creatinine 25 mg/dl and deranged electrolytes. His blood pressure was raised 230/100 mmHg and fundoscopy showed hypertensive retinopathy. USG showed features of cystitis with hypoechoic areas extending into the thickened bladder wall with features of pyelonephritis. An infective pathology was offered as diagnosis on imaging. A clinical diagnosis of bilateral obstructive uropathy due to tumor with chronic kidney disease was made, and the patient was put on hemodialysis and cystoscopy planned at a later date. Cystoscopy findings were of a growth with small fronds/papillae, prominent at the bladder neck with a very rigid thickened bladder wall. Trans urethral resection of the bladder tumor was done and sent for histopathological evaluation. The microscopic evaluation showed a high-grade tumor arranged in discohesive cords and sheet with plasmacytoid features and prominent retraction artifact [] penetrating through the entire bladder wall into the serosa. Histopathological features of the malignant epithelial cells were uncommon and lead to a differential diagnosis of undifferentiated transitional carcinoma, metastatic or primary lymphoma plasmacytoma, lymphoepithelioma-like bladder carcinoma and a plasmacytoid variant of bladder tumor. Immunohistochemistry was done for CD138, cytokeratin (CK) CK7, leukocyte common antigen (LCA), κ and λ light chain, with positivity for CK7, CD138 [Figures and ] and negative for other markers. Based on these findings, a final histopathological diagnosis of PUC was made. The patient was advised to undergo palliative cystectomy, however he is lost to follow-up.

args:
A 57-year-old male was brought to the emergency department by a neighbor who found him lying on the floor of his home in the late afternoon, with his eyes already open but seemingly unaware of the voices and movement around him. According to brief information gathered from the neighbor, the patient had been in declining health for several weeks, and he had recently complained about feeling persistently weak and lightheaded, though he never clearly explained if he sought any formal medical evaluation. On arrival, he did not utter any recognizable words or make any vocal sounds despite persistent attempts by the emergency team to gain his attention with varied tones, including calmly calling his name and then slowly increasing the volume of commands. Throughout these efforts, he kept his gaze forward, blinking occasionally but failing to follow any moving objects or turn his head toward staff members. His vital signs at the time of entry suggested mild tachycardia and a blood pressure that was within a borderline range, neither acutely alarming nor completely normal. Respirations were slow but rhythmic, prompting the examining clinician to ensure that his airway remained patent, especially given his lack of any verbal output. A look into his pockets provided only a set of house keys and a folded sheet listing several medications, though it was unclear if he had been taking them consistently or if the list was up to date. Examination of his head and neck revealed a small bruise over the left temple, though the skin was not broken and there was no active bleeding; it was unclear whether this finding signaled recent trauma or an incidental bump that had occurred days earlier. Further neurological assessment showed that, while he did not offer words or audible attempts at speech, his eyes remained open the entire time without any prompting to keep them that way. When gentle touch was replaced with more focused pressure to gauge whether he would react, he did indeed respond, yet the nature of his movements was not aligned with purposeful withdrawal or an effort to push the examiner’s hand away. Instead, his limbs stiffened and extended outward, a pattern that repeated each time staff applied even moderate noxious stimuli. No clear attempt to localize or grasp at the source of discomfort was noted, though the sudden tensing of arms and legs revealed he could perceive painful sensations to some degree. A colleague from the laboratory arrived to draw blood for urgent metabolic panels, suspecting that his undetermined condition could be linked to blood sugar imbalances, electrolyte derangements, or possible renal issues given his age and uncertain medication use. The patient’s skin was somewhat cool, and staff observed a faint film of perspiration on his forehead, but there was no frank cyanosis. Pupils were symmetrical and responded to light, though he neither tracked the flashlight nor blinked at direct visual cues. The neighbor mentioned that he had lived alone for at least a decade without close family nearby, so it was unclear if he had a recent infection, an underlying cardiac condition, or any psychiatric history that might have led to his current state. No strong odors of alcohol or other substances were detected, and there were no obvious pill bottles scattered around the immediate area where he was found. Nonetheless, toxicology screening was also arranged to explore whether an unintentional or deliberate ingestion might have contributed to this presentation. Imaging of his head and cervical spine was ordered promptly to rule out an acute traumatic injury or bleed, particularly given the bruise on his temple and the lack of clear historical detail as to how he ended up on the floor. Prior to any interventions, he remained on the stretcher with his eyes still open, his posture rigid whenever discomfort was introduced, and no sign of an attempt to speak or mouth words despite repeated efforts by the team to elicit a verbal reaction. args:
A 61-year-old woman presented to a local hospital with typical chest pain lasting for approximately 1 h. The patient history was significant for coronary angiography performed three years prior to this presentation due to typical angina pectoris induced by exercise, revealing normal coronary arteries. On this presentation, serum troponin level was high and her electrocardiogram revealed 0,5-1 mm ST segment depression in precordial derivations (Figure ). The patient was referred to our hospital for invasive management of myocardial infarction.
Except for hypertension treated with irbesartan/hydrochlorothiazide and metoprolol, the patient had no other risk factors for coronary artery disease (CAD). She denied substance abuse, including cocaine abuse. No structural heart disease was detected by transthoracic echocardiography. Early invasive management was planned, therefore coronary angiography was performed. Left coronary angiography, performed with a 6 French Judkins diagnostic catheter, revealed a severe tubular stenosis in circumflex (Cx) artery, evident on various left and right anterior oblique projections. No other lesions (including atherosclerotic plaques) were observed in the left coronary system. On right coronary angiography, performed with a 6F right Judkins diagnostic catheter, diffuse spasm of RCA was observed, most prominent in the distal vessel. A second right coronary angiography, performed 30 s following a 200 peg nitroglycerin intracoronary bolus, revealed complete resolution of spasm (Figure ). Having suspected spasm as etiology of the severe lesion in Cx, angiography was repeated, revealing complete resolution (Figure ). The patient did not report any symptoms throughout the procedure. Resolution of the RCA and Cx stenosis with nitroglycerin suggested CIV in both territories. Furthermore, concentric lesions with otherwise angiographically normal vessels along with morphology of both stenoses were more compatible with arterial vasospasm rather than an atherosclerotic plaque. Beta blocker therapy was discontinued and the patient was discharged on oral diltiazem. On follow-up, she remained symptom-free with medical treatment.

args:
A woman in her 40s was diagnosed with ADPKD after a renal ultrasound was performed due to hypertension and deranged kidney function on a routine blood test. At that point, she had a slightly enlarged liver with normal liver function tests and massive renal cysts. She reached ESRD requiring RRT in 2005 and received a deceased donor kidney transplant 2 years later. She developed progressive graft dysfunction due to chronic allograft nephropathy that required starting PD in August 2014. In November 2014, she was admitted to our hospital with a 3-week history of lower limb edema with painful skin ulcers, symptomatic hypotension and tachycardia (118 bpm), anorexia, nausea, and incoercible vomiting. Physical examination revealed a blood pressure of 70/45 mm Hg and ulcers with necrotic areas and cellulitis in the external distal third of both legs. Laboratory findings revealed severe malnutrition (serum albumin 1.5 g/dL) and elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein: 288 mg/L; procalcitonin: 1.9 μg/L). A Doppler ultrasound showed bilateral femoral vein thrombosis, and so pulmonary thromboembolism was ruled out and due to the extensive thrombosis, a complete hypercoagulability state check was done, but no abnormalities were detected and tumor markers were normal. An abdominal CT was then performed showing large liver and renal polycystic disease, both organs with giant cysts (>15 cm). A giant hepatic cyst producing an extrinsic compression of the intrahepatic inferior cava vein (ICV) and massive venous thrombosis from ICV to bilateral iliofemoral deep veins involving the kidney graft vein was observed. The CT also demonstrated displacement and extrinsic compression of the stomach, pylorus, and duodenum due to another enlarged liver cyst, shown in Figures and .
After the CT findings, we considered the clinical presentation was consistent with an ICV syndrome due to ICV compression and thrombosis. Furthermore, vomiting and oral intake intolerance with severe malnutrition were caused by a complete pyloric and duodenal collapse by the enlarged liver cyst.
Anticoagulation therapy was started, but vomiting with oral intolerance persisted and the patient remained dependent on parenteral nutrition. Thus, percutaneous drainage and alcohol sclerosis of the compressive liver cyst was decided, with successful return of the stomach to its normal location, shown in Figure . Nausea and vomiting resolved in a few days, and adequate oral intake was resumed. Severe hypotension and tachycardia gradually improved, and a repeated CT revealed complete stomach expansion, as shown in Figure . PD therapy was modified but not disrupted, and 1 year later, the patient remained asymptomatic. As she did not have any coagulation abnormalities nor any evidence of neoplasia, the massive thrombosis of the ICV was deemed secondary to the extrinsic compression of intrahepatic ICV by enlarged liver cysts.
