args: A 61-year-old female arrived at the hospital after experiencing three days of progressively worsening shortness of breath, persistent coughing, and low-grade feverish sensations (measured up to 99.1 °F) that she tried to address at home with rest and increased fluid intake. Family members became concerned when she started to appear confused and had difficulty recalling simple details about her daily routine, prompting them to bring her in for an urgent evaluation. On arrival, she was clearly breathing faster than usual (respiratory rate 29 breaths per minute), and her oxygen readings (arterial PaO₂ 81 mm Hg) confirmed that she was not maintaining an ideal level of oxygenation in her bloodstream. A check of her vital signs revealed that her heart was beating at a moderately increased rate (95 beats per minute), and her blood pressure measurement showed an acceptable but slightly reduced value for someone of her age (systolic 119 mm Hg).  When discussing her past health issues, she mentioned that she had once been diagnosed with an abnormal growth that required medical attention. She also revealed a chronic condition in which her heart has at times struggled to keep up with demands, leading her to experience occasional episodes of fluid build-up that can worsen respiratory distress. Nevertheless, she denied ever having been told that her kidneys were chronically impaired and indicated she had never been counseled about significant issues with her liver. She also denied any episodes in the past suggestive of a serious vascular event in her brain, stating that she had always been free from major neurological deficits. Despite her confusion during the current episode, she was able to give a limited but coherent description of her symptoms, pointing out that her cough started with a mild throat irritation and then progressed to include chest discomfort and, most notably, an unsettling sense that she could not catch her breath even with minimal activity. She described feeling more fatigued than usual and said her mild fever had fluctuated through the afternoons, generally rising above her typical baseline. Her mental status wavered, as she occasionally stopped mid-sentence to gather her thoughts, and her family confirmed that this was distinctly different from her usual level of alertness. Further assessment showed that her breathing rate was quite elevated (29 breaths per minute), reflecting her struggle to move sufficient air in and out of her lungs.  Laboratory tests performed at presentation, before any therapy was initiated, demonstrated certain abnormalities. Her blood acidity was slightly below normal range (pH 7.34), though not dramatically so, and her measurement of a waste product associated with protein breakdown was at the high end of the normal spectrum (BUN 19 mg/dL), suggesting that her body might be dealing with increased metabolic stress. Additionally, her blood chemistry displayed a normal concentration of one of the major electrolytes that helps control fluid balance (sodium 144 mEq/L), and her sugar level in the bloodstream was above ideal limits (glucose 148 mg/dL), although not so severe as to cause immediate concern for a diabetic crisis. A measure of her red blood cells was firmly within reference ranges (hematocrit 46 %), implying that her oxygen-carrying capacity had not dipped significantly below acceptable thresholds. Given her difficulty breathing, a sample of arterial blood was analyzed to determine how efficiently she was oxygenating, and the result (PaO₂ 81 mm Hg) indicated that she was falling short of expected levels, confirming that her lungs were not exchanging gases as well as they should.  Imaging of her chest clearly showed an infiltrate consistent with an infectious process within a focal region of her lung, although there was no evidence of a fluid collection accumulating around the lung fields. The lack of overt fluid buildup, particularly in the area surrounding the lungs, distinguished her presentation from more complicated conditions. However, the presence of ground-glass opacities or consolidation in the radiographic images, coupled with her elevated breathing rate, confusion, mild fever, and concerning blood work, pointed strongly toward an acute lung infection that arose outside of a healthcare facility. Her past cardiac tendency toward fluid retention and her previous diagnosis of abnormal cell growth increased her overall risk profile for complications, so the clinical team remained on high alert. At the time she was first evaluated, she had not received any acute treatments beyond limited oral intake at home. She mentioned that she had been managing her chronic conditions through periodic medical visits but had not needed any hospital care recently. With her family’s prompting, she came in once it became apparent that her confusion was growing and her breathing was deteriorating. On physical examination, her skin was warm, and she displayed no obvious swelling in her legs, but she was clearly working hard to breathe and coughed intermittently during the interview. Although she was able to respond when prompted, her speech contained occasional pauses, which reinforced concerns about her mental clarity. Overall, her clinical picture in the emergency setting pointed toward an acute infectious process in her lungs, accompanied by concerning signs such as reduced oxygen saturation, subtle confusion, and a significantly increased breathing rate, all occurring amid a history that included a past malignancy and episodes of inadequate heart function. These findings warranted immediate attention prior to initiating any specific interventions in order to prevent further deterioration.

args: A 60-year-old female presented to the intensive care unit with complaints of worsening shortness of breath, mild confusion, and a two-day history of fatigue. She had been scheduled for a planned procedure that was not urgent, though her admission was prompted by an acute deterioration in her respiratory status. On arrival, she appeared tired but alert enough to answer questions, though her responses were slightly delayed. Her temperature was measured at 38.2 degrees Celsius, showing a mild fever. Blood pressure assessment showed a systolic reading of 125 mm Hg and a diastolic reading of 66 mm Hg. Cardiac monitoring indicated a pulse rate of 102 beats per minute, while her respiratory rate was noted to be 21 breaths per minute, reflecting some increased work of breathing. She had a longstanding history of kidney problems, requiring regular assessments by nephrology, which had been stable for the most part but remained a notable concern especially in the setting of current illness. Laboratory measurements revealed a sodium concentration of 137 mEq/L and potassium of around 3.7 mEq/L, values that were acceptable but required repeated checks given her renal history. Of greater concern was her creatinine level, measured at 2.9 mg/dL on admission, which was higher than her usual baseline reported in her previous renal follow-ups, raising worries about exacerbation of her chronic kidney issues and consistent with an acute kidney injury superimposed on her chronic renal disease. Her hematocrit stood at 33%, and her white blood cell count was 10,800 per cubic millimeter, indicating a mild leukocytosis. Arterial blood gas analysis showed a pH of 7.37, consistent with near-normal acid-base status, although further trending would be essential to determine any evolving metabolic or respiratory derangements. Her neurological status was evaluated to be moderately diminished, with an overall score (Glasgow Coma Score of 13) that indicated she was drowsy yet rousable, providing mostly appropriate responses but occasionally requiring prompts to remain oriented to place and situation, suggesting a level of alertness that needed close observation. More specifically, she was awake enough to communicate her discomfort but sometimes appeared inattentive, pointing toward a mild alteration in mentation. Her oxygen demands at the time of assessment were substantial, necessitating a fraction of inspired oxygen of about sixty percent to maintain adequate oxygenation. On this higher oxygen setting, analysis demonstrated a widened alveolar-arterial gradient estimated at approximately 200 mm Hg, underscoring impaired gas exchange that merited ongoing respiratory support. She also manifested no overt signs of acute heart failure on initial examination, though further imaging was planned to rule out any evolving cardiopulmonary complications. Overall, her presentation was concerning for an acute on chronic renal situation given her preexisting renal concerns and possible early signs of infection as evidenced by the fever and leukocyte count. Despite her kidney-related vulnerabilities, she remained hemodynamically stable, and her heart rate and blood pressure had not progressed to a severe shock state. Nevertheless, the plan was to manage her carefully in the ICU setting, closely monitor her mentation and laboratory parameters, and coordinate with nephrology for any advanced renal support if necessary. Clinicians also planned repeated complete blood counts and metabolic panels to gauge the trajectory of her condition. With vigilant oversight, her course would be reassessed continuously, ensuring that any further respiratory or renal deterioration could be swiftly addressed.

args:
An otherwise healthy 61-year-old Caucasian woman presented in 2010 with anemia and thrombocytopenia after one month of recurrent upper respiratory infections. White blood cell (WBC) count at the time of presentation was 8.7 with 48% circulating blasts, hemoglobin was 9.6, and platelets were 39,000. Bone marrow biopsy was consistent with a diagnosis of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with translocation of chromosomes 11 and 19 involving the MLL locus [t(11:19)(q23; p13.1); MLL-ENL]. She received two rounds of induction therapy with idarubicin and cytarabine with complete response. She then received one cycle of High-Dose Cytarabine (HiDAC) consolidation. She also received 1 cycle of decitabine as there were significant delays in identifying a suitable stem cell donor.
She underwent a matched unrelated donor (MUD) hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT) with fludarabine, busulfan, and thymoglobulin conditioning seven months following the initial diagnosis from a presumably healthy male donor. Graft versus Host Disease (GvHD) prophylaxis regimen consisted of methotrexate, tacrolimus, and mycophenolate mofetil. Day +30 post-HCT bone marrow showed complete remission with complete donor cell engraftment. Her immediate posttransplant course was complicated by mild acute gastrointestinal GvHD and human polyomavirus (BK) cystitis. Post-HCT bone marrow biopsy on days +100 and +180 again showed complete remission with complete donor cell engraftment. In the absence of any evidence of ongoing GvHD, at 18 months after HCT all immunosuppressive agents were discontinued after a prolonged taper schedule.
Five hundred and sixty days following her transplant, the patient was found to have abnormally high IgG levels during routine follow-up (3080; normal range 700–1450). Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) showed two abnormal restricted peaks in gamma region (a = 0.1, b = 2.4). Serum protein immunofixation classified the protein as IgG lambda monoclonal protein. Additional laboratory work-up was significant for increased lambda free light chain (FLC) (6.54; normal range: 0.57–2.63), normal kappa FLC (1.49; 0.33–1.94), low kappa/lambda FLC ratio (0.23; 0.26–1.65), and a raised beta-2-microglobulin (3.6; normal range: 1.10–2.50). Bone marrow biopsy revealed a normocellular bone marrow with trilineage hematopoiesis with no excess blasts but was significant for infiltrating plasma cells. An immunohistochemical stain for CD138 was performed to better quantify the plasma cells in a tissue context and showed singly dispersed and clustered plasma cells comprising approximately 10% of the marrow cellularity. Flow cytometric analysis showed approximate 1% of total events to be lambda light chain restricted plasma cells that did not express CD56. In situ hybridization for EBV (EBER) on the bone marrow biopsy was performed and was negative. Patient's hemoglobin, calcium level, and kidney function were within normal limits. A bone survey was performed and did not identify any lytic or blastic lesions. To further characterize the origin of plasma cells, CD138+ cells were isolated using a Miltenyi autoMACS device (Miltenyi Biotech) and then short tandem repeat (STR) polymorphic DNA markers were amplified by polymerase chain reaction (PCR), labeled with fluorescent markers, and used to distinguish patient and donor cells as different on a Capillary Electrophoresis Fluorescence Detection instrument. The results were consistent with complete donor cell engraftment (<5% recipient cells present).
Given the presence of monoclonal gammopathy and 10% bone marrow plasma cells in the absence of end-organ damage (lytic lesions, anemia, renal disease, or hypercalcemia) that could be attributed to the underlying plasma cell disorder or other myeloma defining events, a diagnosis of donor-derived smoldering multiple myeloma (SMM) was established. The National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP) was notified. The NMDP has confirmed that the donor does not have a diagnosis of SMM or MM. It is unclear if the donor has ever had a bone marrow biopsy or SPEP to confirm or if he simply lacks the diagnosis.
We continued to observe our patient regularly for changes in her myeloma markers. At the time of preparation of this manuscript, her myeloma markers have been stable for over four years. With regard to AML, she is almost 6 years out of MUD-HCT and has been in remission ever since.

args:
The patient is a 63-year-old man with a past medical history of MALToma of the lung who presented with generalized weakness for the past one month associated with cough, fever, night sweats, and shortness of breath. The patient also had anorexia and weight loss of 15 pounds over one month. He denied a history of smoking. The patient was under treatment with isoniazid and pyridoxine for latent tuberculosis based on a positive skin tuberculin test.
On physical examination, his blood pressure was 110/59 mmHg, heart rate 118 beats per minute (bpm), respiratory rate 18/min, saturating 99% on room air with a temperature of 98.9oF. The patient was in no cardiopulmonary distress. A cardiovascular examination revealed no jugular venous distension, normal S1-S2, and no murmurs. On lung auscultation, the patient had rales at the left lung base.
Laboratory data showed sodium 134 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dl, creatinine 0.9 mg/dl, glucose 96 mg/dl, hemoglobin 6.6 g/dl, hematocrit 20.1%, white blood cell count 5.5 x 103/microliter, and platelets 128 x 103/microliter. The coagulation profile showed prothrombin time (PT) 17.2 sec, partial thromboplastin time (PTT) 44.3 sec, and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.38.
Chest X-ray (CXR) and computed tomography (CT) scan showed right middle lobe opacity (Figures -). Repeat imaging after six months showed multiple masses in the lung and an osteoblastic bone lesion involving the left ilium (Figures -). Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed a normal gastro-esophageal junction with erythematous mucosa in the stomach. The gastric biopsy was normal.
A lung biopsy done at initial presentation from the right middle lobe mass showed dense lymphoid infiltrate with proliferation of monocytoid B lymphocytes consistent with extranodal marginal zone lymphoma (BALToma/MALToma).
A CT-guided core needle biopsy from the left posterior pelvic mass that was noted six months after the initial presentation showed high grade diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving skeletal muscles. The pathology report showed diffuse proliferation of lymphocytes, which were positive for cluster of differentiation (CD) 20 and B-cell lymphoma (BCL)-2 by immunohistochemistry tests. He was started on chemotherapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) with subsequent complete clinical and radiologic remission (Figure ).

A 25-year-old man with no past medical history presented to the emergency department (ED) with two days of intractable vomiting, increasing confusion, and progressive difficulty breathing. The patient denied tobacco or recreational drug use.
Upon arrival to the ED, vital signs were within normal range with a blood pressure of 123/61 mmHg, a temperature of 36.8 degree Celsius, a heart rate of 92 beats per minute, and a respiratory rate of 19 respirations per minute. The physical exam showed an obese, lethargic patient, responsive to verbal stimuli. The lung, heart, and abdomen exam were unremarkable and there was no neck crepitus noted. The laboratory evaluation showed leukocytosis of 25.96 x 1000/mm3, elevated creatinine of 2.17 mg/dL, hyperglycemia of 836 mg/dL, hyponatremia of 128 mEq/L, hyperkalemia of 5.2 mEq/L, and bicarbonate of 5 mEq/L. The anion gap was 25 and a venous blood gas showed a pH of 6.91. Urinalysis was positive for 2+ ketones and 1+ protein.
The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit with a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus complicated with DKA. Intravenous normal saline, bicarbonate, and insulin drip were initiated. The chest X-ray on admission suggested findings compatible with pneumomediastinum (Figure ). Chest computed tomography (CT) was obtained and reaffirmed the findings (Figures -). Pneumomediastinum in the setting of intractable vomiting was worrisome for esophageal rupture and since our facility was not staffed to manage this clinical entity, the patient was emergently transferred to another facility. The patient was treated conservatively with analgesia and respiratory support and was discharged several days later without any complications.

args:
A 33-year-old male presented to our center with chief complaints of profuse per rectal bleed mixed with stool for three days that was associated with easy fatigability for one week prior to the initial presentation. He also had one episode of black tarry stool. However, he had no complaints of blood in vomit, purpuric rashes, or petechiae. He also had no hematuria, weight loss, night sweats, evening rise of temperature or loss of appetite, cough, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitation, limb edema, loose stools, jaundice, and abdominal distension. Bleeding was absent from other orifices. He had no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiac diseases, and pulmonary tuberculosis in the past. He consumed 80 grams of alcohol per day for 15 years, but he did not smoke.
On examination, he was ill looking, conscious, and was well oriented to time place and person. He had pallor and was dehydrated. However, he had no icterus, clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. His pulse rate was 110 beats/minute, blood pressure was 80/60 mm of Hg, body temperature was 98°F (36.6°C), respiratory rate was 19 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation was 95% in room air. The digital rectal examination showed fresh blood over the examining finger and otherwise normal findings. Abdominal and cardiac examination was normal.
Laboratory investigations showed hemoglobin 10.8 g/dl and hematocrit 31.6%. The total leukocyte count was 11510/mm3, neutrophils were 78%, and platelet count was 291000/mm3. The prothrombin time was 14 seconds, and the International normalized ratio was 1.08. The albumin level in the blood was 2.4 gm/dl, and total protein was 6.1 gm/dl, total and direct bilirubin were 0.7 and 0.1 mg/dl in the blood. Alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase level was 97 U/L and 114 U/L, respectively. HbA1C level in the blood was 7.2. The level of blood urea nitrogen (33 mg/dl), creatinine (1.1 mg/dl), sodium (132 mEq/l), and potassium (3.6 mEq/l) were within normal range. Traces of sugar and albumin (++) were present in the urine examination. The fecal occult blood was found in the stool. Mycobacterium tuberculosis was not detected in sputum in the acid-fast bacilli (AFB) stain.
The chest X-ray was normal. Ultrasonography (USG) of the abdomen and pelvis and upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed normal findings. A Computed Tomography (CT) scan of chest and abdomen showed asymmetric circumferential thickening in the ileocaecal region with lobulated thickened caecum, soft-tissue stranding and necrotic mesenteric lymphadenopathy, and indeterminate lobule in the lung (). Colonoscopy showed multiple transverse ulcers with overlying exudates in terminal ileum and ascending and transverse colon. Histopathological examination of the ileum and colon showed patchy ulcers with exudates, granulation tissue, fibrosis, deep lymphoplasmocytic inflammation, and crypt regenerative changes with fibrinoid changes in scattered capillaries and venules and was inconclusive. However, gene XPERT was positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
He was diagnosed with ileocaecal tuberculosis. The hemoglobin level dropped to 6.1 g/dl after two days of admission, and he had an episode of weakness associated with profuse sweating and rigor. Fluid resuscitation followed by two pints of whole blood transfusion was performed. After that, his hemoglobin was increased to 10.2 mg/dl. He was managed with four antitubercular drugs and showed significant improvement. Following this, he was discharged on oral antitubercular medications (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and pyridoxine) after 10 days of admission. On follow-up after two weeks, he was responding well to antitubercular medications with improved symptoms, i.e., no per rectal bleeding, and his liver function test was within the normal range.

args:
A 91-year-old male with hypertension, coronary artery disease, and a longstanding history of CD, noncompliant with a gluten-free diet, presented to the ED for evaluation of intermittent upper abdominal pain and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over a period of two months. His vital signs were initially normal, however, after an episode of hematemesis followed by hematochezia, he became hypotensive to 80/60 mmHg and tachycardic to 100 beats/min. Physical examination was notable for pale conjunctiva and tenderness to palpation in the upper abdomen. Laboratory evaluation revealed a white blood cell count of 13.2 x 103/uL (reference (R): 4.5-11.0 x 103/uL), hemoglobin of 5.9 g/dL (R: 12-17 g/dL), blood urea nitrogen of 34 mg/dL (R: 5-25 mg/dL), and creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL (R: 0.6-1.8 mg/dL). CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a 6.8 cm dilated proximal jejunum (Figure ).
The patient was resuscitated with four units of packed red blood cells and two liters of normal saline. Intravenous pantoprazole was administered. Surgery was consulted and a nasogastric tube was placed for gastric decompression. Small bowel enteroscopy discovered two nonbleeding superficial clean-based ulcers in the duodenum and an ulcerated jejunal mass, which appeared to be the source of bleeding. Biopsies from the mass showed an invasive, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. Subsequently, exploratory laparotomy revealed additional masses in the ileum and transverse colon suspicious for malignancy; thus, partial small bowel and partial colonic resection with end colostomy and Hartmann’s pouch were performed. The mass in the jejunum measured 1.7 cm x 1 cm x 0.2 cm. Pathologic evaluation revealed invasive, poorly differentiated carcinoma with gland formation only in the lesion resected from jejunum (Figure ). Cytokeratin was immunoreactive (Figure ). CK7, CK20, TTF-1, LCA, CD56, synaptophysin, and chromogranin stains were negative. Regional resected lymph nodes and other resected lesions were devoid from cancer. The American Joint Committee of Cancer (AJCC) stage was pT2N0. The patient’s post-operative course was uncomplicated, and he was discharged with outpatient oncology follow up. On follow up visit four weeks after surgery, the patient was recovering well and without any complaints. He was offered chemotherapy, but declined any treatment.

args: An 80-year-old male with past medical history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, and diabetes mellitus non-compliant to the prescribed medications, presented to the emergency department with 1-hour complaint of right-sided chest pain that was described as aching, non-radiating, without any aggravating or relieving factors, 10/10 in intensity, and was accompanied by nausea and two episodes of maroon-colored emesis. The patient denied any ingestion of toxic substances and any prior similar episodes. On presentation, the patient was afebrile, and blood pressure of 109/45, heart rate 75, respiratory rate of 18, and room air oximetry of 98%. Physical examination, including fundoscopy was unrevealing. Labs workup revealed serum sodium of 125 mEq/L [135-145 mEq/L], potassium 5.1 mEq/L [3.5-5.0 mEq/L], chloride 91 mEq/L [98-107 mEq/L], bicarbonate 24 mEq/L [21-24 mEq/L], urea 145,7 mg/dL [14.9 – 51.4 mg/dL], creatinine 2.95 mg/dL [0.6-1.30 mg/dL], blood glucose 492 mg/dL [70-110], WBC 13.5 [4.5-11.0 × 103/mm3], Hb 12.3 g/dL [13.5-17.5 g/dL], platelets 242,000/mm3 [140k-440k/mm3], BNP 154 pg/mL [125-450 pg/mL], and troponin 0.062 ng/mL [0.01-0.30 ng/mL]. Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm at the rate of 94 beats per minute without ST or T-wave abnormalities. Morphine was administered along with nitroglycerine for the chest pain without any improvement in symptoms. Therefore, given the persistent chest pain and concern for non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction, the patient was started on a heparin drip and antiplatelet therapy in the emergency department. Cardiology was consulted who performed a cardiac catheterization which did not show any critical stenosis with an estimated ejection fraction of 55%. They recommended medical therapy including anti-hypertensives, statin, and blood glucose control. The computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast was unremarkable. Gastroenterology was then consulted due to abdominal symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and hematemesis. Patient underwent an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD), which showed circumferential, black-appearing, necrotic esophageal mucosa without active bleeding extending from the mid to distal esophagus (). There was also mild chronic gastritis without any evidence of active bleeding. The patient was treated with a proton pump inhibitor and sucralfate. Patient had an uneventful hospital course lasting 5 days and was discharged with close follow up.

args: A 74-year-old male was brought to the intensive care unit with a high fever, respiratory difficulty, and marked lethargy that raised immediate concerns about a serious ongoing process. He had a known vulnerability regarding his body’s defense mechanisms, placing him at risk for severe complications, and he had recently undergone an operative intervention that had been arranged in advance rather than on short notice, arriving in the ICU in the immediate postoperative period. His Glasgow Coma Score was calculated at 4. His family reported that he had been progressively more tired and confused over the previous day, culminating in an acute decline prompting emergency transport. Vital sign measurements at presentation showed a high body temperature of 39.2 °C, correlating with his flushed skin and possible infectious or inflammatory process. His pulse was recorded at about 89 beats per minute, relatively stable but concerning in the context of the other findings, and his respiratory rate was 26 breaths per minute, reflecting an increased workload of breathing. Measurement of arterial pressures yielded a systolic reading of 128 mm Hg and diastolic of 71 mm Hg. Though these blood pressure indices did not indicate frank shock, they demanded close monitoring given his precarious clinical state. Blood tests drawn immediately revealed a serum sodium of 147 mEq/L and potassium of 4.8 mEq/L, suggesting that some electrolytes were at the higher end of normal ranges and warranting repeated checks to detect any further derangements. Of greater concern was the markedly elevated creatinine level measured at 2.6 mg/dL; this indicated a deterioration of renal function in the absence of any documented longstanding kidney disease. His hematocrit was reduced to 29 %, potentially reflecting either chronic anemia or an acute drop that might impair oxygen-carrying capacity and limit the body’s physiologic reserves. Further laboratory data showed that his total white blood cell population was around 10,400 cells/mm³, a mild elevation that could signal an infectious process or another inflammatory trigger. His arterial blood sample exhibited a pH of 7.46, suggesting a mild shift toward alkalinity or a compensated state that could change quickly depending on ongoing metabolic or respiratory stresses. On arrival, he was placed on a markedly increased FiO2 at 80 %, well above half of the inhaled air, to maintain adequate oxygenation. Despite this high supplemental oxygen, his calculated alveolar–arterial oxygen gradient was markedly elevated at about 220 mm Hg, indicating a significant impairment in gas exchange. Clinically, he appeared tachypneic and demonstrated labored inhalations, with accessory muscle recruitment evident on inspection. He had no documented history of chronically reduced kidney function, and conversations with relatives indicated that he had not faced kidney-related admissions in the past, suggesting a new onset for his current renal issue. Given his severe mental status changes, the team worried about the risk of airway compromise or inability to guard the airway should his neurologic function worsen. At the same time, care had to be taken to evaluate potential causes of his elevated temperature, which ranged beyond typical low-grade readings. Because of the level of sedation required to keep him comfortable and safe for further investigations, the team recognized the urgency in ruling out primary infections and other acute problems, particularly in light of his inherent predisposition to complications from infectious agents or organ insults. Plans were considered for imaging studies to identify possible sources of infection, bleeding, or other triggers for abrupt decompensation, while also evaluating for potential complications related to his recent surgery rather than planning any new operative procedure at that time. By the time he underwent initial laboratory and clinical evaluations, he remained in a state of severely impaired consciousness, and his breathing dynamics signaled the possibility of impending respiratory compromise if interventions were delayed. The clinical picture suggested a complex interplay of high fever, acute renal derangement, and disordered mentation, all superimposed on a background of increased susceptibility to severe illness. Ongoing discussions among the critical care team centered on stabilizing him hemodynamically, preventing further organ dysfunction, and determining whether additional operative management would be necessary after his recent elective procedure or whether more extensive stabilization measures needed to precede any further intervention. Despite the absence of a known chronic kidney process, the elevated creatinine on arrival emphasized the need for meticulous oversight, including repeated checks of his urine output and the potential for renal imaging if indicated. args: A 38-year-old postpartum woman arrived to the ambulatory triage area late this afternoon because her left lower leg had become noticeably heavier and mildly painful over the preceding 24 hours. Six weeks ago she delivered her second child via uncomplicated scheduled cesarean section that required spinal anesthesia and an overnight stay; she was discharged on postoperative day 2 and has since resumed routine household activities without assistive devices. Yesterday morning, while standing at the kitchen counter for an extended period preparing bottles, she felt a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the calf that worsened when she bore weight and eased modestly with elevation. By evening she observed that her sock left an imprint on that side but not on the right. She denies antecedent trauma, ankle sprain, insect bite, recent intense exercise, or local skin infection. She also denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or constitutional complaints. There is no history of malignant disease, hematologic disorder, or prior venous clot, nor has she ever required anticoagulation. She reports no neurologic illness, limb weakness, or application of a plaster cast, and she has remained fully mobile caring for her newborn. She uses no estrogen-containing contraception and takes only prenatal vitamins and 600 mg ibuprofen as needed for incisional soreness. Both parents are alive and free of venous thromboembolic events; no siblings have clotting disorders. She has never smoked, drinks wine rarely, and denies illicit drug use. On presentation she appeared comfortable holding the infant carrier, spoke in complete sentences, and ambulated without assistance. Vital signs before any medications were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar that was nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated subtle fullness confined to the left calf; there was no erythema, hyperpigmentation, varicosity, or brawny skin change. A flexible tape placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38.0 cm on the left and 36.4 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.6 cm. Circumference at mid-thigh and around the ankle was symmetric. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the left foot produced a 1+ indentation that resolved within 15 seconds, whereas identical pressure on the right left no depression. Palpation along the course of the popliteal vein and posteromedial calf elicited focal discomfort described by the patient as “a deep bruise,” compared with no tenderness over the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from the neurovascular bundle produced only minimal diffuse soreness. There was no palpable subcutaneous cord and no crepitus. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain at the Achilles insertion. Skin temperature was marginally warmer over the symptomatic calf compared with the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2+ bilaterally, and capillary refill of all toes was under two seconds. Motor strength in ankle dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and great-toe extension was 5/5, and sensation to light touch and vibration was intact. Straight-leg raise and seated slump test were negative for radicular symptoms, making a spinal etiology improbable. There was no ipsilateral knee effusion, meniscal tenderness, or ligamentous laxity, and range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full. No inguinal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. Basic laboratory studies obtained prior to any antithrombotic therapy showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0 with activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. These values reveal no anemia, thrombocytopenia, or renal impairment that might confound subsequent management. Given the recent abdominal-pelvic surgical intervention within the past two months, the presence of unilateral pitting edema confined to the calf, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway, and the absence of a compelling musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or neurologic explanation, an acute thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system remains the leading consideration at this stage of evaluation.

args: A 70-year-old female arrived at the emergency department reporting two days of productive cough, shortness of breath, and high fevers that she first noted upon waking with chills and body aches. She lives independently and was brought in by a family member who grew concerned when she became increasingly weak and required more effort to breathe. She has dealt with a chronic issue in which her heart has struggled to pump effectively, leading to periodic swelling in her lower extremities and exertional fatigue. She also has a longstanding liver-related condition that has intermittently required monitoring by her primary physician. She denies having been diagnosed with any serious tumor-related illness, nor has she had any significant kidney problems in the past. She notes no established history of lapses in mental clarity, and she has always received care at her own home. On examination, she appeared ill and fatigued yet still oriented, with significant tachypnea reflected by a respiratory rate of 28 breaths per minute. She was febrile at 39 degrees Celsius with a heart rate of 104 beats per minute, and her blood pressure measured 122 mm Hg systolic. Auscultation of her lungs revealed coarse crackles on the right side consistent with an infiltrative process, although imaging did not show fluid accumulating in the pleural space. Laboratory testing at presentation indicated several notable findings. Her blood urea nitrogen measured 20 mg/dL, and serum sodium was recorded at 138 mEq/L, which did not deviate significantly from typical ranges. Her hematocrit level was 36%, suggesting a mild decrease from typical reference values, and her serum glucose was 142 mg/dL, slightly above normal limits. Analysis of her arterial blood gases revealed a partial pressure of oxygen of 80 mm Hg, suggesting reduced oxygenation yet not profoundly low, and her pH was measured at 7.44, which was within a physiologic range without significant evidence of acidemia. The patient had a documented pulse oximetry reading that correlated with her observed shortness of breath, prompting concern for community-onset pulmonary infection. She reported sputum production that was pale yellow, though occasionally streaked with darker discoloration, and her cough had grown more frequent in the 48 hours leading up to presentation. She indicated some mild discomfort in her upper abdomen, which she attributed to her preexisting liver grievances, though she denied any recent changes in her appetite or bowel habits. There was no suggestion of new neurologic deficits, and her medical records showed no events of stroke or transient neurologic complications. Before coming to the hospital, she tried over-the-counter medications for her fever but did not experience relief from her respiratory symptoms. She has not experienced any significant change in urine output, and she has never required dialysis or experienced issues that might suggest prominent renal dysfunction. A chest radiograph revealed patchy consolidation in the right lower lobe but no layering fluid universally suggestive of a complicated process. Although she does have a cardiac condition that has required occasional follow-up, her current chest imaging changes appear distinct from fluid accumulation around the heart. Further evaluation and management have been initiated promptly, given her age and comorbidities, but at the time of initial assessment, she was untreated other than her own attempts at self-care at home. Her clinical course in the emergency department will be monitored carefully, especially with concerns of infection given her chronic heart and liver conditions. All in all, her presentation is consistent with a concerning pulmonary infection acquired outside of healthcare settings, and her vital signs and lab values suggest she needs close attention for potential complication risk.

args:
A 73-year-old female presented with a chief complaint for evaluation of an incidental finding of peripancreatic lymph node.
She underwent computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen and pelvis as part of her routine surveillance for extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT-lymphoma) of the lung and was found to have peripancreatic lymph node. She denied any abdominal pain, change in bowel habit, weight loss, nausea, or vomiting.
Her medical history was significant for MALT-lymphoma, invasive lobular breast carcinoma, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, mitral valve prolapse, mitral valve stenosis, and actinic keratosis. Her surgical history included a mastectomy with sentinel lymph node dissection, laparoscopic cholecystectomy, tonsillectomy, left knee replacement, and bilateral carpal tunnel repair.
Her family history was significant for colon cancer in maternal grandmother at the age of 65 years, prostate cancer in brother at the age of 63 years, and melanoma in mother. She had no history of alcohol or tobacco abuse. She has 2 children and attained menopause at the age of 52 years. Her medications included aspirin, furosemide, carvedilol, rosuvastatin, amiodarone, digoxin, anastrozole, and Eliquis.
Her physical examination was unremarkable, and her abdomen was soft nontender, nondistended with no palpable mass.
Laboratory exam including fractionated metanephrines, chromogranin, and gastrin were negative.
CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed 2 cm × 1.1 cm lymph node adjacent to the pancreatic head (Figure ).
EUS showed a 19 mm × 11.5 mm hypoechoic lesion near the pancreatic head (Figure ). Two FNA passes using a 25-gauge needle were performed via transduodenal approach (Figure ).
Direct FNA smears showed tumor with neuroendocrine features. Initial immunoperoxidase stains performed on cell block sections were positive for synaptophysin and chromogranin, which seemed to confirm the morphologic impression of PNET. The pathologist was subsequently informed about the peripancreatic location and lack of a definite pancreatic lesion.

args: An 85-year-old male presented from a senior care residence with several days of progressive respiratory complaints, including elevated body temperature, persistent coughing, and an overall decline in endurance. According to the caregivers who accompanied him, he had shown increasing fatigue and reduced mobility in the days leading up to this evaluation, which contrasted with the relatively stable level of day-to-day functioning he previously maintained. He recalled feeling feverish the evening before arrival, noting that his measured temperature on admission was 39.2 degrees Celsius, which was significantly higher than his usual readings. His past medical background includes a notable neurological event some years ago that affected his speech for a time but did not leave him with any severe motor limitations, and he also has a known condition requiring periodic monitoring of his liver health. He denied any longstanding or recurring problems with his kidneys and stated that he has never been told of structural or functional abnormalities in those organs upon previous checkups. When asked about his current symptoms, he described a harsh, productive cough that made it difficult to sleep, as well as an intense sensation of breathlessness that worsened with minimal exertion. Acquisition of his vital signs showed he was breathing at a rate of 34 breaths per minute and had a heart rate of 116 beats per minute, suggesting a heightened physiological response. His systolic blood pressure was measured at 136 mm Hg, which he mentioned was close to the upper range of normal during routine assessments in the past. An arterial blood gas test, conducted prior to any therapeutic intervention, demonstrated a partial pressure of oxygen of 93 mm Hg and a pH of 7.50, suggesting a particular shift in his acid-base balance that the medical team found important to track. Additional lab findings revealed an elevated concentration of a waste product associated with protein metabolism, measured at 36 mg/dL, raising some concern about whether his body was acutely stressed by this infection. His serum sodium was found to be 151 mEq/L, placing him above desired limits, and his hematocrit was 32 percent. He showed a blood glucose value of 110 mg/dL at presentation, which was not extremely high but still noted by the treating clinicians, as they aimed to identify any metabolic derangements that might complicate his acute illness. When asked about chest symptoms, he described feeling heaviness that intensified whenever he attempted to walk for more than a few steps at a time, though he denied having to pause due to sharp pains or palpitations. He did report chills and sweats during the preceding couple of nights, stating that he had awakened on more than one occasion with damp sheets and an uneasy feeling that his breathing was not as smooth as usual. Despite these challenges, his mental clarity appeared surprisingly intact for his age; he was able to answer questions coherently and recalled recent events without difficulty, showing no overt signs of confusion or disorientation. Physical examination of his torso exposed reduced air movement in certain areas, and imaging of his chest confirmed the presence of a fluid collection in the pleural space along with an opacity suggestive of infection. When questioned about physical limitations, he mentioned he was accustomed to his current level of activity in the long-term setting and usually participated in communal mealtimes and light exercise programs; however, his abrupt decline in the past week alarmed the staff enough to prompt urgent transport. He admitted to occasionally having mild abdominal discomfort related to his longstanding hepatic issue, and he was clear that previous workups had not identified malignant processes in his history. During the initial assessment, his orientation to time and place was excellent, and while he appeared weak, he did not display any overt signs of delirium. His reluctance to perform tasks that required deep breathing was evident; even walking a few meters required a pause for rest. No acute therapies were administered before these findings were documented, ensuring the data reflected his status upon arrival. Overall, his age, prior neurological episode, and liver-related concerns placed him at heightened risk, necessitating close observation and further diagnostic steps to clarify the full extent of his respiratory condition before initiating definitive treatment.

A 68-year-old woman was admitted to the hospital with dizziness, disequilibrium, and dysarthria, which she had had for about 2 weeks. She had a history of hypertension, but no history of diabetes or heart disease, no familial history of cardiovascular disease, and no use of preventive medication for stroke.
On admission, her neurological examination was normal except for the presence of dysarthria, right central facial and glossary palsy, and bilateral positive Babinski signs.
Laboratory tests, including blood routine test, serum electrolytes, renal and hepatic function, blood sugar, coagulation profile, syphilis and HIV tests, and blood fat, were all normal. There were no abnormalities on her ECG, TEE, or 24-hour Holter monitoring.
DWI showed multiple high signal intensities in the territories of both anterior and posterior circulations. MR angiography showed no stenosis of large vessels. Her chest CT scan showed lung cancer and multiple metastases in the lungs.

calculator_name: Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk

args:
A 77-year-old woman presenting with a one month history of progredient dyspnea underwent an unenhanced computed tomography (CT) scan of the chest, which incidentally revealed a largely hyperdense upper pole lesion in the left kidney with a maximum diameter of 5.3 cm (). Parts of the lesion attained the renal pelvis. This finding was confirmed by ultrasound. An ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration biopsy revealed RCC of the clear cell type (T1b N0 M0) (). She also suffered from primary hypertension (BP [blood pressure] 140-150/100 mmHg), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, dilated cardiomyopathy and implantation of a cardiac pacemaker, mitral insufficiency (NYHA [New York Heart Association] III), insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (fasting glucose value: 100-110 mg/dl), chronic renal failure (creatinine level: 1.5 mg/dl), and alcoholic liver cirrhosis. Due to the markedly elevated perioperative risk (ASA [American Society of Anesthesiology] IV) the patient refused surgery.
Subsequently, the patient was referred for interventional treatment of the tumor. A percutaneous RF ablation was considered to be the most suitable therapeutic option. Due to the size of the lesion, the patient was scheduled for pre-interventional embolization of the central part of her tumor in order to achieve central necrosis and a more homogeneous heat distribution in the devascularized parts of the tumor. Iodinated oil (Lipiodol Ultra-Fluid®, Guerbet, Roissy, France) was considered for lesion tagging since it provides post-embolization contrast and allows for CT-guided placement of the RF-probe without use of additional contrast agent.
Embolization was performed via the right femoral approach using a 4 Fr introducer sheath. After selective catheterization of the left renal artery using a 4 Fr sidewinder catheter (Merit Medical, South Jordan, UT), a 3 Fr microcatheter (Progreat, Terumo, Tokyo, Japan) was placed in the tumor feeding vessels in the upper pole of the kidney. The tumor was subsequently embolized using 10 ml of iodinated oil (Lipiodol, Guerbet, Roissy, France) (). Considering the patient's delicate renal condition, the use of iodinated contrast agent was limited to 10 ml (Visipaque 320, GE Healthcare, Princeton, NJ).
Prior to the RF ablation, a single-J catheter was placed in the left ureter with its tip in the collecting system, to accomplish external renal pelvis cooling. Preliminary testing of the single-J catheter only allowed for a drop-by-drop injection, which was insufficient for cooling and protection from thermal damage. Since the patient rejected conscious sedation, the procedure was performed under general anesthesia. Accordingly, the patient was positioned in the right lateral decubitus position; and, following an unenhanced CT (SOMATOM Sensation 16, Siemens, Forchheim, Germany; 16 × 0.75 mm, 120 kV, 165 mAseff), a CT-guided nephrostomy was performed using the trocar technique with a 6.5 Fr drainage (Resolve, Merit Medical, South Jordan, UT) to achieve sufficient cooling via the anterograde approach. Continuous cooling of the upper calices was performed with pre-chilled (8℃) 0.9% saline infused at a flow rate of 500 ml/h.
For RF ablation, four grounding pads were placed on the patient's proximal thighs. The procedure was carried out using a monopolar RF system (RF 3000, Boston Scientific Corporation, Natick, MA) in combination with a 5 cm umbrella-shaped LeVeen probe (Boston Scientific). Prior to the RF probe placement, the distal 1 cm of the probe insulation was removed to allow for post-interventional coagulation of the puncture tract. The electrode was placed under CT-guidance in the centre of the lesion (). After the final probe position was confirmed, energy was applied according to the manufacturer's recommendations for soft tissue ablation. Ablation was performed using the pullback technique at two positions, and achieving two complete roll-offs at each position. The total time of ablation was 74 minutes. Arterial and venous phase CT scan (SOMATOM Sensation 16, Siemens, Forchheim, Germany; 16×0.75 mm, 120 kV, 165 mAseff) with a reduced amount of contrast agent (80 ml Iodixanol, Visipaque, GE Healthcare) showed a completely devascularized tumor, with no sign of any residual contrast enhancement. Thereafter, the RF probe was gradually retracted under continuous coagulation of the puncture tract by applying 20 W.
No early or delayed complications were noted; however, due to her general condition, the patient was temporarily admitted to the intermediate care unit for observation. She recovered well and the single-J splint was removed a day after and the nephrostomy five days after the ablation. The patient was discharged the following day in good condition. A CT performed at eight months after the procedure, showed residual Lipiodol in the area of the ablation without signs of local renal tumor recurrence ().

What is the patient's score of the Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

I need to calculate the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) score for this patient. The RCRI is a clinical scoring system used to assess perioperative cardiac risk. step_info.output:
Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) Calculation:
- High-risk surgery: Not present (0)
- Ischemic heart disease: Not present (0) [Note: Pacemaker history does not necessarily indicate ischemic disease]
- Congestive heart failure (CHF): Present (1) [dilated cardiomyopathy, mitral insufficiency NYHA III]
- Cerebrovascular disease: Not present (0)
- Insulin-dependent diabetes: Present (1)
- Pre-operative creatinine >2.0 mg/dL: Not present (0) [creatinine 1.5 mg/dL]

Total = 0 (surgery) + 0 (ischemic) + 1 (CHF) + 0 (cerebrovascular) + 1 (insulin) + 0 (creatinine) = 2

step_info.thought:
<thought>
The tool returned an RCRI score of 2, but I need to double-check this calculation. The patient has dilated cardiomyopathy and mitral insufficiency NYHA III, which should count as congestive heart failure (1 point). Insulin-dependent diabetes also counts (1 point). The creatinine is 1.5 mg/dL, which is below the 2.0 mg/dL threshold, so no point for renal insufficiency. The patient is not having high-risk surgery (the note mentions she refused surgery due to perioperative risk), so no point for high-risk surgery. No ischemic heart disease or cerebrovascular disease mentioned. Let me verify the arithmetic sum of these points.
</thought>

<thought>
The calculation confirms the RCRI score is 2. The patient has two risk factors: congestive heart failure (from dilated cardiomyopathy and mitral insufficiency NYHA III) and insulin-dependent diabetes. The other RCRI criteria are not met. This gives a total score of 2, which indicates an intermediate risk category for perioperative cardiac events.
</thought>

args: A 68-year-old female presents to the surgical clinic with plans to undergo a minimally invasive abdominal procedure that involves small incisions and camera guidance. She reports that this operation has been scheduled due to her chronic epigastric discomfort and a concern for possible adhesions from prior treatments, though she clarifies she has not undergone any substantial open interventions or larger-scale procedures in the recent month. She describes having been in reasonable health overall and denies any new-onset cardiac symptoms or troublesome changes in breathing during this time; in fact, she recalls completing her usual daily activities without limitation, including light exercise and regular household tasks. She shares that she has no recent history of persistent cough, fever, or respiratory compromise that might suggest a lingering lung-related illness. Furthermore, she has not experienced significant limb swelling or tightness, and her legs appear free of external abnormalities on inspection, which aligns with her lack of complaints regarding lower extremity heaviness or superficial venous concerns. She explains that she is able to move about readily, indicating a normal level of physical function for her age, as she lives independently, navigates stairs without assistance, and usually walks for mild exercise. Although she does not require assistive devices, she acknowledges a slight decrease in her stamina that she attributes to general stress and intermittent pains in the upper abdomen that led to her planned laparoscopic procedure. She denies having been diagnosed with occlusive clots in the legs or the lungs in the past, stating she has never been placed on extended courses of anticoagulant medication nor been hospitalized for vascular blockages. However, she mentions that a blood relative suffered a serious clotting event earlier in life, which makes her somewhat cautious about her own risk profile. She recalls discreet discussions with her healthcare providers in previous years, during which she was tested for known genetic contributors to excessive clotting, although those screenings did not highlight any specific worrisome mutations at the time. Still, she remains attentive to possible signs of circulatory problems, given her family’s unexpected experience. With respect to her broader medical background, she acknowledges that she was once diagnosed with a malignant process, for which she received appropriate therapy and follow-up. According to her account, she remains under regular surveillance to ensure no recurrence of that disease, and her most recent evaluations have not flagged any concerning developments. She also reports no recent difficulties pointing toward heart dysfunction, such as fluid retention, changes in exercise tolerance, or prominent orthopnea, and she emphasizes that her current ability to climb stairs and walk moderate distances without shortness of breath reassures her about her cardiac status. She denies involvement in any major accident or incurring extensive injuries that might confine her to bed for an extended period, and explicitly states she has not endured repeated trauma incidents or undergone any rigorous bone repairs in recent weeks. Her daily vital sign recordings at home have been stable without significant fluctuations, and she has had no episodes of fainting, severe dizziness, or chest pain that might prompt emergent cardiovascular evaluation. She also asserts that she has had no neurological deficits, explaining she has not experienced any abrupt weaknesses, facial drooping, or speech disturbances consistent with a cerebral event in the last few weeks. The patient remarks that her plan to proceed with this keyhole-type surgery stems primarily from her desire to clarify the abdominal findings and alleviate her episodic distress, which intermittently hinders her enjoyment of meals. She has felt otherwise well-nourished, with no indication of bowel inflammation or related gastrointestinal disorders requiring special therapy, and she remains free of chronic pulmonary conditions that cause ongoing wheezing or frequent infections. Overall, she appears coherent and calm during the clinical interaction, expressing readiness to move forward with the next steps, anticipating a likely short inpatient stay and a return to her normal routine once she recovers from the procedure.

On review of systems, the patient also endorses chills and sweats as well as several episodes of diarrhea over the past three days. Her husband also reports that the patient seemed confused this morning when he spoke to her before he left for work. The patient’s past medical history is significant for diabetes mellitus type II for which she takes metformin and glipizide. She drinks 8-10 beers per week and has a 15 pack-year smoking history. She works as a sales manager at a local retail company. Her temperature is 102.6°F (39.2°C), blood pressure is 126/72 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. On physical exam, she is alert and oriented to person and place only. She has crackles bilaterally extending up to the mid-lung fields. Her abdomen is soft and non-distended without rebound or guarding. Laboratory testing is performed and reveals the following:

calculator_name: Revised Cardiac Risk Index for Pre-Operative Risk

args:
A 22-year-old Caucasian male with Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM), presented with acute kidney injury (AKI), serum creatinine (SCr) 387 μmol/L and nephrotic syndrome (timed urinary protein excretion 14 g/day, serum albumin 23 g/L (35–50 g/L), underwent a diagnostic kidney biopsy. Clinical assessment 1 year prior indicated minimal proteinuria (albumin-to-creatinine ratio 1.5 mg/mmol) and normal kidney function (eGFR [] >90 mL/min/1.73 m2) at that time. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic, extractable nuclear antigen (ENA) and anti-GBM antibodies were undetected. Urine studies showed protein-to-creatinine ratio 1.133 g/mmol, dysmorphic erythrocytes (>1000 × 106/L) and lipiduria.
T1DM was diagnosed at age 2 and insulin therapy delivered via insulin pump. Glycosylated haemoglobin (HbA1c) at presentation was 7.2%. The patient weighed 100 kg and had smoked 10 cigarettes per day for the past 7 years. There was no history of retinopathy or hypertension, illicit or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug use or alcohol dependence. He had been commenced on frusemide and irbesartan/hydrochlorothiazide 3 weeks earlier to treat oedema.
The kidney biopsy demonstrated a focal segmental necrotizing GN with cellular and fibrocellular crescents involving 29 of 40 glomeruli; another 6 glomeruli were sclerosed. Glomeruli had moderate nodular mesangial expansion and hypercellularity with several showing Kimmelstiel–Wilson nodules. Numerous tubular red blood cell casts were present. There was moderate interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy with a patchy interstitial mixed chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate (see A and B and A and B). Immunofluorescence showed strong linear GBM staining for IgG. Weaker anti-albumin staining localized in a linear fashion to the GBM, Bowman's capsule (BC) and tubular basement membrane (TBM) staining (A and B). Under oil immersion (×1000 magnification), a dual pattern of linear and granular glomerular peripheral capillary wall staining was seen. This was confirmed by confocal microscopy (A–C). Electron microscopy showed Stage 1 MN with small subepithelial electron-dense ‘immune-type’ deposits with early GBM spike formation (A and B). The overall diagnosis was that of anti-GBM GN and Stage 1 MN superimposed on Class III DN with Kimmelstiel–Wilson lesions.
The simultaneous diagnosis of seronegative anti-GBM GN, MN and DN was consistent with clinical presentation of proteinuria, haematuria and AKI, although unexpected, as anti-GBM antibody serology was negative (Orgentec, Mainz, Germany). A more sensitive biosensor assay was unavailable. The patient's human leucocyte antigens (HLA) were: A1, 24; B8, 39; DR4, 16. Screening for serum anti-secretory phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R) antibodies was not detected using an indirect immunofluorescence test (Euroimmun AG, Lubeck, Germany) []. In addition, PLA2R was not detected by immunofluorescence in the paraffin-embedded kidney biopsy using anti-rabbit PLA2R antibody (Atlas Antibodies).
Treatment included corticosteroids (3× intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day then 75 mg/day oral prednisolone), oral cyclophosphamide (2 mg/kg/day) after fertility protection measures and 10 alternate-daily plasmapheresis treatments (3 L volume exchanges, replacement albumin 4%). He was discharged with mildly improved stable kidney function, with SCr 253 μmol/L.
One week later, he developed small-volume haemoptysis that settled with oral antibiotics, cessation of smoking and cyclophosphamide reduction (1.5 mg/kg/day). Significant oedema persisted despite maximal oral diuretic therapy. Five weeks later, he developed neutropaenia necessitating withdrawal of cyclophosphamide. Prednisolone 60 mg/day was continued in light of further deterioration in kidney function, with SCr 383 μmol/L and worsening proteinuria at 19 g/day.
Another nine alternate-daily plasmapheresis treatments were initiated once neutropaenia resolved, attempting to salvage kidney function. The renal function remained severely impaired and proteinuria worsened to 21 g/day. His clinical course was complicated by H. Zoster reactivation (T7 dermatome) that resolved with valaciclovir treatment. Anti-GBM serology remained undetectable throughout.
Progressive renal disease and inability to monitor disease activity serologically led to a repeat kidney biopsy, which showed segmental sclerotic lesions and fibrous crescents in 13 of 22 glomeruli and another 3 glomeruli sclerosed. No segmental necrotizing lesions or cellular crescents were seen. Immunofluorescence showed a strong dual pattern of staining for IgG as described in the first biopsy (not illustrated). The overall diagnosis of the subsequent biopsy was inactive anti-GBM GN and MN superimposed on DN. Persistent strong linear GBM staining for IgG despite lack of active lesions in this biopsy.
The patient commenced maintenance haemodialysis 14 weeks after initial presentation when refractory fluid overload ensued, coupled with no renal recovery. Immunosuppression was discontinued. Currently, 5 months after presentation, he is training for home haemodialysis and suitability for combined kidney–pancreas transplant is being assessed.

args:
A 55-year-old man was first evaluated in a nephrology unit because of hypertension associated with progressive kidney failure and proteinuria. At that time, the patient presented a history of hypertension since the age of 42, hypoacusia, a superficial venous thrombosis event, and incidental evidence of cerebral ischemia.
Blood and urine tests revealed a IIIb CKD stage renal insufficiency (serum creatinine 1.92 mg/dL, MDRD GFR 39 mL/min/1.73 mq) with mild proteinuria (0.821 g/24 h). Urine sediment was clear. A kidney biopsy was performed to explore renal disease etiology, showing non-specific advanced chronic lesions (global glomerulosclerosis in 70% of glomeruli and segmental glomerulosclerosis in 10% of glomeruli, moderate interstitial fibrosis/tubular atrophy, and severe arteriolosclerosis). Therapy with ACE-inhibitor was hence started.
Two years later, at the age of 57, a significant concentric LVH (basal septum region 18 mm) was detected during an echocardiographic evaluation for hypertension.
Renal follow-up showed a progressive decline in renal function reaching end-stage renal disease (ESRD) at the patient’s age of 61. Chronic peritoneal dialysis was started, and one year later, he underwent renal transplantation from a deceased donor without complications.
When the patient was 63 years old, he began complaining of dyspnea during intense exercise. Echocardiography showed worsening LVH (maximum wall thickness of 24 mm) associated with pronounced tissue hyperechogenicity and right ventricular hypertrophy, finally raising the suspicion of Fabry disease. The diagnosis was confirmed by the detection of reduced α-galactosidase A activity measured on dried blood spots (DBS) (0.5 µmol/L/h (reference values > 2.8)) and with molecular analysis revealing the N215S GLA mutation. Increased levels of lysoGb3 on DBS (6.4 ng/mL (reference values 0.0–3.5)) were detected. At diagnosis, serum creatinine was 1.23 mg/dL, and proteinuria was 0.3 g/24 h.
Thus, in March 2019, the patient was referred to our Rare Disease Center for Fabry disease management and follow-up. The Mainz Severity Score Index (MSSI) was 40/76 (General score—3; Neurological score—3; Cardiovascular score—16; and Renal score—18), and the total assessment Fabry Disease Severity Scoring System (DS3) was 46/80 (Peripheral Nervous System domain—0; Renal domain—23; Cardiac domain—18; Central Nervous System domain—1; and Patient-Reported domain 4).
Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) showed severe biventricular hypertrophy with reduced native T1 values, suggesting myocardial glycosphingolipid storage. The left ventricular mass index was 198.4 g/m2. Extensive late gadolinium enhancement was also detected in the infero-lateral wall, with an increased T2 value indicating myocardial edema ().
An initial 24-h Holter monitoring revealed two runs of atrial fibrillation, which required the introduction of anticoagulant therapy.
One year later, in 2020, two runs of monomorphic non-sustained ventricular tachycardia were detected during a further 24-h Holter monitoring. Thus, an ICD was implanted.
Therapy with agalsidase beta at a standard dose of 1 mg/kg every two weeks, administered at our center, was initiated in June 2019. In March 2020, the outbreak of the Coronavirus pandemic dramatically affected the Northern regions of Italy. Thus, the patient asked to switch to oral therapy to avoid hospital access or contact with health care workers in the case of home treatment activation. No contraindications to chaperone therapy were found (serum creatinine 1.28 mg/dL, MDRD GFR 60 mL/min/1.73 mq). We thereby started migalastat at a standard dose of 123 mg orally every other day. The patient did not show any side effects of migalastat such as headache, runny or stuffy nose, urinary tract infection, nausea, or fever. After one year of migalastat treatment, the patient was stable in terms of symptoms, renal function (serum creatinine 1.30 mg/dL, proteinuria 0.275 g/24 h), and cardiac function (CMR in March 2021 showed substantially stable cardiac features). No adjustments in the immunosuppressor dosage, namely prednisone, mycophenolate mofetil, and tacrolimus, were required. In addition, we detected a reduction in lysoGb3 levels (4.3 ng/mL; a reduction of 33% from the patient’s basal values) and an increase in α-galactosidase A activity measured on dried blood spots (1.1 µmol/L/h; an increase of 120% compared to the patient’s basal values).
To confirm the kidney disease etiology, we recently analyzed the residual material from the native kidney biopsy by electron microscopy, finding abundant electron-lucent microvacuoles in many cell types and multivacuolized appearance of podocytes ().

calculator_name: HEART Score for Major Cardiac Events

args: A 58-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with 90 minutes of central chest pressure that began while carrying groceries from her car. She described the discomfort as a heavy, squeezing sensation located retrosternally, radiating to the left arm and lower jaw, accompanied by diaphoresis, mild shortness of breath, and nausea without emesis. She noted two briefer episodes over the past week with brisk walking, each resolving after several minutes of rest, but today’s pain persisted despite stopping activity and sitting. She denied pleuritic features, cough, fever, rhinorrhea, trauma, or recent upper respiratory symptoms. She reported no similar pain from reflux and had not noticed burning or sour taste; antacids at home did not change her symptoms. She denied chest wall tenderness or positional component to the pain. There was no antecedent prolonged immobility, calf swelling, hemoptysis, or known hypercoagulable condition. She has a history of coronary artery disease with a drug-eluting stent placed four years ago after a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction; she follows with cardiology and reports taking a daily antiplatelet agent. She does not use tobacco or nicotine products and reports no exposure to secondhand smoke at home. She has never been on insulin or any oral glucose-lowering medications and states prior routine labs have consistently shown normal fasting glucose; triage comprehensive metabolic panel drawn prior to any treatment today shows glucose 96 mg/dL with normal electrolytes and creatinine. She reports no prior episodes of sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, facial droop, speech arrest, or transient monocular vision loss. Weight at triage is 92 kg with a height of 165 cm (BMI approximately 33.8 kg/m²). She denies ever being told her lipids were elevated, is not taking any lipid-lowering medication, and reports a recent outpatient lipid panel within goal range; she was not started on additional therapy at that time. She drinks alcohol rarely, denies recreational drug use, and works in an office setting. There is no recent surgery, immobilization, or known malignancy. On arrival she appeared mildly uncomfortable and diaphoretic but not in acute respiratory distress. Vital signs prior to any interventions: temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 94 bpm regular, blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Physical examination showed clear lungs to auscultation bilaterally without rales or wheeze, normal work of breathing, and no chest wall tenderness to palpation. Cardiac exam revealed a regular rhythm with normal S1 and S2, no audible murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and no jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses were 2+ and symmetric without edema. Abdomen was soft and non-tender without epigastric tenderness. Neurologic exam was nonfocal with intact cranial nerves and normal motor strength. At triage, an electrocardiogram obtained prior to medication showed significant repolarization abnormalities characterized by horizontal ST depressions measuring 2 to 3 mm in leads V4–V6 with associated T-wave inversions in the lateral leads and 1–2 mm ST depression in the inferior leads; QRS duration and intervals were within normal limits, there were no ST elevations, and no pathologic Q waves were noted. No prior ECG was immediately available for comparison. Initial laboratory testing drawn on arrival showed a high-sensitivity cardiac troponin I level of 176 ng/L (reference ≤18 ng/L), obtained before any analgesics or antiplatelet agents were administered in the emergency department. Complete blood count was unremarkable with no leukocytosis or anemia. Renal function and electrolytes were within normal limits as above. A portable chest radiograph performed at the bedside demonstrated a normal cardiac silhouette without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The nature of the pain (exertional onset evolving to pain at rest, pressure quality, radiation to arm and jaw, associated diaphoresis and nausea, and lack of reproducibility with palpation or positional changes), the known history of coronary stent placement for prior myocardial ischemia, and the ECG pattern with lateral ST segment depressions were concerning for an acute coronary syndrome. She remained hemodynamically stable without hypotension throughout the initial evaluation, was alert and oriented, and did not display features of an alternative urgent noncardiac condition necessitating admission independent of the chest pain evaluation. She had not taken any medications prior to arrival for this episode and no treatments had been administered at the time these findings were documented.

calculator_name: CHA2DS2-VASc Score for Atrial Fibrillation Stroke Risk

args:
A 75-year-old man with persistent AF, a CHA2DS2 VASc score of 4 and a HAS-BLED score of 4 with recurrent gastrointestinal bleedings was referred to a tertiary clinic for a LAAC. His medical history consisted of a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), coronary artery disease, and a two-thirds gastric resection performed in the 1980s. The intervention was successfully performed with an ABBOTT® Amulet 18 mm device being implanted (). After the implantation, he received acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) and Clopidogrel for 6 months. Approximately 5 months post-intervention, the patient expressed acute dyspnoea and called the emergency medical services (EMS).
On arrival of the EMS, the patient was conscious, severely distressed and hypotensive with a blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg and a heart rate of 90/min. Peripheral oxygen saturation of 85% measured percutaneously was noted. After the administration of bronchodilator medication for a presumed COPD exacerbation, the patient’s clinical condition deteriorated with a resultant cardiac arrest with pulseless electrical activity. The patient received cardiopulmonary resuscitation including chest compressions, endotracheal intubation, and the administration of 3 mg Adrenaline. Return of spontaneous circulation occurred approximately 10 min later at which point he was transferred to a local hospital. During the transport, the cardiocirculatory parameters were relatively stable with a systolic pressure around 100 mmHg. The capnometry after return of spontaneous circulation measured a pCO2 of 60 mmHg. Upon arrival at the hospital, the physical examination showed jugular vein distension but no peripheral oedema.
Echocardiography performed in the emergency department revealed a pericardial tamponade requiring pericardiocentesis via the subxiphoidal route, 800 mL of blood were initially evacuated. The differential diagnosis included primary respiratory failure or a sudden cardiac death with secondary pericardial effusion induced by cardiac compressions under double antiplatelet therapy, a ruptured aortic aneurysm, a myocardial rupture, or a primary laceration of a cardiac structure by the LAA occluder. A ruptured aneurysm of the ascending aorta was excluded by ultrasound. Electrocardiogram examination did not demonstrate any ST-segment elevation or low voltage. Blood gas analysis documented a stable respiratory situation with a pO2 of 230 mmHg and a pCO2 of 55 mmHg, the pH was 7, 3 and Lactate elevated with 10 mmol/L (<2 mmol/L). The haemoglobin was measured at 9 g/dL (12–16 g/dL). The chest X-ray demonstrated the proper placement of the endotracheal and pericardial tube and ruled out a pneumothorax or pulmonary oedema.
In the hours following pericardiocentesis, approximately 2 L of blood were evacuated. Conventional methods of coagulopathy management including application of tranexamic acid, fresh frozen plasma, and fibrinogen did not stop the bleeding. To stabilize the patient a total of eight packs of red blood cells were transfused.
In preparation of cardiac surgery, a coronary angiogram was performed (). It revealed active leakage of contrast agent in the proximal circumflex artery and the patient was transferred to the cardiac surgery department immediately. Intra-operatively a perforation of the tissue at the basis of the LAA close to the left main coronary artery was discovered. Under protection of cold blood cardioplegia on cardiopulmonary bypass the occluder was excised through the opened left atrium. The LAA was closed by endocardial sutures and two pledget sutures were used to seal the epicardial lesion at the LAA basis which stopped the bleeding. Fortunately, after prolonged treatment in the intensive care unit and weaning ward the patient was discharged and alive 4 months after the event without neurological deficit and on ASA monotherapy. Transthoracic echocardiogram in the weaning ward ruled out a persistent pericardial effusion.
