calculator_name: HEART Score for Major Cardiac Events

args:
A 47-year-old man presented to the emergency department (ED) in April 2021 for altered mental status. His medical history consisted of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, and there was no history of malignancy. Per Emergency Medical Services, the patient lives at home with a roommate who witnessed a seizure-like episode. He arrived at the ED with glucose in the 700s. Home medications included insulin glargine 25 units twice daily, gabapentin 800 mg three times daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and metoprolol succinate 200 mg daily. He was treated for hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state in the ED and was admitted for further management. The patient was found to be COVID positive on SARS-CoV-2 RNA testing. He left against medical advice two days later.
Approximately two weeks later, the patient presented with pleuritic chest pain associated with shortness of breath. Vital signs at the time of admission: temperature 37.9° Celsius, heart rate 92 beats/minute, blood pressure 117/79 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Cardiac examination revealed normal S1 and S2 and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lung auscultation was clear to auscultation bilaterally, without wheezes, rhonchi, or rales. Bilateral 2+ pitting edema was present to the level of his knees. Initial troponin was 0.481 ng/ml (reference <0.012 ng/ml) but subsequently downtrended to <0.02 ng/ml, and pro B-type natriuretic peptide (pro-BNP) was 2800 pg/ml (reference 0-300 pg/ml). Electrocardiography (ECG) showed normal sinus rhythm with low voltages in the limb leads; there were no diffuse ST-segment elevations (Figure ). Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% with severe global hypokinesis, increased wall thickness of the right ventricle, and a small circumferential pericardial effusion without evidence of tamponade physiology. Unfortunately, the patient again left against medical advice.
Three months later, the patient presented to the hospital with shortness of breath and was found to be volume overloaded due to medication noncompliance. TTE showed a large pericardial effusion with diastolic compression of the right atrium, consistent with cardiac tamponade physiology. He underwent pericardiocentesis with the removal of 800 ml serosanguinous fluid that was negative for malignant cytology. Acid-fast bacilli culture and smear of the pericardial fluid were negative. Approximately one month later, the patient saw his cardiologist for follow-up. The patient was referred to the ED for bilateral lower extremity cellulitis. On this most recent ED visit, approximately four months after the initial presentation, the patient agreed to stay in the hospital and undergo a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation.
In addition to evaluation of his cellulitis, bedside ultrasonography showed that a moderate to large pericardial effusion had recurred, though without evidence of right ventricular diastolic collapse or right atrial systolic collapse. CT chest also confirmed an incidental large pericardial effusion (Figure ). Blood cultures were negative. Testing of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and antinuclear antibody (ANA) serology was negative. His ECG on this admission was unchanged from prior ECGs. Hemoglobin A1c was 8%, and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) was 58 mg/dl. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), alpha fetoprotein (AFP), cancer antigens (CA 19-9), and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) were negative. Repeat TTE showed large pericardial effusion (2.3 cm) and evidence of tamponade physiology (exaggerated respiratory variation in mitral inflow velocity (E) on Doppler echocardiography).
Of note, a previous left heart catheterization with coronary angiography did not show significant obstructive coronary artery disease. A prior echocardiogram seven months prior revealed an ejection fraction of 65%, without pericardial effusion. The patient underwent placement of a pericardial window, and a chest tube was inserted. Repeat blood cultures were negative. The chest tube drained approximately 165 ml. The patient’s clinical presentation improved, and he was discharged home.

args:
A white British, 78-year-old previously healthy female, non-smoker and with no known pulmonary disease, was admitted with collapse and shortness of breath with arterial oxygen saturations of haemoglobin of 80% on room air. The background included a history of persistent dry cough for 3 months without any other complaint or systemic symptoms. Her past medical history comprised of type 2 diabetes, increased uric acid and ongoing pain in the right knee. Her medication history consisted of gliclazide, allopurinol and diclofenac. The patient denied any recent travel or contact with animals (even pets). Physical examination revealed central cyanosis, tachypnoea, bilateral reduction in breath sounds and right-sided bronchial breathing. Cardiac examination was normal. There were no adenopathy noted. Arterial blood gases analysis on 5 litres of O2 per min showed mixed acidaemia. Serum biochemistry revealed raised urea (15.1 mmol/L) and creatinine (114 umol/L) levels consistent with acute kidney injury, positive D-dimmer's levels and increased CRP (48.2 mg/L). Chest radiography showed a loss of volume in the right lung (Figure ) and an electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with a heart rate of 94 bpm, left axis deviation and an old inferior myocardial infarction. At this time the patient was started on treatment for both a possible community acquired pneumonia (benzyl penicillin and clarythromycin) and for pulmonary thromboembolism (therapeutic doses of Dalteparin). The patient was stable for 48 hours but then her oxygen requirements increased progressively. Contrast-enhanced chest CT scan and CT pulmonary arteriography with venous phase imaging failed to demonstrate any evidence of pulmonary artery thrombus. However thrombus, with associated fibrosis and volume loss, was seen to affect one of the right lower lobe pulmonary veins (Figure ). An echocardiogram was performed which showed a normal sized left ventricle with good function, normal aortic and mitral valves. The right heart although poorly visualized, appeared to be mildly enlarged with moderate function. The patient was started on methylprednisolone 1 g once a day and transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU).
The patient continued to deteriorate over the next 24 hours following admission to the ICU. She developed biochemical and electrocardiographic evidence of an acute anterior-lateral myocardial infarction and aspirin, clopidogrel and simvastatin were added to her anticoagulation therapy. Unfortunately the patient's oxygen requirements continued to increase and she was ventilated, initially supported by biphasic positive airway pressure (BIPAP) and then subsequently by invasive mechanical ventilation. Over the next eight days, despite maximal therapy she continued to deteriorate and died from multiple organ failure.

args: A 40-year-old man was referred to gastrointestinal surgeons for pancreatic mass pointed out by an abdominal ultrasonography in a medical check-up. The patient did not show any symptoms. Abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) revealed dissection from the CA (), DPA, and splenic artery (SA). This aneurysm was not seen on an abdominal unenhanced CT taken 6 years ago in a medical check-up. The enhancing inner lumen of the DPA was derived from false lumen and that of the SA was derived from true lumen. The stenosis of proximal side of SA, an aneurysm measuring 2.5 cm on DPAA with unenhanced mural thrombus, and the connection of the distal side of the DPAA to transverse pancreatic artery (TPA) with stenotic change were confirmed (). A connection with DPA and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) was undetermined. The current diagnosis was the dissection of the CA to DPA and SA, resulting in DPAA and SA stenosis. After the diagnosis was made, the patient was referred to the department of radiology by a vascular surgeon for the consultation of endovascular surgery to prevent aneurysm rupture because open surgery was thought to be quite invasive as it required distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy and CA repair. He had received medications for hypertension. There were no remarkable findings in his initial laboratory data. We planned to embolize DPAA, SA, and dissected area of CA that allow to preserve vascular supply to hepatic artery from SMA via peripancreatic arcade and to distal side of SA via gastroduodenal artery (GDA) to TPA. An angiography from the CA revealed the enhancing inner lumen of DPAA from false lumen of dissected CA connecting to TPA (). TPA was revealed between the peripancreatic arcade from GDA and pancreatic tail artery that connected to the distal side of SA. A 4.5 Fr. Shepherd hook–type guiding sheath (Parent Plus 45: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), carrying a triple coaxial system, consisted of a 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (Cerulean G: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), 2.6 Fr. high-flow catheter (Masters HF: Asahi Intech, Aichi, Japan), 1.9 Fr. microcatheter (Carnelian Marvel: Tokai Medical Products, Aichi, Japan), was hooked on the CA. To prevent the coil migration into the TPA, a microballoon catheter (Logos: Piolax, Yokohama, Japan) was advanced to the distal side of the DPAA via the GDA to pancreatoduodenal artery and inflated at the sufficient point of the TPA through the additional 4 Fr. shepherd hook catheter placed in the CA from the contralateral groin site (). The 4.5 Fr guiding sheath and the 4 Fr. Shepherd hook catheter were inserted from the right and left side of the common femoral artery, respectively. Under microballoon catheter inflation, the distal side of the DPAA was embolized with detachable coils (PRESIDIO 6 mm × 20 cm, GALAXY 6 mm × 15 cm) through a 1.9 Fr microcatheter (). Afterward, the microballoon catheter was deflated. Then, intra-aneurysmal coil embolization was performed with detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 20 cm) through the 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (). Later, the microcatheter in the intermediate catheter was carefully advanced through the dissected portion of the SA and embolization of the affected area was performed with detachable coils (Penumbra POD 6 mm × 50 cm, Penumbra 6 mm × 15 cm, 4 mm × 8 cm, GALAXY 2 of 7 mm × 21 cm, 2 of 4 mm × 15 cm) (). Finally, coil embolization of the dissected CA was performed using detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, 4 mm × 10 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, Penumbra coil 400 2 of 10 mm × 30 cm) (). The final angiography from SMA after pulling out all the systems revealed complete occlusion of the DPAA, the proximal side of SA, preserving GDA to the TPA to the distal side of SA and preserving GDA to common hepatic artery to left gastric artery (). No complication including post-coiling ischemia or transient laboratory abnormalities in transaminases, amylases, or lipase was observed during and after the endovascular surgery. The patient was followed up for 2 years at the near hospital; however, DPAA has not shown regrowth by contrast enhancement CT.

args: A 74‑year‑old male presented with profound confusion and severely diminished responsiveness, prompting immediate evaluation for possible acute multi‑organ compromise. According to bystanders who accompanied him, he had been increasingly somnolent over the preceding two days, becoming nearly unarousable by the time of hospital arrival. Initial assessment revealed that he was breathing without the assistance of a tube or other advanced apparatus but required supplemental oxygen at a concentration of 33 %—roughly one‑third above ambient levels. Despite this added oxygen, his arterial sample showed a partial pressure of 68 mm Hg, indicating sub‑optimal gas exchange. He had dangerously low arterial pressure on arrival, with measurements of 76/32 mm Hg;  he was started on an intravenous medication at 5 mcg/kg/min of DOPamine along with 0.07 mcg/kg/min norEPINEPHrine. Of note, two other intravenous therapies that are sometimes considered when significant cardiac or vascular support is necessary were not added at this time. Laboratory evaluations performed urgently revealed several abnormalities. His platelet count was found to be 200 000 / µL, which remains within acceptable limits yet will be watched closely in the context of possible systemic dysfunction. Another noteworthy finding was a total bilirubin level measured at 2.5 mg/dL, signifying a degree of hepatobiliary distress that could stem from either pre‑existing liver vulnerability or a more acute systemic insult. Renal indicators likewise raised concern: his serum creatinine was 2.4 mg/dL, suggesting precipitous kidney stress or an acute‑on‑chronic injury. Despite this worrisome finding, he managed to produce 2 000 mL of urine over a day’s time before presentation, showing that at least some degree of filtration and fluid excretion was continuing, though the ultimate viability of this function remained in question. Clinicians were particularly alarmed by his severely decreased cognitive responses. They noted that he did not reliably open his eyes spontaneously, rarely demonstrated coherent verbal skills, and occasionally withdrew from painful stimuli but did not exhibit the purposeful resistance that might be expected in a less compromised state, giving a GCS of 9. This level of unresponsiveness could carry implications for airway protection and further respiratory compromise, so the care team kept him under constant observation in a setting equipped to intervene quickly. Despite his markedly reduced alertness, he exhibited no major indications of widespread bleeding tendencies, maintaining an intact platelet count without unwarranted bruising or bleeding from venipuncture sites. There was no obvious sign of shock as determined by standard circulatory parameters, yet the mild infusion of two intravenous agents to bolster cardiac output and systemic blood flow seemed prudent given his risk factors. Imaging studies were ordered to explore possible causes of acute mental status change; early suspicions included metabolic derangements, hepatic dysfunction, occult infection, or other inflammatory triggers that could precipitate such a presentation. In the absence of overt respiratory failure requiring a breathing apparatus, he was allowed to breathe spontaneously with carefully titrated supplemental oxygen. Healthcare providers remained especially mindful of his advanced years and unknown baseline organ performance, contemplating whether his kidney dysfunction might be longstanding or newly emergent. Additional diagnostic steps, including a thorough review of historical labs and potential nephrotoxic exposures, were initiated. Plans called for ongoing neurological checks, repeated measurements of his arterial oxygen, and close tracking of his urine output and evolving laboratory parameters. Although he displayed worrisome neurologic depression, the absence of alarmingly low blood pressure and the presence of continued, albeit possibly tenuous, urine production offered some measure of cautious optimism. The immediate treatment efforts aimed to stabilize oxygenation, preserve end‑organ perfusion with carefully dosed intravenous medications, and identify any factors that could explain or exacerbate hepatic and renal strain. No significant prior medical records were available at the time of his initial evaluation, which introduced another layer of complexity when formulating a definitive care plan. Nonetheless, the early provision of supportive therapies, vigilant hemodynamic monitoring, and timely diagnostic workup stood as the principal approach to prevent further deterioration. His condition at presentation underscored the gravity of severe neurologic depression, elevated bilirubin, deteriorating renal indicators, and sub‑optimal oxygen exchange, all unfolding despite a near‑normal range of blood pressure measurements. Close multidisciplinary collaboration was deemed essential to elucidate the underlying cause, tailor potential treatments, and determine the best path forward in safeguarding both his neurologic prognosis and multi‑organ function.

args: A 62-year-old female with a history of diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and mild congestive heart failure presented to the emergency department with progressive respiratory distress and confusion over the past several days. She required immediate airway support using mechanical ventillation to deliver oxygen at 45 % concentration. Her PaO2 was 65 mm Hg. She was only intermittently responsive, and on formal neurologic assessment her Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) was 10, based on her ability to open her eyes, offer limited verbal responses, and localize to pain but not consistently follow complex commands. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 70 mm Hg systolic and 50 mm Hg diastolic. Laboratory results at presentation showed a platelet count of 120 000 / µL, indicating a mild reduction, and her total bilirubin level was 2.3 mg/dL, which represented a noticeable elevation when compared to her known baseline. Additional testing revealed that her creatinine was 1.9 mg/dL, supporting the suspicion of impaired kidney function beyond her previously documented mild abnormalities, and her urine output had decreased to 650 mL/day over the past 24 hours, suggesting a decline in renal performance. Given her compromised cardiovascular and respiratory status, she was placed on a moderate infusion of dopamine at  2 mcg/kg/min to support her circulation, and norepinephrine at  0.02 mcg/kg/min to further aid hemodynamic stability. Despite these interventions, she displayed persistent fatigue and disorientation. In the days leading up to her arrival, she had experienced increasing shortness of breath, decreased appetite, and reduced fluid intake, which likely contributed to both her respiratory compromise and evolving metabolic derangements. Physical examination was notable for diminished breath sounds in all lung fields without clear focal consolidation, and she exhibited trace pedal edema. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular rhythm without significant murmurs, though mild jugular venous distension was observed. The family reported that she had been increasingly somnolent, but they attributed it initially to fatigue and had delayed seeking care. Since her admission, she had undergone preliminary imaging that did not reveal obvious signs of major acute changes, such as large pleural effusions or gross consolidation, though subtle infiltrates could not be ruled out. Her mildly elevated bilirubin was investigated for possible hepatic congestion given her history of heart failure, though other causes such as hemolysis were also considered. Furthermore, her relatively low platelet count raised concern about underlying inflammation or early bone marrow suppression, necessitating closer hematologic monitoring. The administration of intravenous fluids was approached with caution due to her borderline renal status and potential risk for fluid overload. Nonetheless, every effort was made to optimize her ventilation and perfusion, with a focus on stabilizing her oxygen saturation and ensuring adequate organ perfusion. The presence of a creatinine level at 1.9 mg/dL on arrival marked a significant change, prompting inquiries into the timing of her renal deterioration and whether recent infections, medications, or hypoperfusion might have contributed. Of particular importance, her daily urine output of 650 mL suggested ongoing renal compromise that warranted vigilant surveillance. Throughout this evaluation, her sedation requirements were kept minimal so that her mental status could be serially assessed, although her baseline cognition remained at a GCS of 10. Carefully balancing her ventilation parameters, intravenous infusions at the specified rates, and potential need for further diagnostic work formed the backbone of her immediate therapeutic strategy. A detailed plan focusing on stabilizing her respiratory function, guarding against worsening renal impairment, and reevaluating her liver function was set in motion, all while closely observing her neurologic status and platelet trends.

args: A 47-year-old man with a history limited to well-controlled mild asthma and seasonal rhinitis presented to the ambulatory triage area mid-morning complaining of an aching sensation and progressive enlargement of his right calf that began roughly thirty-hours prior while he was at work installing cabinetry in a two-story townhome. He recalls kneeling frequently and carrying heavy toolboxes up a flight of stairs but denies any single twisting injury, audible pop, or sharp pain; instead, he noticed a vague tightness behind the knee late that evening which intensified overnight, prompting him to inspect both lower limbs side-by-side in the bathroom mirror. The following morning his work sock left a noticeable indentation at the upper ankle and the posterior calf looked fuller than its counterpart, so he measured the circumference with a flexible sewing tape he keeps for home projects: at a point exactly 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity the right leg measured 41 cm whereas the left measured 37 cm, a disparity of 4 cm that persisted when rechecked two hours later before clinic arrival. He reports no comparable swelling of the thigh, knee, or foot, no discoloration, and no warmth appreciable through his trouser leg. He has not been bedridden—he walks his dog twice daily, drove himself to the clinic, and continues normal activities except for avoiding squats because they provoke discomfort. He has not undergone surgery, endoscopy, or invasive dental work in the past year, and the most recent hospitalization was an overnight observation for an asthma exacerbation four years ago. He has never received chemotherapy, has no known malignancy, and has not required a cast, splint, or immobilizing boot. He flew to Denver on a two-hour flight last week but sat in an aisle seat and walked the terminal without prolonged stasis. He denies fever, chills, sweats, rash, trauma, insect bite, recent soft-tissue infection, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, hemoptysis, or syncope. Medications include inhaled budesonide-formoterol twice daily and an albuterol rescue inhaler used perhaps once a month; he takes no hormonal preparations, anticoagulants, or herbal supplements. He never smokes, drinks two beers on weekends, and does not use illicit drugs. There is no family history of thromboembolic disease or hypercoagulable states. On examination he appears fit, ambulating with a slight antalgic gait favoring the right side but otherwise in no distress. Vital signs: temperature 98.4 °F (36.9 °C), pulse 88 beats/min regular, blood pressure 128/74 mm Hg, respiratory rate 14/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiac auscultation reveals a normal S1/S2 without murmurs; lungs are clear with no wheezes; jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Inspection of the lower extremities shows asymmetric fullness confined to the mid-calf region of the right leg with a subtle glossy sheen of overlying skin; the right ankle and foot appear normal in size, and thigh girth measured 15 cm proximal to the patella is symmetric at 50 cm bilaterally. Gentle finger pressure over the anterior tibia of the right calf produces a 2-mm pit that rebounds within 15 seconds, whereas no indentation forms on the left. The right calf feels slightly warmer but not erythematous. Palpation along the course of the popliteal and posterior tibial venous channels elicits focal tenderness deep to the medial gastrocnemius; squeezing the muscle belly itself produces only diffuse soreness. There is no palpable cord, no varicosities, and no cyanosis of the toes. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ and equal; capillary refill is less than two seconds. Range of motion at the knee and ankle is full; dorsiflexion of the ankle with knee extended reproduces a pulling discomfort but no lancinating pain. Neurologic examination shows intact sensation and 5/5 motor strength. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is present. Bedside urine dip is unremarkable. Baseline laboratory tests obtained before any anticoagulant therapy demonstrate hemoglobin 15.0 g/dL, platelet count 242 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.92 mg/dL, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, AST/ALT within normal limits, prothrombin time 12.1 seconds with an INR of 1.0, and aPTT 29 seconds. An electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm. Clinically, the unilateral calf enlargement greater than 3 cm, discrete pitting edema confined to the symptomatic limb, and pinpoint tenderness tracking the deep venous pathway raise strong concern for an obstructive process within the deep veins of the right calf; musculoskeletal strain or ruptured popliteal cyst remain possibilities but appear less compelling given the objective girth discrepancy and focal venous tenderness. Duplex ultrasonography of the entire right lower extremity has been ordered on an urgent basis, and the patient has been instructed to limit strenuous activity and to alert staff immediately should he notice sudden chest discomfort or worsening shortness of breath while awaiting imaging.

args: A 45 year-old male developed flushing and urticaria 15 minutes after eating pineapple, then he felt precordial squeezing pain radiating to the left shoulder, lasting for several minutes, accompanied by palpitation and excessive sweating. He is a smoker with no other cardiac risk factors and no history of CVD, he was allergic to pineapple before but only manifested as slight itching and skin rashes which spontaneously disappeared in hours. Before this onset, he had been working day and night for 1 month with doubled tabacco smoking, though he did not smoke after eating pineapple. The patient was sent to the emergency room, on arriving, his symptoms had been relieved by sublingual nitroglycerin, his blood pressure was 120/70mmHg, pulse rate was 80 beat per minute, and ECG revealed no abnormalities while the symptom had relieved. 24h Holter (Figs. , and ) revealed ST segment elevation for 2~3 mm in leads II, III, aVF, V5, V6 at 13:40-13:42 (when he felt malaise, chest pain and palpitation at rest) and 20:20-20:22 (when he was driving, he felt chest pain and left arm numb), as well as ventricular bigeminy and nonsustained ventricular tachycardia at 13:20 and 13:42. He was admitted to cardiology department, blood test showed normal troponin I level and elevated eosinophil of 7.6% on the first day of admission. Coronary angiography showed subocclusion in the proximal segment of LCX with TIMI grade 3 flow, which was relieved by 3 times of 200μg intracoronary nitroglycerin. He was diagnosed as coronary vasospasm on the basis of anaphylaxis to pineapple, and received anti-vasospasm therapy of mononitrate and nifedipine as well as antiallergic agent, the eosinophil rate declined to 6.1% on the 7thday, chest pain didn’t relapse during hospitalization. After 12 months of mononitrate and nifedipine medication, anti-vasospasm therapy was discontinued. During 3 years follow-up, he kept free of cardiac events.

args:
A previously healthy 62-year-old woman living in a rural area developed fever, headache, and fatigue starting on July 27, 2018. She also had gastrointestinal symptoms, such as anorexia, nausea, and diarrhea later, and visited a local clinic on July 30. She was prescribed antipyretic analgesics and returned home. The following day, she developed a mild consciousness disorder and visited a local clinic again accompanied by her family. She underwent a magnetic resonance imaging examination, but no abnormality was noted. However, laboratory data showed leukocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and increased aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and creatine kinase (CK) levels. She was transported to Kagoshima University Hospital.
On admission, she had a slight disturbance of consciousness [Glasgow Coma Scale of 14: E3, V5, M6], a body temperature of 37.4 °C, a respiratory rate of 22/min, blood pressure of 109/73 mmHg, heart rate of 97/min, SpO2 of 99% (room air), normal heart and respiratory sound, no swelling of the superficial lymph nodes, crusty bite wounds in the left inguinal region, although there was no tick. Laboratory testing showed leukocytopenia (total white blood cell count of 9100 cells/mm3); thrombocytopenia (platelet count of 63,000 cells/mm3); mildly elevated AST (68 U/L), lactate dehydrogenase (364 IU/L), and CK (317 U/L) levels; elevated ferritin level (1947 ng/mL); and normal CK-MB fraction (CK-MB) (4 U/L) and C-reactive protein (CRP) level (< 0.02 mg/dL). A urinary general examination showed protein (4+) and occult blood (3+). Electrocardiography (ECG) and chest radiography findings were normal on the day of admission (day 1).
Because of these clinical findings, she was suspected of having SFTS and transferred to the intensive care unit. Several hours after admission, SFTSV RNA was detected in her serum samples by RT-PCR, so she was definitively diagnosed as having SFTS.
Her clinical course is shown in Fig. . Her blood pressure gradually started to decrease from day 2 evening. Fluid resuscitation (100 ml/h for 10 h; crystalloids 500 ml, colloids 500 ml) was performed until day 3 morning because hand-held ultrasound on day 2 midnight showed collapsed IVC (inferior vena cava). Then, repeat hand-held ultrasound showed recover from collapsed IVC; nevertheless, her blood pressure had not recovered and administration of low doses of dopamine and norepinephrine was started. To exclude cardiogenic shock, trans-thoracic echocardiography was performed and showed diffuse left ventricular (LV) wall motion depression (with an EF: ejection fraction of 34.4%), diffuse LV wall thickening, and small amount of pericardial effusion. Further, ECG showed ST elevation in the II, III, aVf, and V2–6 leads and a low-voltage complex in all leads (Fig. ), and the level of troponin I was elevated (120.9 ng/mL), but that of CK-MB was not. She did not have any chest symptoms. She met several diagnostic criteria for clinically suspected myocarditis [], which include unexplained cardiogenic shock, elevated level of troponin I, abnormal ECG and echocardiographic findings. In addition, she also met criteria for fulminant myocarditis [], which include acute illness (history of < 2–4 weeks since the onset of symptoms), hemodynamic instability due to cardiogenic shock, and need for hemodynamic support (inotrope). Therefore, she was suspected of having fulminant myocarditis caused by SFTSV. However, we did not perform coronary angiography (CAG) or endomyocardial biopsy because thrombocytopenia and leukocytopenia greatly increased the risk of an invasive procedure. We screened for other viruses, including enterovirus, adenovirus, and influenza viruses that may cause myocarditis, but none of them were detected in nasal swab samples.
On day 5 of hospitalization, repeat echocardiography showed further LV dysfunction (with an EF of 27.9%), diffuse LV wall thickening (septum/posterior wall: 14.7 mm/13.5 mm), and narrowing of the left ventricular cavity (Fig. ). On the basis of these results, administration of low doses of dobutamine and milrinone was added. On day 6 of hospitalization, repeat echocardiography showed cardiac tamponade findings of the dilated IVC without respiratory variations and right ventricular diastolic collapse (Fig. ). However, no surgical pericardial puncture was performed, because there was no safe puncture space and a circulation agonist was able to maintain blood pressure. After that, echocardiography showed steady improvement of LV function (EF) and wall thickness, the urine output also continued to increase and the water balance gradually leaned toward negative as a result (Fig. ). Additionally, the pericardial effusion gradually decreased and the cardiac tamponade findings naturally disappeared. On day 11 of hospitalization, her mild disturbance of consciousness from admission had improved, and she no longer needed a circulatory agonist. On day 13 of hospitalization, ECG showed improvement of ST elevation in the II, III, aVf, and V2–6 leads. She was eventually transferred to a general ward on the same day. On day 17 of hospitalization, repeat echocardiography showed normal LV function (with an EF of 70.3%) and wall thickness (septum/posterior wall: 8.9 mm/9.4 mm) (Fig. ).
On day 20 of hospitalization, laboratory testing and ECG showed no abnormal findings. After exercise rehabilitation, she was discharged home on day 28 of hospitalization.
She had no history of cardiac diseases, allergy, or use of drugs that may cause myocarditis. During 28 days of hospitalization, CRP and bacterial culture were always negative, and there was no evidence of eosinophil elevation. Viruses, other than SFTSV, were not detected by virus isolation test. Based on these results, she was clinically diagnosed as having fulminant myocarditis caused by SFTSV.

args: A 52-year-old female arrived in the intensive care unit with a high fever and confusion that had developed rapidly over the preceding day. According to her family’s account, she had been complaining of headache and body aches and was found to be sweating profusely on the morning of admission. On initial evaluation, her temperature was measured at 102.2 degrees Fahrenheit, and she appeared significantly lethargic, offering only faint, incoherent sounds when spoken to and failing to open her eyes or move purposefully in response to voice or discomfort. Her blood pressure readings showed a systolic measurement of 105 mm Hg and a diastolic measurement of 59 mm Hg. Her pulse was 91 beats per minute, neither profoundly rapid nor exceptionally slow, and her rate of breathing was 13 breaths per minute, which seemed within normal limits on the surface. However, clinicians noted the patient was receiving a notably elevated proportion of her inhaled air from supplemental sources, well above the standard atmospheric fraction, specifically an FiO2 of 65%. Despite this considerable assistance, her blood gas analysis revealed an oxygen concentration in the arterial system that was suboptimal for someone on such a high level of support. Once the alveolar-to-arterial gradient was calculated, the team identified a markedly widened difference of 200 mm Hg, highlighting ongoing problems with gas exchange. Supporting laboratory data demonstrated evidence of a possible infectious or inflammatory process, with a white blood cell count of 10,700 mm^3, slightly above ten thousand, but without an extreme reaction one might see in fulminant infection. Her core chemistries, including sodium and potassium, were mildly deranged, with her sodium measured at 134 mEq/L, slightly below the mid-normal range and her potassium positioned at 3.5 mEq/L, the lower edge of acceptable limits, prompting careful monitoring for potential shifts. More concerning was her noticeably elevated marker of kidney function, with a creatinine level of 2.4 mg/dL, which was found to be nearly twice the upper boundary of normal, and this rise had reportedly not been documented on previous measurements in her chart, implying a new decline in her renal handling of metabolic byproducts consistent with acute renal failure. She had no known history of chronic kidney issues, severe damage to other vital organs, or any major immunologic deficiency, making this abrupt rise in her kidney marker all the more alarming. Additional circulatory labs confirmed that her red cell volume percentage was 43%, suggesting that her oxygen-carrying capacity was fairly preserved in terms of hemoglobin concentration, though her overall acid-base status was skewed toward a slightly lower than optimal pH. In this setting, her pH was 7.33, signifying a mild form of acidemia that could reflect early compromise of her metabolic processes or inadequate removal of acids due to the newly identified kidney concern. Neurological assessment was particularly worrisome with a Glasgow Coma Score of 3. Given her unresponsiveness, fever, and evidence of systemic stress, the clinical team initiated a thorough search for possible triggers, including focus on possible bloodstream infection, acute inflammation within the lungs, or a complicated abdominal source. It was also discovered that she had been scheduled for urgent operative intervention, and this procedure was categorized under an unplanned, time-sensitive scenario that could not be delayed if certain pathologies were confirmed. Throughout her admission workup, clinicians labored to stabilize her, balancing fluids and carefully observing her electrolyte shifts along with her oxygen requirements. Multipronged diagnostics, including imaging studies and microbiologic cultures, were pursued to ascertain the underlying cause of her precipitous decline. At this earliest point in her ICU admission, before any substantial therapies had been administered, her presentation was dominated by a pronounced disturbance in mental status, moderate changes in her infection markers, high fever, and an unexpected reduction in kidney performance, all while receiving a considerably increased supply of inhaled oxygen to maintain acceptable saturation levels. As her condition was deemed critical, the team took every measure to support her precarious physiology and strategized about how best to address the emergent condition that had prompted her swift operative evaluation.

args: A 68-year-old male presented to the emergency department after three days of progressive shortness of breath, productive cough, and generalized malaise. He noted an intermittent fever at home, which worsened on the day of admission, along with chills and night sweats. He has been feeling more fatigued than usual, struggling to complete his routine morning walks without pausing to catch his breath, and has also experienced mild chest tightness upon exertion. On initial evaluation, he was awake, alert, and oriented, conversing clearly without lapses in memory or concentration. He denied experiencing any acute episodes of unilateral weakness, facial droop, or slurred speech in the past, and he reported no longstanding liver problems. According to him, fluid overload symptoms such as progressive leg swelling, difficulty breathing while lying flat, or episodes of nighttime coughing have not been issues. He described a past difficulty with kidney function, requiring follow-up with specialists, and also revealed a remotest history of a malignant growth for which he received treatment several years ago. On further questioning, he stated that his usual medications include those needed to monitor both his chronic conditions and his past oncologic management, although he did not recall any medication specifically for significantly compromised cardiac output. He claimed that he has never been told he suffered from sudden neurologic attacks or blood supply problems to his brain and had not visited any facility for such concerns. He reported no sign of abdominal issues, and he had never been diagnosed with disorders that would impair liver enzymes or clotting factors. Recent activity levels have diminished because of his respiratory discomfort, which he first attributed to a seasonal change. However, with the escalation of his breathing difficulties and fever, he became worried. Despite staying hydrated, he felt persistently thirsty, and routine checks at home indicated elevated fingerstick readings, prompting him to mention prior abnormal glucose measurements to medical providers. On arrival, his vital signs showed a heart rate of 84 beats per minute, a respiratory rate of 26 breaths per minute, and a systolic blood pressure of 140 mm Hg. His core temperature measured 101.3 degrees Fahrenheit, aligning with his complaint of fever. An arterial blood sample revealed a partial pressure of oxygen of 75 mm Hg, consistent with his labored breathing, and his serum chemistry was notable for a blood urea nitrogen level of 30 mg/dL. Further evaluation indicated that his serum sodium concentration was 136 mEq/L, and an arterial blood gas analysis showed a pH of 7.38. His hematocrit was 35%, suggesting a mild decrease in red blood cell concentration, and a random glucose test was 158 mg/dL, confirming the elevation he had suspected at home. Although he endorsed a history of kidney issues, he described meticulous monitoring of his fluid intake and ongoing consultations with specialists. He also confirmed that his previous cancer treatment had gone well, with no recent reactivations or complications, yet he undergoes periodic surveillance. There were no remarkable findings on examination that suggested significant fluid accumulation in his legs or lungs beyond what might be expected with an infection. He did not require continuous supplemental oxygen therapy at home, so the noted hypoxemia was new for him. Nonetheless, he remained conversational, followed commands appropriately, and displayed no confusion. The physical exam revealed inspiratory crackles over a focused region on one lung field, in keeping with his complaints of cough and breathlessness. After calmly recounting his symptoms and history, he awaited further imaging and laboratory assessment to clarify the etiology of his pneumonia and guide management.

args:
A 64-year-old Taiwanese woman presented with a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. She had been self-treating regularly with NSAIDs from pharmacy (diclofenac, 50 mg three times daily) for more than two years because of chronic low back pain caused by intervertebral disc herniation. General malaise, poor appetite, intermittent postprandial nausea, urinary urgency and frequency were present one month before admission. She visited local clinics and received no specific diagnosis. Due to progressive shortness of breath and drowsiness for one week, she was sent to a medical center. She appeared acutely ill but afebrile, with a blood pressure of 74/34 mmHg, a pulse rate of 86/min, a respiratory rate of 22/min, and an oxygen saturation level of 93% in ambient air. Physical examination revealed no specific local findings. A blood assay revealed the following findings: leukocyte count, 44,400/μL with 85% neutrophils; hemoglobin, 12.3 g/dL; platelet count, 466, 000/μL; C-reactive protein, 235.76 mg/L; total bilirubin, 1.17 mg/dL; direct bilirubin, 0.17 mg/dL; aspartate aminotransferase, 38 IU/L; alanine aminotransferase, 21 IU/L; blood urea nitrogen, 96.77 mg/dL; creatinine, 7.8 mg/dL; sodium, 140 mmol/L; potassium, 8.6 mmol/L; and lactate, 20.3 mmol/L. The arterial blood gas analysis revealed severe metabolic acidosis (pH, 7.02; HCO3, 7.1 mmol/L). A chest radiograph revealed no pulmonary lesions. Urine analysis via catheterization revealed only mild hematuria.
She was admitted to the intensive care unit due to septic shock, acute respiratory failure, acute renal failure, and hyperkalemia. She received mechanical ventilation, intravenous vasopressors, continuous veno-venous hemofiltration, and cefmetazole (2 g/day) was prescribed empirically. All blood cultures yielded K. pneumoniae. The urine culture was sterile. Contrast-enhanced abdominal computed tomography (CT) revealed multiple abscesses in the spleen and bilateral kidneys, with the largest lesion (approximately 5.2 cm in diameter) located in the right kidney (Figure ). In addition, CT also revealed segmental bowel wall edema in the submucosal layer over the terminal ileum, cecum, ascending colon, and descending colon with perifocal fat stranding. The hyperemic change was compatible with the inflammatory reaction in the bowel. The CT also revealed more intense wall enhancement at the terminal ileum (Figure ). A colonoscopy revealed diffuse ulceration with exudation in the distal terminal ileum and segmental ulceration in the colon, with a reduction in the lumen caliber in the sigmoid colon (Figure ). The pathological analysis of colon biopsy specimens revealed ulcers, necrotic debris, and the infiltration of lymphocytes into the edematous lamina propria (Figure ). Three weeks later, a follow-up abdominal ultrasonography demonstrated partial resolution of the renal and splenic abscesses.
The patient was treated in the outpatient department with 500 mg of oral cephradine, four times daily; 500 mg of metronidazole, three times daily; and balsalazide (2.25 g daily) for two months. The follow-up abdominal CT scan revealed complete resolution of the abscesses two months later, and the colonoscopy revealed multiple segmental longitudinal ulceration with skip lesions in the rectum, sigmoid, and terminal ileum. A colon biopsy showed necrotizing inflammation with the infiltration of mixed inflammatory cells ,including neutrophils, histiocytes, and lymphocytes. Moreover, focal ulcerated colonic mucosa with inflammatory infiltration was found in the edematous, fibrous lamina propria. Some distorted glands were observed, but no crypt abscesses were noted. Balsalazide was discontinued after a course of four months. She had an uneventful course in the out-patient department, and a follow-up colonoscopy two years later revealed melanosis coli and internal hemorrhoid only, without ulcers or stenosis over the entire colon.

args: A 59-year-old male arrived at the hospital complaining of worsening shortness of breath, lightheadedness, and a decline in his usual alertness over the past 48 hours. On formal assessment, his Glasgow Coma Score was 12. He was observed breathing spontaneously upon arrival, with no advanced mechanical assistance in place at that time. Arterial blood gas analysis showed an oxygen partial pressure of 82 mm Hg. He was receiving an inspired oxygen fraction of 32 % (FiO₂ 0.32). His blood pressure readings were notable at 90  mm Hg systolic and 50  mm Hg diastolic, reflecting a pattern of persistently low arterial pressure, accompanied by cool extremities and delayed capillary refill. Clinicians noticed a diminished pulse pressure that further supported the suggestion of compromised cardiovascular perfusion. To maintain adequate circulation, a continuous infusion of norEPINEPHrine was delivered at a rate of 0.05 mcg/kg/min, while other intravenous agents often employed for circulatory support were not administered at this stage. Notably, his heart rate was elevated, consistent with a compensatory tachycardia in response to diminished blood pressure. His respiratory examination revealed symmetric chest movements with moderate accessory muscle use, while auscultation did not uncover significant adventitious sounds such as pronounced rales or wheezing, though mild tachypnea was recognized. The patient did not describe a history of chronic pulmonary conditions, yet he reported recent onset of increasing dyspnea even with minimal exertion at home. In terms of his laboratory results, measured shortly after presentation, his platelet count was 190,000 per microliter, indicating an overall stable hematologic profile without immediate risk of severe thrombocytopenia or significant bleeding. Liver-related parameters showed a total bilirubin of 1.2 mg/dL, lying at the upper boundary of normal for some reference ranges, prompting attention to any possible hepatic impact but not suggesting major hyperbilirubinemia at that point. Renal function testing revealed a serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL, slightly above his reported baseline, suggesting mild to moderate deterioration in kidney performance. This finding was further supported by a record of approximately 800 mL of urine output in the last 24 hours, an amount that was lower than his typical daily volume, raising concern about reduced renal clearance and potential prerenal azotemia or an acute kidney insult. The medical staff noted that he had not undergone any recent procedures requiring nephrotoxic contrast, nor had he taken non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory medications in the previous week. Additional evaluation pointed toward possible triggers for his presentation, including a history of inadequate fluid intake over the previous days and a possible subacute infection, although no explicit source of infection was immediately identified on initial imaging or clinical inspection. Review of systems indicated mild gastrointestinal discomfort, but no sustained episodes of vomiting or diarrhea. His skin examination showed slightly cool extremities and mild mottling on the lower legs, though no overt lesions or rashes were observed. Historically, the patient had experienced borderline blood pressure readings, but he had not been on long‑term antihypertensive therapy. No prior admissions for chronic cardiac or respiratory illnesses were documented. He expressed intermittent chest tightness, though routine cardiac enzymes were within normal limits. Clinical suspicion remained high for a systemic inflammatory etiology, or less likely, an evolving obstructive process that was not clinically overt. Ongoing diagnostic work included a review of abdominal and chest imaging, alongside bacteriologic cultures, to target any hidden source of infection or inflammation. He denied recent travel or exposure to unusual pathogens but reported working in an indoor environment where he had occasional contact with individuals who had flu‑like illnesses. The care team planned to reassess his intravascular volume status carefully and consider fluid resuscitation measures if indicated. Consultation with nephrology was requested given his reduced urine output and slight creatinine elevation, though dialysis was not immediately contemplated in the absence of other indications. Neurologic checks were arranged at regular intervals to track any changes in his level of responsiveness or orientation. The primary concern was to identify and address the root cause of his hypotension, mild respiratory insufficiency, and renal impairment without further compromise to organ systems. As he remained in the evaluation area, the team monitored his oxygen saturation, blood pressure, urine output, and mental status closely, with plans to proceed to a higher‑acuity inpatient setting for vigilant observation and treatment once preliminary investigations were concluded.

args:
A 78-year-old man was referred to the Department of Cardiology of Matsushita Memorial Hospital for dyspnea. The patient had been stable at a nursing home until approximately 18 hours before presentation, when the staff noticed that his appetite decreased during dinner. The next morning, the patient reported difficulty breathing and exhibited pallor.
His medical history included heart failure, cerebral infarction, cognitive impairment, chronic kidney disease, and hypertension. Medications included carvedilol at a dose of 2.5 mg daily, furosemide at a dose of 40 mg daily, isosorbide dinitrate at a dose of 40 mg daily, and laxatives as needed. He quit smoking 10 years earlier after a 48-pack-year history, did not drink or use illicit drugs, and had no known allergies.
On examination, he was lethargic and drowsy, but responsive to simple commands. His blood pressure was 144/87 mmHg, pulse was 108 beats per minute, body temperature was 36.7 ºC, and respiratory rate was 28 breaths per minute; his oxygen saturation was 79% while breathing ambient air and 94% while receiving oxygen through a face mask with a reservoir bag at a rate of 10 liters per minute. The jugular venous pulse was high at 10 cm of water. Chest auscultation revealed a gallop rhythm and bibasilar lung crackles. The abdomen was soft and nondistended, and there was no edema of the legs.
An electrocardiogram demonstrated a normal sinus rhythm, right bundle branch block, small q waves in the inferior leads, slight ST-elevation in aVR, and ST-segment depressions in leads V3 to V6 (). Bilateral opacities in the lower lung fields were observed on an anteroposterior chest radiograph (), and diffusely decreased wall motion in the left ventricle was noted on bedside echocardiography. Arterial blood gases, obtained with supplemental oxygen, revealed a pH of 7.256, partial pressure of carbon dioxide of 29.5 mmHg, partial pressure of oxygen of 93.8 mmHg, and lactate of 121 mg/dL. His white blood cell count was 13,400 per cubic millimeter with 88.2% neutrophils. The levels of lactate dehydrogenase, total bilirubin, and creatinine were 687 U/L, 2.4 mg/dL, and 2.98 mg/dL, respectively. The levels of creatine kinase and its MB fraction were 1072 U/L and 146 U/L, respectively. The troponin T level was 3.800 ng/mL (reference value, ≤0.100) and brain natriuretic peptide level was 4256.4 pg/mL (reference value, ≤18.4).
A diagnosis of non-ST-segment elevation acute myocardial infarction accompanying heart failure was made. Emergency coronary angiography demonstrated total occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery and left circumflex coronary artery with collateral flow from the left anterior descending coronary artery, in which severe stenoses were present at the proximal site and the first branch (). Given his comorbidities and preferences, subsequent angioplasty was waived.
Myocardial imaging with thallium-201 (111 MBq) and 99mTc-PYP (740 MBq) was performed four days after admission. A total of 36 images over a 180-degree anterior arc were acquired 5 minutes and 3 hours after tracer injections, respectively, using a digital gamma camera equipped with a low-energy, high-resolution, parallel-hole collimator. The acquisition lasted 50 beats per projection, was stored in a matrix of 64×64 pixels, and the images were reconstructed using a Hanning filter without attenuation or scatter correction. Single-photon emission computed tomography () revealed slightly reduced thallium-201 uptake in the inferior wall and apex, and extensive uptake of 99mTc-PYP in the entire left ventricle and the free wall of the right ventricle. Of note, the uptake of 99mTc-PYP in the left ventricle was distributed to the inner layer with some overlap with that of thallium-201, a finding consistent with myocardial infarction and/or injury in the left ventricular subendocardial regions.

args: A 72-year-old male presents for a planned operation to address advanced degenerative changes in his left knee that have substantially limited his mobility for several weeks. Over the past two months, he has experienced progressively worsening joint pain along with intermittent swelling and stiffness, prompting imaging studies that confirmed significant arthritic deterioration. The patient has remained largely confined to bed due to his decreased ability to ambulate, and he notes that any attempt at prolonged standing or short-distance walking causes marked discomfort. He reports undergoing a substantial surgical intervention approximately three weeks earlier for an unrelated abdominal issue, from which he recovered adequately without major complications. He states that in distant years past, he was diagnosed by imaging with a venous clot in his leg that required medication to address the issue at the time, and he remains aware of the risk of future episodes. Despite that past event, he does not recall ever having any severe chest symptoms that might suggest other complications, and he denies any familial patterns of abnormal blood clotting. His respiratory history is notable for a diagnosis of chronic lung disease requiring occasional inhaler use, and he admits to a past history of smoking, although he claims to have reduced this considerably in recent years. He describes episodes of mild shortness of breath on exertion, but he has not noticed any recent infectious symptoms such as fever or productive cough, nor does he relate any fluid retention or significant swelling in his lower extremities beyond what he attributes to pain and limited movement. He does not complain of chest pain or palpitations suggestive of cardiac failure, and there is no mention of new neurologic concerns such as sudden weakness or speech disturbance that might point toward an acute brain insult. There is likewise no history of bone injuries to the hip or leg in recent weeks, and he denies suffering any major traumas. On review of systems, he expresses no difficulty with swallowing or gastrointestinal bleeding, and he states that he has never been told of any impaired platelet function or dangerous reaction to certain medications that thin the blood. He is uncertain about distant family health disorders but knows of no major heritable predisposition to clotting among his close relatives. Physical examination reveals a man in no acute respiratory distress, though he becomes mildly winded when attempting to shift position on the exam table. His left knee demonstrates decreased range of motion with crepitus, and he exhibits discomfort upon passive flexion and extension. His vital signs are largely stable, with a slight elevation in respiratory rate at rest. There is no visible varicosity on either leg, and peripheral pulses are palpable and symmetric. He shows no sign of an indwelling vascular device, and no current external support or immobilizing device is in place. He appears alert and oriented, with intact cognitive function, though he is notably fatigued from resting in bed for most of the day. Laboratory results obtained prior to surgery reveal no abnormality in basic hematologic or metabolic profiles. He denies any personal history of malignancy or prior chemotherapy, and his last imaging did not suggest any new suspicious masses. Accordingly, he awaits the scheduled procedure to replace the damaged portion of his joint, with the expectation of improving his quality of life and restoring a degree of independence in ambulation.

calculator_name: HEART Score for Major Cardiac Events

args:
A 49-year-old woman without significant cardiovascular risk factors was transferred to our hospital with a 1-hour history of sudden-onset chest pain. She had taken no medication and experienced no physical or emotional stress preceding the chest pain. On arrival, physical examination revealed stable vital signs with no abnormal findings. Electrocardiography revealed no clear findings of ischemic ST-T changes or prior Q-wave myocardial infarction, but laboratory testing showed elevated cardiac enzymes such as troponin T (0.855 ng/mL) as well as creatine phosphokinase (565 U/L) and its MB isozyme (66 U/L). Bedside transthoracic echocardiography revealed impaired contractility in the focal apical segment of the left ventricle, but there was no clear finding of basal hypercontractility or an apical ballooning sign suggestive of stress-induced cardiomyopathy. Overall, the estimated ejection fraction of the left ventricle was broadly preserved (60%). The patient was diagnosed with non-ST-elevation acute myocardial infarction. Because her condition was stable without chest pain on arrival, we initially administered conservative medical therapy including aspirin, prasugrel, nicorandil, perindopril, pitavastatin, and bisoprolol.
Three days later, we electively performed coronary angiography via the right radial artery using a 6 Fr sheath to determine the etiology of the myocardial injury. Coronary angiography showed no significant atherosclerotic stenosis in the right coronary artery and LCX, but a dissection was found from the midportion of the LMT to the proximal site of the LAD, suggestive of type 1 SCAD (, Video) []. Because the coronary blood flow of the LAD was impaired from thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI) flow grade III to I after contrast injection () and the patient had chest pain with ST elevation, we decided to perform urgent PCI. The first wire (SION Blue; Asahi Intecc Co., Ltd., Nagoya, Japan) was initially introduced into the distal LAD, but IVUS imaging revealed that the guide wire had passed through the true lumen of the left coronary artery ostium, false lumen at the ostium of the LCX, and true lumen of the distal LAD (true-false-true) (). In addition, the true lumen of the proximal LAD was compressed by the false lumen (), and no significant atherosclerosis was observed within the vessel on IVUS. We then inserted the second guide wire (SION Blue; Asahi Intecc Co., Ltd.) into the LCX (), and IVUS imaging confirmed that it had been placed in the true lumen from the LMT to LCX (). Next, we inserted the third guide wire (SION Blue; Asahi Intecc Co., Ltd.) from the true lumen of the LCX to the compressed true lumen of the LAD using a double-lumen catheter (Crusade Type R; Kaneka Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) by referring to the IVUS findings of the dissected segment (Video). We initially inserted the third guide wire into the true lumen of the obtuse marginal branch using a double-lumen catheter that was placed in the true lumen of the LCX (). We then pulled the wire back and controlled it toward the true lumen of the LAD, which was located under the first guide wire from the right anterior oblique and caudal view (Figures and ). The IVUS-guided rewiring technique was successful, and the subsequent IVUS imaging confirmed that the third guide wire had passed entirely through the true lumen from the LMT to LAD (, Video). After the successful rewiring from the true lumen of the LMT to distal LAD, we removed the first wire () and deployed a drug-eluting stent (Resolute Onyx, 3.5 × 30 mm; Medtronic plc, Fridley, MN, USA) from the LMT to LAD (Figures , , and ). Because the blood flow of the LCX was preserved after stenting, we did not perform rewiring of the side branch and final kissing balloon inflation. Final coronary angiography showed acceptable results and TIMI grade III flow in both the LAD and LCX ().
The postprocedural course was uneventful, and no major adverse cardiovascular events were observed. The peak levels of creatine phosphokinase and its MB isozyme were 1155 and 142 U/L, respectively. Follow-up coronary computed tomography (CT) angiography was performed 12 months after the procedure and showed acceptable results with no clear findings of in-stent restenosis or incomplete stent apposition (Figures and ). The patients' clinical course was uneventful during an 18-month follow-up.

args: A 72-year-old male with leukemia was brought to the hospital for urgent evaluation after experiencing several days of progressive fatigue, mild shortness of breath, and lower abdominal discomfort. He appeared mildly flushed on arrival, with a body temperature of 37.5°C and a respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute. Due to the concerning nature of his presentation, he was rapidly admitted to the intensive care unit and underwent prompt assessment. His initial blood pressure measurements showed systolic readings at 120 mm Hg and diastolic readings around 64 mm Hg. Further examination revealed that his heartbeat was regular, averaging approximately 88 beats per minute. He denied any recent episodes of confusion or altered sensorium, and his neurological evaluation was consistent with a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 that reflected full alertness. His arterial blood gas analysis indicated a pH of 7.42, suggesting no major acid-base disturbance at this time, although there remained a heightened concern for potential clinical changes given his risk profile. The fraction of inspired oxygen he required on admission was around 45%, and his measured partial pressure of oxygen was approximately 72 mm Hg, reported in the context of his ongoing respiratory complaints. Laboratory studies performed shortly after arrival showed a sodium concentration of 136 mEq/L and a potassium concentration of 4.3 mEq/L, which were within acceptable limits. However, his serum creatinine level was elevated at 1.8 mg/dL, necessitating careful monitoring of his renal function. Despite this elevated level, there was no immediate concern for acute kidney injury on top of this finding, and he did not manifest signs of significant fluid overload or severe acid-base derangements. His hematocrit was measured at 34%, which was slightly reduced but not acutely critical, and his white blood cell count reached around 12,800 per cubic millimeter, raising the possibility of an infectious or inflammatory process given his background and presenting symptoms. He maintained full orientation according to clinical observation, with preserved verbal and motor responses consistent with a normal level of alertness. Owing to the acuity of his presentation, he underwent an emergent exploratory procedure for a suspected intra-abdominal source of infection or complication, and he was closely observed in the postoperative and critical care setting because of his predisposition to severe infections. Prior medical history included issues that compromise his overall immune defense, so heightened surveillance and aggressive prophylactic measures were implemented. Throughout the initial hours after intervention, he received supportive care while monitoring his respiratory parameters and hemodynamic stability. Additional imaging was obtained to clarify the etiology of his symptoms, and cultures were taken to identify any possible pathogens. Discussion among the care team emphasized vigilance for potential complications, including careful fluid balance and reassessment of his renal parameters. As the hours progressed, he remained hemodynamically stable with no new signs of deteriorating consciousness, and his temperature remained near baseline without further fever spikes. Plans for ongoing management included tailored antimicrobial therapy once specific organisms were identified, potential adjustment of his oxygen requirements if his lung function changed, and continued evaluation of his creatinine level to ensure there was no worsening of renal status. args: A 60-year-old male was brought to the emergency department after a concerned neighbor found him lying on his living room couch, appearing lethargic and largely unresponsive. The neighbor reported that he had not been seen outside of his apartment for several days, and upon checking in, discovered that he was breathing but not reacting in a typical manner. The neighbor alerted emergency services, and upon their arrival, the patient had his eyelids closed and did not rouse spontaneously; however, after they repeatedly called his name in a raised tone, he opened his eyes briefly and appeared to track the source of the voice for a moment before drifting off again. During transit, he was unable to provide coherent words or phrases, instead emitting only indistinct vocalizations, as though attempting to form words but never managing to articulate them in a way that could be understood by paramedics. On entering the emergency department, he was placed on a stretcher and appeared restless yet minimally responsive, occasionally shifting as though uncomfortable but not immediately engaging with staff who were speaking to him in calm, measured voices. Preliminary vital signs showed a moderately elevated heart rate and a blood pressure that was near the higher end of the normal spectrum, while his oxygen saturation on room air remained borderline and warranted closer observation. A cursory check of his personal documents revealed that he had some history of chronic medical issues, including what appeared to be a record of glucose intolerance that might place him at risk for metabolic imbalances, although no overt medication list was on hand to confirm current treatments. Further inspection uncovered no obvious signs of acute trauma, such as scalp lacerations or large bruises on the head or neck, but there was mild swelling above his right eyebrow, which could be an older contusion or a subtle injury not immediately apparent on initial inspection. Neurological evaluation in the emergency bay revealed that he did not spontaneously look around his environment; he generally kept his eyes closed unless a team member loudly addressed him multiple times or gently touched his shoulder in an effort to prompt a reaction. When he did open his eyes in these instances, he seemed to fix his gaze momentarily before allowing his eyelids to fall shut again if the stimulation ceased. Persistent attempts at verbal communication yielded only disjointed sounds that could not be understood, and while there was a sense that he was trying to respond, the utterances became more disorganized if he was pressed for clarity or provided with yes-or-no questions. As tactile assessments were performed, he displayed discernible movement away from weight-bearing pressure or gentle pinches meant to gauge his response to unpleasant sensations, suggesting that he did have some preservation of motor reactivity despite his otherwise subdued presentation. He did not appear to follow specific instructions to raise or lower his limbs voluntarily, and he did not consistently shift position unless startled or probed by painful stimuli. Examination of his cardiovascular and pulmonary status showed no emergent abnormalities beyond the slightly elevated pulse; breath sounds were present bilaterally with no rales or obvious wheezes, and his respiratory rate was steady but on the slower side of normal. Labs were ordered promptly to screen for measures of kidney function, blood sugar levels, and potential toxic substances in his system, while imaging of his head was scheduled to look for any intracranial cause that might explain his disorientation and inability to produce recognizable language. There were no obvious external injuries other than the mild brow swelling and a few superficial abrasions on his forearms, possibly from rubbing against furniture or a nearby object. Staff at the scene had not administered sedatives or analgesics apart from ensuring his airway remained clear and stable, so chemical sedation was not considered a factor in his abbreviated speech and fleeting attentiveness. By the end of this initial assessment, he remained largely recumbent on the stretcher, opening his eyes only when staff raised their voices to invoke a response and voicing unclear sounds that did not form appropriate words, all while reflexively pulling away from any physical provocation that he appeared to find unpleasant. Further diagnostic steps were planned imminently, with a focus on identifying any reversible metabolic derangements or underlying central nervous system disorders that might be contributing to his compromised level of alertness.

args: A 70-year-old woman came to the electrophysiology follow-up clinic at her referring internist’s request to re-evaluate long-term oral anticoagulation in the setting of nonvalvular atrial fibrillation that had first been identified on a routine pre-operative ECG sixteen months ago. Since that time she has been taking warfarin, but she relates considerable difficulty maintaining a steady anticoagulation intensity: during the past half-year her home INR log supplied by the anticoagulation service shows values swinging from 1.6 to 4.1 despite dose adjustments, and she recalls two telephone calls from the clinic instructing her to hold doses and repeat testing because the value was above the therapeutic window. She denies ever needing reversal agents, hospital admission, transfusion, endoscopy, or surgical intervention for hemorrhage, but she does remember a prolonged gingival ooze after a dental cleaning three months earlier and a self-limited superficial epistaxis episode after using a home humidifier in winter. She has never experienced black or bloody stools, hematemesis, hematuria, or bruising large enough to raise concern. Her medical history is otherwise notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 500 mg twice daily, dyslipidemia treated with atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, postmenopausal osteoporosis for which she takes weekly alendronate, seasonal allergic rhinitis controlled with nasal corticosteroid spray, and remote nicotine dependence; she smoked one pack per day for twenty years but quit fifteen years ago. She has never been told she has high blood pressure, and serial printouts from her community pharmacy’s automated cuff over the past year show systolic readings consistently in the 110s-120s mm Hg range, with today’s seated manual measurement 123/74 mm Hg and a heart rate of 78 beats per minute that is irregularly irregular. She denies transient ischemic attack, ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, carotid disease, or peripheral embolic events. She has no personal history of chronic viral hepatitis, autoimmune hepatitis, alcohol-related liver injury, or jaundice; a comprehensive metabolic panel drawn last week for her primary care visit documented AST 24 U/L, ALT 26 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, and albumin 4.2 g/dL. Kidney function has been unremarkable, with a stable creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL and estimated glomerular filtration rate of 68 mL/min/1.73 m²; she has never had nephrolithiasis, proteinuric disorders, or dialytic therapy. She is not taking antiplatelet agents, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, or herbal supplements known to increase bleeding tendency; for episodic knee discomfort from old athletic injuries she uses acetaminophen 650 mg as needed, typically twice a week. She denies a family history of hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, aneurysms, or premature sudden death. Review of systems today is negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, exertional intolerance, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, abdominal pain, early satiety, pruritus, overt ascites, confusion, or peripheral edema. She remains physically active, walking her dog two miles most mornings and participating in low-impact aerobics at the community center. Socially she lives with her spouse and enjoys a nightly glass or two of white wine with dinner, plus a couple of champagne mimosas during Sunday brunch, amounting to roughly ten alcoholic drinks each week; she does not use recreational drugs and only drinks coffee otherwise. On examination she is well appearing with a body mass index of 26 kg/m², afebrile, and breathing comfortably. Cardiac auscultation reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without organomegaly or palpable masses. There is no scleral icterus, telangiectasia, spider nevi, palmar erythema, or digital clubbing. Extremities show no edema, petechiae, ecchymoses, or palpable purpura, and peripheral pulses are full and symmetric. Neurologic screening exam demonstrates intact cranial nerves, normal motor strength and tone, and no cerebellar signs. Laboratory tests drawn immediately before the visit reveal hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40%, platelets 232 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.1 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, and INR 1.8. A twelve-lead electrocardiogram obtained in the office shows atrial fibrillation with an average ventricular rate of 76/min, normal QRS axis and duration, and no acute ST-T abnormalities. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed three months earlier indicated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%, normal right ventricular size and function, mild left atrial enlargement, trivial mitral regurgitation, and no intracardiac thrombus. She expresses interest in learning whether continuing warfarin, switching to a direct oral anticoagulant, or considering rhythm control strategies would best balance her desire to avoid both stroke and future bleeding episodes as she plans international travel later this year.

args: A 61-year-old male arrived in the intensive care setting with reports from those close to him that he had been unusually fatigued, breathing heavily, and showing signs of worsening confusion for roughly 24 hours leading up to admission. He has long been known to have a compromised internal defense mechanism, which places him at risk for more significant complications when even minor health issues arise. On examination at the time of arrival, his body temperature registered at 99.8 degrees Fahrenheit, a little under triple digits in Fahrenheit, indicating a mild elevation rather than a high-grade spike. He appeared quite tachypneic, taking 30 breaths per minute, and his heart rate was also elevated at 115 beats per minute, suggesting his body was under considerable stress. His blood pressure readings showed a systolic measurement of 121 mm Hg and a diastolic of 75 mm Hg. Initial laboratory tests indicated that his serum sodium was 131 mEq/L, on the lower end of normal for most healthy adults, while his serum potassium was 5.2 mEq/L, slightly above what many would consider the upper threshold of normal. Of even greater concern was his significantly elevated measurement reflecting kidney filtration efficiency, with a creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL, which came back much higher than hoped, confirming that he has both a baseline impairment in excretory function and a sudden worsening of that deficit at present. This was consistent with his clinical story of having had trouble maintaining stable fluid and electrolyte levels over the years, periodically requiring close monitoring. Another lab finding showed that his white blood cell count was 9,500/mm^3, falling within a reasonable range, although it did not exclude an underlying infectious or inflammatory process. His GCS score was 12. He also displayed some difficulty maintaining appropriate gas exchange, requiring a moderate level of supplemental oxygen at an FiO2 of 46% to keep his oxygen saturation acceptable. Hematocrit was 30%. Laboratory analysis of arterial blood gases revealed that his partial pressure of oxygen was 74 mm Hg despite this intermediate boost, indicating only partial success in gas transfer. Further details in the same evaluation revealed a distinct acidity in his blood chemistry, with his pH measuring 7.28, well below the midpoint of normal, which could reflect the strain his body was under in trying to eliminate waste products and maintain equilibrium. Although he has a longstanding vulnerability in more than one vital organ system, there is no plan at this moment for an invasive procedure, as current findings do not point to a lesion or process that would benefit from surgical intervention. Clinicians have opted to support him medically, investigate potential triggers for his deteriorating condition, and keep him under intensive observation to ensure that if his blood pressure dips any lower or his breathing worsens, immediate therapeutic actions can be taken. It is of particular concern that his ability to fight off infection or other stressors is reduced, so aggressive monitoring of potential sources of sepsis or other complications is underway. Importantly, no active measures had been initiated before these data were acquired, ensuring that the temperature, cardiovascular parameters, respiratory rates, and laboratory findings accurately reflect his true baseline upon entering the unit. In view of his compromised ability to handle metabolic byproducts, his acid-base disturbance, and his need for moderated oxygen support, his care team is focused on staving off further organ injury and preventing any precipitous drops in alertness while formulating a more definitive diagnosis for his current downturn.
