args: A 58-year-old woman came to the pre-operative medicine clinic this afternoon for assessment before a scheduled open pancreaticoduodenectomy planned in four days for a 2.8-cm peri-ampullary mass that was discovered on surveillance imaging for hereditary pancreatitis; the operation will be performed through an upper midline incision with anticipated operative time of 6–8 hours and possible need for portal vein reconstruction. She reports intermittent epigastric discomfort, early satiety, and a 6-pound unintentional weight loss over the past three months but denies any change in exercise tolerance, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling, or exertional chest tightness. Walking her two dogs for a mile each morning and climbing three flights of apartment stairs carrying groceries do not provoke shortness of breath or pressure across the sternum. She has long-standing type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 36 and currently administers 24 units of insulin glargine each night with supplemental lispro based on pre-meal capillary readings; the most recent self-monitor log brought to clinic shows fasting glucose between 95 and 145 mg/dL. Oral metformin 500 mg twice daily and empagliflozin were discontinued last week at the surgeon’s request, but she continues liraglutide 0.6 mg subcutaneously each morning for weight control. Hypertension treated with losartan 50 mg and amlodipine 5 mg daily, mild persistent asthma for which she uses a budesonide-formoterol inhaler twice daily, and gastroesophageal reflux managed with pantoprazole complete her active problem list. She has never been told she had a heart attack, underwent stress testing, or received any coronary stents, and she has no history of stroke, transient neurologic events, carotid surgery, or carotid bruit on previous examinations. Obstetric history is remote and uncomplicated. She quit cigarette smoking 12 years ago after accumulating a 15-pack-year exposure and now drinks red wine only on weekends, usually two glasses. There is a family history of pancreatic malignancy in a brother at 52 and insulin-requiring diabetes in a mother who died in her 80s; no premature coronary disease or cerebrovascular accidents are reported in first-degree relatives. Current vital signs obtained after five minutes seated rest are blood pressure 128/70 mm Hg in the right arm, pulse 68 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, afebrile at 36.7 °C, oxygen saturation 98% breathing ambient air, and body mass index 29 kg/m². On examination she is alert, comfortable, and speaking full sentences. Neck veins are not distended at 45°; carotid upstrokes are brisk and without bruit. Cardiac auscultation reveals a normal S1 and S2, no extra sounds, and no murmurs. The point of maximal impulse is nondisplaced, and peripheral pulses are 2+ bilaterally with intact capillary refill. Lung fields are clear to percussion and auscultation without wheezes or crackles, and there is no dullness or egophony. The abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the epigastrium but no guarding, rebound, or ascites; the liver edge is palpable 1 cm below the costal margin, and a faint midline scar from a prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy is noted. No hepatosplenomegaly or abdominal bruits are present. There is no ankle edema, calf tenderness, or skin discoloration. Neurologic screening finds cranial nerves II–XII intact, symmetrical strength 5/5 in all extremities, normal sensation to light touch, and a steady tandem gait. Pre-operative laboratory results drawn immediately before the visit show hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, hematocrit 38%, white blood cell count 7.4 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 230 × 10⁹/L, sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 16 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL, consistent with measurements over the prior year; estimated glomerular filtration rate is 82 mL/min/1.73 m². Aspartate aminotransferase is 34 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 29 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, albumin 3.9 g/dL, fasting glucose 118 mg/dL, and HbA1c measured last month was 7.0%. A baseline electrocardiogram performed today demonstrates normal sinus rhythm at 66 beats per minute, normal intervals, and no ST-T wave abnormalities. Chest radiograph shows clear lung fields and a normal mediastinal silhouette. The anesthesia service has evaluated her airway as Mallampati class II with a thyromental distance of 6.5 cm and full neck extension. She has been counseled to continue her nighttime basal insulin at a 75% dose the evening before surgery, with morning rapid-acting doses omitted and intra-operative glucose managed on protocol. Antihypertensive therapy will be continued except for the angiotensin receptor blocker, which she will hold on the morning of the procedure. Pulmonary function has been optimized with strict adherence to controller inhaler therapy and daily incentive spirometry practice. After a comprehensive discussion covering surgical details, potential risks—including bleeding, pancreatic leak, delayed gastric emptying, pneumonia, and cardiovascular complications—post-operative disposition to the surgical intensive care unit for at least 24 hours, and expected recovery timeline, the patient expressed understanding and agreement to proceed; written informed consent has been documented.

args: A 66-year-old female presented to the emergency department with increasing fatigue, mild shortness of breath, and discoloration of the sclera. She reported a few days of feeling generally unwell, with no clear precipitating event such as trauma or infection. According to her family, she has been complaining of mild right-sided discomfort beneath her rib cage for the past week and has had decreased appetite. Past medical history includes longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and mild osteoarthritis. On arrival, she was alert and oriented, speaking coherently without any obvious signs of confusion or disorientation. Her mental status was thoroughly evaluated, and she was observed to be awake, responsive to questions, following commands appropriately, giving a GCS of 15. Further clinical examination revealed that she had stable hemodynamics with a blood pressure reading of 105 mm Hg systolic and 60 mm Hg diastolic, which did not necessitate the use of any intravenous medications to support her circulation. She was afebrile and her heart rate was regular without arrhythmias. Although her breathing was evidently more labored than usual, she was still able to converse without significant difficulty. However, arterial blood gas analysis revealed an initial partial pressure of oxygen at around 95 mm Hg while she was receiving a moderate fraction of inspired oxygen, which stood at 36%, suggesting the need for closer monitoring of her respiratory status. She was placed on continuous positive airwary pressure. Additional blood tests revealed slightly elevated bilirubin at 2.0 mg/dL, potentially indicative of some compromise in hepatic processing, though she did not have a known history of chronic liver disease. Her platelet count was noted to be 210,000 per microliter, which fell within acceptable limits for most standard reference ranges. Although she sustained her blood pressure without chemical support, close monitoring was advised due to her history of hypertension. Her kidney function tests showed a creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL, which raised concerns regarding a possible acute on chronic kidney issue, especially as her daily urine output was measured at around 400 mL, signifying reduced excretory capacity. She reported no recent changes in her medications that could have acutely influenced her kidney function, but further evaluation with renal ultrasound and investigations into her diabetic history were planned to elucidate the etiology of her elevated creatinine and limited urine output. Despite her complaints of fatigue, she denied chest pain or any severe discomfort beyond mild abdominal fullness on the right side beneath the rib cage. Her oxygen saturation was maintained at an acceptable level, and her mental clarity underscored the absence of acute intracranial pathology. She reported no history of major recent illness, no recent hospital admissions, and no known exposure to hepatotoxic substances, although she did admit to occasionally using over-the-counter pain medications for her joint aches. Her appetite had been mildly decreased, and she attributed her general fatigue to a possibly worsening diabetic control, yet her fingerstick glucose readings were reportedly within a reasonable range according to her home log. At the time of presentation, there was no evidence of severe circulatory compromise. She was not receiving intravenous infusions to support blood pressure or augment cardiac output, as her measured parameters remained relatively stable. In summary, her profile featured moderate respiratory concerns warranting supportive therapy with a device that provides a constant flow of pressure to ease her work of breathing, mildly elevated bilirubin, an acceptable platelet count, alert mental status, a stable blood pressure at the time, no requirement for vasoactive medication, elevated creatinine suggestive of renal impairment, and decreased urine output that required further diagnostic workup to determine the chronicity and possible underlying causes. Despite the complexities, she remained hemodynamically stable upon initial evaluation, and her respiratory status, while strained, responded to noninvasive positive airway assistance.

At baseline, she ambulated with a rollator walker, but the pain was starting to limit her ability to walk. She had a history of low back and posterior left buttock pain, but she denied current pain in these regions. She also denied weakness or pain radiation into her lower extremities.
On examination, she had no pain with lumbar or hip range of motion. She had full, symmetric, and pain-free lower extremity strength, and deep tendon reflexes and sensation to light touch were intact and symmetric in her lower extremities. She had a positive Trendelenburg sign with single-leg stance bilaterally, and she was tender to palpation over the left greater trochanter but not the right. Ober’s, seated slump, and straight leg raise tests were negative. (See and for descriptions of Ober’s and the seated slump test.)
Focused ultrasound performed by a musculoskeletal physiatrist with ultrasound training revealed evidence of bursitis of the left greater trochanter, which correlated with the patient’s point of maximal tenderness.

She was educated on hip abductor strengthening exercises, but she was not interested in performing the exercises regularly. Instead she requested a corticosteroid injection, so an ultrasound-guided greater trochanteric bursa injection was performed by the ultrasound-trained physiatrist.

args: A 48-year-old man arrived at the intensive care unit with complaints of progressive weakness and mild shortness of breath that had been worsening over the past 48 hours. On examination, he appeared fatigued and somewhat confused, with limited ability to follow commands and only partial orientation to the environment. He was breathing on a moderate level of supplemental oxygen (FiO2 at 30%), and initial observation revealed a borderline level of oxygenation, confirmed by arterial blood gas showing a mildly reduced partial pressure of oxygen of 78 mm Hg. His temperature was slightly above the upper limit of normal at 99.2 degrees Fahrenheit, which prompted concerns of a possible inflammatory or infectious trigger for his presentation. Blood pressure readings showed a systolic value of 118 mm Hg and a diastolic of 67 mm Hg. The rhythm of his heart was regular but somewhat elevated in frequency, measured at 94 beats per minute, and his respiratory efforts were checked at 17 breaths per minute, suggesting that he was compensating but not in overt distress. Laboratory evaluation uncovered no evidence of longstanding abnormalities in major body systems other than a sudden rise in a key marker of kidney function, pointing to an acute process distinct from any chronic form of kidney disease. His creatinine level was 1.7 mg/dL, significantly above the normal reference range, which fit with the overall picture of a newly developing but potentially serious problem involving renal function. Providers were also reassured upon seeing his electrolytes, which included a serum sodium of 139 mEq/L and potassium at 4.2 mEq/L, both comfortably within acceptable limits. A hematologic panel showed a hematocrit of 38%, regarded as borderline but not severely low, alongside a white blood cell count of 10,100 per cubic millimeter, mildly suggestive of an underlying stress or mild infection, though not definitively pointing to a marked leukocytic flare. The care team also noted a near-normal blood acidity level upon obtaining arterial blood for analysis, as the pH was 7.36, which did not indicate substantial acidemia or alkalemia. In assessing his neurologic status, he was assessed with a Glasgow Coma Score of 9. Of note, he had never been documented as having any major long-term disorders that damage immune defenses or other critical organ systems, and his family indicated that he rarely required hospital care in the past. The team gave special attention to the new-onset changes in kidney function, recognizing the possibility of an acute insult that might further compromise hemodynamic stability if not managed promptly. His inflammatory markers called for a thorough workup to identify a source of infection if present, and the mild fever heightened the suspicion that an infectious or inflammatory etiology could be playing a role in his acute clinical state. While he did not exhibit overt respiratory failure, the borderline oxygen parameters prompted careful observation and immediate supportive measures. Vital signs, including the marginal elevation in temperature, the pulse rate in the low-to-mid 90s, and the respiratory pattern, all painted a picture of physiologic stress at the time of arrival. His baseline mental status, essential to follow given the concerns of suboptimal alertness, would remain a central focus in the hours ahead. No major procedures had been performed on him before this admission, and his presentation on arrival to the critical care setting reflected the raw clinical state without any therapeutic interventions altering his laboratory or physiologic parameters. The plan involved close monitoring of kidney function, potential infectious sources, and frequent reassessment of his cognitive changes to intervene rapidly if signs of deterioration arose. Clinicians paid particular attention to potential shifts in fluid balance, acid-base equilibrium, and ventilatory status, all of which could influence overall outcomes in similarly acute scenarios. His blood pressure remained stable enough to avoid immediate pharmacologic support, but the team remained vigilant about any subtle signs of decompensation. Given the absence of deeper systemic defects on review of his past records and his relatively stable vital signs outside of the mild aberrations noted, the immediate priorities included ensuring adequate oxygenation, addressing the likely cause of this new renal insult, and clarifying the etiology behind the elevated temperature and altered sensorium. Throughout this process, clinicians were aware that the prompt identification and management of acute factors, along with the prevention of further injury to major organs, were essential to improving his prognosis.

args: A 74-year-old male presents for evaluation in anticipation of a scheduled procedure intended to replace a deteriorating joint in his lower limb, as his orthopedic team has advised that such an operation could significantly improve his pain control and mobility. He reports that, roughly three weeks ago, he underwent a substantial abdominal operation for a separate issue, requiring a brief hospitalization and a structured recovery period. Following discharge from that previous intervention, he notes that his overall condition improved until he experienced an acute neurological episode about two weeks ago, described by him as a sudden onset of left-sided weakness and a transient difficulty with speech. According to his account, those symptoms have since largely resolved, and he has been continuing a mild exercise routine at home to maintain strength in his limbs. He emphasizes that he is now able to walk unassisted around his home and even complete short errands on foot, which reassures him that his baseline function remains close to normal. However, lingering fatigue sometimes slows him, prompting him to proceed cautiously and use rest periods throughout the day. He denies any new significant cardiac or respiratory problems since his recent hospital admissions, remarking that he has not noticed concerning fluid retention, persistent cough, or episodes of feeling breathless that would indicate an emerging cardiopulmonary issue. Over the course of his adult life, he occasionally experienced troublesome gastrointestinal flare-ups for which he was eventually diagnosed with a chronic condition affecting his bowel, and he recounts multiple strategies used in the past to control bouts of abdominal pain and irregular bowel habits. He states that at present, these gastrointestinal symptoms are fairly well controlled, and he has not required emergency evaluations related to that problem for several years. When asked about vascular health and family history, he remarks that his relatives have not reported unusual clotting events, and he himself has never been told that his blood tests showed concerning hereditary factors or autoantibodies implying predisposition to vascular blockages. Additionally, he specifies that no healthcare provider has previously diagnosed him with any irregularities in his veins, such as conspicuous surface-level abnormalities or pockets of fluid accumulation in his legs. He points out that even though he has struggled with knee discomfort for some time, he has never been advised that specialized supportive devices or immobilizing casts were required, and his clinical team has targeted this upcoming procedure as the most definitive way to restore his quality of life. During the interview, the patient underscores that he does not recall any direct trauma to the hip or leg bones recently, nor does he describe any systemic infections in the latest period that might have interfered with his recovery. On the day of evaluation, he appears oriented and cooperative, with stable vital signs and no evident respiratory distress. Physical examination of his lower limbs reveals limited range of motion in the affected joint, although this is not accompanied by swelling or signs of acute inflammation. He appreciates that his ability to move around has fallen short of his usual activity level, yet he still manages his daily household routines without outside assistance. He expresses optimism that once the new joint is in place, his ambulation will improve drastically, allowing him to resume longer walks and potentially engage in light recreational pursuits. While he is aware of the risks associated with an operation of this magnitude, he believes it is necessary given the persistent pain and mechanical difficulties that have compounded over time, particularly after his recent health challenges. Hospital notes confirm that his neurologic recovery is ongoing, and his clinicians plan to monitor him closely in the perioperative stage, especially in regard to ensuring stable circulation and avoiding potential complications. He has already met with nutritional experts to confirm that his diet is suitable for perioperative healing and to address his longstanding gastrointestinal background. As he awaits final approval for the planned joint replacement, the patient maintains a hopeful outlook, believing that careful attention to rehabilitation protocols and compliance with follow-up visits will allow him to emerge from surgery with an enhanced capacity to walk, climb stairs, and handle all the activities of daily life more comfortably.

args:
A 42-year-old female arrived at the emergency department via private vehicle after experiencing an abrupt stop in traffic while driving home from work, causing her to jolt forward and strike her head against the steering wheel. She was wearing a seatbelt at the time, and no other occupants were in the car. On arrival, she was awake, looking around the brightly lit corridor, tracking both staff and visitors without requiring any prompting or stimulation. She appeared calm and displayed no overt signs of disorientation, as she identified herself by name when asked, recounted her typical work schedule earlier that day, and correctly named the city and facility in which she found herself. In explaining her situation, she described how the sudden braking caused her chest to press forcefully against the seatbelt, prompting concern for potential thoracic or neck injury. She denied any loss of consciousness at the scene and stated she felt only momentary dizziness that subsided soon after the incident. When asked by the triage nurse about the current date and circumstances, she articulated her answers clearly and coherently. She summarized the sequence of events that brought her to the hospital, referencing the roads she had taken and the approximate time the abrupt stop occurred. Family members who arrived with her confirmed her baseline health status as generally sound, with no prominent history of hypertension, diabetes, or other chronic conditions. She did note having occasional migraines that she managed with over-the-counter pain medication, although she denied experiencing any severe headache at the moment. At the time of initial examination, she was seen holding her head upright and turning it freely to look at different staff members as they entered and exited the evaluation room. When instructed to reposition her arms or flex her wrists, she complied without hesitation, demonstrating a robust ability to follow spoken commands. She also helped an assistant nurse move her upper body forward so a thorough skin assessment could be conducted, particularly checking for bruising along her shoulders and lower neck from the seatbelt. A cursory neurological check revealed no speech deficits and no focal weakness. Her vital signs were stable, showing a blood pressure near the higher range of normal, a pulse that was slightly elevated but still acceptable considering the stressful event, and respirations that remained deep and regular. Auscultation of her lungs and heart did not disclose any acute abnormality. She reported mild tenderness across the sternum, presumably from the tension of the seatbelt during the sudden stop, but denied any radiating chest pain. Palpation of her neck elicited mild discomfort, though not severe enough to restrict her range of motion or cause significant pain on lateral rotation. She said she had not taken any medication since leaving the car and insisted that, apart from feeling a bit shaken by the incident, she was in stable condition. Imaging studies of her cervical spine and chest were ordered to rule out soft tissue injury or occult fractures. Laboratory panels were also drawn to check for any underlying issues that might complicate discharge planning. Nonetheless, at the time she was first seen, she conversed articulately about her day and displayed consistent capability to adjust her body position, proving responsive to all directions from the clinical team. Observing her level of awareness and her quick, coordinated movements, staff documented her condition as reassuring, even as they remained vigilant for any late-emerging signs of whiplash or other trauma-related complications. While waiting for imaging results, she engaged politely with the attending nurse, who asked her to carefully describe the sensation in her arms and legs; she confirmed no numbness, tingling, or unusual weakness. Throughout these discussions, she showed full clarity of thought and a ready willingness to assist in her own care. No interventions had been initiated at that point, and she had not been given any medication besides a supportive brace for comfort. Her calm demeanor and coherence, as well as her capacity to precisely follow commands, suggested a favorable clinical picture pending confirmation that there were no hidden injuries.

args:
A 67-year-old woman presented to the hospital complaining of fever, shortness of breath and dry cough during the previous month. She had been treated for presumed bronchitis with wide-spectrum antibiotics without response and her complaints had gradually worsened. Her past medical history was significant for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, which was incidentally diagnosed on a routine chest radiograph 8 years previously, a finding that was confirmed, together with right paratracheal node enlargement, by a chest computed tomography (CT) scan. She had then undergone a non-diagnostic bronchoscopy and was advised to repeat the chest CT scan after 6 months, advice she ignored. She had never smoked and she had not taken any medication in the past. She had no environmental or occupational history of beryllium or other metal exposure. She had never traveled outside Greece. She had never had a tuberculin test.
On physical examination, the patient appeared to be in good condition, mildly dyspneic with 22 breaths per minute, a temperature of 39.4°C, blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg and a heart rate of 100 beats per minute. Apart from mild bilateral inspiratory fine crackles in the lower lung fields, no other physical abnormalities were observed. There was no skin involvement. Laboratory investigations showed normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 11.8 g/dl), white blood count 6370/mm3 (neutrophils 67%), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 95 mm and C-reactive protein 90 mg/l (normal value <3 mg/l). Serum electrolyte levels and renal function indices were normal. A mild decrease in albumin levels was observed on serum protein electrophoresis. Serum concentrations of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) were normal, as was a 24-hour urinary calcium excretion analysis. Arterial gas testing (while the patient was breathing in room air) indicated PaO2 70 mmHg, PaCO2 31 mmHg, pH 7.47 and bicarbonate 22.2 mmol/l. The tuberculin skin test was positive (20 mm). A chest X-ray on admission disclosed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy together with bilateral interstitial lung densities in the lower lung fields (Figure ). A chest CT scan that was performed 3 days after her admission disclosed bilateral interstitial opacities in the middle and lower lung lobes and mediastinal lymphadenopathy, which was unaltered compared with that observed 8 years ago (Figure ).
An ophthalmologic evaluation was normal. The patient underwent bronchoscopy, which revealed slight mucosal edema in the bronchial tree. All endobronchial biopsies disclosed non-specific inflammation. Transbronchial biopsies were not performed because of the patient's intolerance. The bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) cell differential count was total cell count 37 × 104/ml, neutrophils 2.8%, lymphocytes 61.2%, macrophages 34.5% and eosinophils 1.3%. The CD4 to CD8 ratio was 2.25 while the number of CD4 and CD8 cells was within normal levels. Multiple blood and urine cultures as well as bronchial washing cultures were negative for bacteria and fungi. Results from sputum, gastric fluid and BAL Ziehl-Neelsen staining were negative. Serology studies for human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), Brucella species, Legionella species, Coxiella burnetti, Chlamydia species, Mycoplasma species, Leismania species and Toxoplasma species were negative. A venereal disease research laboratory test was also negative. Antinuclear antibodies were 1/80 positive while anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies, anti-RO, anti-LA, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies and antimitochondrial antibodies were all negative. The patient underwent mediastinoscopy, and histological examination from the biopsy of a right paratracheal lymph node showed a non-caseating granuloma formation compatible with sarcoidosis. Routine tissue bacterial, fungal and acid bacilli cultures were negative.
The patient received broad-spectrum antibiotic treatment without any response, while her dyspnea progressively increased and she was supported with oxygen therapy. A chest X-ray disclosed extended interstitial lung densities in both lungs as well as consolidation in the left lower lung field (Figure ). As she was clinically and radiologically deteriorating, treatment with the standard anti-TB drug regimen (isoniazid 300 mg daily, rifampin 600 mg daily, pyrazinamide 2 g daily and ethambutol 1 g daily) was started in addition to prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily. The patient's symptoms moderately improved but her fever continued to spike. During the second week of anti-TB therapy, initial BAL cultures turned out to be positive for M. tuberculosis and glucocorticoids were stopped. During the fourth week of treatment the fever declined and the patient became apyrexial 1 week later. After 2 months of therapy, pyrazinamide and ethambutol were stopped and a 7-month continuation regimen with isoniazide and rifampin was administered. During a 9 month period of follow-up, the patient continued to have the same chronic mediastinal lymphadenopathy with no parenchymal involvement and normal pulmonary function tests.

args: A 34-year-old Caucasian female with prior history of hypertension was admitted at the Department of Ophthalmology of our hospital for bilateral red eye. She had no previous ocular trauma or surgery, and no other complaints. Ophthalmological examination showed bilateral diffuse anterior scleritis. Visual acuity was preserved. On admission, blood pressure was of 150/100 mmHg, and heart rate was of 72 beats per minute. Ear, nose and mouth examination was normal, and cardiac, pulmonary, abdominal, neurological and lower limbs examination also revealed no changes. Neither joint tenderness or effusion nor rash, nor peripheral lymphadenopathies were detected. There was no genital ulceration. The laboratory disclosed normal renal function (creatinaemia 1.18 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 40 mg/dL) and normal haemoglobin (12.9 g/dL). Urinalysis featured microscopic haematuria (150 erythrocytes/microlitre) and proteinuria of 3.59 g/day. Serum albumin was 3.4 g/dL, and there was evidence of high total cholesterol level (total cholesterol, 254 mg/dL). Serum protein electrophoresis, serum protein immunoelectrophoresis, hepatic function tests, erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein were normal. Serology for lupus (antinuclear, anti-double strand deoxyribonucleic acid, anti-Smith, extractable nuclear and anti-ribonucleoprotein antibodies) and vasculitis (anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies) as well as the search for cryoglobulins was negative. Rheumatoid factor was negative, and serum C3 and serum C4 were on the normal range. Serology for human immunodeficiency virus types 1 and 2, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis, and Lyme disease was also negative. Chest X-ray was normal. Renal ultrasound revealed normal-sized and normoechoic kidneys, normal differentiation, and no hydronephrosis. A kidney biopsy was performed, and revealed diffuse mesangial hypercellularity () and diffuse granular mesangial deposits of IgA (). According to these, the diagnosis of IgAN was established. She started oral prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day), lisinopril (20 mg/day), losartan (50 mg/day) and simvastatin (20 mg/day). Six months later, she was asymptomatic, and no other episodes of scleritis occurred. Ophthalmological examination was normal. Proteinuria decreased but still persisted in non-nephrotic range (2.3 g/24 h). Renal function still remains normal.

args:
A 41-year-old man was initially evaluated in the emergency department for a 2-month history of shortness of breath, abdominal distension, scrotal swelling, and left inguinal pain. Past history included long-term use of intravenous drugs, extensive and current alcohol use with evidence of cirrhosis based on laboratory findings, and imaging and seropositivity for hepatitis C antibody without measurable viremia. He also had a history of sustaining an upper abdominal stab wound requiring exploratory laparotomy in the early 1990s. He denied fevers, rigors, night sweats, weight loss, or skin rashes.
Initial evaluation revealed an ill appearing man with tachycardia (heart rate 120 beats/min) with mild hypotension (blood pressure 85–105/50–60 mmHg), and tachypnea (respiratory rate 20–25/min) requiring 2 L/min supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula to maintain a blood oxygen saturation >95 %. He had distant heart sounds with tachycardia and regular rhythm without murmurs or gallops. He had jugular venous distension with a positive Kussmaul’s sign and pulsus paradoxus. There was significant abdominal distention present with a fluid wave. He had stigmata of chronic liver disease with spider angiomata noted and collateral venous distension in the abdominal wall. Furthermore, he had scrotal swelling and bilateral lower extremity edema. He was admitted to the hospital’s Medicine Service.
On admission, a chest radiograph revealed enlargement of the cardiac silhouette (Fig. ). A bedside echocardiogram illustrated a large pericardial effusion with evidence of hemodynamic compromise, including right atrial and right ventricular diastolic collapse, a >30 % respiratory variability in the tricuspid and mitral valve flow rates, and a swinging cardiac motion consistent with the presence of cardiac tamponade demanding urgent intervention.
Admission laboratory values revealed a normal white blood cell count, a mild macrocytic anemia (mean corpuscular volume 95 fL) with a hemoglobin of 12.1 g/dL, a hematocrit of 36 %, and thrombocytopenia with a platelet count of 84,000/μL. Tests of liver function revealed serum concentrations of albumin of 2.2 g/dL (N > 3.5 g/dL), total bilirubin of 3.1 mg/dL, with direct bilirubin of 1.9 mg/dL, an aspartate aminotransferase of 83 U/mL, an alanine aminotransferase of 37 U/mL, an alkaline phosphatase of 106 U/mL (upper limits of normal 150 U/mL), and a γ-glutamyl transpeptidase of 48 U/mL (upper limits of normal <98 U/L). Studies of coagulation revealed an elevated prothrombin time (PT) of 21 s and an international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.87. Abdominal paracentesis revealed a serum albumin-to-ascites gradient (SAAG) >1.1 g/dL, and a total protein concentration of <2.5 g/dL, consistent with portal hypertension: The total white blood cell count was 220/mm3, with 22 % neutrophils. These results were compatible with the diagnosis of an uninfected ascites due to portal hypertension as a probable consequence of his cirrhosis. The initial chest radiograph revealed cardiomegaly, bilateral pulmonary edema, and minimal pleural effusions (Fig. ). Ultrasound scan of the right upper quadrant revealed a nodular liver consistent with cirrhosis with evidence of portal hypertension such as mild splenomegaly and large volume ascites with no space-occupying lesion was identified. Admission pericardiocentesis had an opening pressure of 20 mmHg and, after removal of 1.5 L of serous pericardial fluid and placement of a drain, a closing pressure of 9 mmHg. Pericardial fluid analysis revealed protein concentrations and other characteristics identical to those found in the ascitic fluid: All pericardial cultures were negative, and no organisms were seen. Otherwise, the echocardiogram was relatively normal, as was cardiac function. Over the succeeding 24 h, 3 L of fluid drained from the pericardial sac, as did an additional 3 L during the subsequent 24-h period. It was suspected that ascitic fluid was exiting through the pericardial drain.
During investigation, a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 28 mm/h and serum concentrations of C-reactive protein of 1.1 mg/dL, creatine phosphokinase of 101 U/L, aldolase of 6.1 U/L, and lactate dehydrogenase of 398 U/L were all noted. Serum lipids and triglycerides were unremarkable. Investigation for the etiology of chronic liver disease revealed a serum ferritin of 113 ng/mL, negative anti-mitochondrial antibody, anti-smooth muscle antibody, antinuclear antibody, and anti-liver kidney microsomal antibodies, and negative virologic evidence for hepatitis A or B. Hepatitis C Ab was positive, but serum hepatitis C virus was undetectable. Serum α-1 anti-trypsin and ceruloplasmin, serum copper, and TSH concentrations were normal. Serological investigations for a possible rheumatological disease were unremarkable as well as infectious evaluation for HIV, Lyme disease, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. A PET/CT scan revealed no FDG-avid focus in any area.
Since decreased pericardial fluid volume was noted on the fourth and fifth hospital days, the pericardial drain was removed. Two days later, he became progressively dyspneic with repeat chest X-ray showing bilateral pleural effusions, cardiomegaly, and diffuses pulmonary edema.
A radionuclide SPECT scan was then performed following injection of 5.11 mCi 99mTechnetium-labeled, macroaggregated albumin (MAA) into the peritoneal cavity. There was essentially no delay between the infusion of 99mTc-MAA and imaging. Although dynamic imaging was performed, the uptake in the pericardial sac was only faint at 15 min, but clearly visible at 1 h. The most impressive images were obtained at 4–24 h (Fig. ), where there was circumferential pericardial radiotracer distribution.

A 56-year-old woman was referred to our center, with a complaint of abdominal pain and constipation, for a week. From the time of onset, the pain was categorized to be severe, not colic and was localized to the epigastric region only. The patient was alert and ill, while she has had nausea and anorexia, but was not been accompanied by vomiting. Her vital signs were as follows: BP: 85/50, PR: 130, T: 37.2, and RR: 18. Her head, neck, and chest (heart and lung) were normal, and abdomen was fatty and without scarlet and reduced intestinal sounds. She had generalized tenderness with maximum pain intensity in epigastric regions, where her organs were normal. Her medical and corresponding drug history was as follows: antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, DVT, a history of two abortions, and type II diabetes.
Her drug history included: prednisolone tablets, 5 mg: once a day; methotrexate: three pounds a week; hydroxychloroquine tablet: 3 days a week; and warfarin tablets: daily half a pill and insulin.
For further examination, her cardiac activity was monitored, and serum therapy was given to the patient. She was also provided a nasogastric tube, Foley catheter for urinary drainage along with hydrocortisone and antibiotics.
After about 60 minutes, her vitals were as follows: BP: 100/60 and PR: 120.
The result of the preliminary tests showed WBC: 4200, Hb: 7.7, PLT: 80 000, and INR: 4.7 and other tests: normal. Ultrasound from the patient's bedside showed free fluid in the abdomen. Following these tests, the therapeutic intervention was continued as follows: serum, wo packed cell (PC) units, and fresh frozen plasma (FFP) units. Meanwhile, the patient was prepared for laparotomy where, during the surgery, her sugar levels kept under control, control, stress-dose cortisol was provided, and she received 5 units of PC and 4 units of FFP.
During the laparotomy, about 3 L of blood and clot were removed from the abdomen. Following this, a cystic lesion was observed in the vicinity of the large stomach flexion that bled due to the invasion of the gastroepyloid vessels (Figure ). After controlling the bleeding, the lesion was fully resected, and samples were examined for pathology (Figure ).
Postoperatively, complete control of the bleeding was achieved with sustained vital signs. However, given the state of preoperative hemorrhagic shock, the patient was kept in ICU until satisfactory recovery was achieved. She also received 5 units of PC and 4 units of FFP, and the vital signs were maintained stable.
Despite there was no recurrence of hemorrhage, she presented pancytopenia perhaps due to the underlying disease (antiphospholipid antibody syndrome). She was hyper-coagulopathic, due to thrombocytopenia and hemorrhagic shock (cause of referral); therefore, anticoagulant administration was not possible.
After 24 hours and ensuring the stability of vital signs and the absence of Ileus, the patient oral feed was resumed. The postoperative examination for pancytopenia was as follows: WBC: 1500 (PMN: 60%), Hb: 11, PLT: 50 000, INR: 1.7, and K: 3.2. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (GCSF) therapy was prescribed to the patients after which, her platelets and WBCs showed improvement. The patient's general condition improved, she tolerated the diet, and the bleeding was controlled. Her hemoglobin level did not drop, and therefore, she was transferred to the general ward.
The continuation of treatment was as follows: hydroxychloroquine, prednisolone: half a tablet twice a day, methotrexate was discontinued for 2 weeks, and folic acid and GCSF for 3 days.
Laboratory results showed: platelet <100 000, potassium <3.5, and INR: 1.6. She was administered anticoagulant prophylaxis.
Nine days after the operation, the patient suffered from shortness of breath and tachypnea and a feeling of pressure in the chest. According to the history of the disease, the first diagnosis was a pulmonary embolism, for which anticoagulant therapy was immediately initiated and the patient was transferred to ICU. Her laboratory findings revealed: a decrease in platelets 20 000 and an increase in INR > 3 while ECG was normal. Anticoagulant therapy was restarted. Nonetheless, she went under respiratory distress and despite intubation, she expired.

args: A 62-year-old male presents for admission and evaluation prior to a planned extensive open operation aimed at addressing residual complications following a significant gastrointestinal procedure he underwent roughly four weeks ago. According to his account, that previous intervention involved a large incision and required several hours in the operating room, as surgeons worked to remove a diseased segment of bowel. Although his immediate postoperative course was generally uneventful, he experienced pronounced fatigue and mild discomfort at the incision site, which prompted careful monitoring. He states that over the past month, he has had regular follow-up appointments where his surgical team assessed his wound healing and overall recovery. He recalls that there were no episodes of large-volume fluid accumulation, major blood flow deficits, or new cardiac symptoms in the intervening weeks. In particular, he denies any episodes of significant breathing difficulty or coughing fits that might point to an infectious process in his lungs. He likewise reports feeling free of systemic inflammatory responses such as marked fever or severe chills. During conversations with his care providers, he explains that he is not aware of having any lasting issues with compromised airway function. He also highlights that he has not needed strong intravenous antibiotics for an overwhelming bloodstream infection during his convalescence, and he has not been reliant on intravenous lines for medication or nutrition outside of a short period immediately after his initial surgery. Regarding his musculoskeletal condition, he denies that he has been immobilized in a hard cast or that he suffered any serious bone breaks in recent memory. He has not encountered high-impact injuries affecting multiple areas of his body, nor has he dealt with sudden neurological deficits or spinal cord problems that could leave him weak or disabled. He further mentions that he has not noticed bulging superficial veins on his legs, nor has he developed tendon or joint complications beyond routine postoperative aching related to his abdominal incision. At no point in his life has he been informed of a clot forming in a deep vessel of the legs, nor has he ever been told he experienced a traveling clot into the lung circulation. When asked about his relatives, he discloses that several of them have struggled with serious blood-flow blockages, prompting health professionals to suggest a broader familial predisposition to undesired clot formation. Despite that family history, he states that his own prior workups for inherited susceptibility markers were unrevealing, with all relevant laboratory assays showing no significant evidence of a particular gene alteration or immune-mediated clotting abnormality. He emphasizes that he has not dealt with chronic bowel inflammatory conditions, nor does he recollect being told of malignant changes or a need for oncology-focused care, despite having regular evaluations for other issues. He denies any long-standing cardiac disease that would compromise his pump function, explaining that no specialist has flagged unusual swelling in his extremities or shortness of breath upon exertion. He also underscores that he is able to walk around the house independently and has maintained the capacity for light exercise, though he admits that his stamina has decreased somewhat while recovering from his last procedure. On examination, he appears alert, with stable vital signs, clear lungs on auscultation, and no signs of fluid overload. His heart rhythm is unremarkable, and there is no peripheral edema in his lower extremities. Looking ahead to the forthcoming major operation, he voices concern about the risks and hopes to optimize his status in anticipation of anesthesia. He expresses determination to follow any medical directives that might reduce the chance of circulatory complications, highlighting that he is eager for relief from the persistent abdominal discomfort and the limitations brought on by his lengthy healing process. Overall, he appears motivated to proceed, and the surgical team plans to admit him for final testing and careful preparation before moving forward with the planned open intervention.

args:
A 66-year-old Caucasian man with no significant past medical history presented with flu-like symptoms, progressively worsening shortness of breath, difficulty in breathing (NYHA class III) and dry cough over the past 2 weeks prior to presentation. He denied any fever, chills, sputum production, orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, anorexia, weight loss, recent or past exposure to tuberculosis, sick contacts, pets, recent travel or past exposure to cigarette smoke (active or passive), asbestos, silica, coal dust or chemicals. He maintained an active lifestyle walking 5 miles three times a week without overt dyspnea. He had no prior history of connective tissue disease or HIV and denied any history of skin rash or joint pains. Past surgical, social and family histories were non-contributory. He also had no history of prior use of any long term medications. He had presented to the hospital 4 days prior to present admission and a provisional diagnosis of community acquired pneumonia was made based on chest radiograph findings and he was discharged home on 2L of oxygen and Levofloxacin, but came back to the hospital due to lack of obvious improvement.
On examination, he appeared tired with shortness of breath. The temperature was 99.9°F, the pulse 124/min, respiratory rate 26/min and oxygen saturation of 86% on 2L. Skin was diaphoretic. There was no evidence of rash, pallor, lymphadenopathy, clubbing, joint swelling or edema. Auscultation of chest revealed diminished breath sounds with bilateral velcro-like fine inspiratory crackles. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, metabolic panel, liver function tests, cardiac enzymes, BNP, lactate, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen and TSH. D-dimer was elevated at 15.4 (normal <1.6). ESR was 52 mm/hour. Rheumatoid factor was <5 (negative). ANA, p-ANCA and c-ANCA were negative. Blood cultures (bacterial/fungal/mycobacterial), urine legionella and streptococcal antigen were negative. Viral respiratory panel, including cytomegalovirus & Herpes Simplex Virus PCR were also negative. ABG showed hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis. Pulmonary function tests showed restrictive pattern of lung disease. Transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal cardiac chamber sizes and left ventricular ejection fraction of 81%.
Plain chest radiograph (Fig. ) showed bilateral basilar infiltrates and a peripheral reticulonodular pattern superimposed on generalized interstitial changes, involving the upper lobes as well as lung bases. High Resolution Computed Tomography (HRCT) of the chest (Fig. and ) revealed moderate to severe thickening of intralobular septa, septal line formation, parenchymal band formation and peribronchial thickening, ground glass opacities and mild mediastinal lymphadenopathy (likely reactive, largest lymph node being 1.1 cm) was noted. This was consistent with idiopathic interstitial pneumonia (IIP) without a specific pattern. At this time, treatment with levofloxacin was continued and solumedrol was added for empirical therapy. Computed Tomogram (CT) of the abdomen/pelvis was normal with no evidence of retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Despite steroid therapy, the patient's respiratory status deteriorated over the next day requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation. Consequently, consent for wedge biopsy of the lung was obtained for a pathological diagnosis to guide further therapy.
A right lung wedge biopsy was obtained by video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS). A polymorphic lymphoid infiltrate composed of large atypical cells, small lymphocytes and many plasma cells was noted, with lymphoid cells infiltrating blood vessels and bronchial walls (Fig. ). Multinucleated large Reed Sternberg-like cells were also present along with foci of necrosis. Bronchial washings showed atypical benign bronchial cells and pulmonary macrophages with a few rare atypical Reed-Sternberg-like cells. Bone marrow biopsy was normal.
Immuno-histochemical studies of lung tissue showed predominance of T cells expressing CD3, CD5 and CD43 with a smaller population of large atypical cells expressing CD20 and CD79a (B cell markers) (Fig. ). Many CD138+ plasma cells exhibiting polyclonal staining pattern for kappa and lambda immunoglobulin light chains were also seen. Bone marrow biopsy revealed normal cellularity with no evidence of lymphoma. In situ hybridization for EBV encoded RNA (EBER) was positive within scattered large lymphoid cells throughout the biopsy specimen (Fig. ).
Based on the pathological and immuno-histochemical findings, a diagnosis of Lymphomatoid Granulomatosis was made. Treatment with high dose steroids and rituximab showed significant clinical improvement and he was extubated 4 days after starting therapy. Treatment was continued with cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin and prednisolone chemotherapy and he showed gradual but slow resolution of clinical and x-ray findings. However, he subsequently developed pancytopenia consequent to chemotherapy, septic shock requiring increasing doses of pressors and acute renal failure which did not respond to aggressive management and he was terminally weaned per family wishes 4 weeks later. A chest-only autopsy revealed diffuse alveolar damage, likely secondary to sepsis or chemotherapy or both, but no evidence of residual LG was noted within the lungs (Fig. ).

args:
A 40-year-old Hispanic woman called 911 for worsening chest tightness, generalized weakness and vomiting of two days. On arrival, paramedics performed an EKG that revealed sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia at 160–180 beats/minute. Blood pressure and oxygen saturation were normal. She was treated with 40 mg intravenous lidocaine after which she converted to normal sinus rhythm. In the emergency department (ED), she was alert and complained of weakness of all four extremities. She denied shortness of breath. She had a history of hyperthyroidism and hypertension but took no medicines or oral contraceptives. Vital signs were blood pressure of 126/70 mm of Hg, pulse of 90 beats/minute and respiratory rate of 18 breaths/minute. She was afebrile, and oxygen saturation was 96% in room air. Cardiac and lung examinations were normal. There was no thyromegaly. Neurological examination showed 2/6 strength and hypoactive reflexes in all four extremities. Cranial nerves and sensations were normal. Initial laboratory tests showed potassium of 2.3 mEq/L (normal: 3.5–5.5mEq/L) with normal anion gap. Magnesium, chloride, calcium and creatinine were normal. Urinary toxicology screen was negative. A 12-lead electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. In the ED the patient received 40 mEq of oral potassium chloride, and intravenous (IV) potassium was started at 20 mEq/hour. Repeat laboratory testing after three hours showed potassium of 1.9 mEq/L. Thyroid-stimulating hormone was low (0.01μunit/ml; normal: 0.5–5.0 μunit/ml) with high free thyroxine (7.77 ng/dL; normal: 0.75 to 1.8 ng/dL). The patient complained of worsening weakness in her legs. She was given additional 40 mEq of oral potassium. Potassium was increased to 30 mEq/hour IV, and the patient was admitted to medical intensive care unit.
A diagnosis of TPP was made based on clinical presentation and laboratory results. Blood drawn after eight hours showed potassium of 6.6 mEq/L. Intravenous potassium was stopped, and intravenous dose of 1 gm calcium, 50% solution of 50 ml dextrose, and 10 units of insulin were given urgently to correct hyperkalemia. Despite these, the patient developed episodes of ventricular fibrillation and despite several attempts at electrical cardioversion, she died. Her potassium level was 10.1 mEq/L at the time of her death.

args: A 49-year-old woman came to the urgent care area of the medical ward late this morning because of new-onset pain and fullness in her right upper calf that started while she was grocery shopping yesterday afternoon. She describes the discomfort as a deep, pressure-like ache that intensifies when she flexes the knee or when the examiner presses along the line from the medial calf toward the popliteal fossa; she can localize it with one finger and says it is clearly worse when weight-bearing. She denies redness, warmth, itching, fever, chills, or recent skin trauma. She had been back at work as a high-school principal for the last three weeks after undergoing an open sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis for complicated diverticulitis exactly six weeks ago; the postoperative course was uneventful, she was walking the hallways on postoperative day one, and she never required prolonged bed rest at home. Her medical history is notable for a documented iliofemoral venous thrombosis on the opposite side five years earlier that was confirmed by duplex ultrasound and treated with a six-month course of warfarin until complete recanalization was demonstrated. She has no history of malignancy, chronic neurologic deficits, lower-limb casting, pelvic fractures, or recent air travel, and she has remained physically active, walking two miles most evenings with her spouse. Medications include a daily multivitamin, rosuvastatin 10 mg, and ibuprofen as needed for postoperative abdominal wall soreness; she is not taking estrogen-containing therapy, glucocorticoids, or anticoagulants. She quit smoking 10 years ago after a 12-pack-year history and drinks wine on weekends; there is no family history of inherited clotting disorders, premature myocardial infarction, or malignancy. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, or unexplained weight loss. On arrival her vital signs were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/76 mm Hg, heart rate 90 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % breathing room air. She appeared well and was able to stand on the examination stool without assistance. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; lungs were clear to bases without crackles. The abdominal incision was healed with no tenderness or mass. Examination of the lower limbs showed the right calf to be slightly fuller to the eye but without taut skin or discoloration; when measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity the circumference was 38.5 cm versus 37.2 cm on the left, a discrepancy she said she had not noticed before. Gentle compression over the course of the posterior tibial and popliteal veins elicited focal tenderness, whereas squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from those structures was much less uncomfortable. The overlying skin was warm but dry, and no indentation was produced after five seconds of firm thumb pressure over the tibia. A network of bluish, non-tortuous superficial channels was visibly prominent along the medial aspect of the calf and became more obvious when the leg was placed in a dependent position for several seconds. There were no spider angiomas, no stasis dermatitis, and no varicosities elsewhere. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ bilaterally and symmetric; capillary refill was brisk, and sensorimotor testing of both lower extremities was normal. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, hip tenderness, or back pain. Point-of-care laboratory testing obtained prior to any treatment showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, platelet count 212 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and INR 1.0. Electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm without acute changes. No obvious musculoskeletal or infectious etiology was identified on history or examination, and the examiner judged that other causes of unilateral calf pain—such as cellulitis, muscular strain, Baker cyst rupture, or arterial insufficiency—were less consistent with the physical findings than an occlusion in the deep venous system. She was counseled about signs of pulmonary embolism and remained in the department awaiting formal duplex ultrasonography to confirm the suspected diagnosis.

args: A 67-year-old male was brought to the emergency department by his neighbor after being found lying on his kitchen floor early in the morning, reportedly in a state of reduced responsiveness that had not been evident the previous night. According to the neighbor, the patient had seemed more lethargic than usual over the past several days but had not complained of any specific illness. At the time of arrival, his eyes remained closed when quietly addressed, and he did not lift his gaze or blink upon a mild tap to his shoulder. Only after repeated, louder calling of his name did he open his eyelids, peering around in a brief moment of apparent confusion before drifting back into a drooping posture without making further consistent contact. Attempts to elicit verbal responses yielded only unintelligible mumbling and sporadic groans, which did not coalesce into comprehensible words, nor did he appear able to articulate basic information such as the current day or his own location. Periodically, he would emit low-pitched exhalations that suggested some awareness of discomfort, but no coherent phrases were produced. His past medical records, obtained through a local clinic, revealed a background of advanced peripheral vascular disease, osteoarthritis, and a remote history of heavy tobacco use, although it remained unclear whether he continued smoking. Additional documentation indicated a prior episode of confusion connected to suspected metabolic derangements, but the specific tests and treatments were not detailed. At present, his vital signs suggested borderline hypertension and a slightly elevated pulse, with breathing that was shallow yet rhythmic. There was no obvious external trauma such as bruising to his scalp or lacerations around his head, though a small contusion on his right elbow raised the possibility of a recent minor fall. His skin appeared somewhat clammy, prompting further inquiry into whether he might have experienced an acute infection or another systemic cause of his presentation. When confronted with a firm press to his sternum and a pinch along his upper arm, he displayed sudden outward straightening of the limbs rather than a protective or purposeful withdrawal, indicating a significant alteration in normal motor coordination. This reaction presented consistently during multiple brief attempts to evaluate deeper neurological pathways, suggesting that while he registered unpleasant stimuli, his response pattern was not focused on localizing or removing the source of discomfort. No further interventions had yet been administered, as the team needed additional data before taking definitive steps. Laboratory studies were ordered to explore possibilities such as electrolyte imbalance, infection, or other metabolic disturbances; staff also arranged imaging to rule out intracranial lesions or evidence of an ischemic or hemorrhagic event that might account for his abrupt status change. Throughout these initial evaluations, the patient intermittently vocalized low moans that never formed identifiable words, and despite occasional fleeting awareness when addressed at higher volume, he did not track examiners consistently or demonstrate stable orientation. A relative who arrived soon after described him as previously able to carry on a conversation despite some age-related forgetfulness, making this current state of near-total incoherence and minimal visual engagement particularly striking. Further complicating the picture was the patient’s long-standing vascular condition, which could predispose him to circulatory insufficiencies, and his history of arthritis, though neither alone would typically result in this degree of mental status change. As the clinical team prepared to conduct more thorough imaging studies and consult specialists for both neurological and medical secondary causes, he remained on a stretcher, occasionally responding to louder stimuli by haltingly opening his eyes but lapsing back into a subdued posture with only disorganized sounds escaping his lips, reinforcing the need for urgent investigation into the etiology of his profoundly altered consciousness.

args: A 62-year-old man was referred by his general surgeon for pre-operative assessment three weeks before a planned elective right inguinal herniorrhaphy under general anesthesia. He reports that the bulge has been enlarging over the past year and is now intermittently painful, particularly when lifting boxes at his job in a warehouse, but it has never incarcerated. The operation is anticipated to be completed through an open anterior approach with an expected surgical time of less than two hours and minimal blood loss. He arrives to clinic walking unassisted from the parking lot and states he can easily climb a flight of stairs and mow his lawn with a push mower without stopping, although he slows his pace when the weather is hot. He denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower-extremity swelling, or exercise-induced dyspnea beyond what he attributes to deconditioning. His weight is stable. He has a remote cardiovascular history notable for a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction eight years ago that required percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a single drug-eluting stent in the proximal left anterior descending artery. Since that event he has not experienced further chest discomfort, emergency visits, or revascularization, and he follows regularly with cardiology. He takes aspirin 81 mg daily, clopidogrel was discontinued more than a year ago, and he remains on high-intensity atorvastatin. His other chronic medications include lisinopril 10 mg daily for blood pressure, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, and metformin 500 mg twice daily to keep his fasting glucose in the 110–120 mg/dL range. He explicitly states he has never required injections to control his blood sugar and has not experienced hypoglycemia. There is no personal history of heart failure, valvular surgery, arrhythmia requiring therapy, peripheral vascular intervention, or carotid disease. He underwent colonoscopy screening at age 60 without complication and has no previous general anesthesia issues. Family history is positive for premature coronary artery disease in two older brothers; no familial sudden death or bleeding diathesis is reported. He quit cigarette smoking fifteen years ago after a 20-pack-year history and drinks one beer on weekends. Review of systems is negative for syncope, palpitations, transient neurological deficits, hematuria, gross edema, abdominal pain, or fevers. On examination his blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg, heart rate 62 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14, oxygen saturation 97 % on room air, and temperature 36.7 °C. He is a well-appearing male in no distress. Jugular venous pulsation is not elevated. The point of maximal impulse is not displaced; S1 and S2 are normal without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lung fields are clear bilaterally with no rales or wheezes. Abdomen is soft with a reducible right inguinal defect; no ascites. Extremities show no pitting edema; peripheral pulses are palpable and symmetric. Neurologic examination reveals normal strength and sensation. Laboratory testing obtained this morning demonstrates hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, platelet count 225 × 10³/µL, sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 35 mg/dL, and creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, a stable finding compared with his outpatient panel two months ago. Estimated glomerular filtration rate is 38 mL/min/1.73 m². Electrolytes otherwise unremarkable, hepatic panel normal, and coagulation indices within reference ranges. Resting 12-lead electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm at 60 bpm, Q-waves in inferior leads consistent with old infarct, and no acute ST-T changes. A recent transthoracic echocardiogram from six months prior revealed left ventricular ejection fraction of 55 %, normal wall motion, and no significant valvular lesion. Chest radiograph today demonstrates clear lung fields and normal cardiac silhouette. The patient expresses understanding of surgical risks, including potential cardiac complications, and is eager to proceed so he can return to work. Plan is to continue beta-blocker and statin therapy through the peri-operative period, maintain renally dosed analgesics, ensure adequate hydration pre-operatively, and coordinate with anesthesia for routine intra-operative monitoring without the need for invasive arterial pressure lines given the expected low blood loss and stable hemodynamic profile.

args:
Our patient is a 38-year-old woman with a past medical history most significant for a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery in March 2017, complicated by a marginal ulcer at the anastomosis site with subsequent perforation. The patient could not tolerate oral nutrition and became severely malnourished, and a PICC line was placed for TPN in December 2017. Chronic TPN use led to recurrent candidemia with blood cultures growing Candida albicans resistant to fluconazole and voriconazole. Over several months, her PICC line was replaced thrice, and she was ultimately treated with micafungin. The patient had multiple admissions over the past few months for workup of altered mental status and unsteady gait. Her other past medical history included systemic lupus erythematosus treated with mycophenolate mofetil and hydroxychloroquine, left portal vein branch thrombosis on enoxaparin therapy, type 2 diabetes mellitus not on insulin, fibromyalgia, depression, and anxiety. She presented to our hospital in July 2018 with complaints of tremors, impaired mobility, confusion, gait instability, falls, and lethargy.
On admission, she was noted to be confused, with horizontal nystagmus and dilated pupils as well as bradypnea. Dilated fundus examination was indicative of candida retinitis. She was lethargic but arousable. Her speech was difficult to understand, and the history of presenting illness was provided primarily by the patient's mother who relayed to the team that she had been having tremors, episodes of confusion, and numerous falls for the past several months. Her mother also noticed that she had increased irritability and emotional lability during this time. She was initially admitted to the intensive care unit for airway management due to her acute encephalopathy. Due to a lack of meningeal signs, a lumbar puncture was not performed.
The infectious disease service was consulted, and she was started on liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine for a total of 6 weeks. MRI brain showed worsening T1 hyperintensities from June 2018 to July 2018 in the bilateral globus pallidi, as evidenced by the arrows in Figures and , which were thought to be secondary to manganese toxicity from chronic TPN use. Furthermore, a repeat MRI brain was performed in August 2018, one month after discontinuation of TPN, which showed improvement of the T1 hyperintensities in the bilateral globus pallidi, as evidenced by the white arrows in , approaching the intensity level noted in June 2018. Other supporting findings included T1 hyperintensities in the substantia nigra, as evidenced by the arrows in , and decreased signal in the bilateral globus pallidi on T2-weighted images, as evidenced by the arrows in . With antifungal treatment and cessation of TPN, her mentation and neurological symptoms improved within 3-4 days of cessation of TPN.
General surgery was consulted for gastrostomy tube placement in her remnant stomach, as the patient was unable to consume more than 500 calories by mouth per day due to complaints of nausea and vomiting. Although our patient presented to the hospital seven months after initiation of TPN therapy, her Parkinsonian symptoms had begun to manifest after three months on TPN.

args:
In September 2016, a 28-year-old Vietnamese male patient visited the National Geriatric Hospital in Hanoi due to epigastric pain, weight loss, and painful swollen lymph nodes in his neck for the past 5 months. He had no history of gastric disease. The subsequent workup included a gastroscopy that revealed an ulcer in the body of the stomach measuring more than 3 cm in diameter. The biopsy from gastroscopy was assessed for Helicobacter pylori by the Campylobacter-like organism (CLO) test and histopathological examination. Histopathology results for the ulcer were negative for H. pylori and revealed stage IV diffuse-type gastric cancer (DGC), characterized by signet ring cells. Unfortunately, for financial reasons, the patient refused additional diagnosis including neck lymph node biopsy, computed tomography (CT) scan, and positron emission tomography (PET)/CT to evaluate distant metastasis, and also refused to follow up on further treatments. The patient's health deteriorated, and he died 4 months post-diagnosis.
The patient’s family history did not show DGC. However, because of the clinicopathological presentation including early onset, severe clinical symptoms, and histopathological test results showing diffuse-type characteristics, we performed genetic screening for mutations in the CDH1 gene by E-cadherin protein expression.
With the patient’s consent, we used the Exgene™ Blood SV kit (GeneAll Biotechnology, Korea) to extract DNA from the whole blood sample according to the instruction manual. This was followed by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification and DNA sequencing using both forward and reverse primers for all exons and introns of the CDH1 gene. The primers were designed using National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) Primer-BLAST [Basic Local Alignment Search Tool] and the QIAGEN CLC Main Workbench (version 8.1.2.) and were reanalyzed using the OligoAnalyzer tool. After the polymerase chain reaction (PCR), we separated the DNA by size using gel electrophoresis (1.5% agarose gel). Finally, we performed gel analysis using the UVP GelDoc-It2 Imaging System (Fisher Scientific). The determination of DNA sequences for all samples was done on Applied Biosystems ABI 3500 (JP0 and ABI 3730xl; Thermo Fisher) deoxyribonuclease acid (DNA) analyzers, followed by gene analysis using BioEdit (version 2.0) and ApE (version 1.0) software.
We found a novel heterozygous nonsense mutation that resulted in a premature termination codon (PTC) UGA at p.213 in exon 5 (CDH1 c.639G>A, p. W213) (Fig. ). The mutation shortened the CDH1 gene length from 882 aa to 213 aa. The immunohistochemical staining of the patient’s tissue sample showed decreased expression of E-cadherin (Fig. ).
We collected blood samples from the patient’s family members (11 participants) for mutation screening in the CDH1 gene exon 5. The participants included three first cousins once removed (III-1, III-2, III-3), two uncles (III-4, III-5), the father (III-6), the mother (III-7), a male first cousin (IV-1), a female first cousin (IV-2), an older sister (IV-3), and a younger brother (IV-7) (Fig. ). At the time of testing, no participants showed any symptoms of HDGC. Upon analysis of the pedigree, we found the patient’s parents to be healthy, and there was no family history of the disease. The DNA sequencing in CDH1 exon 5 showed a similar mutation (c.639G>A, p. W213*) in two members of the family: the father (III-6, 59 years old) and an uncle (III-4, 65 years old), with both heterozygous. Gastroscopy performed on the patient’s father revealed inflammation and antral gastritis. Six-core biopsy specimens showed chronic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, and low-grade dysplasia. High iron diamine staining for metaplasia typing was negative.

args:
A 22-year-old South Indian man with a previous history of Fontan surgery at the age of 13 for double outlet right ventricle with severe pulmonary stenosis and straddling tricuspid valve presented with a spiking high-grade fever of 25-day duration. He had no history of cough, ear discharge, respiratory infection, dysuria, diarrhea, gastrointestinal distress or vomiting.
His past history included surgery for a brain abscess at the age of 13, Fontan surgery at the age of 13, ocular surgery for retinal detachment at the age of 16, and multiple small skin abscesses chiefly on his left foot, which recurred after treatment and led to an excision of an abscess on his foot. At the age of 20, he was diagnosed with protein-losing enteropathy.
The last echocardiography performed before his hospitalization showed a right to left flow in the Fontan circuit, signifying a flow of de-oxygenated blood from the intended pulmonic to the systemic circulation.
On physical examination, he was found to be febrile with a temperature of 39°C on admission, a heart rate of 88 per minute, a respiratory rate of 26 per minute, blood pressure of 98/60 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 89 percent in room air. A head to toe examination identified clubbing of his nails, a median sternotomy scar, mild abdominal distension and pedal edema. His teeth and oral cavity were found to be normal. He was 174 cm tall and weighed 51 kg.
His laboratory data on admission showed the following: white blood cells (WBCs) 14,000/mm3, neutrophils 84.5 percent, lymphocytes 8.3 percent, eosinophils 0.2 percent, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hour, hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, urea 19 mg/dL, serum creatinine 0.6 mg/dL, sodium 122 mmol/L, potassium 3.7 mmol/L, total protein 3.7 g/dL, serum albumin 1.3 g/dL and serum globulin 2.4 g/dL. He tested negative for hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C virus and human immunodeficiency virus. A further work-up for immune deficiency could not be performed for logistical reasons. A urine analysis showed 20-25 red blood cells and 10-12 WBCs per high-power field.
A 2 D echocardiography revealed no vegetations. An ultrasound scan revealed a mass in his left kidney measuring 7.2 × 4 cm. A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a hypodense area in the lower pole of his left kidney measuring 5.28 × 6.22 cm, consistent with a renal abscess, which was percutaneously aspirated and grew highly sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Special staining for acid-fast bacilli was negative. Figure shows the CT images. His blood cultures were repeatedly negative. One of the urine cultures grew Escherichia coli and Enterococcus species. The E. coli was sensitive to amikacin, cefoperazone and/or sulbactam, gentamicin, imipenem, meropenem, natamycin, nitrofurantoin, and piperacillin and/or tazobactam. The Enterococcus species was sensitive to amoxicillin and clavulanic acid, gentamicin, imipenem, linezolid, meropenem and nitrofurantoin.
On the basis of the sensitivity of the S. aureus isolated from the abscess, he was treated with intravenous gentamicin, 80 mg at eight-hourly intervals, and with intravenous teicoplanin, 400 mg once per day.
He continued experiencing spikes of high-grade fever, and a repeat ultrasound after 12 days of appropriate therapy showed only minimal resolution of the lesion. Surgery was anticipated. A technetium-99 m renogram was performed to see the split function of the kidney with the abscess and to determine whether or not a partial nephrectomy could be performed. The renogram revealed a total glomerular filtration rate of 94 mL/min, with the left kidney contributing 36 mL/min and the right kidney 58 mL/min, and no evidence of obstruction. Figure shows the results of the technetium-99 m renogram.
In view of the persistence of the abscess, he underwent a surgical exploration of the renal bed. An attempt was made to carry out a partial nephrectomy of the affected region. However, this failed and so a left nephrectomy was performed. Figure shows the nephrectomy specimen. Figures and show the histopathological picture.
Post-operatively, his fever subsided and the antibiotic coverage was continued for one week with teicoplanin and gentamicin. At the time of his discharge, his serum creatinine level was 1.1 mg/dL.
A histopathological examination of the diseased kidney revealed infiltrates of lymphocytes, plasma cells and histiocytes. The replacement of renal parenchymal tissue by sheets of foamy histiocytes admixed with neutrophils was observed and this was consistent with xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis. A special stain for acid-fast bacilli was negative. Clinical, radiological and histopathological examinations failed to provide any evidence of an obstructive lesion in his urinary tract or of renal calculi.
Two weeks after his discharge from hospital, he complained of fever. A CT scan of his abdomen was performed and a residual renal bed abscess was found. A pigtail catheter was inserted and daily aspiration and antibiotic instillation were performed. A week later he was discharged again, with oral antibiotics.
About seven months after the surgery, he remained in a perfect state of health without reports of further infection. This also signified the absence of inherent immune deficiency.
