args:
A 69-year-old Japanese man underwent treatment for liver dysfunction 3 years after aortic valve replacement. Later, rapid elevation in his serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) level was recorded and he was readmitted to determine the etiology. His body temperature was 36 °C, blood pressure 164/65 mmHg, and pulse rate was 66/minute. Laboratory data revealed mild anemia and liver-renal injury: white blood cells (WBC) 4600/uL, hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL, platelet 18.9 × 104/dL, C-reactive protein (CRP) 0.29 mg/dL, ALP 1138 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 40 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 37 U/L, and γ glutamyl transpeptidase (γ-GTP) 298 U/L. His blood urea nitrogen (BUN) was 22.4 mg/dL, creatinine 1.14 mg/dL, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) 45.6 seconds, and prothrombin time-international normalized ratio (PT-INR) 2.67. He also had a past history of duodenal ulcer perforation and was currently being treated with warfarin, angiotensin receptor blocker, and proton pump inhibitor. In addition, he was taking orally administered ursodeoxycholic acid for unknown liver function disorder. He had no alcohol consumption or tobacco smoking history and no relevant family history.
A plain radiograph showed no significant findings, but computed tomography (CT) revealed an enhanced tumor within the hilar bile duct and dilatation of the left hepatic duct (Fig. ), which are typical findings for hilar cholangiocarcinoma. In addition, endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) revealed tumor shadow in his bile duct, and the cytology confirmed malignant cells in the bile (Fig. ). As no lymph node and distant metastases were detected, we inserted endoscopic nasobiliary drainage (ENBD) to reduce jaundice as preparation for surgery. We performed extended left hepatectomy with resection of his bile duct; his postoperative course was good without severe complications. After 3 months postoperatively, he was readmitted for subacute cholangitis and obstructive jaundice. Immediately, percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography drainage (PTCD) was performed, followed by cholangiography that exhibited the intrabiliary tumor growth in the remnant liver.
Simultaneously, histological examination of resected specimens revealed tumor growth in the hilar duct across the left hepatic duct (Fig. ). Microscopic findings at the same site revealed a dilated bile duct filled with well-differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma (Fig. ). On immunohistochemical examination, tumor cells were positive for cytokeratin (CK) 20 (Fig. ) but negative for CK7 (Fig. ). Furthermore, CK18 (Fig. ) as control and CDX2 (Fig. ) were stained. Although these findings were not typical of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, hepatic metastasis from another primary lesion was strongly suspected [, ]. Furthermore, CT revealed an enhanced tumor-like lesion at the descending colon, followed by diagnosis of type 2 cancer in total colonography. Then, left hemicolectomy was performed; the immunohistochemical-identified feature matched with an intrabiliary tumor. Meanwhile, the PTCD fluid turned bloody, which was considered to indicate bleeding from a residual bile duct tumor (Fig. ). Accordingly, we planned chemotherapy with orally administered capecitabine but our patient experienced a spike fever because of refractory cholangitis. Thus, we abandoned chemotherapy and initiated radiotherapy to stop the tumor bleeding around the hilar bile duct. After completing radiotherapy (total 50 Gy) for approximately 1 month, we observed an improvement in his liver function because of tumor shrinkage. Unfortunately, the effects were short-lived, intrabiliary growth and cholangitis rebooted after 1 month leading to his death due to septic liver failure (Fig. ). Autopsy revealed a remnant tumor in the bile duct (Fig. ), but no noticeable nodular metastasis was observed, except for a single small metastasis in the lower lobe of his left lung.

args: A 55-year-old man presented to the intensive care unit with concerns related to his longstanding kidney issues and recent episodes of generalized weakness. He was brought in from a primary care setting after complaining of mild fatigue, reduced appetite, and occasional dizziness. On arrival, his temperature was noted to be 37.8 degrees Celsius, and his breathing appeared slightly labored but not distressing; he had a recorded respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute. A blood pressure reading of 130/70 mm Hg was documented. His pulse rate stood at 95 beats per minute. Despite his chronic medical challenges, he denied any recent abrupt changes in urine output or other acute declines in organ function, suggesting no new onset of serious renal complications at the time of presentation. However, he acknowledged that he had been under periodic evaluation for persistent renal concerns over the past few years, which had raised the question of the need for closer nephrologic follow-up. He also stated that he had never experienced any condition severely compromising his immune system or other major organs, and he had not required medications aimed at suppressing immune function. During the initial assessment, arterial blood gases revealed a pH of 7.45, indicating a normal acid-base balance. Further laboratory values showed a creatinine of 3.3 mg/dL, suggestive of significant though stable renal impairment that was known to be part of his past medical history. Electrolytes demonstrated a sodium concentration of 138 mEq/L and a potassium level of 3.9 mEq/L, both within acceptable ranges. Hematologic evaluations indicated a hematocrit of 31%, pointing to mild anemia that he explained had been previously noted during routine checkups. His white blood cell count was 9400 mm^3, which did not suggest a marked infectious or inflammatory state on admission. His GCS was 14. Regarding respiratory support, it was noted that he was receiving a moderate fraction of inspired oxygen at 35%, and his arterial oxygen tension measured at 80 mm Hg. He was observed to be speaking in full sentences, and there were no clear signs of respiratory exhaustion or the need for invasive measures based on initial clinical judgment. The patient reported no major fevers or chills aside from a subjective sense of feeling warmer than usual, which correlated with the recorded temperature but did not appear to be a persistent concern. He was admitted in anticipation of a planned operative procedure aimed at improving a non-urgent condition, and the team wanted his renal status optimized beforehand. In addition to his kidney-related history, he asserted that he had no notable illnesses in the past that would drastically weaken his immune defenses or predispose him to severe opportunistic infections. On physical examination, he had only minimal pedal edema, and lung fields were clear to auscultation without crackles or wheezing. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal heart sounds without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. His skin was warm to touch and showed no significant rash or breakdown. Given the need for ongoing monitoring and further evaluation of his baseline chronic condition, the critical care team proceeded with admission for closer observation, with plans to reevaluate his circulatory and renal parameters regularly. He was alert enough to converse about his situation and did not exhibit any extreme restlessness or agitation. At the time of arrival, no intravenous fluids or adjunctive treatments had been initiated, as the immediate focus was on stabilizing his existing medical issues and ensuring that his cardiovascular, respiratory, and renal systems remained compensated before considering any definitive intervention.

args: A 79-year-old male with symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the cardiology clinic for reassessment of his antithrombotic regimen after several recent episodes of rapid ventricular response managed in the emergency department. He was first diagnosed with atrial fibrillation eight years ago and was started on warfarin shortly thereafter. Over the past year, however, the anticoagulation clinic has documented wide fluctuations in his international normalized ratio, with several readings below 1.8 and occasional spikes above 4 despite stable dosing and diligent dietary counseling; these excursions prompted multiple dose adjustments and temporary withholding of the medication. Six months ago he was hospitalized for melena and symptomatic anemia secondary to a bleeding duodenal ulcer confirmed on endoscopy; he required two units of packed red blood cells but recovered without the need for surgical intervention. Since that admission he has had no recurrent gastrointestinal hemorrhage, yet he remains concerned about another bleed if warfarin is continued. His medical history is notable for three decades of systolic-predominant hypertension diagnosed in his late forties, treated with a long-acting calcium-channel blocker and an angiotensin-receptor blocker, although home blood-pressure logs provided today still demonstrate systolic values frequently running in the 150s mm Hg. He denies any prior cerebral ischemic or hemorrhagic events, peripheral vascular disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney pathology, or known hepatic disorders. He does not smoke and states that he consumes 0 alcoholic drinks per week, having abstained completely since his early seventies after the passing of his spouse. Review of systems is negative for jaundice, pruritus, abdominal distention, easy bruising, hematuria, or decreased urine output. Vital signs in clinic reveal blood pressure of 156/72 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats/min with irregularly irregular rhythm, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, temperature 36.6 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Physical examination is notable for an audible S1 and variable S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and no carotid bruits. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no peripheral edema or petechiae. args: A 68-year-old man with the left hemiparesis from 2 h previously visited the emergency room. His medical history included hypertension and bilateral emphysema due to heavy smoking. Vital sign assessment revealed tachycardia; examination of the heart revealed atrial fibrillation (AF). Neurological examination revealed left hemiparesis and mild disturbance of consciousness. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score was 12. A magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (diffusion-weighted image) showed right corona radiate infarction []. MR angiography (MRA) revealed right middle cerebral artery (MCA) occlusion []. Chest X-ray showed no remarkable findings on admission. Initial investigations performed included a white blood cell (13.9 × 109/L; normal 4–11 × 109/L), hemoglobin (14.6 g/dL; normal 13.1–17.3 g/dL), and platelet (147 × 109/L; normal 130–400 × 109/L) count. Prothrombin time (16 s; normal 11.5–14.5 s), activated partial thromboplastin time (40.1 s; normal 27.5–41 s), D-dimer (<0.5 mg/mL; normal <0.5 mg/mL), arterial blood gas (room air; pH 7.35), PaO2 (89.0 mmHg), and PaCO2 (45.1 mmHg) were also analyzed. The patient was negative for antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody. Intravenous tPA was administered according to the accelerated regimen (0.6 mg/kg) 3.5 h after onset. Four hours later, consciousness gradually improved, the right MCA recanalized [], and volume of infarction was not changed. The patient experienced hemoptysis and mild shortness of breath 18 h later, with no chest pain or fever. Oxygen saturation dropped from 97 to 90%. Chest computed tomography (CT) revealed multifocal diffuse ground-glass attenuation and patchy consolidation in both lungs [ and b]. Immediate chest X-ray revealed bilateral upper lobe intra-alveolar infiltrate []. The hemoptysis gradually improved after treatment with dopamine, corticosteroids, and bronchodilators, followed by fluid replacement, mechanical ventilation (MV), and administration of rFVIIa (75 mg/kg) with corticosteroids. The improvement was noted on day 3 and resolved completely by day 4. Hemoglobin dropped from 14.9 g/dl on admission to 11.7 g/dl on day 5, with no evidence of bleeding in other sites. Two weeks later, he was put off of the artificial respirator. After 1 month, the chest X-ray was normal []. He was transferred to a rehabilitation hospital after 6 weeks of hospitalization with modified Rankin scale (mRS) score of 3.

calculator_name: HEART Score for Major Cardiac Events

args: A 62-year-old woman presented to the emergency department for evaluation of intermittent central chest discomfort that began earlier this evening. She described a pressure-like sensation in the mid sternum, without sharp or tearing quality, that came on while walking up a flight of stairs at home. The discomfort reached a moderate intensity, was accompanied by mild shortness of breath and a brief sense of clamminess, and eased after several minutes of rest. A second, milder episode occurred while she was waiting in triage, currently rated 2/10 and again characterized as heaviness rather than pain. She denies a pleuritic component, no increase with deep inspiration or coughing, no reproducibility with palpation, and no positional worsening. She notes mild queasiness without vomiting and denies syncope, presyncope, palpitations, hemoptysis, calf swelling, or recent long-distance travel. She has a longstanding history of elevated blood pressure for which she takes lisinopril daily, and she reports continued cigarette use, approximately half a pack per day for decades. She specifically denies a history of diabetes, and her most recent routine bloodwork with her primary care clinician did not indicate abnormal glucose control. Body habitus is average; she reports stable weight and does not endorse weight-related limitations in daily activity. She has known vascular disease, previously evaluated by a vascular specialist for exertional calf discomfort; outpatient noninvasive testing at that time was consistent with lower extremity arterial disease, and she has been advised on exercise therapy and risk factor modification. She does not recall any first-degree relatives with coronary events at a young age; her father’s cardiac issues occurred later in life, and her mother is alive without known heart disease. She has not had recent respiratory infections or fevers, no recent chest trauma, and no gastrointestinal symptoms suggestive of reflux triggering the episodes. She is not on hormonal therapy. Allergies are none known. Home medications include lisinopril; she is not taking medications for blood sugar control. On arrival, she is alert, in no acute respiratory distress, and conversant in full sentences. Vital signs show blood pressure 152/86 mmHg, heart rate 84 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 per minute, temperature 36.8°C, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. She appears well perfused. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. There is no jugular venous distention. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezes or crackles. The chest wall is non-tender to palpation. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. Extremities show no edema; distal pulses are palpable though slightly diminished at the dorsalis pedis compared with proximal pulses, consistent with her prior vascular history. Neurologic exam is non-focal. Her body mass index by triage measurement is within the normal range, and she does not appear overweight. A 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained on presentation demonstrates normal sinus rhythm with a rate in the low 80s, with mild, diffuse T-wave flattening and subtle ST-segment changes that do not meet criteria for acute ischemia and without any ST-segment elevation. There are no arrhythmias on the initial rhythm strip. A chest radiograph performed at the bedside is without focal consolidation, pneumothorax, or widened mediastinum. Initial laboratory evaluation shows a high-sensitivity cardiac troponin level marginally above the laboratory upper reference limit and clearly less than threefold that threshold; basic metabolic panel and complete blood count are unremarkable. Given the exertional onset of substernal pressure with brief associated dyspnea and diaphoresis in a patient with long-standing high blood pressure, ongoing tobacco use, and established peripheral vascular disease, acute coronary pathology is considered, although the relatively short duration of symptoms, improvement with rest, and the absence of focal pleuritic features or chest wall tenderness render the overall picture neither classic nor dismissive. She has not taken any medications for the current episode prior to arrival. She remains hemodynamically stable with ongoing mild chest pressure at the time of the initial evaluation.

args:
A 62-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis visited outpatient clinic for unexplained weight loss. He had a good performance status (grade 0). Laboratory tests showed serum albumin level of 5.3 g/dL, bilirubin level of 1.3 mg/dL, and prothrombin time international normalized ratio of 1.1. Neither ascites nor hepatic encephalopathy was found, which corresponded with Child-Pugh class A. Serologic markers for hepatitis B and C were negative. Serum α-fetoprotein level was 311.7 ng/mL, and PIVKA-II level was over 2,000 mAU/mL. Other laboratory findings including tumor markers and thyroid function tests were within normal limits. About a 5 cm sized ovoid enhancing mass in arterial phase involving the caudate lobe with underlying cirrhotic configuration of the liver was shown on the dynamic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (, respectively). There was no vascular invasion and no intraabdominal lymphadenopathy. On positron emission tomography-CT, maximum standardized uptake value for the mass was 6.3 without evidence of distant metastasis. Overall, early stage of HCC was assigned according to the Barcelona-Clinic-Liver-Cancer (BCLC) HCC staging system.
Surgical resection was recommended for the treatment of the HCC, however, he refused the surgical treatment. TACE using DEB was performed after obtaining written informed consent for the procedure. During the first TACE session, hepatic angiogram revealed a definite hypervascular mass supplied by a small branch of the left hepatic artery without apparent arteriovenous shunt (). After positioning the microcatheter tip at the left hepatic artery close to the tumor-feeding branch, a mixture of DEB (DC Bead®, 1 vial of 300~500 µm diameter, Biocompatibles, UK) loaded with doxorubicin (50 mg) and contrast agent was infused until the flow through the tumor-feeding artery was slowed down. After the infusion, angiogram showed occluded tumor-feeding branch and no tumor staining ().
He had no serious adverse event after embolization except mild abdominal pain that was controlled with oral analgesic (tramadol 50 mg per os tid). One-month post-embolization CT () and six-month post-embolization MRI () showed complete necrosis without viable portions. Follow-up α-fetoprotein level was 3.89 ng/mL, and PIVKA-II level was 21 mAU/mL. This patient remains in good health without cancer recurrence 6 months after the procedure.

args: A 45-year-old man presented to the outpatient cardiology clinic for routine review of his recently initiated long-term anticoagulation in the context of symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation that was first documented on ambulatory telemetry three months ago after episodes of palpitations and lightheadedness. He additionally carries a diagnosis of chronic kidney disease—baseline serum creatinine has hovered between 2.0 and 2.3 mg/dL for the last year, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of approximately 25 mL/min/1.73 m²; he has never required renal replacement therapy but follows nephrology quarterly. He denies ever being told he has elevated blood pressure; office readings over the last decade, including today’s, have remained in the low-normal range, and he is not taking any antihypertensive medications. There is no personal history of intracranial hemorrhage, gastrointestinal bleeding, easy bruising, hereditary coagulopathy, peptic ulcer disease, or malignancy. He recounts that his international normalized ratio has remained between 2.1 and 2.7 on every weekly check since warfarin was started, with an average around 2.4, and he has not needed any dose holds or vitamin K reversals. The patient does not take aspirin, clopidogrel, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, or herbal supplements; his only medications are warfarin 5 mg nightly, low-dose furosemide 20 mg daily for intermittent lower-extremity edema, and propranolol 20 mg twice daily that had been initiated primarily for portal vein pressure reduction but offers rate control as well. He reports no recent falls, head trauma, or blood in the stool. Family history is unremarkable for bleeding or clotting disorders. He has an established issue with the heart’s ability to handle circulatory demands, and past episodes of fluid retention have required close medical follow-up, though he states that he has not been hospitalized in recent months for this concern. He also has a known vulnerability involving his kidneys, and his care over the last few years has included periodic laboratory testing to confirm that his kidney function remains stable. He denies having ever been diagnosed with significant liver disease or any abnormal growths in his body, mentioning that his physician has never expressed any concerns regarding unusual masses or malignancies. He has lived independently within his own residence, performing most of his daily activities without professional assistance, and he arrived at the medical setting with only the help of a neighbor who noticed that he seemed more lethargic than usual. At the time of presentation, his vital signs revealed a temperature of 36.8 degrees Celsius, indicating near-normal body heat. His heart rate measured 99 beats per minute, and his systolic blood pressure was 120 mm Hg, placing him in a relatively stable hemodynamic range, though he admitted to feeling lightheaded if he stood up too quickly. His respiratory rate was documented at 16 breaths per minute, which did not appear overtly elevated, yet he described a sensation of difficulty taking full, comfortable breaths. Preliminary arterial blood gas measurements done prior to any interventions demonstrated that his partial pressure of oxygen was 83 mm Hg, suggesting he was not adequately oxygenating on room air, though he remained awake and oriented throughout his assessment and never displayed tinges of confusion or delirium. The same sampling indicated an alkalotic shift in his blood, with a pH of 7.47, prompting consideration of whether subtle respiratory or metabolic factors might be at play. Additional laboratory data at admission included a blood urea nitrogen value of 18 mg/dL, which was within an acceptable range overall yet was still scrutinized in light of his history of compromised kidney function. His serum sodium level was found to be 146 mEq/L, which is at the upper boundary of normal but not necessarily alarming on its own. He also had a hematocrit reading of 47%, reflecting a reasonable concentration of red blood cells, and a serum glucose measurement of 117 mg/dL, indicating that he was not overtly hyperglycemic at presentation. Examination of his thorax revealed diminished breath sounds and coarse crackles in one region, prompting imaging studies that confirmed the presence of an infiltrative process consistent with infection in the lung parenchyma. Notably, no layering fluid was detected around the lungs, and he shows no signs of fluid accumulation that might require invasive drainage. Despite appearing tired and somewhat short of breath, he was cooperative, with no indications of disorientation, and he was able to recall key events leading up to this visit. He described a mild but persistent cough productive mainly of clear sputum, with occasional streaking that seemed darker than usual. He had not recorded any truly high fevers and was surprised that his measured temperature was in the normal range, given his subjective feelings of warmth earlier in the day. He added that he felt his muscles fatigue more quickly, which he attributed to poor sleep and a general sense of malaise. Further questioning revealed that he has taken steps to reduce dietary salt and follows up regularly with his heart specialist, though he acknowledges challenges in strictly adhering to all recommended measures to prevent fluid overload. On physical examination, in addition to the abnormal breath sounds, his ankles had no obvious pitting edema at the time, and his jugular venous pulsations were not remarkably elevated. He denies any problems with appetite, though he has eaten less in the past couple of days due to fatigue. His neurological assessment was non-focal, and he conversed easily without confusion, allowing the team to rule out significant mental status changes at that juncture. Although he does require ongoing observation given his underlying medical vulnerabilities, he was notably calm and conversational, which reassured the staff that he was stable enough for further workup without immediate intensive measures. Overall, his presentation suggests a concerning pulmonary process that merits careful management, particularly in the context of his chronic heart and kidney issues. With no overt signs of extra fluid in the pleural space and clear primary signs of respiratory infection, additional diagnostic testing and supportive interventions were initiated to stabilize his breathing and address the likely infectious cause, all while monitoring his kidney status, cardiac performance, and electrolyte levels as treatment proceeds.

args: A 59-year-old male presented to the hospital after experiencing three days of progressively worsening cough, moderate shortness of breath, and a recent onset of confusion that prompted concern among those close to him. He reports feeling generally unwell for the past week, with intermittent chills and low-grade fevers that seemed to peak at around 37.9 degrees Celsius upon initial evaluation. Over the last month, he has noticed a gradual decline in his usual stamina, particularly when performing physically demanding tasks at home, although he continues to live independently and does not receive routine care in an institutional setting. He describes no history of episodes that would suggest emergent neurological deficits, and he denies preexisting problems with kidney function or chronic issues related to his liver. However, he has been managing a malignant process that was previously diagnosed and has involved occasional follow-up visits with oncology, although he states it remains under relatively controlled circumstances at this time. On examination, he appeared disoriented at times yet cooperative, occasionally struggling to focus on the conversation and needing repeated clarification of certain questions. His cardiovascular status reveals a heart rate of 88 beats per minute and an initial blood pressure reading of 118 mm Hg systolic, with no significant irregularities in rhythm or signs of volume overload apparent on auscultation. His respiratory rate is measured at 20 breaths per minute, accompanied by mild use of accessory muscles, and his pulmonary examination reveals diminished breath sounds at the right lung base together with dullness on percussion. Subsequent imaging, obtained promptly, confirms an underlying inflammatory process in the right lower lobe of his lung with accompanying fluid accumulation in the pleural space, consistent with an effusion that could partly account for both his respiratory discomfort and some of the abnormal exam findings. An arterial blood gas analysis shows that his baseline oxygenation is suboptimal, with a partial pressure of oxygen registering at 85 mm Hg on room air, aligning with clinical signs of compromised gas exchange. Concurrently, acid-base assessment indicates a pH of 7.41, which is within normal limits, suggesting no immediate systemic derangement in either metabolic or respiratory compensation beyond what might be expected in the setting of an acute pulmonary infection. Laboratory testing at admission reflects a blood urea measurement of 25 mg/dL, a mild elevation compared to typical reference ranges, and a serum sodium value of 139 mEq/L, which is well within normal parameters. His hematocrit is documented at 42%, demonstrating adequate if not robust erythrocyte volume, whereas his measured glucose level is 130 mg/dL, slightly above ideal but well within a range commonly observed in individuals experiencing acute stress or infection. He reports never having experienced a clinical event that would suggest a major vascular insult to the brain, and he consistently denies any history whereby he faced significant compromise in hepatic function. When asked about prior living arrangements, he affirms that he has been residing at home without needing specialized residential support, although he acknowledges feeling increasingly fatigued in recent weeks. His present condition is defined by persistent cough, feverish episodes, and the confusion that alerted those around him to a concerning change, spurring his decision to seek medical attention. Physical assessment and preliminary imaging both highlight findings suggestive of a bacterial etiology, with the presence of fluid in the pleural cavity raising additional management considerations. Despite the absence of longstanding kidney or liver problems, his mild metabolic derangements underscore the possibility of stress on multiple organ systems in the face of an acute infectious process, especially given his known background of a serious medical condition requiring interval oncologic oversight. He exhibits signs of moderate respiratory compromise, necessitating close monitoring and supportive measures, and his mental status fluctuation remains a prime concern requiring further elucidation to ensure there is no separate complicating factor. Overall, his presentation is consistent with an acute pulmonary process that demands prompt intervention to address both the infectious source and any associated pleural fluid accumulation, while carefully balancing existing health considerations linked to his past medical history.

The patient is a 23-year-old with a temperature of  101°F female presented with acute tonsillitis. The patient has no personal illness and no drug allergy. Throat examination reveals red enlarged tonsils with exudate (the patient did not allow taking a photo of her throat).
The patient has a history of repeated visits to the physicians for more than ten times per year with the same problem of acute tonsillitis. In each previous visit, the patient received penicillin as the antibiotic for the treatment, and the clinical problem was resolved, but the recurrence occurred within a few weeks.
The patient consulted with the physician in charge, who is a family physician, and gave the history that she was a prostitute and has to perform oral sexual intercourse with many different males. Nevertheless, she has no history of sexually transmitted infection, and her annual blood check, according to the legal regulation for the prostitute, for HIV serology and VDRL was negative. Furthermore, her annual gynecological examination result is within the normal limit. The differential diagnosis of recurrent tonsillitis in this patient is Group A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus pyogenes infection that requires microbiological examination for discrimination. In this case, the tonsil and throat swab were done, and the microbiological study revealed gonorrhea infection. The patient was treated with dual-therapy regimen with single-dose ceftriaxone intramuscular injection and single dose 1 g oral azithromycin according to the 2015 STDs Treatment Guidelines of CDC (). Her clinical symptoms disappeared within 1 week. In this case, health education on safe sex was also given to the patient. However, the patient lost follow-up, and it cannot verify the success and impact of health education. Whether there is any impact on her profession, a prostitute is still questionable.

args:
In March 2014, a 39-year-old Korean professional male soldier presented with a 6-month history of various nonspecific symptoms. He received hospital treatment and underwent several health examinations; however, no specific findings were observed. The symptoms were dizziness, fatigue, asthenia, irritability, elevated blood pressure (above systolic 160 mmHg), palpitation, eyestrain, and tinnitus, which aggravated with time.
The patient had an unremarkable medical history and was a current smoker (0.7 pack/day for 15 years). He consumed 700 mL of beer once a week. The patient had worked for 13 months in a newly constructed indoor firing range. He performed general management of the indoor firing range. His work included setting the firing range (preparing the bullets and targets) before the firing exercises, and cleaning and handling the disposed warheads after the firing exercises. There was much gray-black dust during the cleaning process; however, he did not wear any special protective equipment.
His BLL was measured owing to suspicion of heavy metal poisoning; 0.1 mL of whole blood was diluted with 0.8 mL 1%Triton™ X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) and analyzed for BLL using graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry (novAA 400p;Analytik Jena AG, Jena, Germany). The BLL was estimated at 64 µg/dL; therefore, the patient was diagnosed with lead poisoning and immediately withdrawn from his work to avoid further lead exposure. The standard for elevated BLL among adults is 10 µg/dL, as determined by the Adult Blood Lead Epidemiology & Surveillance from the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) (). The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)'s occupational exposure limit for BLL is 40 µg/dL (). Korean blood-lead criteria for the diagnosis of lead poisoning in workers is 40 μg/dL (). The patient was immediately admitted to the military hospital and investigated thoroughly. His examination revealed elevated blood pressure (150/110 mmHg), and the peripheral blood smear showed poikilocytosis. The liver function test, renal function test, complete blood count, and differential count were within normal ranges. His urine tested negative for microproteinuria. According to the examination by the neurologist, there were no significant findings in the electromyography, nerve conduction velocity, brain magnetic resonance imaging, and neurologic tests. The hearing test indicated mild hearing loss (hearing threshold: right/left = 35/35 dB) at 4 kHz, and he was diagnosed with dry eye by the ophthalmologist; he received treatments from the respective departments.
The treatment for lead poisoning included avoidance of the harmful factor (prohibition of work) for 3 weeks following which he underwent BLL measurement. The BLL was 39 µg/dL and the blood zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP) level was 45.1 µg/dL (reference range: 10–38 µg/dL). He showed no improvement of symptoms; hence, for an intensive BLL decrease chelation treatment was administered. For the chelation treatment, 1 gram of calcium-disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (CaNa2 EDTA) was mixed with 250 mL of 5% dextrose (JW Life Science Corp, Seoul, Korea) in water and was administered intravenously over 6 hours. The chelation treatment was given for 5 days, and the renal functions and liver functions were assessed daily. He completed 5 days of chelation treatment with no adverse effects. He was discharged after his blood pressure normalized (130/85 mmHg) and the subjective symptoms improved. Three weeks after the discharge (7 weeks after lead exposure avoidance), the BLL was 9.7 µg/dL ().
As evident from the workplace environmental monitoring (April 2 to April 15, 2014), the exposure in the air was an 8hours, time-weighted average of 0.015–0.387 mg/m3, which exceeded the acceptable standards (0.05 mg/m3) () (). From the field investigations, it was confirmed that a swirl formed in the indoor firing range from inappropriate filter use (using a charcoal filter instead of a dust filter) and poor design of the ventilation system. The dust accumulated as it was not discharged outside the indoor firing range, and entered the body through the respiratory tract and oral intake before and after the firing and during cleaning and disposal of the warheads.

args:
Our patient is a 43-year-old male with no significant past medical history who presented to our emergency room with a chief complaint of shortness of breath for the duration of 2 weeks. Two weeks before presentation, the patient was prescribed azithromycin by his primary care provider for a presumed diagnosis of pneumonia. On further history, the patient endorsed a non-productive cough associated with subjective fevers, chills, weakness and malaise for 1 month. He denied any past medical history of autoimmune, systemic or immune-compromising conditions. He denied any sick contacts, recent travel, tuberculosis or chemical exposure. He denied any nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, muscular weakness or neurological symptoms, orthopnea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. At baseline, he was able to walk approximately five blocks without getting dyspneic. He had no known drug allergies, took no other medications and his social history was negative other than 20 pack year smoking history. His family history was unremarkable as well. Our patient worked a desk job and his occupational history was negative for any kind of environmental pollutants known to be associated with lung disease. On admission to the emergency room, the patient was afebrile, with a respiratory rate of 26 breaths/min, heart rate of 115 beats/min, with a room air saturation of 75% and blood pressure of 90/60 mm Hg. Physical exam was pertinent for hyperkeratosis over the index fingers and his thumbs bilaterally along with thickening noticed over the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints. The rest of the physical exam was unremarkable. Early resuscitation was initiated and the patient was treated with 6 L of oxygen via nasal cannula and given 30 cc/kg of normal saline for fluid resuscitation with normalization of his blood pressure and heart rate prior to admission to the general medical telemetry unit.
On admission, his labs were remarkable for a white blood cell count of 25,000 units/L, hemoglobin level was 13.5 g/dL and platelet count of 385 × 109/L. On his metabolic panel, the patient’s sodium level was 133 mg/dL with a BUN of 13 mg/dL and creatinine of 0.69 mg/dL, with troponin being 0.09 μg/L and B-type natriuretic peptide being 79 pg/mL. His liver function was remarkable for a normal alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin level with aspartate transaminase and alanine transaminase of 259 units/L and 266 units/L, respectively with a CPK of 7,500 International Units/L. Albumin was measured at 2.4 g/dL with a globulin level of 4.2 g/dL. His arterial blood gas values were remarkable at 7.37/43/52 on 6 L nasal cannula oxygen. His lactic acid was elevated at 2.4 mg/dL. ESR on admission was 35 mm/h, CRP was 24 mg/L and procalcitonin level was 34 ng/mL. ANA showed a speckled patter with 1:320 titers, anti-Jo-1 antibody was also positive at 8 and anti-CCP antibody was positive at 153.
A CT scan of his chest on admission () showed a pericardial effusion measuring 1.3 cm in thickness. Non-confluent areas of ill defined opacities were also seen in both lungs with denser consolidations and air Broncho-grams mainly in the right middle lobe and both the lower lobes which were thought to be a combination of infiltrates and atelectasis. A chest X-ray () showed a small right-sided pleural effusion with a patchy right sided opacity. An echocardiogram (ECHO) evaluation revealed a moderate to large pericardial effusion (). A chest X-ray at the end of the course of treatment showed complete resolution of the prior findings ().
The patient required intensive care unit transfer 2 days into admission for worsening hypoxia and hypotension initially treated with 24 h of non-invasive positive pressure ventilation and subsequent mechanical ventilation. Despite maximal ventilator support and pulmonary artery catheterization for optimal fluid management, the patient continued to decline. A decision to pursue veno-venous (VV) extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) was undertaken for a diagnosis of severe acute respiratory distress syndrome 4 days into admission.
Owing to the radiographic and clinical findings suspicious for acute interstitial pneumonia and diagnostically negative bronchoscopic, viral, fungal, bacterial and mycobacterial serology, the patient was treated with broad spectrum antibiotics, namely, vancomycin, zosyn and azithromycin. The radiographic appearance, serologic findings combined with physical exam findings like “mechanic’s hands”, non-specific interstitial pneumonia with a pattern of ILD with elevated CK and positive anti-Jo-1, heightened the suspicion of a rare form of acutely progressing AS. Hence, the decision was made to treat the patient with high dose methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days, followed by 125 mg, four times a day for 4 days, followed by a taper of 60 mg, four times a day for 4 days. The steroid medication was further weaned down to prednisone 40 mg daily, a dosage which he was discharged on.
With regard to his cardiovascular status, the patient had minimal vasopressor requirements despite his initial shock physiology. Follow-up serial echocardiography noted worsening pericardial effusion, necessitating the need for both diagnostic and therapeutic pericardial window. Fluid analysis showed no signs of infection with discharge surveillance ECHO showing no re-accumulation of pericardial fluid.
The patient developed non-oliguric renal failure thought to be secondary to acute tubular necrosis from a combination of hypotension and sepsis. The patient did not require renal replacement therapy however. Rhabdomyolysis was not felt to contribute to renal failure with CPKs peaking at 7,500 International Units/L followed by a gradual fall.
The course was also complicated by an upper gastrointestinal bleed which endoscopically was attributed to erosive gastritis managed conservatively via proton pump inhibitors.
Over the course of his stay, the patient showed significant improvement in his respiratory, cardiovascular and renal status and was decannulated from ECMO in 2 days (yes sure it was 2 days) with transition to mechanical ventilation. The patient was extubated shortly afterwards and had significantly improved clinical dyspnea.

args:
Twenty-year-old male presented with an influenza-like illness (LI) with fever, cough, wheezing, shortness of breath, and myalgias. His symptoms began 3 days prior to hospitalization, and he visited an Urgent Care Center and was started on amoxicillin. On admission, his temperature was 38 °C (101.8 °F), pulse was 125/min, and respiratory rate was 22/min. Physical exam was unremarkable except for wheezing and conjunctival suffusion. His white blood cell (WBC) count was 5.7 K/mm3 (81% PMNs, 8% lymphocytes, 11% monocytes) and platelet count was 158 K/µL (n > 160 K/mm3). Serum transaminases and alkaline phosphatase were normal. His C-reactive protein (CRP) was 110 mg/L (n < 3 mg/L) and procalcitonin (PCT) was 0.12 mg/mL (n < 0.5 mg/mL). CXR showed a right middle lobe infiltrate thought to represent a bacterial CAP, and the patient was empirically started on ceftriaxone. Respiratory viral PCR (Film Array) was positive for adenovirus. Moxifloxacin was started on hospital day (HD) #3. He was transferred to the medical intensive care unit (ICU), and his temperature was 102.6 °F, pulse was 116/min, respiratory rate was 30/min, and oxygen saturation was 92% on oxygen at 4 L/min. Repeat CXR showed worsening pneumonia, involving right middle and lower lobes. Multiple sputum and blood cultures—obtained prior to antibiotic therapy—were negative. Empiric antibiotics were changed to meropenem, vancomycin, and levofloxacin for presumed bacterial co-infection.
On HD #4, fevers continued, and he was intubated. WBC count remained normal at 6.2 K/mm3. His erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) was 35 mm/h, CRP was 92 mg/L, and his PCT was 0.08 mg/mL (n < 0.5 mg/mL). Cold agglutinin titer was negative. Legionella sp. antibody and urine antigen were negative. Sputum and nasopharyngeal viral cultures were both positive for adenovirus. Repeat respiratory PCR remained positive for adenovirus, and his adenovirus titer was elevated at 1:128 (n ≤ 1:8). A dose of cidofovir was given. Since the fevers were clearly due to adenoviral pneumonia, multiple empiric antibiotics had no effect on his fevers. Accordingly, the infectious disease consultant repeatedly recommended discontinuing antibiotics for “presumed bacterial coinfection”. However, meropenem, levofloxacin, and vancomycin were continued for “possible bacterial co-infection” because the patient remained febrile ().
He was extubated on HD #8, but his 102 °F fevers continued. Repeat PCT was again unremarkable at 0.17 mg/mL (<0.5 mg/mL). CXR showed much improved right lung infiltrates, and by HD #12 he became afebrile and his CXR infiltrates had resolved. The eventual resolution of his severe adenoviral CAP should not be ascribed to a single dose of cidofovir.

args: A 76-year-old female presented to the hospital reporting several days of gradually increasing congestion, a frequent cough, and a sensation of breathlessness that worsened on the morning of admission. She recounted a low-grade fever that she measured at 37.5 degrees Celsius upon arriving, along with generalized fatigue and reduced appetite. In addition, she noticed an uncomfortable pressure in her chest when attempting minor tasks around the house, and climbing stairs caused her to pause for additional breaths more often than usual. At home, she usually manages her own daily routine and has not relied on extended-stay support, although she admits to slowing down somewhat over the years. She has a background of persistent difficulties related to fluid overload, necessitating ongoing attention to her cardiovascular status, especially during periods of physical activity or when she experiences any new exertional symptoms. She also has a long-standing hepatic issue that has required periodic evaluations by specialists, largely because she occasionally experiences mild upper abdominal discomfort and must avoid medications that could aggravate her condition. She denies ever having had a major vascular event in her brain, stating that she has not experienced sudden limb weakness, transient speaking problems, or other neurologic deficits that raised suspicion for a serious circulatory disturbance. She likewise denies significant kidney impairment, recounting that her laboratory values and urine output have remained within a stable range year after year, even when she has been mildly ill. In the week prior to her arrival, she dealt with chills and a productive cough, initially chalking them up to a routine seasonal ailment. However, her breathing challenges escalated, and she began feeling increasingly lethargic, prompting her to seek a thorough evaluation. On review of her condition at presentation, she was noted to have a distinctly elevated heart rate at 107 beats per minute and a respiratory rate of 27 breaths per minute, both of which placed her in a more acute physiological state than what she typically reports during routine clinic visits. Measurement of her systolic blood pressure showed a value of 130 mm Hg, which she stated was near the higher end of her usual range. Laboratory investigations performed on arrival indicated that her blood urea measurement, while not severely elevated, was above average at 24 mg/dL, drawing the attention of the admitting team given her new respiratory symptoms. Her serum sodium stood at 134, a level that bordered on the lower limit of normal, and further probing of her metabolic equilibrium through blood gas analysis revealed an arterial pH of 7.45, which pointed to a near-normal acid-base balance that did not immediately suggest complicated respiratory or metabolic derangements. The partial pressure of oxygen in her arterial blood was 76 mm Hg, consistent with the shortness of breath and mild hypoxia she was exhibiting, particularly since she had not required supplemental oxygen at home prior to this episode. Imaging of her chest uncovered an infiltrate along with a clear sign of fluid accumulation in the pleural space, which fit well with her complaints of increased coughing, shortness of breath, and an intermittent sense of heaviness on the affected side. A measured hematocrit of 43% did not point to severe anemia or an acute hematologic abnormality, and her serum glucose was discovered to be 145 mg/dL, an elevated value that she attributed partly to the stress of feeling ill and possibly her limited oral intake of any balanced nutrition. Despite her condition, she remained oriented, answered questions appropriately, and showed no change in her level of consciousness, suggesting that she maintained clarity in her thinking and was able to keep track of recent events without confusion. She described her cough as mainly productive of off-white to faintly yellow sputum, though it had become more frequent and occasionally triggered coughing fits severe enough to leave her short of breath. She recalled a single episode of waking in the night drenched in sweat, though she was uncertain if this was related to her low-grade fever or simply her disrupted sleep due to coughing. The admitting team noted that her functional capacity prior to this illness allowed her to handle mild household chores, including preparing meals, with only occasional breaks to rest due to fatigue linked to her chronic cardiovascular condition. At the time she arrived, no formal interventions or treatments had been administered, and her current status provided an unaltered snapshot of her physiologic state while she continued to complain of difficulty inhaling deeply and exhaustion after minimal activity. The clinical impression was that she presented with a pulmonary infection acquired outside a healthcare setting, made potentially more complex by her history of fluid overload and hepatic concerns, and the uncovered effusion in her chest imaging heightened the team’s suspicion of an evolving inflammatory process. She was taken for further testing, but throughout her initial workup, her vital numbers remained closely monitored, and her lab profile was consistently documented to capture her status before any therapeutic intervention, thus facilitating a complete understanding of her risks and formulating a targeted plan of care.

args: A 54-year-old man was brought to the emergency department by local emergency medical services after being found on the ground outside his home earlier in the morning. According to a neighbor, he had been complaining of dizziness and a throbbing headache for a few days prior, although he had not sought medical attention. On arrival, he was lying supine on the stretcher, and his eyes remained largely closed unless healthcare staff addressed him repeatedly and with clear verbal prompts. When spoken to in a calm but firm manner, he made an effort to open his eyes, but the action seemed delayed, as though he were struggling to maintain focus and follow the voice. Once his eyes were open, he appeared to look around the surroundings for only a brief moment before closing them again if nobody continued to speak. Accompanying him was a medical history that indicated mild atrial fibrillation managed with medication for several years, along with occasional episodes of poorly controlled hypertension. He reportedly had no known recent trauma or major surgeries, though the paramedics noted several superficial abrasions on his forearms and knees that may have resulted from a fall. Upon initial examination, the man’s spoken words were audible in volume but unusually strung together, showing limited coherence, and sometimes consisting of abrupt exclamations that did not align with the questions posed to him. For instance, when asked about the date and location, he mumbled a series of unrelated words, occasionally interjecting what sounded like random items or places that held no direct relevance to the setting. He showed no signs of fully understanding when asked straightforward orientation questions, and he seemed unable to express his own name or birthdate in a meaningful manner. The patient’s tone shifted unpredictably, as he sometimes yelled out sudden phrases that were unrelated to the conversation. Nonetheless, between these periods of disorganized speech, he attempted to form partial sentences, suggesting some awareness that he was being questioned, but the content of his speech did not provide accurate or context-appropriate responses. A family member who arrived shortly after the paramedics stated that it was unusual for him to speak in such a manner, as he had not displayed significant communication deficits in the past. On the physical exam, the man had a faint odor of alcohol on his breath, though it remained unclear whether he had been consuming it recently or if the smell was residual from prolonged ingestion earlier. His blood pressure measured high, though the exact values were not markedly outside his known hypertensive range. Heart sounds were relatively regular, with no new murmurs, but there was a mild irregular rhythm consistent with his history of atrial fibrillation. Respiratory rate was near the upper limit of normal but did not require immediate intervention. During the neurological assessment, when a gentle painful stimulus was introduced, such as slight pressure on the nail bed, the patient displayed a discernible reflex to pull his hand away, though his overall movement lacked full coordination and force. However, he did make enough of an effort to actively retract from the unpleasant sensation, suggesting that he retained some purposeful movement in response to noxious stimuli. He did not spontaneously reposition himself toward or away from the examining practitioner at other times, nor did he attempt to follow more complex motor commands if the stimulus was not noxious. Despite persistent questioning, he failed to demonstrate consistent orientation to time and place, and he offered little help in detailing any precipitating event that could have led to his current condition. Throughout this initial evaluation, his primary challenge appeared to involve his difficulty in producing meaningful, context-appropriate language, coupled with a delayed reaction in opening his eyes unless staff intentionally called out to him, and a reflexive withdrawal in response to uncomfortable sensations that suggested he could detect and react to pain, albeit in a disorganized manner.
What is the patient's Glasgow Coma Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

From the note, I can see:

- Verbal response: The patient has disorganized speech with limited coherence and inappropriate responses
- Motor response: The patient withdraws from painful stimuli but lacks coordination

This appears to be a clinical scoring system, so I should use the apply_clinical_score tool to calculate the GCS based on the documented behaviors.

This score indicates a moderate level of impairment in consciousness.

args: A 72-year-old male presents for evaluation following a minimally invasive abdominal operation completed a couple of weeks ago, during which small incisions were used to address his surgical needs. He reports that this recent intervention was considered a significant procedure, as it involved careful intra-abdominal manipulation requiring general anesthesia and extended postoperative monitoring. About three weeks before arriving at the clinic, he experienced a severe bloodstream infection that demanded aggressive intravenous antibiotics and close inpatient observation. He has not had any episodes of heart dysfunction or fluid overload during this same interval, and he denies needing any orthopedic devices that would immobilize a limb. He affirms that he has not suffered bone injuries to his hips, pelvis, or legs. Furthermore, he has not experienced multiple traumatic events leading to hospitalization. He notes an absence of superficial venous changes that would suggest engorged vessels on inspection. Currently, however, there is noticeable swelling in his lower extremities, predominantly at the end of the day, which is a newer complaint for him. He denies having any kind of specialized line placed into a central vein. He also reports no personal track record of prior occlusive venous events, including any clots in his legs or related problems in his lungs, and there is no awareness of similar clotting tendencies among his blood relatives. Previously, genetic testing failed to disclose the particular hereditary irregularity sometimes seen in individuals with elevated risk for coagulation problems, and he has never been told he carried a specific circulatory inhibitor that predisposes to clots. He recounts no history of an unusual and abrupt drop in platelets when exposed to common anticoagulants, and he has not been informed of any other abnormal condition that might predispose him to excessive clot formation. The patient has spent considerable time confined to bed in recent days, citing both fatigue and postoperative pain as reasons for remaining largely inactive. He does not describe any ongoing or previous issues with chronic intestinal inflammation, and he denies ever having a myocardial event characterized by damage to the heart muscle. He also states that he has never been diagnosed with a persistent condition affecting his airways or lung function. In his current state, he appears somewhat weak, yet alert, with vital signs largely within age-adjusted limits. Physical examination is notable for pitting edema over both lower legs, which is more pronounced on prolonged dependency. Cardiac auscultation does not reveal murmurs or extra sounds suggestive of acute heart compromise, and his pulmonary exam is free of rales or wheezes. His surgical incisions are healing appropriately, with only mild tenderness on palpation and no overt signs of local infection. He denies gastrointestinal complaints such as bloody stool, persistent diarrhea, or abdominal cramping. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, and he calmly recounts the details of his previous bloodstream infection without indicating any persistent fever or chills at present. He has no memory of ever requiring urgent intervention for chest discomfort suggestive of coronary artery blockage, nor has he carried a diagnosis requiring an inhaler or supplemental oxygen. Laboratory investigations at this juncture do not reveal abnormal blood counts or significant metabolic derangements, though imaging studies are underway to determine the extent of the swelling in his legs. Overall, he is concerned about his reduced mobility and lingering postoperative discomfort, which has contributed to him remaining predominantly in bed, but he expresses optimism about making a full recovery and returning to normal activities once he completes his rehabilitation plan.

A 57-year-old male arrived at the emergency department following a reported fall down a flight of stairs at home, where he was found lying unconscious by family members. On arrival, his airway was patent, though he appeared to be struggling to maintain a clear and consistent breathing pattern, with shallow respirations and occasional brief pauses between breaths. Family members reported that he has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both of which had been managed inconsistently due to multiple missed appointments. They also noted that he had complained of occasional headaches in the weeks leading up to this event, although he had not sought medical attention for them. At the time of initial assessment, his face and scalp showed abrasions consistent with a traumatic impact, including a small laceration near the left temporal region that was oozing a mild amount of blood. Further inspection revealed several contusions on both forearms, suggesting that he may have attempted to protect himself during the fall. His blood pressure was slightly elevated, and his pulse was somewhat rapid but still within a range that did not necessitate immediate cardiovascular intervention. He did not respond to verbal requests with any clear words, instead producing only muffled vocalizations without apparent meaning. When staff members addressed him loudly or attempted to gain his attention, there was no evident reaction in terms of rational speech or acknowledgment. Rather, he made low, throaty sounds that indicated some level of arousal but lacked coherent structure. In addition, he did not open his eyes spontaneously, even when spoken to or gently shaken by a nurse. It was only after a stronger stimulus, such as a firm press on the nail bed, that he briefly parted his eyelids, though he appeared to track nothing in the room and exhibited no purposeful gaze. His pupils were equal and reactive, but his gaze remained largely unfocused. On further neurological examination, when he was subjected to a more rigorous pressure stimulus—applied centrally—he bent his arms and pulled them inward in a manner suggestive of a non-purposeful withdrawal pattern. There was no attempt to push away the examiner or localize the exact site of discomfort, indicating a diminished ability to perform more discriminating motor responses at this time. Additionally, his legs twitched slightly during these stimuli, but he did not produce any overriding extension pattern in the lower extremities, nor did he attempt to move his body away in a directed manner. Vital signs revealed a heart rate elevated above normal resting levels, a blood pressure that remained high relative to his baseline as reported by his family, and a respiratory rate on the lower end of normal, with occasional erratic variations. Auscultation of his chest did not reveal definitive signs of aspiration, but his breathing pattern warranted close observation, and preparations were made to secure his airway if his respiratory effort continued to fluctuate. A review of his past records showed no documented history of cerebrovascular accidents or prior major trauma, though he did have a past admission for diabetic ketoacidosis, indicating some degree of metabolic vulnerability. At the time of presentation, he showed no purposeful communication and evidenced only fragmentary reactions to painful provocation, prompting immediate imaging studies to rule out intracranial hemorrhage or significant structural injury. args:
An 84-year-old man presented to our department with a 12-hour history of painless gross hematuria. This was his first ever episode. His past medical history included anemia, benign prostatic hyperplasia, appendicectomy, duodenal ulcer (treated surgically 40 years ago), and an abdominal aortic aneurysm. He was taking tamsulosin, finasteride, levocarnitine, and ferrous sulphate. The physical examination revealed a soft, nontender abdomen and isolated left leg edema. His blood pressure was 140/85 mmHg and his pulse was 75 beats/min. The per rectum examination revealed a smooth, slightly enlarged prostate. Laboratory investigations on admission showed the following: hemoglobin 10 g/dl, hematocrit 31.5%, platelets 162×109/l, BUN 36 mg/dl, and creatinine 1.5 mg/dl. Midstream urine (MSU) sample investigations revealed the following: specific weight 1,030, pH 5.5, white cells 2-4, and red cells >200 per optic field. Culture showed mixed growth of 104-105 cfu. Bladder irrigation was instigated, and urine samples for cytological examination were also taken. Cystoscopy showed decreased expansion suggesting compression from outside the bladder. At the point of compression, increased vascularization was noted in the bladder mucosa with no evidence of active bleeding. No trace of blood was identified coming from the ureteric orifices, the bladder neck, or the prostate. There was no evidence of intra-vesicular masses or other inflammatory changes. The kidney-ureter-bladder X-ray revealed no abnormalities.
The abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan with intravenous contrast revealed left-sided hydronephrosis and dilation of the upper third of the left ureter with no evidence of stones or related peri-ureteric mass. There was an abdominal aortic aneurysm starting 3 cm below the origin of the renal vessels and involving the aortic bifurcation and both internal iliac arteries (, ). The greatest diameter of the aortic aneurysm was 8.0 cm, and the left internal iliac artery's diameter was 11.5 cm (). The iliac artery aneurysm contained a peripheral thrombus, whereas the aortic aneurysm was partly calcified (). Inflammatory changes were present in the lower right peri-renal area. The right iliopsoas muscle was enlarged and of a heterogeneous texture. There was adjacent soft tissue swelling.
The cytological examination was negative for malignant cells. The hematuria was controlled by the fourth day of admission. The patient was discharged awaiting review by the vascular surgical team.

args: A 63-year-old male was brought to the emergency department by coworkers who discovered him collapsed in a restroom at their workplace, where he was reportedly last seen conversing in an agitated but largely incoherent manner earlier that morning. According to his colleagues, he had been complaining of feeling unusually fatigued and appeared somewhat disoriented over the past week, though no one had witnessed any overt trauma or sudden event that might explain his current state. On arrival, he remained in a supine position on the stretcher and did not raise his eyelids even when addressed by name or in a louder tone, nor did he seem to react when a nurse gently touched his shoulder or applied other benign prompts intended to gain a visual response. His past medical records, which arrived later in the hands of a family member, indicated he had been managing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease for several years and had a history of occasional episodes of poorly controlled blood pressure. Family also reported that he had experienced intermittent forgetfulness recently, though he had never been formally evaluated for cognitive decline. On examination, the patient produced words that were not responsive to simple questions, instead blurting out intermittent phrases that failed to match the context of the situation, including references to objects and people not present in the room. When asked directly if he was in pain or if he could state his name, he strung together fragments of speech that had little bearing on reality, occasionally pausing as though uncertain of what he was trying to convey before repeating similar disordered expressions. His voice rose in volume sporadically, but he never formed a coherent statement, nor did he respond with clarity when asked to identify his surroundings. Vital sign measurements showed a marginally elevated heart rate, a blood pressure that was near the higher end of normal but not critically elevated, and oxygen saturations that remained adequate on room air. His respiratory pattern was somewhat shallow, though it did not necessitate immediate airway intervention. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm without extra heart sounds, while auscultation of his lungs indicated distant breath sounds likely related to his chronic pulmonary condition rather than an acute process. There were no obvious signs of head injury, such as external bruising or lacerations, though a small contusion on his right forearm suggested a possible minor impact, possibly related to a fall or bump prior to his coworkers finding him. During the neurological assessment, he was subjected to mild but definite painful stimuli in the distal upper extremity, to which he responded by retracting the arm in a relatively brisk motion. However, he did not attempt to locate the source of discomfort or push the offending hand away, and he made no targeted effort to remove himself from the examiner’s grip beyond that initial recoil. Despite repeated attempts to prompt him with simple commands—such as asking him to squeeze fingers, raise a single arm, or move his legs in a controlled fashion—he provided no consistent, purposeful movements and showed no further reactivity beyond abruptly pulling back at moments of perceived irritation. Throughout the evaluation, he did not open his eyes of his own accord or in response to any combination of verbal or tactile attempts by the clinical team. Given his disorganized verbal outbursts, lack of visual engagement, and limited motor reaction apart from withdrawing from unpleasant sensations, arrangements were promptly made for imaging studies and laboratory tests to investigate potential metabolic derangements, central nervous system pathologies, or other acute causes behind his compromised condition. Prior to any interventions, he remained in the same unconscious posture on the stretcher, periodically uttering nonsensical words and sighs that did not correlate to external cues, while staff members prepared for a more detailed workup to identify the underlying etiology of his presentation.

args: A 63‑year‑old male with a history of chronic liver issues and mild hypertension arrived in the emergency department following several days of escalating confusion, jaundice, and a progressively altered level of consciousness. According to accompanying information, he was found at home in a state of near‑unresponsiveness, speaking incoherently when initially discovered and displaying minimal spontaneous movement. Upon presentation, he showed up up with an assessed Glasgow  Coma Score of  5. Due to this severely compromised mental status and concern for airway protection, he was placed on mechanical ventillation. Early testing of his arterial oxygenation revealed a relatively low concentration of inspired gases (FiO₂ 25 %). His measured partial pressure of oxygen was 56 mm Hg. Further laboratory results painted a picture of possible hepatic stress: his total bilirubin was measured at 3.0 mg/dL, suggesting a marked degree of hyperbilirubinemia higher than his reported baseline, and there was mild thrombocytopenia with a recent count of about 130 000 per microliter, which heightened concern for the progression of underlying liver impairment or another process affecting platelet production or consumption. Despite these worrisome laboratory findings, his cardiovascular parameters were relatively stable. He arrived with a systolic blood pressure around 130 mm Hg and a diastolic pressure near 85 mm Hg, indicating that he retained sufficient circulation without requiring any intravenous medications to raise or stabilize his pressure. Subsequent assessment of renal function showed a creatinine at 0.8 mg/dL, which is within a generally acceptable reference range and suggests that his kidneys were effectively filtering waste at the time of admission. Corroborating this was a robust daily urine volume of about 2 200 mL, which gave reassurance that his renal perfusion was not severely compromised, even in the context of his multiple other abnormalities. Clinicians noted that his depressed cognition, coupled with a noticeable yellowish discoloration of the sclera, posed questions regarding the extent of his hepatic dysfunction, and the moderate drop in platelets raised the possibility of a broader systemic process. He was not observed to have any notable bleeding tendencies, nor did he display bruising or rash that might suggest profound coagulopathy. There was, however, a subtle hue to the skin consistent with elevated bilirubin levels, and his abdominal exam did not reveal overt ascites but did elicit vague right upper quadrant discomfort on gentle palpation. Cardiopulmonary examination was relatively unremarkable aside from distant breath sounds transmitted through the ventilating device, while cardiac auscultation showed a regular rhythm absent of extra gallops or murmurs. Family reported that he had been increasingly drowsy over the preceding week, sleeping through most of the day, and that they noticed a gradual change in his skin color but had not sought immediate care until he became nearly impossible to rouse. The present plan included continued support of his breathing with the inserted airway tube connected to the machine delivering a controlled fraction of inspired gas, ongoing careful monitoring of his neurologic examination, repeat laboratory evaluations to assess trends in bilirubin and platelets, and a thorough search for precipitating factors that might have led to his deterioration. Although no overt circulatory compromise was identified upon initial assessment, the team remained vigilant for any sudden changes, given his fragile hepatic profile and significantly decreased awareness. The intention was to explore possible causes for his acute worsening, which could range from a new infection to a decompensation of an underlying condition, and to intervene accordingly once the precise etiology surfaced. Throughout his workup, meticulous attention was directed toward preventing further organ injury, and any interventions were guided by real‑time evaluation of his respiratory status, neurologic function, and laboratory trends.

args:
A 69-year-old man presented to the emergency room with two weeks of exertional dyspnea, unable to ambulate greater than 50 feet. Cardiopulmonary examination showed blood pressure of 131/82 mmHg, heart rate of 77 beats/min, a laterally displaced point of maximal impulse, bilateral dependent lung crackles, and bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Medical history included uncontrolled hypertension, uncontrolled hyperlipidemia, peripheral artery disease, 50 pack-year smoking history, and alcohol use disorder. No history of recreational drug use was reported. Patient had no prior surgical history. The differential diagnosis for the etiology of clinical heart failure in this patient included ischemic cardiomyopathy, alcoholic cardiomyopathy, hypertensive heart disease, and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.
On admission, a 12-lead electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with QS waves in leads V2-V4. Laboratory studies showed troponin I of 0.04 ng/ml and brain natriuretic peptide of 2498 pg/ml. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) showed left ventricular ejection fraction of 30%-35% with moderate to severe global hypokinesis. Given patient’s high likelihood of coronary artery disease, he underwent coronary angiography for workup of his cardiomyopathy, which revealed a diffusely diseased left anterior descending coronary artery (LAD) with worst stenosis up to 70%-80% (Figure -).
Patient was deemed not a candidate for coronary bypass graft surgery (CABG) by cardiothoracic surgery; thus, PCI with intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) guidance was performed. The LAD lesion was crossed using a Choice PT wire (Boston Scientific, Marlborough, Massachusetts, USA) and predilated using multiple compliant balloons with good expansion. A 3.0 x 28 mm drug-eluting stent (DES) was implanted in the distal LAD, 3.5 x 38 mm DES in the mid-LAD, and 3.5 x 28 mm DES in the proximal LAD with a very good final angiographic result (Figure ). The IVUS was used prior to stent implantation for lesion characterization and vessel sizing and post-stenting to confirm adequate stent expansion and apposition without any edge dissection. However, there was an occlusion of a septal perforating artery due to being jailed by the LAD stent struts (Figure ). We opted not to intervene on this septal branch to avoid disruption of LAD stent struts. Moreover, the patient did not have any clinical signs or symptoms from losing this septal branch. He did well and was subsequently discharged on appropriate medical therapy.
Six-month post-PCI, TTE showed improved left ventricular ejection fraction to 55%, but thinning of the midanteroseptum and midinferoseptum was noted (Figure -). No ventricular septal perforation was detected after evaluating the defect on multiple views with color Doppler.
