args:
An 82-year-old Japanese man presented at the emergency department with 4 weeks of generalized weakness and 2 days of dyspnea. His medical history included hypertension and prostate cancer, which had been treated by radical prostatectomy 5 years earlier. He was taking losartan and amlodipine for the hypertension. An initial physical examination revealed bibasilar coarse crackles in both lungs, and scratch-like erythematous skin lesions on his back (Fig. A). On admission, his blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation were 88/65 mmHg, 110 beats per minute, 22 breaths per minute, and 92% in ambient air, respectively. He was afebrile with a temperature of 36.3 °C. He had no signs of paralysis. Manual muscle testing revealed mild weakness of the iliopsoas and quadriceps muscles.
Laboratory evaluation revealed a white blood cell count (WBC) of 8900 cells/μL, hemoglobin (Hb) of 11.3 g/dL, and normal platelet counts (PLT). The level of creatine kinase (CK) was elevated at 924 U/L (normal range, 124–222 ng/mL). The levels of C-reactive protein (CRP), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), aspartate aminotransferase, and alanine aminotransferase were elevated at 5.6 mg/dL, 682 U/L (normal range, 124–222 U/L), 305 U/L, and 180 U/L, respectively. Kidney dysfunction was also detected (creatinine [Cr] at 2.81 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen [BUN] at 74.0 mg/dL). Chest radiography revealed multiple small pulmonary infiltrates in both lungs and additional high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest revealed the presence of peripheral consolidation in the left lower lung base, and ground-glass opacities in all 6 lung fields (Fig. ). Expectorated sputum was not good quality, and the sputum culture results were unremarkable; however, a pneumococcal urinary antigen test was positive. The patient was initially diagnosed as having community-acquired pneumonia and severe sepsis, and was subsequently started on fluid replacement and antibiotic treatment with ceftriaxone and azithromycin.
After antibiotic and fluid replacement treatments, the patient's blood pressure and kidney function improved, but his symptoms of dyspnea and weakness worsened on the fourth day after admission. Physical examination revealed fine crackles at the lung base. The patient also developed slight skin thickening on the lateral side of the fingers (consistent with mechanic's hand) and ulcerated palmar papules (Fig. B) in addition to already-existing multiple vertical linear streaks with spotty crusting on his back (flagellate erythema). A chest HRCT scan revealed exacerbation of the ground-glass opacities in the bilateral lung fields, without improvement of peripheral consolidation in the left lower lung base (Fig. B). A bronchoscopy was performed to examine the possibility of an infectious cause, but no noteworthy colonies were isolated in bacterial or mycobacterial cultures from the bronchoalveolar lavage sample.
Based on the clinical suspicion of acute interstitial pneumonia due to DM, high doses of methylprednisolone pulses (1 g/d for 3 days), 1000 mg of intravenous cyclophosphamide (IVCY), and oral tacrolimus (6 mg/d) were administered on the fourth day after admission (Fig. ). The laboratory data showed the following results: white blood cell count (WBC 7400 cells/μL, Hb 14.3 g/dL, PLT 11.4 × 104/μL, LDH 728 U/L, CK 1208 U/L, CRP 5.78 mg/dL, and KL-6 1146 U/mL). The patient's ferritin level was also elevated at 1995 ng/mL (normal range, 30–300 ng/mL). Following immunological and serological testing, anti-MDA5 Ab was found to have a high titer index (>150; normal range, 0–39). Anti-aminoacyl tRNA synthetase (anti-ARS) antibody, anti-transcription intermediary factor 1-gamma (TIF1-γ) antibody, anti-Mi-2 antibody, antinuclear antibody (ANA), proteinase 3-anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibodies (PR3-ANCAs), and myeloperoxidase anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibodies (MPO-ANCAs) were all negative. The patient was eventually diagnosed as having anti-MDA5 Ab-positive DM with RPILD. The patient received intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in addition to triple immunosuppressive therapy. However, his respiratory status exacerbated, and this was complicated by upper gastrointestinal bleeding without apparent coagulation abnormality on the ninth day after admission. Although the patient received intensive treatment with mechanical ventilatory support, continuous infusion of vasopressors, and continuous hemodiafiltration, he died of respiratory failure on the twelfth day after admission. The postmortem CT examination revealed widespread consolidations in both lung fields and dramatic loss of lung volume (Fig. C).
An autopsy was performed 2 hours after death. Both lungs revealed a congestive increase in weight (745 g for the left and 760 g for the right) with sporadic alveolar hemorrhages (Fig. A). Autopsy specimens taken from the lungs were consistent with DAD, with findings of hyaline membrane formation and fibrotic tissue covering the alveoli (Fig. B). A specimen taken from the right iliopsoas muscle showed intramuscular bleeding (Fig. C); however, a specimen from the quadriceps muscle showed no definitive presence of inflammatory cells (Fig. D). In addition, extensive intraparenchymal hemorrhages were observed, including the gastric and bowl mucosa, spleen (Fig. E and F), and left adrenal gland.

args: A 57-year-old male arrived at the emergency department after being discovered sitting on a narrow curb near a busy intersection, appearing disoriented and slightly unsteady. According to the individual who called for help, he had been there for at least thirty minutes, moving very little and occasionally calling out for assistance in a voice that was not entirely coherent. Emergency medical responders noted that his clothing was wrinkled as though he might have been lying down earlier, but they did not observe any obvious external wounds requiring urgent surgical attention. At the time of initial evaluation by hospital staff, he did not spontaneously lift his eyelids or show any particular awareness of the commotion around him. It was only after a nurse spoke loudly and repeatedly addressed him by an assumed name that he blinked and slowly looked toward the source of the voice, keeping his eyes open for just a limited interval before drifting closed again. When clinical personnel attempted simple questioning about his current location and reason for being there, he mumbled incomplete sentences that lacked clarity, sometimes repeating words or abruptly shifting subjects in a manner that suggested he was struggling to organize his thoughts. Despite these individual phrases being recognizable as speech, the overall meaning behind them was difficult to establish, and he seemed unable to account for recent events or to state any clear plan for the day prior to his arrival at the curb. His personal effects did not include any identification cards or medical alert tags, so further background information was initially lacking. On physical assessment, his vital signs showed a slightly rapid pulse but otherwise acceptable parameters in terms of blood pressure and respiratory rate. He displayed no overt signs of acute respiratory distress, and his oxygen saturation remained within normal limits without supplemental support. Although he would respond to questions in a disconnected manner, he still appeared able to pronounce words and form short phrases, albeit with an unfocused quality that made him repeat the same items or stray into tangential topics unrelated to the examination. In an effort to assess his neurological status more thoroughly, a healthcare professional applied firm pressure to his upper arm and later to his shoulder. On each occasion, he reached precisely toward the area being pressed, attempting to move the examiner’s hand away from the spot rather than simply pulling his limb back reflexively. This behavior indicated that he was aware of the specific location of discomfort, even if he was not fully oriented enough to provide a coherent history. Additionally, the patient exhibited no clear focal deficits such as one-sided weakness, facial asymmetry, or unequal grip strength upon cursory examination. However, the team remained concerned about the combination of his incoherent statements and lapses in attention, which left him unable to consistently engage in conversation. No advanced imaging or laboratory results were available at this early point in his evaluation, though blood was drawn and an urgent cranial scan was ordered to rule out any structural lesions or metabolic derangements that could explain his presentation. While waiting for initial results, he continued to exhibit the same pattern of intermittently glancing up when spoken to, uttering words that reflected a confused understanding of his whereabouts, and offering minimal detail about possible past medical issues. He was found to have minor bruises on both forearms, the origin of which he could not explain, raising uncertainty about whether he might have experienced a fall or other incident prior to being found. The admitting personnel initiated close neurological monitoring and documented his fluctuating level of attentiveness, with plans to consult additional specialists if abnormalities surfaced in his initial workup. Throughout this early evaluation, he did not show signs of combativeness or agitation, but the disorganized comments and vague recollection of circumstances signaled the need for prompt investigation into the cause of his condition.

args:
A 28-year-old female from Nepal who recently immigrated to the United States presented to the ED with complaints of abdominal distention, fatigue, and six weeks of progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness. She was recently discharged from an outside hospital with a new diagnosis of decompensated liver cirrhosis with ascites. She reported drinking homemade alcoholic beverages daily while in Nepal but was unable to quantify the amount, given inconsistent history due to language and cultural barrier. She was taking thiamine 100 mg oral daily, which was not continued on admission. On evaluation, she was extremely malnourished with a BMI of 14.6 due to lack of appetite and a diet consisting mostly of rice and beef broth soup. Physical exam demonstrated scleral icterus, abdominal distention with tenderness in the right upper quadrant, shifting dullness,hepatosplenomegaly, and jaundice. Laboratory studies were notable for platelet count of 96,000/uL, albumin 2.8 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 52 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 30 U/L, total bilirubin 34.5 mg/dL, international normalized ratio (INR) 1.8. Her initial model for end-stage liver disease (MELD) score was 26, and she was evaluated for liver transplantation. Other causes of cirrhosis, including viral, hemochromatosis, autoimmune, Wilson’s disease, primary biliary cirrhosis, and primary sclerosing cholangitis, were excluded, and it was determined that she had alcohol-induced cirrhosis. Due to her extreme malnutrition and weakness, she was listed inactive on the transplant list.
The patient had bilateral lower extremity weakness and paresthesias preventing her from standing unassisted. She also demonstrated areflexia, tenderness to palpation, and pain with movement of lower extremities. Initial workup for weakness included vitamin B12, folate, and vitamin B6 levels. Vitamin B12 and folate returned within normal limits, but she was found to be deficient in vitamin B6 at 11.7 nmol/L and was started on replacement therapy. After 10 days of supplemental vitamin B6 and physical therapy, she had not improved, and neurology was consulted. An electromyogram was performed and consistent with severe sensorimotor axonal polyneuropathy with active denervation. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis was also recommended and showed a slightly elevated protein at 47 mg/dL but not elevated enough to be consistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS). Additional labs were drawn, and she was found to have a low thiamine level at 55 nmol/L (normal: 70-80 nmol/L). She was started on replacement therapy with slow improvement. By six months of aggressive thiamine replacement, she could walk 200 feet with a walker and stand without assistance.

args:
A 60-year-old Chinese male presented to the hospital with acute shortness of breath and lower limb swelling. He has a past medical history of stroke disease, chronic kidney disease, and ischaemic cardiomyopathy with a mildly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 45%. His cardiovascular risk factors include diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and hyperlipidaemia.
Clinical examination revealed bibasal crepitations with bilateral pitting oedema. The blood pressure on admission was 150/87 mmHg with sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 129 beats per minute.
Initial arterial blood gas (ABG) on admission () revealed Type 2 respiratory failure, while chest X-ray done showed bilateral pulmonary congestion.
He was initially started on non-invasive ventilation and intravenous frusemide and glyceryl trinitrate infusion. Despite initial clinical improvement, the patient became increasingly restless with worsening hypoxaemia and decision was made for intubation and mechanical ventilation. However, he suffered a pulseless electrical activity cardiac arrest peri-intubation with a low-flow time of 20 min before return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC).
He was started on our institution’s protocolized post-cardiac arrest care bundle () which included continuous rSO2 monitoring and TTM with mild induced hypothermia at 33°C. An oesophageal probe was inserted for core body temperature monitoring. Initial ABG post-ROSC revealed a Type 2 respiratory failure with pH 7.14, pCO2 54, pO2 110, HCO3 18, and SaO2 97%. Patient was sedated and paralyzed and ventilator settings were optimized to correct the respiratory acidosis. Patient was ventilated with a tidal volume of 7 mL/kg predicted body weight and at a ventilator rate of 28 breaths per minute. Initial cerebral oximetry revealed an rSO2 66% (left) and 67% (right).
Post-resuscitation SpO2 was maintained above 94% and mean arterial pressure (MAP) was constantly above 80 mmHg without the need for inotropes or vasopressors. However, it was subsequently noted that the cerebral rSO2 had decreased to 33% (left) and 35% (right) and a repeat ABG showed resolution of the respiratory acidosis with pH 7.3, pCO2 33, pO2 138, HCO3 16, and SaO2 99%.
Ventilator settings were immediately adjusted and the minute ventilation reduced by lowering the ventilator rate to aim for mild therapeutic hypercapnia with a target pCO2 of 50–55 mmHg. PEEP was reduced from 12 cmH2O to avoid an excessively high intrathoracic pressure which may impede cerebral venous drainage. Patient was also started on dobutamine infusion to improve the global and cerebral perfusion as the central venous oxygen saturation (ScvO2) of 62% reflected a systemic oxygen delivery/consumption mismatch, while a Pcv-aCO2 gap of 14 mmHg suggested a low-flow state. The above interventions resulted in an improvement in cerebral rSO2 to 55% (left) and 51% (right) ().
Patient was cooled for 24 h and then gradually rewarmed. A repeat transthoracic echocardiogram revealed severe global hypokinesia with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 26%.
He was subsequently extubated after 6 days in the cardiac intensive care unit with improvement in Glasgow coma scale score and underwent rehabilitation in the general ward. On discharge, he had a cerebral performance category of 1 and is now on follow-up in the outpatient clinic.

args:
A 57 year old female from Sindhuli, Nepal, with no known medical comorbidities other than smoking, presented to the emergency department with two episodes of hematemesis over 24 hours. She had a 4-month history of dyspepsia, melena and fatigability. On physical exam, she was pale, blood pressure was 90/50 mm Hg with a heart rate of 130 beats per minute. Other examinations, including abdominal exam, were unremarkable. She was resuscitated with intravenous fluids and packed cell transfusion.
Laboratory parameters with normal ranges in parenthesis, are as follow:
Complete blood count before transfusion: white cell count 7.9 (4–10) × 10
9/L; neutrophils 70%; lymphocytes 26%; monocytes 4%; red blood cells 2.8 (4.2–5.4) × 10
12/L; haemoglobin 9 (12–15) g/dL; platelets 295 (150–400) × 10
9/L.
Biochemistry: random blood sugar 124 (65–110) mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 49 (17–45) mg/dL; creatinine 0.9 (0.8–1.3) mg/dL; sodium 140 (135–145) mmol/L and potassium 4 (3.5–5) mmol/L.
Hepatic panel: bilirubin total 1 (0.1–1.2) mg/dL and direct 0.6 (0–0.4) mg/dL; alanine transaminase 35 (5–30) units/L; aspartate transaminase 40 (5–30) units/L; alkaline phosphatase 98 (50–100) IU/L; albumin 3.5 (3.5–5) g/dL
After stabilization, she underwent upper gastrointestinal endoscopy the same day which revealed an ulcer (10 mm × 6 mm) in lesser curvature of the stomach without active bleeding. The provisional diagnosis was gastric ulcer due to
H. pylori infection. The main differential diagnosis was gastric carcinoma, hence biopsy was taken from the ulcer.
She was started on triple therapy regimen empirically for
H. pylori eradication containing clarithromycin 500 mg, amoxicillin 1 gm and pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily for 14 days, and discharged. When she returned for follow-up, the histopathology showed fungi with broad ribbon like morphology, fruiting bodies consistent with mucor in the ulcer, as well as in granulation tissue but no necrosis or vascular thrombi (
). It was also positive for
H. pylori in Giemsa staining. She was diagnosed with gastric mucormycosis with
H. pylori coinfection. But the confirmation of mucormycosis via culture or molecular tests could not be done because of unavailability of these tests in our facility. The abdominal imaging was not done because there was no evidence of invasive form of the disease in histopathology.
She was readmitted and started on liposomal amphotericin B, 5 mg/kg on the first day followed by 10 mg/kg for the next 13 days. She completed a total of 2 weeks of therapy without any complication. She had no dyspepsia, melena or hematemesis by then. She was then discharged with posaconazole 300 mg once daily, as an oral step-down therapy. However the blood level of posaconazole to ensure the therapeutic level was not tested due to its unavailability in resource- limited setup in Nepal.
On repeat follow up in 1 month, she was asymptomatic. Repeat endoscopy showed a healing ulcer in lesser curvature of stomach. Repeat biopsy showed absence of fungal hyphae. She took posaconazole for total 2 months and stopped the medicine due to financial constraints. She was doing well when she was last followed up 4 months later.

args: A 63-year-old woman with chronic impairment of host defenses was brought to the hospital by her family after becoming nearly unresponsive over the preceding 24 hours. On arrival she was difficult to awaken, produced only minimal motor responses to painful stimulation, and did not open her eyes or speak. Temperature was 36.5 °C. Blood pressure measured 119/68 mm Hg. The monitor showed a heart rate of 97 beats/min and a respiratory rate of 25 breaths/min. No therapeutic interventions had been started prior to the critical care team’s assessment. Arterial blood gas analysis showed a near-physiologic pH of 7.39, indicating that systemic acid-base status had not yet shifted markedly despite the profound neurologic depression. Serum chemistries revealed sodium 146 mEq/L and potassium 4.7 mEq/L. Hematocrit was 42 %, and the leukocyte count was 9,300 / mm³. Serum creatinine was notably elevated at 3.1 mg/dL, without a documented history of long-standing renal dysfunction, raising concern for an acute decline in filtration capacity. Because of her immunologic vulnerability, the team kept a high index of suspicion for occult infection or other atypical processes that could explain her severe encephalopathy. Supplemental oxygen was provided at an inspiratory fraction of 52 %. While on this level of support, the calculated alveolar-to-arterial oxygen gradient measured of 220 mm Hg, suggesting moderate impairment of pulmonary gas transfer. She remained deeply obtunded with a Glasgow Coma Score of 4, made no purposeful movements to voice, and required close airway surveillance in case ventilatory drive deteriorated further. Relatives stated that until this episode she had been conversational, though more fatigued in recent weeks. She had not undergone any recent procedures, and no operation was being planned at the time of admission. At transfer to the intensive unit, no intravenous fluids or vasoactive agents had yet been administered while the team pursued a rapid diagnostic work-up for metabolic, infectious, or structural causes of her life-threatening change in mental status.

args: A 62-year-old female with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of a dull ache behind the right knee that began yesterday afternoon while she was attempting to ambulate from her bedroom to the bathroom. Nine days ago she was discharged from an outside hospital after an elective right hemicolectomy for complicated diverticulitis; during that admission she remained largely supine for the first four postoperative days, and since returning home she reports spending most hours in bed because of fatigue and abdominal discomfort, rising only for meals and toileting. She denies any history of malignancy, clotting disorder, or prior vein thrombosis, and she has not required a cast, brace, or splint on either lower extremity. Today she noted that her right ankle and lower calf appeared somewhat puffier than usual and that the skin over that region left an indentation when she pressed with her thumb, yet she did not observe comparable changes in the opposite limb. She specifically denies redness, warmth, or diffuse swelling from groin to foot, and she has not experienced sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitations. She denies recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, night sweats, or urinary complaints. Current medications include enteric-coated aspirin 81 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and postoperative oxycodone 5 mg taken sparingly for incisional pain; she is not on hormone replacement, oral contraceptives, or anticoagulation. Vital signs on arrival: temperature 98.6 °F, heart rate 86 bpm, blood pressure 138/78 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. She appears tired but in no acute distress, speaking full sentences without dyspnea. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable with regular S1/S2, no murmurs, clear bilateral breath sounds, and no jugular venous distention. Abdominal surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact with mild perincisional tenderness expected for postoperative day 9, and no abdominal distention or guarding. Musculoskeletal and vascular examination of the legs reveals normal skin temperature bilaterally and symmetric palpable posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses. The right lower calf and ankle demonstrate 1+ pitting edema that is not present on the left side; tape-measure circumference at 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity shows a difference of 1.2 cm. There is no cord-like induration, varicosities, or engorged superficial channels over either calf or thigh. Gentle palpation over the right femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and along the course of the peroneal veins elicits no focal tenderness. Range of motion at the right knee and ankle is full and pain free except for the vague ache she describes deep in the posterior knee while standing. Sensation is intact and motor strength is 5/5 in all major muscle groups; there is no weakness, foot drop, or spasticity. Neurologic examination is otherwise nonfocal. Laboratory studies drawn in triage demonstrate hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelet count 235 × 10^3/µL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, INR 1.0; comprehensive metabolic panel is within normal limits. Given her recent major abdominal surgery less than two weeks prior, the prolonged time confined to bed greater than three consecutive days, and the new unilateral dependent edema, venous thromboembolic disease is high on the working differential. Cellulitis is considered less likely in the absence of erythema or warmth, and musculoskeletal strain does not fully account for the discrete pitting edema. No alternative explanation appears as compelling, and there is insufficient evidence to suggest a competing diagnosis of equal probability. Duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity has been ordered to further evaluate for deep venous occlusion, and anticoagulation decisions will be deferred pending imaging results and discussion with surgery regarding postoperative bleeding risk.

args:
A 33-year-old man, without known co-morbidities, was brought into the emergency department with sudden onset of headache and severe central chest pain, radiating to the back. The patient arrived 3.5 hours after the onset of symptoms and was still in severe pain.
The blood pressure was 180/125 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute (bpm), temperature 36.1°C, oxygen-saturation 98% and the respiratory-rate was 28 per minute. An arterial blood gas (ABG) revealed significant lactate acidosis with pH 7.14, pO2 of 15.4 kPa, pCO2 of 4.5 kPa, bicarbonate of 12 mEq/L, base excess of −16 mmol/L and lactate value of 12 mmol/L. A 12 lead electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with slight ST-depressions in the leads V4–6. 
Bedside echocardiography revealed regional hypokinesia in the lateral area of the left ventricle, and an estimated left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 20–30%.
The patients’ blood samples demonstrated abnormal with a troponin T of 300 ng/L, white blood cell count of 39 × 109/L and normal c-reactive protein.
An immediate thoracic/abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan was performed, testing negative for aortic dissection or large pulmonary embolism. However, a 6 cm large tumor in the left adrenal gland was found in .
During the first hours of observation, the patient’s symptoms spontaneously resolved. His vitals normalized and a repeated ABG showed a rising pH (7.33) and decreasing lactate (5.6 mmol/L). 
Meanwhile, the patient’s previous history was explored in more detail. The evening before his admission, he had consumed multiple energy drinks, but denies the intake of alcohol or any other substances. 
Nine years earlier, he presented with similar symptoms at another hospital, likewise had spontaneously recovered and was quickly discharged without a firm diagnosis. Similarly, 4 years prior to this admission, the patient was acutely hospitalized with headache, back pain, nausea, elevated lactate, white blood cell count and blood pressure. That admission summary described that all symptoms remitted, his vitals as well as blood values normalized over a few hours and the patient was discharged after a day of observation, without finding a plausible explanation to his symptoms. 
Due to these findings and the suspicion of pheochromocytoma-induced cardiomyopathy, the patient was admitted. During the 7-day admission, the patient’s vitals remained normal and blood samples including troponin T slowly normalized. 
A repeated transthoracic echocardiography showed a LVEF of 45% in .
Plasma cortisol, renin, aldosterone and a 24-hour urine sample for steroid metabolites were within normal range. 3-Metthoxyadrenalin was elevated to 2,5 nmol/L (normal range: < 0,46 nmol/L) as well as 3-Metthoxynoradrenalin at 29,6 nmol/L (normal range < 1,09 nmol/L). 
During this observation, the patient reported no symptoms and was discharged for planned surgery in an outpatient setting. 
Due to the size of the tumor (6 cm in diameter) and hence a slightly increased risk of malignancy, open left-sided adrenalectomy was performed []. 
The patient was discharged on the fifth postoperative day after uncomplicated surgery and observation period. Follow-up showed normalization of 3-metthoxyadrenalin and 3-metthoxynoradrenalin, and the patient had not experienced any symptoms or complications. 
The left adrenal gland was found with a well-defined pheochromocytoma with a maximum diameter of 25 mm. The tumor morphology was with the growth of large nests and solid areas, high cellularity and areas with spindle cells as well as areas with profound nuclear pleomorphism and nuclear hyperchromasia. Thus, the Pheochromocytoma of Adrenal Gland Scales (PASS) score was 8 in []. It is not possible to predict the malign potential of a pheochromocytoma from the histopathology, but a PASS score > 4 indicates potential for aggressive clinical behavior. Thus, the patient will be followed with annually blood tests (3-metthoxyadrenalin, 3-metthoxynoradrenalin) lifelong. [].

args: A 62-year-old female was brought to the emergency department after being found in a drowsy and incoherent state on her front porch by a neighbor who noticed her lying partly slumped against a railing early in the morning. Upon her arrival, she was initially unresponsive to casual conversation and did not make any attempt to open her eyes when medical staff spoke to her or gently touched her shoulder. Only when a more forceful physical stimulus was applied did she briefly lift her eyelids, appearing momentarily startled before letting them close again. Her past medical records, quickly retrieved by staff, indicated a longstanding history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and intermittent stages of chronic kidney disease for which she had been prescribed medication, though it was unclear whether she had been taking her prescriptions reliably. Family members later stated that she had recently been complaining of increased fatigue and episodes of confusion over the past week, which they attributed to stress or possible hypoglycemia, though she apparently did not use a home glucometer regularly. At the time of examination, she was able to form short sentences when asked direct questions, but her words frequently strayed into disorganized recollections of irrelevant topics, and she struggled to name her current location correctly. Despite efforts by the clinician to orient her, she provided inconsistent answers about the date and was unable to recall precisely what had happened prior to her discovery outside. She would sometimes repeat portions of staff questions back as if echoing them, suggesting some incomplete grasp of her surroundings, yet she retained enough coherence to occasionally respond “yes” or “no” in a manner that hinted at partial understanding, though it lacked clear accuracy. Her pulse was slightly elevated, and her blood pressure was moderately high, which could have reflected pain, stress, or chronic underlying issues. Her skin was somewhat cool but without notable discoloration, and she demonstrated no obvious focal neurological deficits such as unilateral weakness or facial droop on initial inspection. Pupillary reactions appeared intact, but her overall level of alertness remained diminished. When more pronounced pressure was applied to her arms and sternal area, she responded by drawing her limbs inward in a flexed posture rather than using purposeful strength to push away or locate the source of discomfort. This repetitive pattern occurred each time similar stimuli were introduced, signaling that she was capable of a definite reaction yet lacked the coordinated movement needed to remove or stop that stimulus. Throughout the assessment, she made several confused references to people who were not present, spoke about tasks she claimed to have completed earlier that day, and repeatedly expressed uncertainty about why she was in a medical setting. Laboratory tests and imaging studies were ordered to clarify whether metabolic derangements, infections, or an intracranial event could be contributing to her presentation. Blood samples were sent to evaluate her renal function and glucose control, given her history of diabetes and kidney issues. Although she did produce coherent speech on occasion, her inability to maintain consistent orientation and her repeated attempts to return to a passive, semi-unresponsive state raised concern that she might deteriorate if the underlying cause was not promptly addressed. Despite these sporadic moments of relative lucidity, the overarching picture was one of reduced awareness and partial responsiveness to stimuli, necessitating immediate neurologic, metabolic, and cardiovascular evaluation while she remained under close observation and supportive care.

args:
A 41-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and previous non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction with normal coronary arteries (MINOCA) three years prior presented with symptoms of sharp chest pain, dry cough, and shortness of breath, which had started two days prior to the presentation. Her vital signs were remarkable for a blood pressure of 122/91 mmHg, heart rate of 180 beats per minute with an irregular rate, a temperature of 36.4 °C, respiratory rate of 24 breaths per minute, and an oxygen saturation of 98% on 5 L nasal cannula. On examination, the patient appeared to be in mild distress. No jugular venous distention was appreciated. Her chest auscultation revealed bilateral crackles.
A nasopharyngeal swab polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was positive for COVID-19 and blood cultures showed no growth. Chest X-ray revealed patchy bilateral peripheral lung infiltrates (Figure ), and CT angiography of the chest showed no pulmonary embolism but revealed bilateral patchy ground-glass lung infiltrates consistent with COVID-19 pneumonia (Figure ). EKG showed a new atrial flutter with a rapid ventricular response (Figure ). Transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a new low left ventricular (LV) ejection fraction of 35-40%, with LV hypertrophy, as well as a granular appearance of LV myocardium suspicious for infiltrative cardiomyopathy (Figure ). Echocardiogram obtained one year prior had shown LV ejection fraction of 50-55% with LV hypertrophy.
The patient was started on intravenous (IV) diltiazem drip, but her heart rate was difficult to control; hence she was started on IV amiodarone and eventually converted to sinus rhythm. She was started on IV heparin for anticoagulation. Her respiratory status deteriorated, and she was subsequently intubated and mechanically ventilated. The COVID-19 pneumonia was treated with a combination of steroids, antibiotics, remdesivir, and convalescence plasma. She improved clinically and was discharged home on oral amiodarone, metoprolol succinate, and warfarin.
CMR obtained about two months from the date of admission showed extensive myocarditis with a typical pattern of sub-epicardial and mid-wall delayed enhancement (Figure ). There was no edema to indicate the acuteness of the disease. LV systolic function was normal with an ejection fraction of 60-65%. These findings confirmed that the patient had suffered from an acute illness at the time of her initial presentation, causing acute symptoms, a drop in LV ejection fraction, and the development of new arrhythmia, which had all resolved by the time of CMR acquisition.

args: We report a case of 49-years-old male who received a kidney from a live unrelated donor since 7 years ago. During these seven years multiple low grade fevers was being appeared every year that healed without a diagnosis. On 29 May 2013, he presented with 39 °C fever associated with dysuria, oliguria, right lower quadrant abdominal pain, anorexia, cough and dyspnea. The patient had also history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension and gout. Blood pressure measured 130/90 mmHg, pulse rate 96 per minute, and respiratory rate 18 per minute. Laboratory tests were as follow: BUN= 19 mg/dl, Cr= 2 mg/dl, Na= 133 mEq/l, K=4.5 mEq/l, FBS=130 mg/dl, ESR= 50 mm/hour, CRP= 44.1 mg/dl, WBC= 5.8 ×106/µL, RBC= 4.02 × 106/µL, Hg= 11.3 g/dl, hematocrit= 33.6%, MCV 83.58 fl, and platelet= 171 × 106/µL. In sonography investigation, his kidney graft was 123×55 mm with normal parenchymal echo. The test for cytomegalovirus (CMV Ag-pp65) was negative and enterobacter growth was seen in the blood culture. In high resolution computed tomography (HRCT), an increase in heart size, mediastinum vascular swelling, reticular densities, septal thickness, bronchial wall thickness, mild bronchiectasis, patchy and nodular alveolar densities on the inferior lobe of both lungs were seen. Spiral CT scan showed micro-consolidation patchy lesions on the inferior lobe of both lungs (). Bronchoscopy showed hyper-vascularity in the trachea, and in broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) growth of non-A, non-D streptococci were detected with no growth of acid fast bacilli or mycobacterium tuberculosis. After 17 days of antibiotic and antifungal therapy, his fever did not corrupt. Spirometry results, showed a decreased FEV1 to 75%. On June 23th, inferior lobe wedge resection of the left lung was performed. In macroscopic pathologic examinations, fragments of lung tissue measuring 5×2×2 cm and gray brown and soft tissue in serial cross sections were observed. Moreover, microscopic evaluations conducted on issue removed during a procedure (). Finally, the diagnosis of BOOP was reported with no evidence of malignancy. Sirolimus was withdrawn and cyclosporine was started and dosage of prednisolone was increased. His fever was discontinued and general condition as well as pulmonary symptoms were gradually improved.

args:
A 43-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of dyspnea and chest pain after shouting in a quarrel with his wife. He had presented to a local clinic with fever, cough, sputum, and odynophagia 3 days previously, and was treated for acute pharyngitis. Before the onset of symptoms, he was in good health and had no significant past medical history except for 50 pack-years smoking history. Vital signs on admission were blood pressure 120/70 mm Hg, heart rate 100/min, respiration rate 28/min, and body temperature 38.7℃. On physical examination, there was swelling and tenderness in the left cervical area. Crackles were noted in both cervical areas. Coarse breath sounds were noted in both lung fields, and crackles were noted in the left lower lung field.
Leukocyte count was 13000/mm3 (85.2% neutrophils, 9.4% lymphocytes, 4.7% monocytes, 0.2% eosinophils), hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL, and platelet count 229000/mm3. The results of arterial blood gas analysis in room air were pH 7.491, PaCO2 25.2 mm Hg, PaO2 62.4 mm Hg, HCO3- 21.8 mmol/L, and SaO2 94.2%. Liver function tests were normal. Serum total protein was 6.5 g/dL, albumin 3.5 g/dL, blood urea nitrogen 20.0 mg/dL, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, sodium 135 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatine phosphokinase 431 IU/L, lactate dehydrogenase 658 IU/L, and high sensitivity C-reactive protein 30.7 mg/dL. Urinalysis was normal.
An initial chest radiography showed linear air trapping parallel to the border of the trachea, bilateral pleural effusion that was more severe on the left side, and consolidations in both lower lungs (). A chest computed tomography (CT) scan revealed air collection around the airway and mediastinum, infiltration around the mediastinum, and bilateral pleural effusions ().
Laryngoscopy was performed by an otolaryngologist, and a small pharyngeal perforation in the right side of the vallecula was seen. A cervical CT scan also showed lacerations in the same area (). Analysis of the pleural fluid on the left side was consistent with parapneumonic effusions (pH 8.0, red blood cell count 3840/mm3, white blood cell count 2880/mm3, neutrophils 95%, total protein 4.6 g/dL, albumin 2.5 g/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 27198 IU/L, glucose 27 mg/dL). Intravenous moxifloxacin was started. In addition, chest tubes were inserted on both sides and supplemental oxygen was administered. Laryngoscopy was repeated 2 days later, and the previously-noted small pharyngeal perforation was not seen. Blood and pleural fluid cultures were negative, but fever and leukocytosis persisted. Antibiotics were broadened to piperacillin-tazobactam and amikacin. Fifteen days after admission, a follow-up chest radiography showed resolution of air trapping around the mediastinum and regression of parapneumonic effusions. The patient's symptoms and laboratory results continuously improved, and the patient was discharged 29 days after admission.

args: A 46-year-old woman with recurrent left flank pain for the previous 5 months was admitted to the Cancer Hospital of Guangxi Medical University and Guangxi Cancer Research Institute in October 2020. She presented with the following symptoms: absence of urinary frequency, urinary urgency, painful urination, and haematuria visible to the naked eye. The patient had undergone right radical mastectomy 5 years previously, and pathological examination had confirmed a non-specific type of invasive breast cancer with no vascular or nerve invasion within the slice. Immunohistochemistry results at that time were as follows: E-cadherin (+), cytokeratin (CK)5/6 (–), epidermal growth factor receptor (–), androgen receptor (+, 90%), oestrogen receptor (ER) (+, 80%), progestin receptor (PR) (+, 80%), and proliferation marker protein Ki-67 (+, 40%). With human epidermal growth factor receptor (HER)-2 (2+), a diagnosis of invasive breast cancer T1N1M0 stage II was established. Adjuvant chemotherapy after surgery and at least 5 years of endocrine therapy were the treatment modalities advised to this patient, with local radiotherapy as an alternative; however, she declined chemotherapy and local radiotherapy and received endocrine therapy with tamoxifen only for 5 years. Physical examination during the latest hospital admittance revealed pressure pain in the left upper middle ureter. Serum tumour marker tests showed the following elevated values: carcinoembryonic antigen: 39.61 ng/ml (normal value, ≤ 5.0 ng/ml); cancer antigen 125, 57.2 U/ml (normal value, ≤ 35.0 U/ml); carbohydrate antigen 15-3, 258.9 U/ml (normal value, ≤ 31.3 U/ml); and CK19 fragment, 4.58 ng/ml (normal value, ≤ 3.3 ng/ml). No significant abnormalities were found in other laboratory tests. Ultrasonography revealed that the left renal collecting system was separated by approximately 2.0 cm, indicating that left hydronephrosis was not obvious. Abdominal CT and MRI showed tumours in the pancreas, left adrenal gland, and upper part of the left ureter, and showed folding back of the double J stent at the lesion, suggesting narrowing of the ureter lumen (). The wall adjacent to the upper part of the left ureter was slightly thickened. 18F-FDG PET/CT revealed multiple lymph node metastases in the thoracic cavity and abdomen, and larger lymph nodes of approximately 3.5 × 2.2 cm were located next to the abdominal aorta with radioactive concentration. The maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) was 10.7. The tumours invaded the pancreas and the transition area between the left ureter and renal pelvis. Radioactive concentration was observed in the ureteral lesion (), but no radioactive concentration was found in the urine of the left dilated renal pelvis. The tumour in the upper ureter and the hilum of the kidney showed increased metabolism; however, the shape, contour, and path of the left upper ureter was not significantly altered (). Transurethral ureteroscopy showed a rigid upper and middle ureteral wall, narrow lumen, and no endogenous tumour. Ureteral stenting was challenging to perform, and a viable route for obtaining tumour tissue using fine-needle aspiration could not be found for pathological examination. Thus, tumour tissue was obtained from the hilum of the kidney and the upper segment of the ureter using laparoscopy. Postoperative immunohistochemistry results were as follows: CK7 (+), CK20 (–), villin (–), GATA-3 (+), ER (+, 80%), PR (+, 90%), HER-2 (–), and Ki-67 (+, 40%), and the primary tumour was confirmed to be breast cancer. Based on the advice of chemotherapists, systemic chemotherapy was recommended to the patient, however, the patient did not return to the hospital for treatment and was lost to follow-up.

args: A 65-year-old female presented to the emergency department complaining of several days of progressively worsening cough, intermittent chills, and mild difficulty breathing. She noted that her cough became more persistent over the past 48 hours with occasional production of light yellow sputum. At home, she recorded a low-grade fever and used over-the-counter fever reducers without significant relief. On examination, she appeared slightly tachypneic, with a measured respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute. Her pulse was recorded at 110 beats per minute, and her blood pressure was 115 mm Hg systolic on arrival. She admitted to experiencing increased fatigue and some generalized malaise. A temperature of 100.1 degrees Fahrenheit was documented, and auscultation of the chest revealed crackles in the right lower lung field consistent with an infectious process. She had dealt with a significant cardiac issue in the past, which required medical management to prevent fluid buildup in her lungs and peripheral tissues, and she continued to follow a regimen to mitigate these episodes. She also disclosed a remote neurological event that led to a period of rehabilitation, although she had largely regained her usual level of mobility afterward. She denied any chronic problems that would suggest persistent kidney dysfunction, outlining that her laboratory tests had historically been stable. She was unaware of any concerning conditions characterized by abnormal cell growth, stating that she had never received such a diagnosis. Laboratory findings at this presentation showed a sodium level of 133 mEq/L, which was slightly below normal, and a blood urea nitrogen measurement of 27 mg/dL. Her serum glucose was measured at 135 mg/dL, and hematocrit was recorded at 38%. An arterial blood gas test revealed a pH of 7.36, and the partial pressure of oxygen measured 82 mm Hg, indicating some degree of respiratory compromise but without the need for immediate assisted ventilation. A chest radiograph demonstrated a focal opacity in the right lower zone strongly suggestive of pneumonia, and there was no radiographic indication of a significant fluid collection within the pleural space. She reported no recent significant changes in exercise tolerance, although she had chosen to rest more often than usual in the past week due to her cough and low stamina. She mentioned occasional dizziness, which she attributed to feeling under the weather rather than any chronic underlying issue. Physical examination revealed unremarkable findings apart from the noted right-sided lower lung findings and mild tachycardia. Her skin was warm, and peripheral perfusion remained adequate. The patient’s past experiences with episodes of fluid retention and her previous neurological recovery shape her current risk profile, especially in the context of an acute infection. Her symptoms arose a few days prior to presentation, and she initially tried over-the-counter treatments, which failed to alleviate her feverish sensation and shortness of breath. She did not mention any new exposures to sick contacts outside of her household and denied recent travel. She was fully conscious and oriented, conversing well despite short bursts of cough. According to her account, she had not experienced changes in bowel or urinary habits. She seemed somewhat concerned about her progressive breathing symptoms, prompting her to seek urgent evaluation. Overall, her initial presentation was suggestive of a straightforward infectious process, but clinicians took into account her documented background of cardiac and neurological vulnerability when assessing her overall condition, particularly given her borderline low sodium and elevated heart rate. All findings were documented prior to any acute interventions or aggressive management in the emergency department, and her vital signs and laboratory data pointed toward an infectious etiology that required prompt diagnostic and therapeutic measures.

args: A 70‑year‑old male with a Glasgow Coma Score of  3 arrived at the hospital after being found at home in a profoundly altered mental state, with family reporting that he had been minimally responsive for the past day and unable to communicate or follow commands. Reportedly, he had been complaining of fatigue and nausea for about a week, but no one noted any overt injuries or acute events that might explain his sudden decline. Measurement of his arterial blood gases revealed a partial pressure of oxygen of 76 mm Hg while receiving FiO₂ 29 %, still suggesting compromised respiratory efficiency. Despite breathing spontaneously, his circulatory status was precarious upon arrival: his blood pressure measured 85/51 mm Hg, and his extremities were cool to touch. He had arrived already attached to several intravenous medications: DOPamine at 1.5 mcg/kg/min to provide mild cardiac support, DOBUTamine at 0.2 mcg/kg/min to bolster contractility, norEPINEPHrine at 0.09 mcg/kg/min to boost vascular tone, and EPINEPHrine at 0.01 mcg/kg/min for additional heart and vascular effects. No other vasoactive or inotropic infusions were running at that time. Laboratory evaluation showed a platelet count of 185 000 / µL, indicating no immediate bleeding risk, but his total bilirubin was 2.6 mg/dL, pointing toward liver compromise. Kidney function was also impaired with a creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL, and his recorded daily urine output was 950 mL, well below normal expectations and suggestive of reduced renal perfusion. Heart sounds were distant on auscultation, and no distinct murmurs or gallops were heard, though assessment was limited by his compromised state and ongoing infusions. His skin was mottled and cold, a yellowish tinge in the sclera hinted at bilirubin buildup, and no obvious signs of infection were present, although sepsis remained on the differential. Because his vital signs were tenuous from the start, a meticulous approach was taken to manage the low readings, monitor his breathing status, and expand the workup with imaging tests and further laboratory panels. Despite the near‑normal platelet count, the elevated bilirubin, impaired kidney function, and severely depressed level of consciousness demanded urgent attention. Preparations were made for intensive care admission and for possible advanced airway protection, acknowledging that his condition could progress rapidly without aggressive intervention.

args: A 54-year-old male arrived in the emergency department after experiencing two days of progressively worsening shortness of breath, fatigue, and lightheadedness. He reports minimal oral intake over the previous day, along with a new onset of weakness that caused him to spend most of his time lying in bed. On arrival, his level of alertness appeared diminished, as he was able to open his eyes spontaneously and respond to questions, although he occasionally required repeated prompts to stay focused and follow commands. His speech was coherent, but he seemed disoriented to the exact time of day, and his responses were delayed. On formal neurologic assessment, his Glasgow  Coma  Score (GCS) was 13. His blood pressure was found to be markedly low at 78 mm Hg systolic and 55  mm Hg diastolic, raising concern for poor peripheral perfusion. Examination of the skin revealed cool extremities and a somewhat delayed capillary refill, further suggesting compromised circulatory status. Cardiac auscultation revealed; a measurement of his arterial blood gases showed a partial pressure of oxygen at 78  mm Hg. His respiratory rate was slightly elevated, and he displayed moderate effort with each breath, though not acute respiratory distress. Routine laboratory investigations disclosed a reduced platelet count of 160 000 per microliter, which was mildly below the typical reference range, but not profoundly low. Total bilirubin was 1.5 mg/dL, suggesting a mild elevation that may be related to either hepatic congestion or early hepatic dysfunction, given his presentation. Renal parameters demonstrated a creatinine level of 1.4 mg/dL, which is elevated for someone without a known history of chronic kidney disease, potentially indicating acute kidney injury. His urine output was self‑reported to be 900 mL in the past 24 hours, which seemed low for his usual fluid intake and might be tied to decreased perfusion. Oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry hovered in the low  90s, and he was tachycardic on exam, with a fast yet regular pulse. A careful neurological examination was performed, revealing that he could maintain gaze, move all extremities to command, and vocalize appropriately; however, his responses to more complex instructions were intermittent, suggesting a mild compromise in higher cognitive function. To support his hemodynamics, he was started on dobutamine at  2 mcg/kg/min to improve cardiac performance and norepinephrine at  0.03 mcg/kg/min to maintain vascular tone. These therapies were initiated with the goal of stabilizing his blood pressure and improving organ perfusion, given his diminished output and suspicion of end‑organ strain. No additional bolus medications had been provided before this infusion, and he had not received any nebulized therapies or other critical interventions at the time of initial assessment. An electrocardiogram demonstrated a sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes. A portable chest radiograph was obtained and showed mild vascular congestion in the lung fields, although no overt consolidation or large effusion was evident. Despite his challenged volume status and potential early organ dysfunction, he remained capable of speaking in coherent sentences, albeit with noticeable fatigue. Further clinical recommendations included careful fluid management, additional imaging, and serial laboratory monitoring to track his metabolic and hematologic parameters, as well as close neurological and cardiopulmonary observation.

args:
A 39-year-old male with a five-year history of moderate ulcerative colitis (UC), as per Montreal classification, was admitted to the emergency department with worsening abdominal and rectal pain secondary to a C. diff infection of one week. Patient had a multi-week history of diarrhea that was waking him up at night leading up to the C. diff diagnosis. One week prior to ED admission, his GI had ordered precautionary leukocytes and C. diff due to history of UC, and both returned positive. Per GI note, "Vancomycin prescribed as this patient has IBD and patients with IBD can develop spontaneous C. diff infections and need to be treated regardless of toxin presence." He had no prior history of antibiotic use over the past three months or triggering factor for C. diff infection.
Over the course of the week, his symptoms progressed, and he claimed to have nausea, vomiting, and hematochezia.
The patient presented with a past medical history of depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, and obstructive sleep apnea. His allergies included trazodone and amoxicillin/clavulanate. His family history was unrevealing. His surgical history included cholecystectomy and thyroglossal duct cyst removal in 2019.
This patient was a nonsmoker. He denied alcohol or drug use. He tried to maintain a healthy diet of lean proteins, fruits, and vegetables.
Initial vitals on presentation included: temperature: 98.6 F, pulse: 90 beats per minute, respiratory rate: 15 breaths per minute, pulse oximetry: 95%, blood pressure: 119/87 mmHg, weight: 217.16 lbs, and body mass index: 32.14.
General surgery was consulted upon CT scan with IV contrast revealing new extraluminal gas in the perineum surrounding the anus and distal rectum (Figures , , , ). He was examined and subsequently diagnosed with rectal perforation.
Preoperatively, the patient’s physical exam revealed an uncomfortable looking young man in mild distress due to abdominal pain. An abdominal exam revealed no distention, masses, rebound, or guarding. The absence of McBurney’s sign, Murphy’s sign, and peritoneal inflammation was noted. There was no active rectal bleeding at the time of physical examination. Laboratory data revealed an elevated c-reactive protein of 66.4. Complete blood count and complete metabolic panel were otherwise within normal limits. Despite infection and imaging reflecting perforation, white blood count was found to be 5.9 k/cmm.
After review of the available data, assessment of clinical condition, and deliberation with the emergency department physicians and the patient’s primary gastroenterology physician, the surgery team decided the patient would require an urgent exploratory laparotomy with total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy due to the diagnosis of rectal perforation secondary to C. diff colitis on chronic UC.
The patient was immediately brought to the operating room. The primary incision was made from the subxiphoid to the pubic symphysis within two hours of initial presentation to the hospital. The colon was visualized and resected; thus, a total colectomy was undergone. The pre-rectal space was opened to identify signs of purulence or injury, which found crepitus and severe UC causing circumferential ulcerations and a deep ulcer in the midline anteriorly. These ulcers traversed all layers of the rectal wall. A drain was placed to drain potential abscess or infection from the pre-rectal space.
The patient left the OR in stable condition after enduring no complications intraoperatively. He remained in the inpatient surgical unit for five days, during which he continued to advance his diet and ambulate successfully. He was discharged after an education session on ostomy bag maintenance and told to follow up with the general surgery team and gastroenterology team in one week. At his one-week follow-up post-op visit, the patient was doing well, ostomy output was draining appropriately, and he denied fever, nausea, or vomiting. No secondary follow-up surgery was required.

args: A 34-year-old female with no prior history of thromboembolic disease, chronic neurologic deficit, or limb casting presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of progressive swelling and tightness of her right lower extremity that began two days earlier. She reports that the entire leg, from the groin down to the ankle, has felt “heavy” and appears visibly larger than the opposite side; she noticed difficulty fitting into her usual trousers and shoes despite no recent weight gain. Using a cloth measuring tape at home, she recorded a circumference at mid-calf of 42 cm on the right compared with 38 cm on the left, a difference of approximately 4 cm, and she states that the swelling seems uniform rather than localized to the ankle or foot. Six weeks ago she underwent an elective laparoscopic total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for symptomatic uterine fibroids; the operative note documented an estimated blood loss of 200 mL, and she was discharged on postoperative day two with instructions for early ambulation. She adhered to these instructions and denies having spent prolonged time in bed at home, on a plane, or in a motor vehicle after surgery. She is otherwise healthy, takes only a daily multivitamin, and has no personal or family history of malignancy, clotting disorders, varicose vein procedures, or miscarriage. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, cough, fever, chills, trauma, or recent infections. There has been no calf tenderness when she presses on the muscle bulk herself, but she describes a diffuse dull ache in the thigh when standing for long periods. She has not noticed bulging surface veins, skin discoloration, ulceration, or rashes. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. On examination she is afebrile with a blood pressure of 118/72 mm Hg, pulse 82 bpm, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. She ambulates into the exam room without aid. Inspection of the lower extremities reveals the right leg to be globally enlarged compared with the left; there is fullness of the thigh compartments and the calf with subtle tautness of overlying skin, but no erythema, mottling, or varicosities. Circumferential measurement 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity confirms 41 cm on the right versus 37 cm on the left, consistent with the patient’s report. Popliteal fossa and femoral triangle palpation elicit no focal point tenderness, though the patient notes a generalized sense of pressure when the examiners’ hands encircle the thigh. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) are 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill is less than 2 seconds, and sensation as well as motor strength are intact. There is no pitting edema over the dorsum of the foot. Abdominal examination shows well-healed laparoscopy port scars without hernia or tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Laboratory results obtained at triage demonstrate hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelet count 235 k/µL, international normalized ratio 1.0, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL. A pregnancy test is negative. She has no drug allergies, uses alcohol socially, does not smoke, and denies illicit drug use. Given the uniform swelling of the entire right leg, the documented 4 cm discrepancy in calf circumference, the recent major pelvic surgery within the past two months, and the absence of an alternative explanation that would better account for her presentation, the clinical concern for an acute proximal venous thrombosis is high. Duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity has been ordered along with additional coagulation studies; therapeutic interventions have been deferred pending imaging results.

args: A 29-year-old man with no history of venous thromboembolism, malignancy, or chronic medical conditions arrived to the urgent care ward late this morning complaining of progressive aching and fullness in his right lower leg that began forty-eight hours earlier. He reports that six weeks ago he underwent an open reduction and internal fixation of a displaced left clavicular fracture sustained during a bicycle accident; the operation lasted roughly three hours under general anesthesia, required inpatient monitoring overnight, and was otherwise uncomplicated. He completed a short course of oral opioids and has since returned to desk work as a software engineer without any prolonged convalescence in bed and without use of limb casts or braces. Since surgery he has been generally active, walking his dog and commuting on public transportation daily, and he denies any extended automobile or air travel. Two days ago he first sensed a cramping discomfort in the posterior aspect of the right calf after a routine ten-minute walk from the train station; the pain intensified when he stood for long periods and lessened somewhat when he elevated the leg. By yesterday evening he noticed that the right pant leg felt tight and, concerned, measured the circumference of both calves with a tape he uses at the gym: at a point 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity the right side was 40 cm compared with 36 cm on the left, a discrepancy of 4 cm that persisted this morning and prompted his visit. He denies swelling of the thigh or groin, denies redness or streaking of the skin, and has not observed any rope-like superficial veins. He further denies fever, chills, night sweats, unintended weight loss, trauma to the leg, recent soft-tissue infection, insect bites, or joint pain. There is no chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, palpitations, or syncope. His only medication is 600 mg ibuprofen taken occasionally for shoulder soreness; he takes no hormonal therapy, supplements, or anticoagulants. Family history is negative for clotting disorders, premature cardiovascular disease, or cancer; both parents are alive in their fifties. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On presentation he is alert and in mild discomfort but able to ambulate independently. Vital signs: temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 124/76 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable with clear breath sounds and a regular cardiac rhythm without murmurs or gallops. Abdominal examination reveals a well-healed 5 cm incision in the left upper quadrant without tenderness or masses. Inspection of the lower extremities shows the right calf to be noticeably fuller than the left, while both thighs and ankles remain symmetric; skin coloration is normal without erythema, cyanosis, or ulcers. No varicose or engorged superficial venous channels are visible. Measurement repeated with a flexible tape confirms a 4 cm difference favoring the right side at the previously described point, whereas thigh circumference measured 15 cm above the patella is identical bilaterally. Palpation along the expected course of the posterior tibial and popliteal veins elicits a distinct, focal tenderness that is absent on the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly itself produces only mild diffuse soreness. There is no Homan sign, though the patient reports a pulling sensation when the ankle is dorsiflexed. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—are 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill is less than two seconds, and sensation and motor strength are intact throughout. There is no palpable cord, no warmth or tenderness over the Achilles tendon, and no crepitus of the knee or ankle joints. Examination of the skin elsewhere shows no rashes or petechiae. Laboratory studies sent prior to any pharmacologic intervention reveal hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL, platelet count 235 k/µL, white blood cell count 6.2 k/µL, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, AST/ALT within reference range, INR 1.0, and aPTT 30 seconds. A urine toxicology screen is negative, and a rapid antigen test for SARS-CoV-2 is negative. Because the patient exhibits a unilateral calf enlargement greater than 3 cm, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway confined to the calf rather than the entire limb, a recent major operative procedure within the past twelve weeks, and no clinical findings to suggest cellulitis, musculoskeletal injury, ruptured Baker cyst, or other more plausible explanation, the working impression strongly favors an acute thrombus in the proximal calf or popliteal segment of the right lower extremity. Compression duplex ultrasonography of the right femoral-popliteal system has been requested urgently, baseline coagulation parameters have been documented, and the patient has been advised to remain in the department until imaging is completed.

args: A 64-year-old post-menopausal woman presented to the emergency ward late this afternoon complaining that her left lower limb had “ballooned” over the preceding twenty-four hours. She explained that two weeks earlier she was admitted to a community hospital for an influenza-associated pneumonia that required intravenous antibiotics and supplemental oxygen; although she never needed ventilatory support, she remained largely confined to bed in a monitored room for five consecutive days and afterwards spent another four days at home reclining on a couch because of lingering fatigue. She finally felt strong enough yesterday to attempt household chores and, upon standing, immediately perceived a dragging heaviness in the entire left leg from groin to ankle, accompanied by a dull ache that intensified when she tried to climb stairs. She denied any isolated trauma to the hip, thigh, knee, or calf, and has not noticed erythematous streaking, pruritus, insect bites, fluctuance, or ulcers. There was no antecedent long-haul automobile or air travel, she has not worn an orthopedic boot or cast, and she retains full voluntary movement of both legs without sensory loss or weakness. Past medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes and stage 1 hypertension, managed respectively with metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril 10 mg every morning; she has never experienced venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, myocardial infarction, or congestive heart failure. She underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy eight years ago and a screening colonoscopy last year, and there have been no operative procedures, endoscopies, or invasive joint injections since. Medications do not include estrogen preparations, glucocorticoids, selective SERMs, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. She quit smoking fifteen years ago after a 20-pack-year history, drinks one glass of wine on special occasions, and has never used illicit drugs. Family history is negative for hereditary coagulopathies, premature coronary disease, or malignancy. Review of systems elicited no fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, palpitations, abdominal distension, or urinary complaints. Upon arrival her vital signs were temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 138/82 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats/minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. She sat upright without respiratory distress and conversed in full sentences. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear to auscultation and percussion throughout. The abdomen was soft with normoactive bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Examination of the lower extremities was remarkable for striking asymmetry: the left limb, from the proximal thigh to the dorsum of the foot, appeared uniformly enlarged with a glossy sheen to the skin; the thigh circumference at mid-femur exceeded that of the right by several centimeters, and the calf and ankle were similarly prominent, though an exact tape measurement was deferred until after imaging to avoid unnecessarily manipulating the limb. A lattice of bluish, non-tortuous superficial channels was conspicuous along the medial thigh and posterior calf and became more distended when the leg was placed in a dependent position for thirty seconds. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia produced an immediate rebound with no lasting indentation, and comparable pressure over the contralateral side yielded identical findings, indicating the swelling was not of a pitting character. Palpation of the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior compartment elicited a vague pressure sensation but no discrete focal tenderness or palpable cord. Knee and ankle joints moved through full active and passive ranges without crepitus, effusion, or localized warmth. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill was less than two seconds, and cutaneous sensation to light touch and pinprick remained intact bilaterally. There was no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no groin masses, and the contralateral limb was completely normal in contour, temperature, and color. Point-of-care laboratory studies drawn prior to any pharmacologic intervention revealed hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, platelet count 254 × 10⁹/L, white-cell count 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, and an international normalized ratio of 1.0; liver enzymes were within reference limits. An electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm at 86 beats/minute without ST-T abnormalities. The history of prolonged recumbency exceeding three days, the global enlargement of the entire symptomatic extremity, the emergence of conspicuous collateral superficial veins, the absence of pitting edema, absence of neurologic impairment or casting, and the lack of any recent surgery, cellulitis, lymphedema, or musculoskeletal injury that could equally account for the presentation make an intraluminal obstructive process within the deep venous system the leading consideration, and a duplex ultrasound from the iliac segment to the ankle has been requested on an urgent basis while anticoagulation decisions await imaging confirmation.

args:
A 65-year-old Hispanic male was brought to the emergency department (ED) complaining of worsening dyspnea, generalized malaise and weakness, and decreased oral intake for two weeks. His past medical history included systemic arterial hypertension, dyslipidemia, and COPD resulting from long-standing nicotine use. Previous pulmonary function test (PFT) results were not available, and the patient denied the use of supplemental oxygen therapy at home. The patient was alert, aware, and oriented to time, person, and place. He denied any recent travels or exposure to sick contacts. His vital signs on arrival to ED were as follows: blood pressure of 85/51 mmHg (mean arterial pressure of 58 mmHg), a respiratory rate of 28 breaths/minute, a heart rate of 114 beats/minute, a temperature of 102.8°F, and an oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry of 88% while breathing ambient air. Physical examination revealed decreased left lung air entry with rales on auscultation, and abdominal examination was unremarkable. Initial laboratory findings showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 19,500 cells/mm3 (normal: 4,000-11,000 cells/mm3), a sodium level of 129 mEq/L (normal: 136-145 mEq/L), a bicarbonate level of 11 mEq/L (normal: 21-31 mEq/L), a blood urea nitrogen (BUN) level of 78 mg/dL (normal: 8-27 mg/dl), a serum ionized calcium level of 0.90 mg/dL (normal: 1.13-1.33 mg/dl), and a creatinine level of 7.7 mg/dL (normal: 0.8-1.3 mg/dl). Other abnormal laboratory results included an alanine aminotransferase (ALT) level of 80 U/L (normal: 7-52 U/L), a lipase level of 473 U/L (normal: 11-82 U/L), an alkaline phosphatase (AP) level of 173 U/L (normal: 34-104 U/L), a troponin level of 113 pg/mL (normal: 0-20 pg/ml), and a creatine phosphokinase (CPK) level of 1,103 U/L (normal: 38-174 U/L). Urine analysis showed large red-blood cells and proteinuria. Arterial blood gas (ABG) showed a pH of 7.22 (7.35-7.45), a pCO2 level of 25 mmHg (normal: 35-45 mmHg), and a paO2 level of 79 mmHg (normal: 75-100 mmHg), while patient receiving oxygen therapy through high-flow nasal canula at 60% fraction of inspired oxygen (FIO2). Lactic acid levels, lipid profile, total bilirubin, and coagulation studies were within normal limits, and urine toxicology screen was negative for recreational substances. Initial chest radiography (CXR) showed left lung opacity with consolidation (Figures , ). A critical care medicine evaluation was requested, and the patient was admitted to ICU with the diagnosis of severe sepsis with septic shock likely resulting from CAP. Aggressive intravenous fluids resuscitation and vasopressors (norepinephrine, vasopressin) were initiated on arrival to ICU, and the patient was subsequently intubated and placed on mechanical ventilation because of worsening tachypnea and increased FIO2 requirements. Empiric intravenous antimicrobial therapy with ceftriaxone (2 g daily) and azithromycin (500 mg daily) was initiated, along with steroids (hydrocortisone 50 mg intravenously every six hours) and intravenous sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) infusion.
Electrocardiogram (ECG) showed sinus tachycardia without ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) demonstrated a normal left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), with a normal right ventricular systolic function. Abdominal ultrasound (US) showed a normal gallbladder with a normal common bile duct. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) with oral contrast revealed mild peripancreatic fat stranding suggestive of non-complicated pancreatitis and duodenitis and confirmed the presence of a left lung base consolidation.
Blood culture and respiratory culture were negative. Severe acute respiratory syndrome-associated coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) test by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) was negative. Legionella as well as Streptococcus pneumoniae urine antigens, by immunochromatographic assay, were reported to be positive.
On day three of ICU admission, vasopressors were titrated off, metabolic acidosis had resolved, and the patient's creatinine,liver chemistries, and CPK levels were trending downward. The patient's FIO2 requirements were decreased to 45%, and he was extubated on day five of ICU admission. After 48 hours, he was transferred to a medical ward. The patient completed a total of 10 days of intravenous antimicrobial therapy with azithromycin 500 mg daily.
