args:
A 53 year-old man with hematemesis consulted the emergency department. He underwent distal gastrectomy and Billroth II reconstruction for duodenal ulcers 32 years ago. He had bronchial asthma and had been taking steroids for a long time. He had a history of relatively high alcohol consumption, and he smoked 15 cigarettes a day. A general examination revealed that he had anemia. The pulse rate was 126 beats per minute, and the blood pressure was 92/46 mmHg. Regarding his laboratory test, his hemoglobin and blood urea nitrogen levels were 12.1 g/dL and 29.0 mg/dL, respectively, with negative Helicobacter pylori infection serologically. Although a large amount of blood clot was found in the remnant stomach, the source of bleeding could not be determined in the first emergency upper gastrointestinal endoscopy (Fig. a, b). On the 3rd day of hospitalization, melena was observed; hence, follow-up endoscopy was performed, which revealed bleeding from the reddened mucosa at the anastomosis site, and tentative endoscopic hemostasis was performed by clipping the bleeding site (Fig. c, d). Despite medical treatment with transfusion, upper gastrointestinal bleeding symptoms, including hematemesis, melena, and progression of anemia, persisted with significant hemodynamic impairment. On the 4th day of hospitalization, vigorous bleeding was observed when endoscopy was repeated (Fig. e). Because it was difficult to secure a suitable endoscopic view due to bleeding, we decided that performing endoscopic hemostasis was impossible and switched to interventional radiology (IVR). The bleeding had subsided spontaneously when IVR was performed, and re-bleeding was not observed during the IVR procedure although endoscopic stimulation was applied to the predicted bleeding site. Arterial embolization with coiling was performed prophylactically on the distribution area, which was predicted as the bleeding site, using an endoclip as a landmark. However, bleeding symptoms persisted, requiring a total of > 20 units of packed red blood cell transfusion. Based on discussions among physicians, surgeons, and radiologist, surgical treatment was necessary because of the uncertainty whether endoscopic hemostasis and IVR could achieve hemostasis and concerns regarding the deteriorating general conditions caused by frequent severe bleeding. With a written informed consent provided, the patient underwent partial resection of the anastomosis site with Roux-en-Y reconstruction. Consequently, he achieved excellent postoperative recovery.
Histopathological examination revealed the presence of massive hemorrhage. The resected specimen was macroscopically examined, showing a caterpillar-shaped elevated lesion with a maximum diameter of 70 mm on the greater curvature of the remnant stomach. Some of the elevated lesions had erosion, which was observed near the endoclip and was considered as the bleeding site (Fig. a, b). Histopathological findings of the resected specimen detected thickening of the mucosa and hyperplasia of the crypt epithelium, as shown in a weakly magnified image (Fig. a). In the highly magnified image, the mucosa exhibited pseudopyloric gland proliferation with cystic dilation. The muscularis mucosa was entangled and elevated radially into the ridge, and glandular structures penetrated into the submucosal layer from the gap. Hence, a histopathological diagnosis of GCP was considered. We also observed mucosal damages, such as fibrin exudation along with necrosis. Moreover, the resected specimen had no atypical cells (Fig. b, c). However, Elastica van Gieson-stained images showed abnormal muscular vessels that had developed with an irregularly thickened lumen occluded by the fibrous tissue (Fig. d–h). Although we could not detect the culprit blood vessels that triggered massive hemorrhage pathologically, we believe that the blood vessel occlusion caused by fibrous tissue buildup resulted from massive bleeding.
What is the patient's Glasgow-Blatchford Bleeding score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

A 38-year-old man presented with nausea, vomiting and loose stools of 2 days duration. Patient gave history of consumption of a tuber taken from the nearby forest with the intent of deliberate self-harm. Patient has consumed the tuber 6 days prior to the presentation. It was followed by nausea, vomiting, loose stools and haematochezia on 4th day. He also complained haematuria and bleeding from gums and rectum on 5th day. On 6th day he observed scalp hair fall, and easy pluckability of beard, moustache and body hair. He also complained oliguria on day 6. Patient's wife has brought the tuber. It was identified as G. superba. Patient revealed that he had consumed 250 g of the tuber along with water.
Patient had no history of any illness. He was apprehensive, with pulse rate of 120 bpm, blood pressure 70/40 mmHg, febrile, with congestion of conjunctivae. The pillow and bed were full of his hair. Patient himself demonstrated easy pluckability of hair. His investigations were, haemoglobin 6.0 g/dl, total leucocyte count 14,000/mm3, differential count: polymorphs: 60, lymphocytes 30, monocytes 5, eosinophils 5%, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm after first hour, platelet counts 0.24 lakhs/mm3, peripheral smear showed no abnormal cells and no features of haemolysis, there was thrombocytopenia, random blood glucose 120 mg/dl, serum creatinine 5.4 mg/dl, blood urea nitrogen 87.85 mg/dl, serum sodium 127 mEq/L, serum potassium 3.9 mEq/L, serum chloride 88 mEq/L, serum bicarbonate 12.0 mmol/L, serum bilirubin 1.0 mg/dl, serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase 45 U/L, serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase: 65 U/L, serum alkaline phosphatase 125 U/L, serum proteins 6.9 g/dl, serum albumin 4.3 g/dl, serum creatinine kinase 65 IU/L (reference range: 40–200 IU/L), fractional excretion of sodium <1%, urine microscopy showed plenty of red blood cells and urine myoglobin was negative. Ultrasound abdomen revealed normal sized kidneys. Electrocardiogram showed T inversion in V4, V5, and V6. Chest radiograph was normal.

args:
A 33-year-old female presented to the emergency room complaining of painful lesions on her right upper extremity that began approximately two days prior to presentation. She also noted generalized malaise, chills, subjective fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and dyspnea on exertion. She became short of breath with minimal activity. Past medical history was significant for hepatitis C, polysubstance abuse, and nicotine dependence. Approximately six months prior to current illness, she was hospitalized and treated for staphylococcal left knee septic arthritis.
Initial vital signs were BP 144/71 mmHg, pulse 107 beats/min, temperature 98.1°F (36.7°C), respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% breathing ambient air. Physical examination demonstrated petechiae and ecchymosis on all four extremities along with several tender erythematous nodules. She also had jugular venous distention to the angle of the mandible. There were bibasilar rales on pulmonary examination and a 2/6 intensity holodiastolic murmur was present at the 2nd right interspace on cardiac examination. There was mild bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. The remainder of the physical exam was unremarkable. Laboratory data demonstrated serum sodium 131 mEq/L, potassium 3.0 mEq/L, chloride 97 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 21 mg/dl, creatinine 1.01 mg/dl, and glucose of 110 mg/dL. Cardiac troponin was undetectable. White blood cell count was 9.3 k/μl, hemoglobin 14.3 g/dl, hematocrit 41.5%, and platelet count 99 k/μl. Blood cultures grew Methicillin Sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). Electrocardiogram demonstrated accelerated junctional rhythm at 99 beats/minute with retrograde P waves ().
Transthoracic echocardiogram revealed normal LV chamber dimension, wall motion, and left ventricular ejection fraction estimated at 60–65%. The aortic valve was not clearly visualized but aortic regurgitation was noted with continuous wave and color flow Doppler. Transesophageal echocardiogram revealed severe aortic regurgitation; pressure half-time of the regurgitant jet was 320 ms. A large mobile mass measuring 1.3 cm by 1.0 cm was attached to the septal wall of the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) (Figures and ). The mass was attached at the site of a jet lesion where the aortic valve regurgitant jet contacted the LVOT wall ().
She was treated with intravenous antibiotics. Due to the size and location of the vegetation, she was referred for aortic valve replacement and resection of the LVOT vegetation. Pathologic analysis of the native aortic valve revealed a 0.6 × 0.4 cm ovoid perforation of the noncoronary leaflet likely due to prior endocarditis with acute vegetation on the septal surface of the LVOT under the right coronary leaflet. The patient did well postoperatively and was treated adequately with a six-week course of intravenous antibiotics.

args: A 65-year-old male was brought to the intensive care unit from the emergency department after being found in an unresponsive state at home by family members earlier in the day. According to the relatives, he had gradually become more lethargic over the previous 24 hours, failing to engage in normal conversation and eventually ceasing to respond altogether. On initial vital sign assessment, he was found to have a normal body temperature of 37.0 °C, with measurements indicating that his core temperature was not elevated in any meaningful way. His blood pressure recorded in the receiving area showed a systolic of 138 mm Hg and a diastolic of 72 mm Hg. His heart rate hovered at 96 beats per minute, slightly elevated but not exceptionally high for someone under acute stress. In contrast, his breathing pattern appeared more labored than normal, with a frequency of 27 breaths per minute. Clinicians noted that he was on a moderate amount of supplemental oxygen (FiO₂ 44 %) to maintain adequate saturations, with an arterial oxygen tension (PaO₂) of 76 mm Hg, yet he remained difficult to arouse. Laboratory results obtained immediately upon arrival revealed critical details about his condition prior to any therapeutic measures. His blood chemistry displayed an elevated measure of kidney-related waste products, with a serum creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL, a striking finding given that no one in his immediate circle had reported a history of long-standing kidney disease. This raised concerns that his renal function had deteriorated precipitously rather than over a protracted period, suggesting a possible new insult that required prompt intervention. Further supporting the suspicion of an acute event, his serum electrolyte panel demonstrated values on the high side for certain parameters, including a sodium level of 148 mEq/L and a potassium level of 5.0 mEq/L. These abnormal numbers highlighted that he might be facing both a circulatory or perfusion-related problem and a risk of metabolic derangements if the situation were not rapidly addressed. His red blood cell concentration, as inferred from the proportion of cells in his blood volume, stood at a hematocrit of 45 %, while his white cell count was 9,900 /mm³, neither severely elevated nor significantly low. Although that count did not signal a dramatic inflammatory or infectious surge, it did not rule out subtle processes that might be contributing to his presentation. Arterial blood analysis indicated a pH of 7.32, reflecting a mild shift toward acidity, which clinicians attributed to either an inability to excrete bodily acids or inadequate ventilation, though the exact cause needed further clarification and close observation. His Glasgow Coma Score was documented as 5. The team noted that no prior documentation existed of compromised cognition in his past, making this sudden decline even more concerning. Relatives denied any long-standing illness that would result in severely depressed alertness or compromised mental functioning. The sudden drop in his level of consciousness, combined with the evidence of severely deranged kidney function that did not appear to be chronic in origin, led to a working impression that an acute condition was at play—namely, acute renal failure rather than chronic renal failure. Additional imaging studies were scheduled to investigate potential neurological triggers, whereas consults with relevant specialists were arranged to determine if there might be a reversible factor behind his newly discovered renal derangements. The hope was to identify whether a specific toxin, infection, or perfusion deficit had precipitated these abrupt changes, particularly given that he had not received any major intervention or specialized treatment before these measurements were documented. The respiratory team remained vigilant as well, noting that he needed supplemental oxygen in amounts beyond room air, yet by no means an extremely high level, to maintain near-normal blood oxygen content. The plan included further monitoring of his airway, especially given his profoundly reduced consciousness, to prevent secondary complications such as aspiration. Concurrently, the attending clinicians emphasized the importance of cautious fluid strategies, balancing the urgent need to support circulation with the concern that his failing kidneys might not handle an aggressive fluid challenge well. Only after a thorough diagnostic approach, including repeated labs and imaging, would the team finalize an explanation for his unresponsive state and determine how to reverse the precipitants if possible. His family remained at his bedside, offering historical insights that, at least up to the previous week, he had been living independently without any remarkable complaints. In light of this stark change, the ultimate goal was to stabilize his physiologic parameters, identify the driver behind his acute deterioration, and implement supportive therapies to prevent further damage in the short term while devising a more definitive plan once a clear culprit was recognized.

args: A 65-year-old female arrived for urgent evaluation following four days of progressive confusion, reduced appetite, and mounting fatigue that culminated in a fainting episode at her home. According to her family, she had become increasingly forgetful, needed repeated prompting to maintain even basic daily tasks, and was visibly weaker with each passing day. Physical examination found that she was breathing on her own, albeit with notable difficulty, and that she had been placed on a moderate amount of external oxygen supply prior to arrival because she complained of air hunger. Analysis of her arterial blood at the time of triage revealed a partial pressure of oxygen of 75 mm Hg while she inhaled 44 % supplemental oxygen. Her respiratory rate was modestly elevated, but there was no evidence of immediate airway compromise. Pleural auscultation did not uncover focal crackles or wheezes, though her breathing pattern seemed somewhat shallow, and she showed signs of mild chest-wall retractions, particularly when she attempted to speak in longer sentences. Beyond her unstable respiratory findings, her circulatory status raised significant concern. Measurements taken just after arrival indicated that her upper pressure reading was 92 mm Hg with the accompanying lower value at 54 mm Hg, suggesting that her tissues might be underperfused. Her extremities felt cool, and capillary refill was delayed, lending further weight to the notion that she was in the early stages of inadequate circulation. Family members who accompanied her recalled that she had fainted after feeling lightheaded that morning, prompting them to seek immediate medical assistance. It was discovered that she had been receiving certain infusions before arrival, likely started in an out-of-hospital setting, including dopamine at 0.7 µg/kg/min to provide mild cardiac support and norepinephrine at 0.12 µg/kg/min aimed at raising vascular tone. An additional agent, epinephrine at 0.03 µg/kg/min. Laboratory work drawn upon presentation offered a window into her current physiologic condition. Her platelet count rested at 198 000 /µL, a relatively reassuring number that did not signal a major decrease in clotting elements. Her total bilirubin level was 0.9 mg/dL, near the high end of normal, implying that her liver function was not severely compromised, at least at that specific snapshot in time. On the other hand, her kidney function results were distinctly worrisome: she had a creatinine measurement of 1.7 mg/dL, suggesting that her renal performance had slipped, possibly from dehydration, low perfusion, or an evolving process affecting her kidneys. Her daily urine volume, reported based on monitoring shortly before her arrival, was 1400 mL/day, which some clinicians would interpret as borderline or potentially reduced depending on her fluid intake and baseline kidney reserve. Her Glasgow Coma Score was 12. Her muscle strength was difficult to quantify in the setting of her confusion, but she did not show grossly asymmetric findings. No major bruising or rash was noted on her skin; however, she appeared pale, and her hands remained chilly despite the heated environment of the evaluation bay. She did not complain of significant pain, but she winced slightly when her abdomen was palpated in the lower quadrants, though it was unclear whether that was simply due to general discomfort or a focal issue. She recalled no recent fevers, but her recollection was unreliable. While her mental status was worrisome, a more robust neurological examination would have to wait until her cardiovascular state was better stabilized, especially considering the risk of further perfusion deficits impairing her cerebration. She was asked about any past medical issues, but her answers lacked coherence. Family mentioned that she had a history of “blood sugar troubles” in the distant past, though they were unclear if it had been fully resolved or was still managed intermittently. Given the concerning picture of diminished cognition, borderline-to-low urine output, elevated creatinine, borderline blood pressure readings, and a compromised respiratory pattern that required a moderately increased fraction of inspired oxygen, the clinical team moved swiftly to source underlying causes. Preliminary steps included drawing blood cultures, arranging imaging of her chest and abdomen, and conducting a thorough infectious workup to evaluate possible sepsis or another inflammatory condition. Plans were drafted to carefully adjust her intravenous support medications, but the final decision awaited further data on her volume status and cardiovascular responsiveness. Although her situation warranted stringent monitoring, she remained breathing without a tube in her airway, and the medical staff awaited final radiologic interpretations before considering more advanced respiratory interventions. In the interim, her confusion demanded repeated reassessment to ensure she maintained protective airway reflexes. Overall, despite no obvious evidence on initial survey of severe hemorrhage, hepatic crisis, or advanced coagulopathy, her presentation raised alarm for a possible evolving shock state complicated by mild organ dysfunction, reinforcing the necessity of close supervision and specialized care to prevent further deterioration.

args:
A 71-year-old male was admitted due to headache, dizziness, and nausea for three days. He was healthy, except that he had been diagnosed with hypertension two years before. His past medical history was unremarkable. His social history included intermittent alcohol consumption without smoking. The initial vital signs revealed blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg, heart rate of 76 beats per minute, respiration rate of 18 breaths per minute and body temperature of 36.9℃. He was alert upon admission. Physical examination revealed clear lung sound without rale or wheezing. The heart beat was regular without any murmur. There was no tenderness or rebound tenderness in the abdomen. Findings from other physical and neurological examinations were normal. There were no other symptoms such as fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, papilledema or other abnormalities. The initial peripheral blood count showed a white blood cell count of 11,200/mm3 (neutrophils 64.6%, lymphocytes 24.5%, monocytes 6.8%), hemoglobin level of 13.0 g/dL, platelet count of 211,000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 38 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein level of 1.11 mg/dL. Blood chemistry showed a total protein level of 7.6 g/dL, albumin level of 4.3 g/dL, total bilirubin level of 0.9 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase/alanine aminotransferase 27/16 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 77 IU/L, BUN/Cr 9.2/0.8 mg/dL, Na/K 136/3.8 mEq/L, and Ca/P 9.0/4.7 mg/dL. Result of urinalysis was normal. Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) antibody, hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody were all negative. Chest and abdomen radiographs were normal. Brain CT scan and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging showed multiple rim enhancing lesions with surrounding edema, which was presumed to be a brain abscess ().
After admission, the patient underwent a craniotomy with stereotactic aspiration of the abscess. During the operation, yellowish and turbid pus was aspirated and a cerebral abscess was diagnosed. Empirical parenteral antibiotics were started with ceftriaxone 2 g bid and metronidazole 500 mg tid. Gram stain of the pus showed beaded branching gram-positive bacilli. Urease test, esculin hydrolysis test, citrate test, and modified acid-fast stain using 1% acid alcohol were all positive and the bacteria grew at 42℃. Nocardia species were suggested by colonial morphology, Gram-stain, and modified acid fast bacillus (AFB) stain (). By manual, culture was identified as Nocardia species. For the accurate identification of Nocardia species, 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) gene-based polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and sequencing was carried out. Primers of 27F (5'-AGA GTT TGA TCM TGG CTC AG-3') and 1492R (5'-TAC GGY TAC CTT GTT ACG ACT T-3') were used for 16S rRNA gene amplification. Size of 1466 bp 16S rRNA gene amplification sequencing was carried out by Solgent (Solgent Co, Daejeon, Korea) company which uses Big Dye Terminator Cycle Sequencing Kit and ABI PRISM 3730 DNA analyzer (PE Applied Biosystem, Foster, CA, USA). It showed 100% concordance to N. farcinica (GenBank accession number: KC478309) by BLAST Similarity Searching in GenBank. The organism was susceptible to amikacin, imipenem, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), ciprofloxacin, and resistant to erythromycin, kanamycin, ampicillin, gentamicin. After identifying the result of pus culture, the antibiotic regimen was changed to TMP-SMX (15 mg/kg of the TMP component per day) and imipenem 500 mg qid. Due to the persistence of the patient's symptoms, re-imaging was performed two weeks later. On day 24 of admission, the patient underwent a repeated craniotomy with stereotactic aspiration of the abscess because the abscess and surrounding edema have increased than before. Ciprofloxacin (400 mg every 12 h, intravenous) was added to the TMP-SMX treatment and changed from imipenem to meropenem. After performance of repeated surgery and administration of additional antibiotics, we observed improvement of the patient's symptoms. Contrast-enhanced brain CT scan performed three weeks after reoperation, showed substantial resolution of the brain lesion. Meropenem and ciprofloxacin were continued for six weeks. On hospital day 67, he was discharged to go home to complete four months of oral TMP-SMX therapy. On three-month follow-up brain CT after discharge, resolution of the brain abscess was confirmed.

args: A 59-year-old female arrives at the hospital for evaluation before a planned extensive procedure aimed at resolving a chronic abdominal issue that has been affecting her quality of life for several months. She states that she has not undergone any major operations in recent weeks, and she has been carefully monitored over the last few months due to breathlessness and fluid retention episodes that arose during that same timeframe. According to her account, she was admitted for shortness of breath and swelling in the ankles a few weeks ago, at which time her clinicians found evidence of compromised cardiac function manifesting as difficulty maintaining a steady fluid balance. She was treated with medications and dietary adjustments to address the issue, and her breathing has since improved, though she still periodically experiences mild orthopnea that requires her to rest with her upper body slightly elevated at night. The patient denies any recent episodes of infection or lung complications, explaining that she has not had a new onset of fever, productive cough, or other worrisome respiratory symptoms in the current month. She clarifies that she has never been immobilized in a rigid support device for bone or muscular injuries, and specifically notes that she has not sustained any fractures involving the hip or leg. She also denies any events in the past few weeks that might suggest a sudden interruption of blood supply to the brain or a history of widespread injuries requiring urgent intervention. When describing her extremities, she reports a persistent heaviness in her lower legs that tends to worsen toward the end of the day, accompanied by noticeable swelling around her ankles. Despite this, she stands and walks without assistive devices, indicating that she has not been forced to remain in bed for extended periods. She explains that she has maintained a lifestyle that allows her to move around her home and do most daily tasks, albeit at a slower pace on days when she feels more fatigued. She does not recall experiencing any significant lumps, discolorations, or prominent superficial vessel patterns in her legs, and she has not previously sought treatment for any such findings. The patient denies any personal history of blood flow blockages in her deep veins, and she states she has never had issues in which a clot traveled to her lungs, causing chest pain or difficulty breathing. Likewise, she does not describe any relatives who have experienced significant vein or lung complications tied to unusual clotting tendencies. She has not been diagnosed with any known genetic disorders affecting blood components, and she has never been told of any abnormal immune processes that disturb normal circulation or platelet function. She also denies having been informed of high levels of certain amino acids or other crosses in laboratory evaluations that might predispose her to clots. She has not grappled with bowel conditions requiring special treatment or follow-up, and she has not reported any long-standing obstructive lung disease. Furthermore, she states there is no known presence of abnormal cells or concerning masses, emphasizing that recurring screenings and image studies have not detected malignant changes. Her ankles, however, are puffy on inspection, and she occasionally needs to elevate her feet to reduce the stiffness. Cardiac examination reveals no ominous murmurs, but there is some resonation consistent with prior challenges managing fluid, which her providers are keeping under surveillance. She expresses apprehension about undergoing a sizable operation, though she believes it is necessary given her current complaints. She hopes that addressing her abdominal condition surgically will alleviate many of the symptoms that have disrupted her routine. During this visit, her vital signs remain stable, including a normal respiratory rate and blood pressure, and she moves about the examination room under her own power, though somewhat cautiously to manage occasional fatigue. Laboratory data have been largely unremarkable aside from slight fluctuations in her electrolytes, which her team plans to optimize prior to the operation. She is resolute in her decision to proceed, especially as she desires definitive relief from her chronic discomfort, but she also seeks reassurance that her previous cardiovascular issues and leg swelling will be managed proactively. She admits to feeling anxious about the length of hospital stay required for a prolonged procedure, yet she acknowledges that surgical intervention may ultimately help restore her overall wellness and improve her daily functionality in the long term.

args:
A 68-year-old non-smoking Caucasian man initially transferred from a different hospital for evaluation of a brain lesion found on imaging. The patient has a medical history pertinent to BPH, and his family history was relevant for a father with colon cancer.
Six months earlier, his primary care physician (PCP) evaluated him for worsening urinary symptoms and subsequently referred him to a urologist. Upon further evaluation, his prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level was normal at 3 ng/mL, and his digital rectal examination was normal, for which a cystoscopy with prostate GreenLight™ (Boston Scientific, Marlborough, MA) enucleation was performed for BPH diagnosis. Five months later and one month before this admission, he discerned progressive worsening of double vision. His PCP examined and referred him to an ophthalmologist, who diagnosed him with third cranial (oculomotor) and sixth cranial (abducens) nerve palsy, which was subsequently confirmed by a neurologist.
Subsequently, the patient was admitted to the other hospital, and upon admission, he was found to have an acute kidney injury (AKI) and hyperkalemia. Laboratory studies showed white blood cells of 27 x 10^3/µL, hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, and platelet count of 201 K/µL. His chemistry was pertinent for sodium of 134 mmol/L, potassium of 6.5 mmol/L, chloride of 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate level of 10 mmol/L, a glucose of 159 mg/dL, creatinine of 13 mg/dL, uric acid of 19.6 µmol/L, chromogranin A of 656 µg/L, and PSA of 22.67 ng/mL. He received hemodialysis and underwent bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy, after which his AKI resolved. An abdominal CT scan showed findings consistent with diffuse metastatic disease to the lungs, liver, and peritoneum, and brain MRI showed brain lesions. Therefore, he was, subsequently, transferred to our hospital for further care.
Upon evaluation in our hospital, imaging showed findings consistent with earlier recognized lesions at the other hospital (Figures -). The patient was, therefore, seen by a multidisciplinary team of a neurosurgeon, an ophthalmologist, a medical oncologist, and a radiation oncologist. Biopsies of a perirectal mass showed a high-grade neuroendocrine tumor. The oncologist who saw him diagnosed him with metastatic SCC of the lung; therefore, he was started on one cycle of carboplatin/etoposide chemotherapy during his hospitalization, which he tolerated well and was discharged home.
A senior oncologist saw the patient, reviewed all his radiographic images, and questioned the initial diagnosis of lung SCC based on the patient’s clinical presentation of urinary obstruction, hydronephrosis, and AKI. Hence, the original tumor location speculated to be consistent with a high-grade neuroendocrine tumor/SCC originating from the urinary tract. The senior oncologist, therefore, advised cystoscopy for prostate biopsy, which was declined by consulting urologist because the “prostate exam was normal.” Consequently, the senior oncologist sent the patient to a second urologist who performed cystoscopy and informed the patient that everything was normal. The senior oncologist had a thorough discussion to explain the necessity of a meticulous endoscopic urethral examination and prostate biopsy. Subsequently, the patient took the senior oncologist’s advice, went back to the urologist, and underwent a repeat diagnostic cystoscopy. This time, biopsies of the urethra were taken and confirmed a high-grade large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma. 
 
The tissue specimen from this case was compared to the patient’s previous perirectal mass biopsy and was found to have similar features. Immunohistochemical (IHC) stains were negative for CK7, CK20, PSA, and prostate-specific acid phosphatase. Scattered individual cells were positive for nuclear NKX 3.1 and cytoplasmic CK5. Moreover, there was diffuse variable positivity for nuclear p63 with greater than 80% of nuclei were positive for p53 and diffuse strong positivity for CD56, thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF-1), and synaptophysin (Figure ).
Based on the clinical history of disease distribution, the pathologist considered the neoplasm to be likely of either prostate or urinary bladder origin. Subsequently, the senior oncologist reviewed the urethral biopsy, remained suspecting the primary site of cancer to be originating from the prostate. Therefore, he advised for prostate biopsy. Finally, the patient underwent transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy, which confirmed that all 12 cores were positive for prostate SCC (Figure ). The pathology findings confirmed the senior oncologist’s initial surmise that the metastatic cancer origin was from the genitourinary tract, likely involving prostate extending into the urethra.

args:
A 66-year-old male with a past medical history significant for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ischemic stroke, coronary artery disease, and asthma was admitted for cardiac catheterization for worsening angina. He had symptoms of crescendo angina with New York Heart Association (NYHA) class IV symptoms and was referred by his primary cardiologist for coronary angiography with the intent to pursue revascularization as warranted. He had established coronary artery disease in 2012, with pharmacological myocardial perfusion imaging demonstrating inferior infarct and peri-infarct ischemia with an overall preserved systolic function for which medical treatment was pursued. In 2014, he reportedly underwent coronary angiography, which demonstrated a chronic total occlusion of the right coronary artery with grade III collaterals to the right coronary system and moderate non-obstructive disease of the left circumflex artery. In the interim, he ceased tobacco smoking and received guideline-directed medical therapy. He was doing well with optimal medical therapy, which included aspirin and clopidogrel, until three weeks prior to this presentation when he noted the onset of recurrent angina. Angina initially occurred with mild exertion, subsequently progressed to angina at rest, and he was admitted to hospital with unstable crescendo angina.
On admission, his vital signs were stable. His physical exam was unremarkable. A 12-lead electrocardiogram (EKG) demonstrated inferior Q waves and left ventricular hypertrophy without acute ST-T abnormalities (Figure ). Cardiac biomarkers were normal (peak creatinine phosphokinase-MB (CK MB) fraction and troponin T were 9.9 ng/mL (normal range 0.0-10.4) and less than 0.03 ng/mL (<0.03 negative), respectively. Coronary angiography revealed multi-vessel coronary artery disease with a likely culprit lesion involving the proximal left circumflex artery (Videos -). He underwent successful drug-eluting stent (DES) deployment to the proximal left circumflex artery (Video ). PCI was uneventful and guideline-directed medical therapy was continued. Despite long-term adherence to clopidogrel, platelet reaction unit (PRU) was 235. Due to a high on treatment PRU, with levels greater than 208 associated with an increased risk of stent thrombosis [-], a decision was made to transition to ticagrelor. He received a single 180 mg loading dose of ticagrelor. Four hours after receiving ticagrelor, he complained of shortness of breath, throat pain, neck discomfort, and swelling of the tongue. His vital signs remained stable, and he did not exhibit any skin eruption. He demonstrated mild swelling of the tongue and significant swelling of his throat and uvula. No wheezing was noted on exam. He received supplemental oxygen and otolaryngology (ENT) consultation was sought. He was treated with dexamethasone, as well as H1 and H2 histamine blockers. After the first dose of treatment, his symptoms improved. He was closely monitored. The following morning, ENT performed flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy, which revealed a mild edematous pharyngeal area. Based on the clinical scenario, a provisional diagnosis of ticagrelor-induced angioedema was entertained. He did not have any history of allergy to any medications, contrast medium, or heparin. The decision was made to discontinue ticagrelor. His symptoms improved over the next few days with a tapering dose of steroids and antihistamines. The final decision was made to double the dose of clopidogrel and the patient was discharged with 150 mg of clopidogrel and 81 mg of aspirin daily. He has not had any ischemic symptoms or coronary events over a six-month period of follow-up.

args:
A 14-year-old female presented to our pediatric tertiary hospital emergency department (ED) with acute dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain following a presyncopal episode while walking up a flight of stairs. She was persistently tachycardic with a heart rate of 120 beats per minute at rest and higher with minimal exertion, tachypneic (20 breaths per minute), normotensive (mean arterial pressure 80-88 mmHg) and had mild hypoxia with baseline saturations of approximately 90% at rest, decreasing to the mid-80s with mobilisation. She was commenced on supplemental low flow nasal oxygen at a flow rate of three litres per minute which improved her oxygen saturations.
The patient was otherwise well with no history of recent viral illness, surgery, trauma, or periods of immobilisation. She had however commenced a COCP consisting of ethinyloestradiol (20 micrograms) and levonorgestrel (100 micrograms) ten weeks prior. The COCP was prescribed by her local doctor for a sharp pelvic pain which started with menstruation and persisted for three weeks, with complete resolution while taking the COCP. The patient reached menarche at twelve years old and experienced dysmenorrhea for the first three days of menstruation with menorrhagia. Apart from her maternal grandmother having endometriosis, there was no significant family history including thrombophilia or thromboembolic events. The patient denied smoking or recreational drug use. She was a competitive sports player who, within a month of commencing the COCP, experienced dyspnea and intermittent chest pain on exertion. Although these symptoms initially resolved with rest, her time to recovery gradually increased.
In the ED, a chest X-ray (CXR) revealed mild prominence of the pulmonary arteries bilaterally. Blood tests revealed elevated levels of D-dimer, 5.01 mg/L (normal range 0.02–0.49) and cardiac troponin I (cTnI) of 194 Hng/L (normal < 10). Given the history and clinical findings, a computer tomography pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) was performed and confirmed the presence of a saddle PE () with thrombus occluding the right main pulmonary artery, left upper lobe segmental artery, and left interlobar artery. There was also evidence of right heart strain on the CTPA. An urgent echocardiogram revealed systolic dysfunction of the right ventricle (RV) and basal segment dyskinesia, and blood flow through the right pulmonary artery appeared absent. Given the presence of right heart strain, elevated troponin levels, and persistent tachycardia and hypoxia, urgent opinions were sought from multiple pediatric specialists including a respiratory physician, interventional cardiologist, haematologist, and cardiothoracic surgeon.
The patient was administered a 10 milligram (mg) bolus of intravenous alteplase, a recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (tPA), and admitted to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) where she then received an infusion of tPA (0.3 mg/kg/h) for 3 hours. The total dose of tPA administered was 80 mg. Following this, the patient was commenced on an unfractionated heparin (UHF) infusion, targeting an anti-Xa level of 0.3–0.7 U/mL. COCP administration was ceased.
Following thrombolysis, the patient's tachycardia resolved, although she remained tachypneic. A repeat echocardiogram within twelve hours showed improvement in the RV systolic dysfunction and definite blood movement through the right pulmonary artery, with ongoing presence of a significant filling defect occupying the vessel. UHF infusion was continued for 48 hours, after which time anticoagulation was changed to 80 mg subcutaneous enoxaparin, a low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), administered twice a day. Given the significant embolic material seen on the initial CTPA, further discussions ensued regarding the potential benefits and risks of catheter-directed intervention with the aim of decreasing clot burden and potentially reducing the risk of chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH).
A repeat CTPA was performed on the fourth day of admission which revealed a substantial decrease in clot burden, no visible saddle embolus, and reduced right heart strain (). The patient no longer required supplemental oxygen and made a steady recovery. Eight days post admission, she was transitioned to a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC), rivaroxaban, at standard adult dosing (15 mg twice a day for three weeks followed by 20 mg daily), prior to discharge home.
A thrombophilia screen performed was unremarkable (). She was referred to an adolescent gynaecologist to explore suitable alternatives to taking the COCP. Outpatient spirometry, body plethysmography, and diffusing capacity tests performed a month post discharge were unremarkable. She returned to her baseline function with resolution of the shortness of breath on exertion. A CTPA and echocardiogram performed six months post presentation revealed complete resolution of all pathological changes, and rivaroxaban therapy was stopped (). Written consent was obtained from the patient's parent for this case report.

args: A 69-year-old female arrived at the emergency department in a state of moderate confusion and abnormal respiratory effort, having been transported by paramedics who placed a breathing tube to ensure her airway remained protected. According to the individuals who accompanied her, she had experienced two days of progressive lethargy and increasing shortness of breath, culminating in a near-inability to communicate clearly. At presentation, she appeared disoriented although still capable of recognizing her surroundings to some extent, occasionally following basic commands and briefly responding to questions, which suggested a relatively intact but clearly altered mental status. Her Glasgow Coma Score was 14 on arrival. Her blood pressure on arrival hovered at 76/42 mm Hg, accompanied by a faint but steady pulse. Respiratory measurements obtained before any additional therapies were instituted showed that her arterial oxygen level was 64 mm Hg despite receiving an inspired oxygen concentration of 43 %, signaling a degree of compromised oxygen transfer and prompting careful monitoring of her lung mechanics and gas exchange. Her coloring was largely normal, albeit slightly pale, and no significant jaundice was evident on casual inspection. Laboratory evaluations, drawn soon after she was brought in, demonstrated mild but notable findings across multiple organ systems. Her platelet count was 143 000 /µL, a level not severely reduced yet potentially significant when viewed in the context of systemic stress. A further analysis of her liver showed bilirubin of 1.1 mg/dL, which, although not alarmingly high, raised questions of nascent hepatic involvement or an early-phase injury. Meanwhile, creatinine registered 1.5 mg/dL, suggesting an element of renal strain that might reflect either acute injury or an exacerbation of prior mild insufficiency. Despite this concern, her urine output was 1350 mL/day in the period prior to specialized intervention, suggesting that her kidneys were still excreting an appreciable volume of fluid without evident complete shutdown. Given her relatively low blood pressure and uncertain perfusion status, the clinical team noted that she had already been started on at least a couple of medicines known to support cardiovascular function: a low-dose infusion of dopamine 0.6 µg/kg/min to slightly strengthen the heart’s pumping ability, and norepinephrine 0.14 µg/kg/min to sustain vascular tone. No additional infusion specifically aimed at enhancing cardiac contractility was identified. After confirming the stability of her breathing tube and verifying that sedation or other external influences did not entirely explain her reduced alertness, clinicians performed a focused neurological assessment. Although she moved all extremities, her efforts were delayed and she required repeated prompting to remain engaged. Her reflexes were generally intact, and her pupils reacted briskly to light, indicating no profound focal damage that could be discerned at the bedside. Nursing staff confirmed that her vital signs, while less than optimal, were at least temporarily stable, with no precipitous dips in pressure or signs of massively unstable circulation that would mandate immediate escalation of infusion rates. Further imaging studies were planned to evaluate her chest, abdomen, and brain for potential triggers of her symptoms, including a possible hidden infection, occult bleeding, or other acute pathologies. Meanwhile, supportive measures focused on preserving adequate oxygenation, maintaining an airway free of obstructions, and watching for any emergent deterioration in her renal or hepatic indices. Past medical history revealed only episodic visits for minor respiratory infections, and there was no mention of chronic cardiac or advanced renal disease. Family members theorized that she might have been taking less fluid in recent days due to nausea, possibly contributing to her apparent dehydration and compromised kidney function. While in the emergency setting, her healthcare team concentrated on stabilizing her physiology, adjusting the dosage of her supportive medications only when warranted by changes in her blood pressure or evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion. The short-term goals revolved around pinpointing the underlying trigger for her sudden downturn, whether it proved to be an infectious cause, an acute respiratory event, or a metabolic disruption that had not yet been identified. All pertinent specialists were alerted, and an inclusive blend of laboratory work, cultures, and imaging was requested to form a comprehensive understanding of her condition. Although her mental status did not plunge to the point of deep unresponsiveness, the presence of a breathing tube underscored the severity of her respiratory compromise, and there was an ongoing risk of further decline if aggression in management was insufficient. The plan, therefore, centered on ensuring her oxygen levels would safely normalize, her circulatory status would be maintained, and her kidney function would not deteriorate further, all while clinicians investigated any factors that might have nudged her liver indicators out of range. Careful titration of the infusions supporting blood vessel tone and mild cardiac assist would remain essential, and the hospital’s critical care unit was prepared to manage any additional steps, including more robust hemodynamic monitoring, should her status shift unexpectedly.

args:
A 70-year-old man with a past medical history significant for hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage 4, and prostate cancer stage 4 on outpatient chemotherapy at another facility presented to our emergency department with complaints of vomiting and nausea a day prior to admission. He had three episodes of nonbloody, non-bilious, and nonbloody diarrhea as well. The patient had one-day history of productive cough with whitish sputum and subjective fevers. Patient, though adherent with chemotherapy, had admitted that he was not able to take his other home medications. In the ED, patient had a temperature of 103.1degrees F, heart rate of 98 beats per minute, blood pressure of 102/55 mmHg, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, glucose of 121 mg/dl. Physical exam was significant for conscious, alert and oriented to person, place, and time; pink and moist mucosa, right surgical scar at the right hypochondriac, soft, nontender, bowel sounds were present. Pertinent abnormal labs include: white blood cell count (WBC), 14.5 units/mcl (reference range: 4.80-10.80 units/mcl) with neutrophils of 82.1% (reference range: 44%-70%), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 39 mg/dl (reference range: 7-18 mg/dl), creatinine (Cr) 3.8 mg/dl (reference range: 0.7-1.2 mg/dl), calcium (Ca) 7.7 mg/dl (reference range: 8.5-10.1 mg/dl). Chest x-ray showed a focal area of consolidation involving the left midlung. Patient was admitted and managed on the medical floor for sepsis likely secondary hospital acquired pneumonia especially given his immunocompromised state. Patient was started on IV vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam in the emergency department.
During the course of the hospitalization, infectious disease was consulted and they recommended to start the patient on IV cefepime 1 g Q24 hours, oral linezolid 600 mg Q12H, and oral clindamycin 450 mg Q8H. In addition, patient was started on calcium acetate for his hypocalcemia and was continued on sodium bicarbonate 1300 mg Q8H for his AKI on CKD. After two days the cefepime dose was increased to 2 mg. Two days after increasing the dose of cefepime, patient started developing altered mental status. Patient was alert and oriented only to person, incoherent and expressed thoughts like “I would kill myself if I had the chance” so the cefepime was discontinued and within one day the patient was more conversant, denied any suicidal ideations and was alert and oriented to person and place. In addition, the BUN and creatinine (Cr) levels trended up to 41 mg/dl and 5.4 mg/dl. So the calcium acetate was switched to calcium carbonate and calcitriol. After making these changes the Cr level trended down to 3.4 mg/dl. In addition, the corrected Calcium level was at normal levels based on the albumin level of the patient. The mental status improved and patient was subsequently discharged. Table provides the electrolyte levels.

args: A 77-year-old female arrived in the intensive care unit with a high fever and concerning changes in mental status. She had experienced a sudden onset of chills the previous evening, and by the time she was evaluated, her temperature had risen to 39.0 degrees Celsius. Blood pressure readings showed a systolic measurement of 115 mm Hg with a diastolic of 62 mm Hg. Although her pulse was not remarkably elevated at 92 beats per minute, she did exhibit some irregularities in her breathing pattern and was found to have a respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute. Her oxygen requirements had increased significantly, with an inspired oxygen concentration of 55%, higher than one might expect in a standard clinical scenario. Laboratory tests revealed a markedly widened alveolar–arterial oxygen difference measured at 180 mm Hg, pointing to significant compromise in her respiratory function and prompting closer monitoring. Additional lab work showed normal serum sodium at 140 mEq/L and a potassium level of 4.0 mEq/L, suggesting that her electrolytes were relatively balanced save for a newly noted rise in her kidney-related markers. Specifically, her creatinine measured 2.2 mg/dL, signaling a recent decline in renal clearance that was not previously documented in her records and suggesting an abrupt change rather than a long-established abnormality. She also had a white blood cell count of 11,500 per cubic millimeter, indicating a probable inflammatory or infectious process. Hematocrit was measured at 37%, which was slightly lower than her personal baseline but not alarmingly so. When assessed for neurological function, she showed signs of confusion and a diminished capacity to follow commands, consistent with a GCS score of 11. This was a notable drop from her previously reported orientation level, as she had been described by her family as independently functioning with no significant cognitive deficits just days prior. Her arterial blood gas analysis indicated a pH of 7.35, which was on the lower side of normal but still within a tolerable range, obliging the clinical team to keep a close watch on her acid-base status to ensure it did not worsen over time. She had no clear history of conditions that severely compromise immune function, and based on her current presentation, there was no evidence that she was in a postoperative state. Despite her advanced age, she had not undergone any significant procedure in the recent past that might have influenced her immediate condition. The sudden nature of her renal issues, in the context of her fever and infection markers, raised concern for an acute process placing further strain on her kidneys. She did not mention any previous longstanding diagnosis of kidney disease, and her prior medical records lacked any indication of a chronic decline in renal function. At the time of initial ICU evaluation, her decreased alertness and the rise in markers of infection were the primary focus, with close attention to her respiratory status given her high oxygen requirement and significant oxygen gradient. The team planned to proceed with a thorough infectious workup, including blood cultures and imaging, while closely following her hemodynamic parameters, ensuring that any adjustments to supportive measures would be guided by her kidney function, mental status, and laboratory trends in the coming hours.

args:
A 64-year-old female was brought to the emergency department by paramedics after being discovered near her front porch in a disoriented state, having apparently fallen while taking out her trash. According to a neighbor who found her, she had been lying on the ground for an unknown duration before help arrived. On initial evaluation, she was breathing on her own but displayed an unsteady pattern in both her rate and depth of respirations, prompting careful observation for any signs of impending respiratory compromise. There was a noticeable abrasion on her left forearm and mild bruising around her shoulders, which she could not recall obtaining, suggesting a confusion of events leading up to the fall. Her past medical records, as later retrieved, indicated a history of hyperlipidemia and mild chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, with no documented major neurological disorders or prior hospital admissions for head trauma. She also carried a family history of stroke on her paternal side, but there was no indication of personal cerebrovascular problems in her file. Upon arrival in the emergency setting, she maintained an alert gaze without any prompting, looking around and visually fixating on people entering her immediate vicinity, though her attention seemed fleeting at times. When questioned directly, she responded in incomplete sentences and showed a tendency to mix details, occasionally referring to events and individuals out of context. She spoke with a variable tone, sometimes trailing off mid-thought, and when staff members attempted to guide her to focus on orientation questions, she offered vague statements that did not fully match reality. These contradictory and incoherent exchanges suggested a disorganized thought process but did not completely obscure her ability to speak words or recognize that she was in a hospital. Physical examination revealed no remarkable asymmetry in muscle bulk or tone under normal conditions, but there were consistent limitations in purposeful control. When a firm and painful pressure was applied to her upper arm to assess deeper neurological responses, both of her arms became rigid and thrust outward in a manner that did not demonstrate any deliberate aim to escape the stimulus. Instead, the movement seemed to reflect a pattern of resistance rather than an attempt to protect herself or localize the source of discomfort. This response raised concern for a potential underlying brain injury or metabolic abnormality influencing her motor pathways. Additional inspection showed that she had some difficulty tracking staff members’ fingers when asked to follow a moving target, although her pupils were of equal size and reacted promptly to light. Her vital signs included a moderate elevation in systolic blood pressure, a pulse that was slightly fast but regular, and oxygen saturation hovering at an acceptable range despite her inconsistent breaths. No overt signs of airway compromise were present at that moment; however, the medical team remained vigilant due to her uncertain mental status. Laboratory evaluations were initiated to rule out electrolyte imbalances or signs of infection, and imaging studies were ordered to assess for any skull fractures, intracranial bleeding, or other acute lesions that might account for her presentation. While she was able to produce intelligible words intermittently, her train of thought often derailed, emphasizing the need for continuous neurological checks and possible consultation with specialists. Her neighbor reported that she normally lived alone and did not have relatives in the immediate area, so further collateral history was sparse. Given the concerns about her cognitive and motor responses, she was monitored closely for worsening symptoms, with preparations to secure her airway if her breathing showed signs of deterioration. Staff documented her fluctuating ability to communicate and her lack of deliberate motor control under painful provocation, which signaled potential compromise of higher-level neurological function. The plan was to review imaging results urgently, consult neurology, and determine if additional measures, such as advanced imaging or critical care intervention, would be required to treat any evolving condition.

args: A 66-year-old female was brought to the emergency department by local emergency services after being discovered unresponsive in her residence by a concerned neighbor who had not seen her for several days. Upon arrival, she was found lying supine on her living room floor without any obvious signs of recent trauma around her, although there was evidence of spilled medication bottles on a nearby table. According to the neighbor’s account, the patient had been complaining of fatigue and episodes of dizziness in the preceding weeks, but she was reluctant to seek medical care despite gentle urging. Past records, available through a local clinic, revealed a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and intermittent anemia for which she occasionally received supplemental iron therapy, though documentation suggested her adherence to follow-up appointments was sporadic. At the time of the initial examination, she did not appear to open her eyes spontaneously or in response to any attempts at verbal engagement, such as calling her name or speaking loudly beside her. Even when gentle physical contact was applied to her shoulders and arms, there was no blinking or any discernable rise in eyelid position, indicating an absence of visible ocular reaction. Her facial expression remained largely fixed, with no purposeful scanning of the environment or recognition of staff members. Vital signs showed a blood pressure that was moderately elevated; a pulse that was erratic, though not critically high; and a respiratory rate that hovered near the lower end of normal. However, her breathing pattern was shallow and interspersed with occasional brief pauses, prompting concern for potential respiratory compromise. Further assessment of her airway indicated that it remained patent, yet there was a distinct risk of obstruction given her depressed level of responsiveness, so careful suctioning was performed to clear any pooled secretions. When clinical staff attempted to elicit a vocal response by calling her name and lightly shaking her shoulder, her only reactions consisted of prolonged, low-pitched murmurs devoid of recognizable words or phrases. These sounds emerged irregularly, without coherent articulation or the forming of any intelligible syllables. Despite repeated attempts by multiple team members to communicate basic questions, she was unable to provide structured replies, and there was no indication that she could understand or follow simple commands. Neurological evaluation revealed that she displayed a distinct posture when exposed to a noxious stimulus, such as a firm press on the nail bed or a pinch to the trapezius region. Rather than pulling away purposefully or trying to pinpoint and remove the source of discomfort, she drew her arms and wrists inward in an uncoordinated, reflex-like manner, failing to extend them outward or accurately push away the examiner’s hand. Throughout these maneuvers, her lower extremities did not demonstrate any clear attempts at coordinated withdrawal, though intermittent subtle stirring of the legs could be observed. There were no overt signs of external head trauma, but it could not be definitively ruled out without imaging. Her pupils were symmetrical in size and reactive to light, yet no spontaneous or divergent eye movements were noted. Preliminary laboratory tests revealed mild borderline anemia, which was consistent with her medical history, while her serum glucose level was slightly reduced. A toxicology screen was pending, given the possibility of inadvertent or intentional overdose, as suggested by the scattered medication bottles found at her home. Given her diminished ability to protect her airway and the potential for rapid deterioration, the clinical team prepared for airway management while arranging for urgent imaging studies. No definitive diagnosis was made at the time of initial presentation, though the leading considerations included metabolic derangements, possible cerebrovascular events, or sepsis. Additional consultations with neurology and critical care were requested to help guide further interventions.

args: A 34-year-old male patient was admitted with complaints of cough and fever for 2 weeks. He had history of similar complaints in the past in the form of frequent episodes of cough with expectoration for which he was hospitalized and treated with intravenous (IV) antibiotics. He had no history of dyspnea, chest pain or pedal edema. General physical examination was unremarkable except for grade I clubbing. Clinical examination revealed blood pressure of 118/84 mm Hg in right upper limb in supine position with pulse rate of 96/min, regular, normal volume with a normal character with no radio-radial or radio-femoral delay and all peripheral pulses equally palpable. The jugular venous pressure was raised 2 cm above the angle of Louis with a prominent c-v pattern. The apical impulse was in the fifth intercostals space medial to the midclavicular line. The S1 was normal, and S2 was narrowly split with loud P2 component. There was pan-systolic murmur along the left lower sternal border radiating all over the precordium. On systemic examination, respiratory system showed barrel-shaped chest with anteroposterior and transverse diameter ratio of 1, widely spaced ribs and increased resonance to percussion, and breath sounds decreased in intensity symmetrically with prolonged expiration. Rest of the examination was normal. On investigations, total leukocytes were 14,200/mm3, polymorphs 72%, lymphocytes 22%, monocytes 4%, eosinophils 2%, hemoglobin 15.1 g%, platelets 211,000/mm3, ESR 14 mm first h and fasting blood sugar was 98 mg%. Blood culture was negative for any growth. Sputum culture showed streptococcus pneumonia sensitive to amoxicillin, and patient was treated accordingly. On chest radiography, there were bronchiectatic changes. Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with heart rate of 102 and right axis deviation. In lead II, III and aVF, P-wave was tall and peaked with maximum P-wave amplitude > 7 mm and larger than QRS complex in lead II (). P-wave axis was +82°. This was referred as Himalayan P-waves suggestive of right atrial enlargement. Precordial leads showed slow R-wave progression with right ventricular strain pattern (). The 2D transthoracic echocardiography revealed enlarged right atrium and right ventricle with severe tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary arterial hypertension and preserved ejection fraction. args: A 68-year-old woman arrived to the surgical pre-admission clinic this afternoon for evaluation ahead of next week’s elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy planned for recurrent, imaging-proven Hinchey I diverticulitis that has failed multiple courses of outpatient antibiotics over the past two years. She ambulated into the room without assistance, stating that over the last month she continues to work part-time as a substitute teacher, walks her dog one mile daily, and climbs a single flight of stairs to her bedroom without shortness of breath, chest pain, or lower-extremity swelling. She specifically denies any prior episodes of exertional angina, coronary stent placement, or cardiothoracic surgery, and reports no hospitalizations for fluid overload, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Her vascular history is notable for a left hemispheric transient ischemic attack three years ago manifested by transient right arm weakness and expressive aphasia that resolved within twenty minutes; she was subsequently started on low-dose aspirin and high-intensity rosuvastatin by neurology, has remained symptom-free since, and follows up annually for carotid duplex surveillance, the most recent study six months ago showing mild (20%) bilateral internal carotid plaque without hemodynamic significance. Past medical problems include well-controlled primary hypertension diagnosed at age 55, diet-managed type 2 diabetes mellitus first identified on routine screening five years ago with most recent hemoglobin A1c 6.3% on dietary modification alone and no history of oral or injectable glucose-lowering medications, stage 1 chronic kidney disease with stable baseline renal function, hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, and remote appendectomy in her twenties. She quit cigarette smoking three decades ago after a 10 pack-year exposure and currently has an occasional glass of wine on weekends; she denies recreational drug use. Family history is pertinent only for a father who died of stroke at 78 and a mother who is alive at 90 with osteoporosis; there is no familial coronary artery disease before age 60. Current medications include aspirin 81 mg daily, rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly, lisinopril 20 mg each morning, levothyroxine 75 µg daily, and a daily multivitamin; she has no medication allergies. Review of systems today is negative for palpitations, syncope, claudication, intermittent chest pressure, dyspnea on exertion, peripheral edema, or weight gain. On examination, she appears comfortable, conversant, and oriented. Vital signs: blood pressure 132/68 mm Hg bilaterally, heart rate 66 beats/min in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, temperature 36.7 °C orally, and oxygen saturation 98% on ambient air. Body mass index is 29 kg/m². Cardiac auscultation reveals a normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, gallops, or extra sounds; jugular venous pulsation is not elevated, and peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric. Lungs are clear to auscultation with good air movement and no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen is soft with mild left lower quadrant tenderness but no rebound, guarding, or masses. There is no pedal edema, and capillary refill is brisk. Neurological examination shows intact cranial nerves, normal strength and sensation in all extremities, and steady gait. Pre-operative laboratory testing drawn one hour prior demonstrates hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL, hematocrit 38.5%, white blood cell count 5.8 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 235 × 10⁹/L, sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 78 mL/min/1.73 m². Fasting glucose this morning was 104 mg/dL. Liver panel is unremarkable. Thyroid-stimulating hormone is 2.1 µIU/mL. Urinalysis is negative for protein or glucose. Twelve-lead electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm at 64 beats/min, normal axis, no Q waves, and no ST-segment deviations. A resting transthoracic echocardiogram performed last year for hypertension workup documented a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%, normal wall motion, and mild concentric hypertrophy; lung ultrasound today shows no B-lines. Chest radiograph demonstrates clear lung fields and normal cardiac silhouette. Pulmonary function tests were deemed unnecessary given excellent exercise tolerance. Airway evaluation reveals a Mallampati class II oropharynx, full cervical extension, and an inter-incisor distance of 4 cm. She has been counseled on peri-operative medication management: aspirin is to be held five days prior to minimize bleeding risk, while rosuvastatin and lisinopril will be continued up to the morning of surgery; no glucose-lowering agents require adjustment. The patient verbalizes understanding of fasting instructions, wound expectations, and possible complications including bleeding, infection, venous thromboembolism, pulmonary compromise, and neurologic or cardiac events. She consents to proceed, and no additional cardiac testing is indicated based on stable functional capacity and lack of ischemic symptoms.

args: A 59-year-old male presented with black tarry stools for two weeks and progressive abdominal distension over the past four years. The patient was stabilized with octreotide, a proton pump inhibitor infusion, and blood transfusions. He was subsequently started on a non-selective beta-blocker to reduce portal pressure.

Triage vital signs before any interventions show a temperature of 36.9°C, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, pulse 81 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Cardiac examination reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally with no wheezes or crackles, and there is no accessory muscle use. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no organomegaly. Extremity exam reveals asymmetry of the calves with the left measuring approximately 2 cm greater in circumference than the right at mid-calf, mild pitting edema over the left lower leg, and focal tenderness along the posterior calf; the right lower extremity is without edema or tenderness. There are no skin color changes or warmth of the right leg; the left leg is warm but not erythematous. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable and symmetric. There is no peripheral cyanosis or clubbing noted. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with intact strength and sensation throughout, and he is oriented and cooperative. He denies pleuritic chest pain in the past, syncope, or prior episodes of sudden unexplained shortness of breath. He recalls the prior leg clot occurring after a period of decreased activity but reports he has been generally active recently, doing light household tasks and walking daily. Review of systems is otherwise negative for palpitations, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, abdominal pain, vomiting, or new rashes. An electrocardiogram obtained on arrival shows normal sinus rhythm in the low 80s without ischemic changes or right heart strain pattern. Bedside pulse oximetry remains at 97% on ambient air throughout the initial assessment, including with ambulation in the hallway, with no desaturation observed. No wheezing or focal crackles are elicited on repeated lung exam. There is no chest wall tenderness to palpation. The patient has not yet received analgesics, anticoagulation, or other therapy at the time these observations were made. Nursing notes confirm stable vital signs since triage and no hemoptysis reported. His prior outside records available through the health information exchange corroborate a history of a diagnosed lower extremity venous clot previously, with completion of a finite course of treatment and no documented episodes of clots in the lungs. Given the combination of pleuritic chest discomfort, exertional dyspnea, and newly apparent asymmetry of the lower extremities on exam, thromboembolic disease remains under consideration. However, he is hemodynamically stable with a pulse of 81 beats per minute and normal oxygenation on room air, and he presently denies any cough productive of blood. He remains comfortable, cooperative with the examination, and is able to ambulate in the department without change in symptoms during the initial evaluation.

args: A 47-year-old man presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of progressive swelling and aching discomfort of his left lower extremity that began abruptly two days ago while he was at work and has worsened despite intermittent rest and elevation. He reports the entire limb, from groin to ankle, feeling “tight” with a heavy sensation by the end of the day, and he has noticed that his trousers now fit snugly on that side while remaining loose on the right. He denies preceding trauma, overuse injury, recent sprain, insect bite, or skin infection, and he has not experienced fevers, chills, or constitutional symptoms. Measurement in triage showed a 4.2 cm increase in maximal calf circumference on the left compared with the contralateral leg, confirming objective asymmetry. Physical examination reveals diffuse enlargement of the left leg with visible dilation of superficial, non-varicose collateral veins tracking over the medial thigh and calf; these channels become particularly prominent when the limb is placed in a dependent position. Moderate pitting edema is elicited along the anterior tibial surface and extends from the dorsum of the foot up to the proximal thigh, yet the right leg remains entirely without edema. Skin temperature is mildly elevated on the affected side, but overlying color is preserved without erythema or cellulitic changes. Distal pulses are palpable bilaterally, and capillary refill is normal. He does not localize tenderness to a single cord-like structure on deep palpation, but rather endorses a generalized dull ache most notable along the posterior calf when standing. He ambulates independently without limping, and neurologic testing shows intact motor strength and sensation; there is no history of stroke, spinal cord pathology, or immobilization in a cast. The patient works as an accountant, spends most days seated but rises routinely, and denies any prolonged travel or recent confinement to bed; he was at work all week with no sick days and has not been hospitalized. He has never experienced a clotting event before, has no known thrombophilia, and is not on anticoagulation. Surgical history is limited to an uncomplicated appendectomy performed many years ago, and he has undergone no operative procedures or invasive interventions within the last three months. He takes an antihypertensive and a statin for well-controlled chronic conditions, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol only socially. Family background is notable for coronary artery disease in first-degree relatives but no venous thromboembolic disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative; he denies chest pain, shortness of breath, pleuritic discomfort, hemoptysis, persistent cough, or flank pain. Current vital signs are stable: temperature 36.9 °C, blood pressure 132/78 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are unremarkable. Abdomen is soft with no masses or hepatosplenomegaly. A focused musculoskeletal exam shows full range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle, with no joint effusion or tenderness. Dermatologic survey reveals no rashes, ulcers, or stasis changes. There is no palpable inguinal adenopathy. Given the uniform swelling of the entire extremity, the measured calf asymmetry greater than 3 cm, the presence of engorged collateral superficial veins, and pitting edema isolated to the symptomatic limb, thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system is high on the differential, while alternative explanations such as musculoskeletal strain, lymphatic insufficiency, heart failure, or infection appear less plausible in this otherwise healthy, afebrile, ambulatory individual.
